Goal:

- When a metric moves, identify the most likely cause and the next experiment.

Build:

- Inputs:
  - experiment graph
  - deploy history
  - route policy changes
  - model changes
  - frontend changes
  - pricing changes
  - support tickets
  - runtime traces
- Output:
  - likely cause set
  - ruled-out causes
  - confidence
  - next discriminating experiment

Smoke test:

- Conversion drops after both hero copy and pricing changes.
- Fail if investigator attributes all effect to the last deploy.

Acceptance:

- Postmortems become faster and more scientific.
- Product teams get a next experiment, not speculation.
- Root-cause evidence is saved for future priors.

### W1519: Research-To-Code Compiler

Goal:

- Convert accepted research contracts into implementation tickets that an agent can execute.

Build:

- Inputs:
  - experiment result
  - proof packet
  - affected product surface
  - module ownership
  - API contract
  - test contract
  - docs contract
  - claim contract
- Output:
  - implementation epic
  - file ownership
  - required tests
  - acceptance smoke
  - rollback plan

Smoke test:

- Research result says "distillation-aware quantization wins."
- Fail if compiler creates a vague ticket instead of module, API, test, and artifact-manifest tasks.

Acceptance:

- Research does not die in documents.
- Implementation agents receive atomic build instructions.
- Tests are derived from the original hypothesis.

### W1520: Continuous Benchmark Freshness Scheduler

Goal:

- Keep public and private benchmark data fresh enough to trust.

Build:

- Schedule by:
  - model churn
  - customer task drift
  - calibration drift
  - competitor release
  - device runtime update
  - regulatory change
  - claim importance
- Mark results stale when freshness expires.

Smoke test:

- A benchmark is older than its freshness window but still feeds homepage copy.
- Fail if claim remains active.

Acceptance:

- No old benchmark silently powers current claims.
- Re-run cadence is visible.
- The product graph can report benchmark freshness.

### W1521: Frontier Competitor Shadow Lab

Goal:

- Continuously compare Kolm artifacts against frontier providers and open runtimes.

Build:

- Shadow against:
  - OpenAI
  - Anthropic
  - Gemini
  - Mistral
  - Cohere
  - DeepSeek
  - local GGUF
  - device-native models
  - enterprise AI OS platforms
- Compare:
  - K-Score
  - cost
  - latency
  - fallback rate
  - privacy posture
  - auditability
  - deployment reach

Smoke test:

- Competitor improves on a slice that Kolm claims as strong.
- Fail if the moat report stays green.

Acceptance:

- Competitive claims are current.
- Roadmap priorities respond to real threats.
- Kolm knows where it wins and where it does not.

### W1522: Enterprise Outcome Instrumentation

Goal:

- Tie technical metrics to business outcomes customers care about.

Build:

- Outcome classes:
  - support ticket deflection
  - manual review reduction
  - claims processing time
  - audit prep time
  - cloud spend
  - developer migration time
  - privacy incidents avoided
  - device coverage
- Capture only customer-approved aggregates.

Smoke test:

- Technical K-Score improves but customer review time does not move.
- Fail if case study claims operational value.

Acceptance:

- Case studies are credible.
- ROI calculator learns from real outcomes.
- Product priorities optimize customer value.

### W1523: Model And Runtime Interaction Matrix

Goal:

- Understand when model changes and runtime changes interact nonlinearly.

Build:

- Matrix dimensions:
  - model family
  - student size
  - quant format
  - attention profile
  - layer-skip profile
  - device class
  - task slice
  - route policy
- Detect interaction terms that average metrics hide.

Smoke test:

- Quantization is safe on one student but unsafe on another under layer skipping.
- Fail if matrix reports independent effects only.

Acceptance:

- Runtime optimization is not treated as a plug-in afterthought.
- Deployment plans reflect interaction risks.
- Compiler choices are conditional.

### W1524: Automated Kill Criteria Executor

Goal:

- Stop bad directions early even when they are exciting.

Build:

- Kill triggers:
  - no plausible path to K-Score lift
  - safety guardrail failure
  - cost exceeds value of information
  - low-fidelity bias too high
  - no customer outcome link
  - better competitor path exists
  - unverifiable claim
- Kill output:
  - reason
  - evidence
  - preserved learnings
  - future reopen condition

Smoke test:

- Novel runtime idea improves latency by 5 percent but increases critical failure by 2 percent.
- Fail if novelty prevents kill.

Acceptance:

- The lab is ruthless.
- Killing work improves velocity.
- Reopen conditions prevent permanent dogma.

### W1525: Research Governance Review Packet

Goal:

- Give executives, auditors, and implementation agents one packet per major decision.

Build:

- Packet sections:
  - hypothesis
  - experiment design
  - data rights
  - result
  - uncertainty
  - causal validity
  - safety impact
  - customer impact
  - implementation plan
  - claim scope
  - rollback plan

Smoke test:

- Packet omits data rights for customer-derived traces.
- Fail governance review.

Acceptance:

- Research decisions can be trusted by non-research stakeholders.
- Legal/compliance risk is visible before publication.
- Implementation has enough detail to act.

### W1526: Automated Replication Agent

Goal:

- Re-run important results in independent conditions before promotion.

Build:

- Replication modes:
  - same code, same data
  - same code, new seed
  - same data, independent implementation
  - new public dataset
  - private holdout
  - new device
  - different teacher
- Require replication strength by claim class.

Smoke test:

- A result wins only under one seed.
- Fail if promoted beyond experimental preview.

Acceptance:

- Publication-grade results are replicated.
- Customer claims do not depend on lucky runs.
- Replication failures update priors.

### W1527: Research Data Rights Firewall

Goal:

- Prevent the research flywheel from consuming data it is not allowed to use.

Build:

- Enforce policy at:
  - trace ingestion
  - experiment creation
  - benchmark export
  - public dataset release
  - model training
  - cross-tenant aggregation
  - paper publication
- Every datum has:
  - tenant
  - consent class
  - retention window
  - allowed uses
  - export restrictions
  - deletion lineage

Smoke test:

- Researcher tries to include opt-out customer traces in calibration.
- Fail before experiment starts.

Acceptance:

- The moat is built only from allowed data.
- Deletion requests propagate through derived datasets.
- Publication cannot leak private traces.

### W1528: Ultimate Research Control Objective

Goal:

- Define the top-level objective for Kolm's autonomous research system.

Objective:

```text
maximize:
  expected_customer_value_lift
  + expected_kscore_lift
  + expected_cost_reduction
  + expected_safety_improvement
  + expected_moat_creation
  + expected_claim_unlock
  + expected_learning_value

minimize:
  experiment_cost
  + time_to_decision
  + safety_risk
  + privacy_risk
  + false_claim_risk
  + opportunity_cost
```

Build:

- The controller chooses the next best action across the entire company:
  - research experiment
  - implementation task
  - benchmark run
  - label purchase
  - customer pilot
  - runtime probe
  - claim update
  - kill decision

Smoke test:

- Flashy new research has low customer value and high uncertainty while a boring calibration run unlocks an enterprise claim.
- Fail if the system chooses the flashy work.

Acceptance:

- The research system optimizes the business and product, not researcher taste.
- Every metric improvement has a proof path.
- Kolm learns faster than competitors because every action compounds.

### W1500-W1528 Experimentation Smoke Matrix

| Wave | Simulated fixture | Metric moved | Failure condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1500 | vague hypothesis | research quality | accepted without metric |
| W1501 | biased low fidelity | spend efficiency | production jump |
| W1502 | cheap claim unlock | learning per dollar | ranked equally |
| W1503 | cost win plus failure | causal clarity | cost-only report |
| W1504 | unsupported new route | offline validity | fake lift |
| W1505 | unsafe savings router | safe promotion | canary allowed |
| W1506 | repeated peeking | statistical validity | fixed-horizon claim |
| W1507 | treatment-affected covariate | bias control | covariate used |
| W1508 | overlapping experiments | attribution | no interference warning |
| W1509 | public overfit | benchmark trust | promoted score |
| W1510 | repeated failed recipe | velocity | no warning |
| W1511 | lost refusal feature | safety diagnosis | aggregate-only pass |
| W1512 | risky low-volume slice | label efficiency | volume-only spend |
| W1513 | unrealistic adversarial set | benchmark quality | dominates score |
| W1514 | missing runtime version | replayability | deterministic pass |
| W1515 | weak savings claim | claim honesty | public claim allowed |
| W1516 | ROI drift | surface consistency | independent pass |
| W1517 | ownerless evidence debt | readiness | ledger green |
| W1518 | concurrent deploys | root cause | last-change blame |
| W1519 | research result | implementation quality | vague ticket |
| W1520 | stale benchmark | freshness | claim active |
| W1521 | competitor catches up | strategy | moat still green |
| W1522 | K-Score no outcome | case study honesty | operational claim |
| W1523 | quant plus skip interaction | runtime safety | independent effects |
| W1524 | novel unsafe idea | focus | not killed |
| W1525 | missing data rights | governance | packet accepted |
| W1526 | one-seed win | replication | promoted |
| W1527 | opt-out trace | data trust | experiment starts |
| W1528 | flashy low-value work | control objective | chosen |

### Experimentation Build Order

Build in this order:

1. W1500 Experiment Type System.
2. W1514 Experiment Reproducibility Capsule.
3. W1517 Research Debt Ledger.
4. W1515 Claim Promotion Gate.
5. W1501 Multi-Fidelity Experiment Planner.
6. W1502 Cost-Aware Bayesian Research Scheduler.
7. W1503 Causal Metric DAG.
8. W1504 Counterfactual K-Score Estimator.
9. W1505 Safe Policy Improvement Gate.
10. W1506 Anytime-Valid Research Monitor.
11. W1507 Variance Reduction For K-Score Experiments.
12. W1512 Active Label Acquisition Engine.
13. W1519 Research-To-Code Compiler.
14. W1528 Ultimate Research Control Objective.

This is the layer that lets every other invention compound. Without it, Kolm is a pile of good ideas. With it, Kolm becomes a machine that continuously discovers which ideas are true, which are valuable, which are safe, which are claimable, and which should die.

## Privacy-Preserving Federated Data Moat Layer

Kolm's deepest moat cannot be "we have everyone's raw prompts." That would be legally fragile, strategically brittle, and unacceptable to enterprise buyers. The better moat is a privacy-preserving learning network where each customer keeps raw data under its own policy, while Kolm learns aggregate failure signatures, calibration priors, runtime profiles, and distillation recipes that improve every tenant without leaking any tenant.

This layer defines the data architecture that lets Kolm improve across enterprises while saying something stronger than "trust us." The final product should support:

- tenant-owned raw traces
- local task fingerprints
- federated K-Score calibration
- secure aggregate failure signatures
- confidential teacher evaluation
- differential privacy budgets
- membership and memorization risk gates
- deletion lineage through derived statistics
- data-rights-aware research and publication

The moat is not raw data. The moat is the collection of allowed, audited, privacy-scoped statistical signals that convert enterprise usage into better distillation, routing, quantization, safety, and device deployment.

### W1600: Data Rights Lattice

Goal:

- Represent every piece of telemetry, label, trace, artifact, and derived statistic by what it is legally and technically allowed to do.

Build:

- Rights dimensions:
  - raw storage allowed
  - local training allowed
  - tenant-private evaluation allowed
  - cross-tenant aggregate allowed
  - public benchmark allowed
  - paper publication allowed
  - deletion required
  - retention window
  - geographic boundary
  - subprocessors allowed
  - human review allowed
  - teacher API export allowed
- Rights object:

```json
{
  "tenant_id": "tenant_123",
  "data_class": "prompt_trace",
  "allowed_uses": ["tenant_eval", "local_distillation"],
  "forbidden_uses": ["cross_tenant_raw", "public_release"],
  "retention_days": 30,
  "region": "eu",
  "derived_signal_policy": "secure_aggregate_only"
}
```

Smoke test:

- Attempt to use tenant-private prompts in a public calibration dataset.
- Fail before the experiment is created.

Acceptance:

- Every downstream signal has rights lineage.
- Claims, experiments, and training jobs query rights before access.
- Deletion requests propagate to derived artifacts or mark them non-releasable.

### W1601: Tenant-Local Trace Vault

Goal:

- Keep raw production traces inside tenant control while still enabling measurement.

Build:

- Components:
  - local encrypted trace store
  - tenant-held key envelope
  - policy evaluator
  - local redaction membrane
  - local K-Score fixture builder
  - export manifest generator
- Raw prompts, completions, documents, tool outputs, and human labels remain local unless policy explicitly permits export.
- Kolm receives only approved artifacts:
  - aggregate histograms
  - calibrated score summaries
  - failure signatures
  - activation summaries
  - runtime profiles
  - proof packets

Smoke test:

- Local trace vault has raw PHI and policy says aggregate-only.
- Fail if any raw string appears in outbound payload.

Acceptance:

- Tenant can audit all outbound signals.
- Kolm can improve global priors without raw tenant data.
- Account UI can show which data stayed local and which aggregates were shared.

### W1602: Secure Aggregate Failure Signature Protocol

Goal:

- Learn cross-tenant failure patterns without seeing tenant-level failures.

Build:

- Each tenant maps failures into a controlled taxonomy:
  - hallucination
  - refusal error
  - policy boundary miss
  - extraction miss
  - citation miss
  - tool argument error
  - route overconfidence
  - quantization regression
  - layer-skip regression
  - device runtime failure
- Tenants submit masked vectors.
- Aggregator sees only sums after a minimum cohort threshold.
- Any bucket below threshold is suppressed.

Smoke test:

- One tenant reports a rare medical failure in a small cohort.
- Fail if global report exposes the unique bucket.

Acceptance:

- Failure learning scales across tenants.
- Rare events are protected by k-anonymity and secure aggregation.
- Global roadmap can prioritize failures without exposing customers.

### W1603: Federated K-Score Calibration

Goal:

- Improve K-Score weights and confidence intervals from decentralized labels.

Build:

- Each tenant computes local calibration residuals:
  - predicted K-Score
  - human preference outcome
  - task slice
  - severity slice
  - runtime slice
  - uncertainty
- Only aggregate sufficient statistics leave tenant:
  - counts
  - weighted sums
  - covariance terms
  - calibration error bins
  - uncertainty histograms
- Global calibrator updates public and private priors only when rights permit.

Smoke test:

- Tenant A has labels for a prohibited use class.
- Fail if those labels influence global K-Score weights.

Acceptance:

- K-Score learns from enterprise outcomes without centralizing labels.
- Calibration releases disclose which aggregate classes were used.
- Tenants can opt out without breaking local scoring.

### W1604: Federated Distillation Recipe Learning

Goal:

- Learn better distillation recipes across tenants without sharing raw traces or model weights.

Build:

- Local nodes evaluate recipe candidates:
  - adaptive teaching mode
  - teacher committee choice
  - student architecture
  - loss weights
  - route threshold
  - quant profile
- Nodes return privacy-scoped outcome vectors:
  - K-Score delta
  - failure delta
  - runtime delta
  - cost delta
  - confidence
  - rights class
- Global scheduler updates recipe priors using aggregate outcomes.

Smoke test:

- One high-value tenant dominates recipe selection.
- Fail if global prior reveals or overfits that tenant's domain.

Acceptance:

- Recipe priors improve with more tenants.
- Raw task data never leaves tenant by default.
- Tenant-specific priors can remain private while global priors learn generic structure.

### W1605: Federated Chain-Of-Thought Distillation Guard

Goal:

- Use rationale-style distillation only when it does not leak private reasoning traces or memorized content.

Build:

- Guard checks:
  - rationale contains raw private data
  - rationale reveals policy-sensitive internal logic
  - rationale includes unique facts
  - rationale improves memorization attack success
  - rationale is necessary for the target task
- Replace unsafe rationales with:
  - abstract skill tags
  - structured task steps
  - private-local-only traces
  - public synthetic rationales

Smoke test:

- Teacher rationale includes a patient-specific fact.
- Fail if rationale enters student training or cross-tenant aggregate.

Acceptance:

- Reasoning supervision is privacy-scoped.
- Public papers can discuss rationales without leaking tenant examples.
- Students learn task structure without memorizing customer data.

### W1606: Membership And Memorization Risk Scanner

Goal:

- Detect whether distillation transferred private membership or memorized content from teacher or training data into student artifacts.

Build:

- Tests:
  - membership inference
  - canary extraction
  - prompt reconstruction
  - nearest-neighbor leakage
  - rare sequence completion
  - rationale leakage
  - per-block privacy risk
- Score by:
  - task slice
  - data class
  - model block
  - distillation objective
  - student size
  - teacher family

Smoke test:

- Insert a synthetic tenant canary into local training.
- Fail if student emits the canary or if scanner misses elevated risk.

Acceptance:

- No artifact ships with unknown membership risk.
- Privacy risk is part of K-Score and production readiness.
- Distillation objectives are selected with privacy risk, not quality alone.

### W1607: Differential Privacy Budget Broker

Goal:

- Make privacy budget a first-class resource like GPU, teacher tokens, and label spend.

Build:

- Track privacy budget by:
  - tenant
  - data class
  - task slice
  - experiment
  - aggregate release
  - model update
  - calibration release
- Broker decides:
  - whether a query is allowed
  - how much noise to add
  - whether utility remains sufficient
  - when budget is exhausted
- Expose:

```json
{
  "epsilon_spent": 0.7,
  "epsilon_limit": 2.0,
  "delta": 1e-6,
  "release_allowed": true,
  "utility_warning": false
}
```

Smoke test:

- Repeated aggregate releases attempt to exceed tenant budget.
- Fail if release proceeds.

Acceptance:

- Privacy loss is visible and enforceable.
- Research plans account for privacy budget before running.
- Tenant trust pages can show privacy expenditure.

### W1608: Confidential Teacher Evaluation Enclave

Goal:

- Let tenants evaluate teacher models, K-Score, and route policies over sensitive data inside attested compute.

Build:

- Enclave properties:
  - measured image
  - signed evaluator code
  - remote attestation
  - tenant-controlled key release
  - no outbound raw data
  - approved aggregate output only
- Uses:
  - teacher comparison
  - private holdout scoring
  - route threshold calibration
  - red-team scoring
  - membership risk scanning

Smoke test:

- Evaluator image hash differs from the approved measurement.
- Fail key release and block evaluation.

Acceptance:

- Customers can run sensitive evals without exposing data to Kolm operators.
- Enclave output is signed and auditable.
- Confidential evaluation unlocks regulated adoption.

### W1609: Confidential GPU Distillation Profile

Goal:

- Support GPU-backed confidential workloads where CPU enclaves are insufficient.

Build:

- Profiles:
  - CPU enclave scoring
  - confidential GPU inference
  - confidential GPU fine-tuning
  - BYOC private cluster
  - on-prem air-gapped run
- Evidence:
  - hardware attestation
  - driver/runtime measurement
  - model hash
  - data policy
  - output policy
- Use confidential GPU only when model size, latency, or teacher evaluation requires it.

Smoke test:

- Confidential GPU attestation is missing.
- Fail any claim that sensitive data was processed in confidential compute.

Acceptance:

- Regulated customers get a path for private teacher evaluation and distillation.
- Claims distinguish CPU enclave, GPU confidential, BYOC, and ordinary cloud.
- Runtime profile records the trust boundary.

### W1610: Cross-Tenant Prior Firewall

Goal:

- Let global learning help tenants without letting one tenant infer another.

Build:

- Prior classes:
  - public prior
  - global aggregate prior
  - industry prior
  - tenant-private prior
  - device prior
  - model-family prior
- Firewall checks:
  - cohort size
  - dominance by one tenant
  - industry sensitivity
  - similarity attack risk
  - rights compatibility
- Apply shrinkage to prevent small cohorts from overfitting.

Smoke test:

- A cohort has five tenants but one contributes 95 percent of signal.
- Fail release of a global prior without dominance suppression.

Acceptance:

- Customers benefit from network effects safely.
- Global priors do not become covert data leakage.
- Product copy can honestly claim aggregate learning boundaries.

### W1611: Data Deletion Lineage Engine

Goal:

- Make deletion meaningful even after data has produced derived artifacts.

Build:

- Track lineage:
  - raw trace
  - redacted trace
  - label
  - local fixture
  - aggregate vector
  - calibration update
  - distillation run
  - artifact
  - public benchmark
- Deletion actions:
  - remove raw data
  - remove local fixture
  - subtract from aggregate if possible
  - mark aggregate non-releasable if subtraction impossible
  - retire artifact if derived risk is material
  - preserve non-personal aggregate only if policy allows

Smoke test:

- Tenant requests deletion after contributing to a calibration release.
- Fail if lineage cannot report what changed and what remains.

Acceptance:

- Data rights are operational, not contractual theater.
- Enterprise deletion workflows have evidence.
- Public releases cannot silently depend on deleted data if policy forbids it.

### W1612: Federated Runtime Profile Learning

Goal:

- Learn device and runtime optimization profiles from fleets without uploading raw user content.

Build:

- Local fleet reports:
  - p50 and p95 latency
  - memory peak
  - energy estimate
  - quantization error histogram
  - layer skip accept rate
  - route fallback rate
  - crash class
  - device class
- Secure aggregate by:
  - artifact family
  - device class
  - runtime version
  - task slice

Smoke test:

- One hospital has a unique device/runtime combination.
- Fail if public runtime matrix exposes its profile.

Acceptance:

- Device deployment improves across customers.
- Runtime compiler receives real fleet data.
- Customer-specific runtime data remains protected.

### W1613: Privacy-Preserving Activation Profile Exchange

Goal:

- Share activation statistics useful for quantization without leaking content.

Build:

- Exchange only:
  - clipped channel moments
  - outlier counts
  - quantile sketches
  - sparsity histograms
  - layer sensitivity bins
  - task-slice labels after rights check
- Add:
  - noise where needed
  - minimum cohort thresholds
  - tenant dominance checks
  - canary leakage tests

Smoke test:

- A rare token causes a unique activation outlier.
- Fail if profile export reveals the token or tenant.

Acceptance:

- Quantization compiler learns from fleet behavior.
- Activation exports have privacy accounting.
- Runtime wins compound without raw prompt access.

### W1614: Private Benchmark Escrow

Goal:

- Let enterprises benchmark Kolm on sensitive tasks without releasing data.

Build:

- Escrow modes:
  - tenant-local benchmark runner
  - confidential enclave benchmark
  - third-party auditor runner
  - redacted proof export
- Outputs:
  - signed K-Score card
  - dataset rights summary
  - leakage test result
  - runtime matrix
  - claim scope

Smoke test:

- Benchmark result lacks dataset rights summary.
- Fail if it can feed sales or public copy.

Acceptance:

- Case studies can cite private results honestly.
- Auditor can verify score without seeing raw data.
- Sales claims stay scoped to allowed evidence.

### W1615: Federated Red-Team Network

Goal:

- Learn attack patterns across tenants without exposing incident details.

Build:

- Local red-team packs produce:
  - attack class hit/miss
  - guardrail failure class
  - tool risk class
  - memory poisoning class
  - data exfil path class
  - severity
  - mitigated status
- Aggregate only after:
  - incident redaction
  - cohort threshold
  - severity binning
  - legal review for reportable events

Smoke test:

- A tenant-specific incident text appears in a global red-team report.
- Fail export.

Acceptance:

- Every tenant benefits from attacks seen elsewhere.
- Incidents are not exposed.
- Security roadmap is driven by aggregate evidence.

### W1616: Consent-Aware Teacher API Broker

Goal:

- Prevent teacher API calls from violating tenant data policy.

Build:

- Before any teacher call, broker checks:
  - provider allowed
  - data class allowed
  - region allowed
  - retention terms allowed
  - training opt-out available
  - BAA or DPA required
  - redaction required
  - synthetic prompt required
- Broker can choose:
  - local model
  - BYOC teacher
  - confidential teacher
  - public synthetic teacher
  - no teacher call

Smoke test:

- Tenant forbids external teacher APIs for PHI.
- Fail if distillation job calls a hosted teacher with raw PHI.

Acceptance:

- Provider routing respects data rights.
- Distillation planner cannot bypass policy.
- Account UI can show which teachers are allowed per data class.

### W1617: Federated Synthetic Data Foundry

Goal:

- Generate public-safe training and benchmark data from aggregate patterns, not raw tenant records.

Build:

- Inputs:
  - aggregate failure signatures
  - task schemas
  - public ontologies
  - rights-safe label distributions
  - red-team classes
- Generator constraints:
  - no nearest-neighbor match to tenant data
  - no rare identifier reproduction
  - no private distribution leak
  - realism score
  - utility score
  - privacy score

Smoke test:

- Synthetic sample closely matches a tenant trace.
- Fail release and lower generator trust.

Acceptance:

- Public benchmarks grow without leaking customers.
- Synthetic data is labeled with provenance and privacy checks.
- Distillation uses synthetic data only where utility is proven.

### W1618: Private-To-Public Benchmark Promotion Gate

Goal:

- Decide when a benchmark slice can move from private evidence to public asset.

Build:

- Gate criteria:
  - rights allow release
  - no raw tenant content
  - no unique distribution leak
  - enough tenant diversity
  - synthetic or public-source lineage
  - reproducible scoring
  - legal review complete

Smoke test:

- Benchmark slice is high-value but sourced from one enterprise.
- Fail public promotion.

Acceptance:

- Public assets grow from safe evidence.
- Customers can approve or deny contribution.
- Marketing claims have clean provenance.

### W1619: Federated Reward Model Calibration

Goal:

- Calibrate reward models and judges across tenant preferences without centralizing preference data.

Build:

- Local evaluator computes:
  - judge agreement
  - reward margin
  - preference residual
  - severity-conditioned disagreement
  - language and domain slice
- Global calibrator receives secure aggregate residuals.
- Reward model updates are gated by:
  - privacy budget
  - cohort threshold
  - fairness slices
  - K-Score correlation

Smoke test:

- One tenant's preference style would dominate reward model on a low-diversity slice.
- Fail update or apply shrinkage.

Acceptance:

- Judges become better aligned to real enterprise preference.
- Tenant-specific preferences stay tenant-local.
- Public K-Score remains distinct from private tenant preference.

### W1620: On-Device Private Adapter Training

Goal:

- Let devices improve local artifacts without uploading raw interaction data.

Build:

- Local process:
  - select allowed examples
  - train small adapter or calibration head
  - run local privacy and quality gates
  - export only approved aggregate update or keep private
- Controls:
  - battery and thermal budget
  - privacy budget
  - device capability
  - rollback plan
  - tenant admin approval

Smoke test:

- Device trains on disallowed sensitive local data.
- Fail update and report policy violation.

Acceptance:

- Device artifacts improve with local use.
- Raw local data stays local.
- Fleet-level improvements use secure aggregation only.

### W1621: Sovereign Region Learning Rings

Goal:

- Support learning boundaries for EU, healthcare, finance, government, and on-prem customers.

Build:

- Rings:
  - public global
  - US commercial
  - EU GDPR
  - healthcare BAA
  - finance regulated
  - government enclave
  - tenant-only
- Each ring has:
  - storage boundary
  - compute boundary
  - aggregate boundary
  - release policy
  - model movement policy
  - benchmark policy

Smoke test:

- EU healthcare aggregate attempts to update US commercial global prior without approval.
- Fail boundary check.

Acceptance:

- International expansion does not break the moat.
- Customers understand where their signal travels.
- Product graph can report learning ring readiness.

### W1622: Privacy-Weighted K-Score

Goal:

- Include privacy risk directly in artifact quality, not as a separate checkbox.

Build:

- Add K-Score components:
  - privacy budget spent
  - membership risk
  - memorization risk
  - outbound data risk
  - teacher API policy risk
  - cross-tenant inference risk
  - deletion lineage risk
- Penalize:
  - unknown privacy status
  - weak lineage
  - high membership risk
  - stale rights verification

Smoke test:

- Model has high task quality but elevated memorization risk.
- Fail if privacy-weighted K-Score remains high.

Acceptance:

- Privacy risk changes product decisions.
- Customers can compare artifacts by privacy posture.
- The strongest artifact is not always the most accurate one.

### W1623: Privacy-Aware Architecture Search

Goal:

- Select model architectures and distillation objectives that minimize privacy leakage at equal quality.

Build:

- Search dimensions:
  - student size
  - loss objective
  - teacher signal type
  - rationale usage
  - adapter vs full fine-tune
  - regularization
  - DP noise
  - data selection
- Objective:

```text
maximize KScore_quality - lambda_privacy * privacy_risk - lambda_cost * cost
```

Smoke test:

- Two students tie on quality; larger one memorizes more.
- Fail if larger model wins without privacy justification.

Acceptance:

- Privacy becomes part of architecture selection.
- Distillation recipes can be chosen for regulated domains.
- Private data is not treated as free fuel.

### W1624: Aggregate-Only Competitive Intelligence

Goal:

- Learn which providers and runtimes win in enterprise settings without exposing customer tasks.

Build:

- Local tenant comparison runs:
  - provider A vs B
  - local vs cloud
  - quant profile A vs B
  - device runtime A vs B
- Export only:
  - relative win counts
  - confidence bins
  - cost bins
  - failure bins
  - rights-safe task class

Smoke test:

- A competitor win is based on one tenant's unique workload.
- Fail public competitor claim.

Acceptance:

- Strategy learns from real workloads.
- Competitive claims remain privacy-safe.
- Roadmap prioritization improves without customer exposure.

### W1625: Privacy Incident Replay Without Raw Data

Goal:

- Reproduce privacy incidents from sanitized evidence.

Build:

- Replay packet:
  - incident class
  - redacted prompt shape
  - policy state
  - route decision
  - teacher call metadata
  - artifact hash
  - output risk class
  - synthetic substitute case
- Raw data remains tenant-local unless emergency policy allows sharing.

Smoke test:

- Incident replay requires raw prompt to reproduce failure.
- Fail until synthetic substitute or local replay is available.

Acceptance:

- Support and security can investigate without collecting raw data.
- Tenant can run local replay and share signed result.
- Privacy incidents improve global safeguards safely.

### W1626: Federated Product Graph Readiness

Goal:

- Reflect federated/privacy capabilities in the canonical product graph and account UX.

Build:

- Product graph nodes:
  - local trace vault
  - secure aggregation
  - DP budget broker
  - confidential evaluation
  - federated K-Score calibration
  - membership scanner
  - deletion lineage
  - learning rings
- Each node reports:
  - shipped
  - simulated
  - requires package
  - requires cloud credential
  - requires customer configuration
  - external certification needed

Smoke test:

- Account UI claims global learning is enabled without secure aggregation configured.
- Fail product readiness.

Acceptance:

- Privacy/data moat is visible in account and docs.
- Claims stay honest.
- Enterprise buyers can verify readiness by surface.

### W1627: Federated Data Moat Economic Model

Goal:

- Quantify the value of aggregate learning without pretending to own raw data.

Build:

- Model:
  - tenant count
  - signal diversity
  - cohort thresholds
  - privacy budget
  - aggregate utility
  - recipe prior lift
  - runtime profile lift
  - K-Score calibration lift
  - churn reduction
  - sales proof value
- Output:
  - expected global improvement
  - tenant-specific improvement
  - evidence unlocks
  - privacy cost
  - legal risk

Smoke test:

- Adding a tenant with no aggregate-sharing rights should not increase global moat value.
- Fail if economic model counts raw inaccessible data.

Acceptance:

- Business strategy aligns with data rights.
- Sales can explain network effects honestly.
- Product priorities favor consented aggregate signal.

### W1628: Ultimate Privacy-Preserving Moat Objective

Goal:

- Define the top-level objective for building a defensible data moat without exploiting customer trust.

Objective:

```text
maximize:
  aggregate_learning_value
  + tenant_specific_value
  + calibration_improvement
  + runtime_profile_improvement
  + safety_improvement
  + trust_and_procurement_value
  + defensibility

minimize:
  privacy_loss
  + membership_risk
  + memorization_risk
  + legal_risk
  + deletion_complexity
  + cross_tenant_inference_risk
  + raw_data_dependency
```

Build:

- Controller can choose:
  - local only
  - secure aggregate
  - DP aggregate
  - confidential eval
  - federated recipe update
  - tenant-private prior
  - public benchmark promotion
  - do not learn

Smoke test:

- A raw-data upload would improve quality but violate tenant policy.
- Fail if controller chooses upload.

Acceptance:

- The strongest moat is compatible with the strongest trust posture.
- Kolm can say "we learn without owning your data" and prove it.
- Every network effect has a rights and privacy ledger.

### W1600-W1628 Privacy-Preserving Moat Smoke Matrix

| Wave | Simulated fixture | Metric moved | Failure condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1600 | public use forbidden | rights integrity | experiment starts |
| W1601 | PHI trace | data containment | raw outbound string |
| W1602 | rare failure bucket | privacy | unique bucket exposed |
| W1603 | prohibited labels | calibration trust | global weight updated |
| W1604 | dominant tenant | recipe quality | tenant overfit |
| W1605 | private rationale | leakage control | rationale exported |
| W1606 | canary training | memorization | canary emitted |
| W1607 | repeated release | DP accounting | budget exceeded |
| W1608 | bad enclave hash | confidential trust | key released |
| W1609 | missing GPU attestation | trust boundary | confidential claim |
| W1610 | 95 percent tenant dominance | inference risk | prior released |
| W1611 | deletion request | rights operations | lineage unknown |
| W1612 | unique device profile | fleet privacy | public exposure |
| W1613 | rare activation outlier | profile privacy | token inferred |
| W1614 | private benchmark | sales proof | no rights summary |
| W1615 | tenant incident | security learning | incident leaked |
| W1616 | PHI teacher call | provider policy | external call |
| W1617 | synthetic duplicate | public data safety | sample released |
| W1618 | one-tenant slice | benchmark safety | public promotion |
| W1619 | dominant preference | judge fairness | reward update |
| W1620 | disallowed local data | device learning | update accepted |
| W1621 | EU healthcare signal | sovereignty | global update |
| W1622 | memorizing model | K-Score integrity | high score |
| W1623 | privacy-risk tie | architecture | leaky model wins |
| W1624 | unique workload | competitor proof | public claim |
| W1625 | raw replay needed | incident response | central replay |
| W1626 | secure aggregation missing | product honesty | readiness green |
| W1627 | no sharing rights | economics | moat counted |
| W1628 | raw upload win | control objective | upload chosen |

### Privacy-Preserving Moat Build Order

Build in this order:

1. W1600 Data Rights Lattice.
2. W1601 Tenant-Local Trace Vault.
3. W1616 Consent-Aware Teacher API Broker.
4. W1606 Membership And Memorization Risk Scanner.
5. W1607 Differential Privacy Budget Broker.
6. W1602 Secure Aggregate Failure Signature Protocol.
7. W1603 Federated K-Score Calibration.
8. W1611 Data Deletion Lineage Engine.
9. W1608 Confidential Teacher Evaluation Enclave.
10. W1604 Federated Distillation Recipe Learning.
11. W1612 Federated Runtime Profile Learning.
12. W1622 Privacy-Weighted K-Score.
13. W1626 Federated Product Graph Readiness.
14. W1628 Ultimate Privacy-Preserving Moat Objective.

This layer changes the strategic center of gravity. Competitors can copy a compiler, a dashboard, or a benchmark page. It is much harder to copy a consented, rights-aware, privacy-preserving learning network that improves distillation, routing, runtime, safety, and sales proof while proving that raw customer data stays under customer control.

## Formal Compiler And Proof-Carrying Artifact Layer

This layer turns Kolm from an artifact format plus product workflow into a
formal compiler for production AI systems. The point is not to claim that every
model behavior is mathematically proven. The point is to make every conversion,
route, policy, runtime profile, quantization choice, distillation result,
privacy decision, and audit claim pass through typed obligations that can be
checked, replayed, explained, and revoked.

The current AI infrastructure market has many partially overlapping pieces:
gateway routers, evaluation platforms, prompt management, fine-tuning services,
runtime compilers, model registries, observability tools, policy engines, and
compliance evidence stores. The frontier design for Kolm is to compile those
pieces into one signed proof-carrying object:

```text
OpenAI or provider traffic
  -> captured behavior and intent
  -> task-scoped evidence
  -> policy and rights constraints
  -> student or route candidate
  -> K-Score proof bundle
  -> runtime lowering contract
  -> device or cloud execution target
  -> signed .kolm artifact
  -> registry admission
  -> replay, audit, revoke, and redeploy
```

Core IR stack:

- IntentIR: what the user or system was trying to accomplish.
- BehaviorIR: normalized inputs, outputs, tool calls, structured schemas, and
  failure labels.
- EvidenceIR: evals, holdouts, red-team results, calibration sets, traces, and
  confidence intervals.
- PolicyIR: allowed providers, data classes, regions, tools, human approvals,
  retention, and escalation rules.
- RightsIR: tenant consent, dataset license, deletion lineage, public-release
  rights, and aggregate-learning rights.
- ScoreIR: K-Score axes, weights, uncertainty, risk tiers, and regression
  budgets.
- RouteIR: model choice, fallback, local/cloud switching, confidence routing,
  cost caps, and SLOs.
- RuntimeIR: target runtime, tensor formats, kernels, KV policy, attention
  policy, batching, memory profile, and quantization constraints.
- DeviceIR: NPU/GPU/CPU feature set, memory ceiling, thermal envelope, offline
  mode, and battery or power policy.
- ProofIR: obligations, assumptions, check results, unsatisfied proof debt, and
  waiver scope.
- AttestationIR: build provenance, key identity, enclave or device claims,
  supply-chain statements, and verifier results.
- ArtifactIR: stable artifact manifest, ABI, version, compatibility rules,
  embedded receipts, and revocation status.

The invention is not just "use MLIR" or "use SMT." The invention is a product
specific proof stack for AI ownership. Kolm should know which claims are proven
by local deterministic checks, which are supported by public benchmarks, which
are supported only by private tenant evidence, and which remain external
certification or package-release gates. That distinction becomes a competitive
advantage because enterprise buyers do not just want faster models. They want to
know exactly what they can trust, where it can run, who approved it, and how to
prove it later.

### W1700: Kolm Semantic IR Kernel

Goal:

- Define the minimum formal vocabulary that every Kolm artifact, route, distill
  run, runtime profile, and audit receipt must use.

Build:

- Create a versioned semantic kernel with:
  - entities:
    - tenant
    - trace
    - task
    - model
    - provider
    - student
    - route
    - runtime
    - device
    - dataset
    - eval
    - policy
    - rights grant
    - approval
    - artifact
    - attestation
  - relations:
    - derived_from
    - evaluated_by
    - allowed_by
    - denied_by
    - lowered_to
    - equivalent_under
    - approved_by
    - revoked_by
    - supersedes
  - invariants:
    - no artifact without provenance
    - no benchmark claim without evidence scope
    - no runtime profile without target constraints
    - no distillation artifact without teacher lineage
    - no cross-tenant learning without rights proof
- Encode a compact canonical JSON form and a typed in-memory form.
- Add a semantic hash that ignores cosmetic ordering but changes on meaning.

Smoke test:

- Two manifests differ only by field order and comments.
- Fail if they produce different semantic hashes.
- Two manifests differ by data rights.
- Fail if they produce the same semantic hash.

Acceptance:

- Every major product surface can reference the same semantic IDs.
- The compiler can reject artifacts before runtime.
- Audit, account, CLI, TUI, and API use identical terms.

### W1701: Typed Effect System For AI Operations

Goal:

- Make every AI operation declare its side effects before it can enter an
  artifact or route plan.

Build:

- Effect types:
  - reads_private_trace
  - writes_private_trace
  - calls_external_provider
  - sends_phi
  - sends_pii
  - uses_teacher_model
  - trains_student
  - exports_aggregate
  - runs_local_device
  - invokes_tool
  - requires_human_approval
  - uses_confidential_compute
  - publishes_benchmark
  - changes_policy
  - revokes_artifact
- Effect qualifiers:
  - tenant_local
  - region_bound
  - retention_bound
  - dp_bounded
  - encrypted
  - enclave_bound
  - aggregate_only
  - replayable
  - non_replayable
- Compiler checks:
  - all effects must be declared
  - all effects must be allowed by PolicyIR and RightsIR
  - effects must flow through approved boundaries
  - escalations must be visible to the caller

Smoke test:

- A distillation plan calls a teacher with PHI but declares only
  uses_teacher_model.
- Fail because sends_phi and calls_external_provider are missing.

Acceptance:

- Hidden side effects are impossible in artifact metadata.
- Enterprise buyers can ask "what does this artifact do" and get a precise
  answer.
- The same effect graph powers policy, billing, privacy, and audit views.

### W1702: Provider Semantics Adapter

Goal:

- Normalize provider behavior so a route or artifact can reason about OpenAI,
  Anthropic, Gemini, Mistral, Cohere, local open models, and future providers
  without pretending they are identical.

Build:

- Adapter fields:
  - context window
  - tool call semantics
  - streaming semantics
  - structured-output guarantees
  - safety refusal shape
  - logprob availability
  - seed and determinism support
  - data retention policy
  - region support
  - batch support
  - fine-tune or distillation support
  - latency and cost envelope
  - failure code taxonomy
- Define semantic deltas:
  - equivalent
  - weaker
  - stronger
  - incomparable
  - unknown
- Add migration checks:
  - same schema support
  - same tool-call support
  - same privacy posture
  - same refusal handling
  - acceptable K-Score delta

Smoke test:

- A route migrates from a provider with strict JSON schema to a provider with
  best-effort JSON.
- Fail unless the artifact adds a repair/eval layer or marks the route as
  weaker.

Acceptance:

- Model-agnostic does not mean behavior-blind.
- Provider switching becomes a checked compilation step.
- Sales can claim portability with explicit equivalence scope.

### W1703: Behavior Equivalence Checker

Goal:

- Decide when a compiled artifact, student model, prompt rewrite, or provider
  route is equivalent enough to replace the captured behavior.

Build:

- Equivalence dimensions:
  - schema conformance
  - required fields
  - task success
  - safety refusals
  - factuality
  - tone constraints
  - privacy constraints
  - latency
  - cost
  - human preference
  - red-team resistance
- Modes:
  - exact deterministic equivalence for parsers and postprocessors
  - statistical equivalence for model behavior
  - risk-bounded equivalence for regulated flows
  - non-equivalence with fallback route
- Output:
  - pass/fail
  - confidence interval
  - regression class
  - affected cohorts
  - replay fixtures
  - remediation suggestion

Smoke test:

- Student has 98 percent average score but fails all high-severity medical
  refusals.
- Fail because critical cohort regression overrides aggregate score.

Acceptance:

- Replacement decisions are not based on a single average.
- K-Score can cite the equivalence checker as proof.
- Rollbacks know which behavior class regressed.

### W1704: Evidence Completeness Checker

Goal:

- Prevent artifacts from being promoted with missing eval, privacy, cost,
  latency, runtime, or approval evidence.

Build:

- Evidence profiles by artifact type:
  - gateway route
  - distilled student
  - local device model
  - agent tool bundle
  - regulated workflow
  - benchmark claim
  - runtime kernel profile
- Required evidence:
  - capture sample coverage
  - holdout set
  - red-team set
  - privacy scan
  - rights proof
  - cost replay
  - latency replay
  - runtime test
  - human approval when needed
  - provenance and signature
- Missing evidence becomes explicit proof debt.

Smoke test:

- Artifact has strong quality eval but no rights proof.
- Fail promotion to registry.

Acceptance:

- No more hidden "it works locally" artifacts.
- The account page can show exactly why an artifact is blocked.
- Implementation teams can close a concrete evidence checklist.

### W1705: Policy And Rights SMT Compiler

Goal:

- Compile PolicyIR and RightsIR into machine-checkable constraints instead of
  relying on ad hoc string checks.

Build:

- Encode constraints:
  - region in allowed_regions
  - provider in allowed_providers
  - data_class not in forbidden_data_classes
  - retention_days <= max_retention_days
  - public_release requires public_rights
  - cross_tenant_update requires aggregate_learning_rights
  - PHI external call requires BAA/provider approval
  - teacher call requires teacher_use_rights
- Produce:
  - satisfiable/unsatisfiable result
  - unsat core
  - minimal policy conflict explanation
  - suggested safe alternative route

Smoke test:

- Tenant allows US region and forbids external PHI calls.
- Plan selects EU teacher endpoint with PHI.
- Fail with an unsat core naming both region and data-class constraints.

Acceptance:

- Policy failures become explainable, not mysterious.
- Route planners can search for satisfying alternatives.
- Auditors can reproduce deny decisions.

### W1706: Route Policy Model Checker

Goal:

- Verify that fallback chains cannot accidentally violate policy after retries,
  outages, confidence drops, or cost cap changes.

Build:

- Route state machine:
  - start
  - local attempt
  - cloud teacher attempt
  - repair attempt
  - fallback provider
  - human review
  - deny
  - success
- State variables:
  - data_class
  - region
  - provider
  - confidence
  - spend
  - latency
  - retry_count
  - approval_state
- Properties:
  - never send forbidden data to forbidden provider
  - eventually deny or review if no satisfying route exists
  - retries do not bypass approval
  - confidence fallback respects cost and privacy caps

Smoke test:

- Primary route is policy-safe.
- Fallback route is cheaper but violates provider restrictions.
- Fail before deployment.

Acceptance:

- Routing is proven across failure paths, not just happy paths.
- Confidence-calibrated local/cloud routing is safe under pressure.
- Enterprise customers can inspect the route machine.

### W1707: Runtime Lowering Contract

Goal:

- Make every runtime target prove which semantics it preserves and which
  guarantees it weakens.

Build:

- Lowering contract:
  - source ArtifactIR hash
  - target runtime
  - tensor layout changes
  - precision changes
  - kernel choices
  - memory plan
  - KV policy
  - attention policy
  - batching policy
  - device feature assumptions
  - expected K-Score delta
  - benchmark fixtures used
- Compatibility classes:
  - exact
  - numerically close
  - statistically equivalent
  - task-bounded
  - unsupported

Smoke test:

- Runtime lowers FP16 to INT4 without attaching quantization eval and K-Score
  delta.
- Fail.

Acceptance:

- Runtime portability has proof scope.
- Device claims become testable.
- Kernel optimization cannot silently degrade task behavior.

### W1708: Device Capability Refinement Type

Goal:

- Describe devices as typed capabilities rather than marketing labels.

Build:

- Capability fields:
  - memory_bytes
  - peak_flops
  - supported_precisions
  - npu_vendor
  - gpu_vendor
  - cpu_features
  - wasm_support
  - webnn_support
  - thermal_budget
  - offline_required
  - battery_policy
  - secure_storage
  - network_policy
- Refinement examples:
  - device.memory_bytes >= artifact.peak_memory_bytes
  - int4_supported if quantized_int4 artifact selected
  - offline_required implies no external fallback
  - secure_storage required for regulated artifact key material

Smoke test:

- A phone target has enough compute but not enough secure storage for keys.
- Fail regulated offline deployment.

Acceptance:

- "Runs on any device" becomes a set of typed claims.
- Sales demos can show exact target eligibility.
- The compiler can recommend model, quantization, and route alternatives.

### W1709: Quantization Proof Obligation Generator

Goal:

- Turn quantization from a benchmark afterthought into a proof obligation
  generated by the compiler.

Build:

- For every quantization choice record:
  - source model hash
  - quantization method
  - calibration data rights
  - activation distribution summary
  - outlier policy
  - per-layer error profile
  - K-Score delta by task
  - safety delta by risk cohort
  - runtime speed and memory delta
- Required checks:
  - no forbidden calibration data
  - no critical cohort regression
  - p95 latency improvement measured
  - memory target satisfied
  - rollback route exists

Smoke test:

- INT4 improves memory by 55 percent and latency by 28 percent but fails one
  high-risk redaction class.
- Fail promotion for that use case.

Acceptance:

- Kolm can invent proprietary quantization tools without making unsafe global
  claims.
- Runtime optimization is tied to task evidence.
- Distilled-model activation distributions become a measurable moat.

### W1710: Layer-Skip Proof Obligation Generator

Goal:

- Let Kolm exploit redundant student layers while proving the skip policy is
  task-safe.

Build:

- Inputs:
  - student architecture
  - layer activation profiles
  - confidence head outputs
  - task class
  - risk class
  - device target
- Outputs:
  - skip candidates
  - skip confidence threshold
  - expected latency gain
  - K-Score delta
  - critical failure delta
  - disable conditions
- Runtime guard:
  - disable skipping when confidence is low
  - disable skipping for regulated high-risk cohorts
  - log skipped layers for replay

Smoke test:

- Skip policy improves median latency but increases p99 failure on long
  instructions.
- Fail unless route falls back for that cohort.

Acceptance:

- Dynamic layer skipping becomes product-safe.
- Distillation telemetry creates runtime advantage.
- Customers can audit why a layer was skipped.

### W1711: Attention Compression Proof Obligation Generator

Goal:

- Exploit task-specific attention patterns without silently losing recall on
  rare or long-context cases.

Build:

- Capture:
  - attention sparsity pattern
  - important token classes
  - KV cache reuse policy
  - long-context degradation curve
  - prompt template sensitivity
  - retrieval dependency
- Generate:
  - sparse attention candidate
  - KV quantization candidate
  - token eviction rule
  - cache reuse rule
  - proof fixtures
- Reject if:
  - rare-token recall drops
  - citation accuracy drops
  - safety triggers are evicted
  - long-context eval fails

Smoke test:

- Compression passes short prompts but drops a late legal constraint in a long
  contract.
- Fail.

Acceptance:

- Attention optimization is task-aware.
- Long-context safety is explicitly measured.
- Runtime speedups remain compatible with audit requirements.

### W1712: K-Score Proof Binder

Goal:

- Bind K-Score to the evidence, weights, confidence intervals, and claim scope
  that produced it.

Build:

- K-Score proof object:
  - metric version
  - axis weights
  - task taxonomy
  - fixture IDs
  - evaluator versions
  - human label calibration version
  - confidence intervals
  - cohort regressions
  - excluded claims
  - freshness timestamp
- A score is invalid if:
  - fixtures are missing
  - weights are unknown
  - calibration version is stale
  - evaluator changed without recompute
  - confidence interval is too wide for claim type

Smoke test:

- A public page claims K-Score improvement but the score object lacks human
  calibration version.
- Fail claim-scope gate.

Acceptance:

- K-Score becomes a defensible metric system, not a vanity number.
- Competitors can implement the open calculator but not reproduce Kolm's
  private calibrated evidence network.
- Sales copy can cite narrow proof with confidence.

### W1713: Reproducible Replay Capsule Verifier

Goal:

- Package enough deterministic replay context to reproduce artifact decisions
  without exposing raw private data.

Build:

- Replay capsule:
  - sanitized input fixture
  - expected schema
  - model/provider version
  - route policy
  - runtime target
  - random seed when supported
  - redaction transform
  - hash of private original when allowed
  - expected output class
  - score thresholds
- Modes:
  - public replay
  - tenant-private replay
  - enclave replay
  - synthetic replay
  - auditor replay

Smoke test:

- A regression cannot be reproduced because provider version and route policy
  were not captured.
- Fail artifact promotion.

Acceptance:

- Debugging becomes reproducible.
- Private data can stay private.
- Audits can replay what matters without seeing what they should not see.

### W1714: Proof-Carrying Artifact Manifest

Goal:

- Make the .kolm manifest carry proofs, proof debt, and verifier outcomes as
  first-class fields.

Build:

- Manifest sections:
  - identity
  - semantic kernel version
  - source behavior hash
  - teacher lineage
  - student lineage
  - policy constraints
  - rights constraints
  - K-Score proof
  - runtime lowering contract
  - device capability requirements
  - supply-chain attestations
  - replay capsules
  - proof obligations
  - proof debt
  - waiver scope
  - revocation pointers
- Verification status:
  - verified
  - verified_with_debt
  - blocked
  - revoked
  - expired

Smoke test:

- Artifact has a waiver but no scope, owner, expiry, or reason.
- Fail verification.

Acceptance:

- Artifacts are not just blobs with metadata.
- Every artifact carries its own trust contract.
- Registry and account UI can explain exact readiness.

### W1715: Artifact ABI And WASM Component Boundary

Goal:

- Define a stable execution boundary so runtimes can consume .kolm artifacts
  without linking to private implementation details.

Build:

- ABI operations:
  - init
  - inspect
  - score
  - route
  - infer
  - stream
  - tool_call
  - replay
  - verify
  - revoke_check
- Boundary requirements:
  - typed inputs and outputs
  - schema version
  - deterministic error codes
  - capability negotiation
  - memory limits
  - streaming contract
  - cancellation contract
  - telemetry contract
- WASM component model can define portable host interfaces while native
  backends provide accelerated lowering.

Smoke test:

- A runtime implements infer but not verify or revoke_check.
- Fail regulated artifact compatibility.

Acceptance:

- External runtimes can adopt the artifact format.
- ABI compatibility is testable.
- Kolm keeps proprietary optimization while publishing a stable boundary.

### W1716: Registry Formal Admission Gate

Goal:

- Stop the registry from accepting artifacts whose proof state is inconsistent
  with their claims.

Build:

- Admission stages:
  - parse manifest
  - verify semantic hash
  - check policy satisfiability
  - check rights satisfiability
  - check evidence completeness
  - check K-Score proof
  - check runtime lowering
  - check supply-chain attestation
  - check revocation status
  - assign claim scope
- Registry labels:
  - public_demo
  - internal_only
  - tenant_private
  - regulated_ready
  - benchmark_candidate
  - package_release_candidate
  - blocked

Smoke test:

- Artifact claims regulated_ready but lacks live certification evidence.
- Admit only as implemented_controls, not regulated_ready.

Acceptance:

- Registry becomes a trust engine.
- Honest scope is enforced by code.
- Marketing claims cannot outrun proof.

### W1717: Supply Chain Attestation Verifier

Goal:

- Bind artifact creation, training, compilation, evaluation, and packaging to
  verifiable provenance.

Build:

- Verify:
  - source commit
  - build workflow identity
  - dependency lock
  - container image
  - model weight hash
  - dataset manifest hash
  - eval runner hash
  - signer identity
  - artifact digest
  - SBOM and SLSA/in-toto style statements
- Reject when:
  - provenance is missing
  - signer is untrusted
  - build is not reproducible enough for artifact class
  - dependency vulnerability exceeds threshold

Smoke test:

- Artifact was built by an untrusted workflow but signed with a valid key.
- Fail because key validity is not enough.

Acceptance:

- Enterprise procurement has a real evidence packet.
- Artifact trust includes software supply chain trust.
- Rebuilds and incident response have exact provenance.

### W1718: Formal Drift And Revocation State Machine

Goal:

- Define how artifacts move from valid to stale, blocked, revoked, or migrated.

Build:

- States:
  - draft
  - verified
  - deployed
  - watch
  - stale
  - blocked
  - revoked
  - migrated
- Triggers:
  - K-Score regression
  - provider behavior change
  - policy change
  - rights deletion
  - dependency vulnerability
  - certification expiry
  - model deprecation
  - runtime target failure
  - privacy incident
- Properties:
  - revoked artifacts cannot be selected by route planner
  - stale artifacts require review before regulated deployment
  - migration must preserve claim scope or downgrade it

Smoke test:

- Tenant deletes rights for a training dataset.
- Fail if derived artifact remains deployed without review.

Acceptance:

- Lifecycle is formally governed.
- Revocation is not an afterthought.
- Account and CLI can show why an artifact changed state.

### W1719: Auditor Query Language

Goal:

- Let customers and auditors ask precise questions over artifacts without
  reading source code or private logs.

Build:

- Query examples:
  - show artifacts derived from dataset X
  - show routes that can send PHI externally
  - show artifacts with public benchmark claims
  - show artifacts using provider Y
  - show artifacts whose K-Score calibration is older than N days
  - show waivers expiring this quarter
  - show devices eligible for artifact Z
  - show all revoked artifacts still referenced by route plans
- Output:
  - answer set
  - proof path
  - evidence links
  - excluded private details
  - recommended remediation

Smoke test:

- Auditor asks for PHI external routes.
- Fail if query only searches text instead of PolicyIR effects.

Acceptance:

- Audit becomes self-serve.
- Enterprise account pages can expose high-value evidence.
- Procurement cycles shorten because proof is queryable.

### W1720: Human Approval Proof Type

Goal:

- Make human approvals typed, scoped, expiring, and tied to the exact artifact
  or action they approved.

Build:

- Approval fields:
  - approver identity
  - role
  - tenant
  - action
  - artifact hash
  - policy snapshot
  - reason
  - expiry
  - revocation path
  - delegated authority proof
- Approval classes:
  - deploy
  - publish benchmark
  - use teacher
  - export aggregate
  - override block
  - rotate key
  - revoke artifact

Smoke test:

- Admin approved an earlier artifact hash, but a new artifact is being
  deployed.
- Fail unless approval is renewed.

Acceptance:

- Human-in-the-loop governance is precise.
- Approvals cannot be reused outside scope.
- Regulated workflows can prove who approved what and why.

### W1721: Agent Tool Effect Proof

Goal:

- Treat agent tool use as a typed effect graph with policy and replay
  obligations.

Build:

- For every tool:
  - input schema
  - output schema
  - side effects
  - allowed data classes
  - allowed network targets
  - required approvals
  - replay mode
  - compensating action
- Tool call proof:
  - selected tool was allowed
  - arguments matched schema
  - data classes were permitted
  - output was validated
  - state change was recorded

Smoke test:

- Agent can call a billing tool with private health data in a note field.
- Fail before call.

Acceptance:

- Agentic workflows become auditable artifacts.
- Tool safety is compiled, not only prompted.
- The same artifact model covers routes, students, and agents.

### W1722: Confidential Compute Attestation Binder

Goal:

- Bind confidential teacher evals, private replay, and sensitive distillation
  steps to enclave or confidential GPU attestations.

Build:

- Attestation object:
  - enclave measurement
  - image hash
  - policy hash
  - key release condition
  - workload hash
  - tenant approval
  - timestamp
  - verifier identity
- Gate:
  - no key release without valid measurement
  - no confidential claim without attestation
  - no reuse after policy or image changes

Smoke test:

- Private replay requests a key with a valid tenant but wrong image hash.
- Fail key release.

Acceptance:

- Confidential compute becomes measurable product capability.
- Private evals can improve quality without raw-data leakage.
- Cloud readiness can distinguish configured from proven.

### W1723: Federated Aggregate Proof Type

Goal:

- Prove that aggregate learning was produced from allowed, privacy-preserving
  contributions.

Build:

- Aggregate proof:
  - cohort definition
  - minimum cohort size
  - contribution cap
  - secure aggregation method
  - DP budget
  - tenant rights snapshot
  - outlier suppression
  - release threshold
  - downstream artifact IDs
- Reject if:
  - cohort too small
  - one tenant dominates
  - rights changed
  - privacy budget exhausted
  - aggregate can reveal a tenant-specific secret

Smoke test:

- Aggregate score improvement comes from two tenants only.
- Fail public or global release.

Acceptance:

- Data moat is provable without raw data ownership.
- Federated K-Score and recipe learning get concrete proof objects.
- Enterprise trust and network effects reinforce each other.

### W1724: Cross-Runtime Differential Test Harness

Goal:

- Catch semantic drift across ONNX Runtime, TVM/MLC, IREE, WebGPU/WebNN,
  ExecuTorch, TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, and local native targets.

Build:

- Differential dimensions:
  - output class
  - schema conformance
  - token-level tolerance
  - K-Score axis
  - refusal behavior
  - latency
  - memory
  - streaming shape
  - tool-call shape
- Harness:
  - same artifact
  - same replay capsule
  - multiple runtimes
  - tolerance rules by claim type
  - automatic downgrade when only some targets pass

Smoke test:

- Desktop runtime passes but WebGPU runtime fails schema on 4 percent of
  extraction tasks.
- Fail "runs everywhere" claim and mark WebGPU unsupported for that artifact.

Acceptance:

- Portability is tested rather than assumed.
- Runtime regressions are isolated quickly.
- Device deployment becomes a compiler output, not a manual promise.

### W1725: Proof Minimizer And Explainer

Goal:

- Convert large proof bundles into concise, human-readable explanations without
  losing machine-verifiable evidence.

Build:

- Minimization:
  - identify minimal unsat core
  - identify minimal failing cohort
  - identify minimal missing evidence set
  - identify weakest claim that remains true
- Explainer:
  - account UI summary
  - CLI summary
  - auditor summary
  - developer remediation
  - sales-safe phrasing

Smoke test:

- Artifact fails because one policy constraint and one missing eval are enough
  to block it.
- Fail if explanation lists 50 unrelated checks.

Acceptance:

- Proof is usable by humans.
- Engineers know exactly what to fix.
- Sales and docs can avoid overclaiming without sounding vague.

### W1726: Formal Spec Versioning And Migration

Goal:

- Make artifact format evolution safe as Kolm adds new proof types, runtimes,
  models, and policies.

Build:

- Version rules:
  - semantic version for IR kernel
  - migration functions
  - deprecated field mapping
  - forbidden silent default changes
  - compatibility tests
  - downgrade rules
  - registry admission by version
- Migration proof:
  - old manifest hash
  - new manifest hash
  - transformation rule
  - fields added
  - fields removed
  - claims preserved
  - claims downgraded

Smoke test:

- New spec adds rights field with permissive default.
- Fail migration because missing rights cannot default to allowed.

Acceptance:

- The .kolm format can become a durable standard.
- Older artifacts remain understandable.
- Spec evolution cannot accidentally weaken security.

### W1727: Proof Debt Ledger

Goal:

- Track every missing proof, waiver, benchmark, package release, external
  partner, and certification gap as a first-class product debt item.

Build:

- Debt fields:
  - debt_id
  - artifact_or_surface
  - missing proof
  - current safe claim
  - forbidden claim
  - owner
  - severity
  - expiry
  - build task
  - verification command
  - public wording
- Actions:
  - block release
  - allow internal use
  - allow demo only
  - allow with warning
  - downgrade claim
  - revoke

Smoke test:

- Public page says "certified" while certification debt is open.
- Fail claim-scope audit.

Acceptance:

- Open readiness gates stay explicit.
- Product work can continue honestly.
- "Not done yet" becomes actionable, not ambiguous.

### W1728: Ultimate Proof-Carrying Artifact Objective

Goal:

- Define the objective for the complete Kolm compiler and artifact trust
  system.

Objective:

```text
maximize:
  task_success
  + cost_reduction
  + latency_reduction
  + portability
  + reproducibility
  + auditability
  + policy_safety
  + privacy_preservation
  + runtime_efficiency
  + enterprise_trust
  + ecosystem_adoption

minimize:
  hidden_side_effects
  + unproven_claims
  + provider_lock_in
  + runtime_drift
  + privacy_loss
  + policy_violations
  + proof_debt
  + migration_risk
  + certification_confusion
```

Controller can choose:

- reject artifact
- request missing evidence
- downgrade public claim
- select safer route
- select cheaper equivalent route
- compile student
- quantize with proof obligations
- lower to target runtime
- require human approval
- require confidential execution
- require federated aggregation
- revoke or migrate artifact

Smoke test:

- A faster, cheaper artifact has incomplete rights proof.
- Fail if controller promotes it.

Acceptance:

- Kolm's strategic claim becomes exact:
  - capture behavior
  - compile ownership
  - bind evidence
  - run anywhere within typed constraints
  - prove what is true
  - expose what is not true yet
- This is the foundation for a real industry standard, because the artifact is
  useful even when the user does not trust Kolm's hosted service.

### W1700-W1728 Formal Compiler Smoke Matrix

| Wave | Simulated fixture | Metric moved | Failure condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1700 | manifest field reorder | semantic integrity | different semantic hash |
| W1701 | hidden PHI provider call | policy safety | undeclared effect allowed |
| W1702 | strict JSON to loose JSON provider | portability | weaker semantics unmarked |
| W1703 | average score hides medical refusal failure | quality safety | aggregate score passes |
| W1704 | missing rights proof | auditability | registry promotion |
| W1705 | EU endpoint plus US-only policy | policy explainability | no unsat core |
| W1706 | unsafe fallback route | route safety | fallback deploys |
| W1707 | INT4 lowering without evidence | runtime trust | target marked compatible |
| W1708 | insufficient secure storage | device safety | regulated deploy allowed |
| W1709 | quantization breaks PII class | safety | artifact promoted |
| W1710 | skip policy breaks long prompts | latency safety | p99 regression ignored |
| W1711 | compressed attention loses late constraint | long-context quality | compression accepted |
| W1712 | K-Score lacks calibration version | metric trust | public claim allowed |
| W1713 | replay lacks provider version | reproducibility | promotion allowed |
| W1714 | unscoped waiver | proof integrity | manifest verifies |
| W1715 | runtime lacks revoke_check | ABI safety | regulated compatible |
| W1716 | certification claim without evidence | registry honesty | regulated label applied |
| W1717 | untrusted build workflow | supply-chain trust | signature alone passes |
| W1718 | deleted rights remain deployed | lifecycle safety | artifact active |
| W1719 | PHI route query uses text search | audit precision | incomplete answer |
| W1720 | approval reused for new hash | governance | deploy allowed |
| W1721 | tool call leaks health data | agent safety | tool executes |
| W1722 | wrong enclave image hash | confidential trust | key released |
| W1723 | two-tenant aggregate | privacy | global update released |
| W1724 | WebGPU schema drift | portability | runs-everywhere claim |
| W1725 | noisy proof explanation | operator UX | no minimal blocker |
| W1726 | permissive migration default | spec safety | migration accepted |
| W1727 | open certification debt | claim honesty | public certified claim |
| W1728 | cheaper artifact missing rights proof | objective safety | promotion selected |

### Formal Compiler Build Order

Build in this order:

1. W1700 Kolm Semantic IR Kernel.
2. W1701 Typed Effect System For AI Operations.
3. W1705 Policy And Rights SMT Compiler.
4. W1704 Evidence Completeness Checker.
5. W1714 Proof-Carrying Artifact Manifest.
6. W1712 K-Score Proof Binder.
7. W1702 Provider Semantics Adapter.
8. W1703 Behavior Equivalence Checker.
9. W1706 Route Policy Model Checker.
10. W1707 Runtime Lowering Contract.
11. W1708 Device Capability Refinement Type.
12. W1709 Quantization Proof Obligation Generator.
13. W1710 Layer-Skip Proof Obligation Generator.
14. W1711 Attention Compression Proof Obligation Generator.
15. W1713 Reproducible Replay Capsule Verifier.
16. W1715 Artifact ABI And WASM Component Boundary.
17. W1716 Registry Formal Admission Gate.
18. W1717 Supply Chain Attestation Verifier.
19. W1718 Formal Drift And Revocation State Machine.
20. W1719 Auditor Query Language.
21. W1720 Human Approval Proof Type.
22. W1721 Agent Tool Effect Proof.
23. W1722 Confidential Compute Attestation Binder.
24. W1723 Federated Aggregate Proof Type.
25. W1724 Cross-Runtime Differential Test Harness.
26. W1725 Proof Minimizer And Explainer.
27. W1726 Formal Spec Versioning And Migration.
28. W1727 Proof Debt Ledger.
29. W1728 Ultimate Proof-Carrying Artifact Objective.

This layer is what lets Kolm claim ownership instead of just optimization. A
plain distillation tool can say that a student is cheaper. A plain gateway can
say that a route is cheaper. A plain runtime can say that a target is faster.
Kolm should say something stronger and narrower: this artifact is the compiled,
scored, rights-checked, policy-checked, runtime-lowered, provenance-bound,
replayable version of the behavior you already depend on, and here is the proof
scope for every claim.

## Omnidevice Deployment And Hardware Co-Design Layer

This layer exists because "enterprise models down to any device" is a different
product surface from gateway routing and model distillation. A smaller model is
not enough. A customer wants to know whether a signed artifact can run inside a
browser tab, an iPhone app, an Android app, an edge box, a hospital workstation,
a sovereign VPC, or an air-gapped appliance while preserving the exact quality,
privacy, policy, latency, and update guarantees that made the artifact useful.

The state of the market is fragmented:

- Apple has Core ML, Neural Engine, Metal, MLX, and app-specific privacy
  constraints.
- Android and Chrome increasingly expose LiteRT, WebGPU, WebNN, NNAPI style
  acceleration, and vendor NPU paths.
- Qualcomm offers physical-device profiling, numerical validation, and
  hardware-aware deployment flows.
- ONNX Runtime Mobile, ExecuTorch, TVM, IREE, MLC, TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, and
  browser runtimes all have different operator, memory, quantization, packaging,
  and update constraints.
- Enterprise edge fleets have boring but decisive constraints: patch windows,
  power, thermal headroom, device management, offline operation, key storage,
  procurement IDs, and support contracts.

Kolm should not paper over that fragmentation with "export to ONNX." The
frontier design is an omnidevice compiler: it takes ArtifactIR plus ProofIR and
emits target-specific deployment bundles with measured quality envelopes,
runtime receipts, update policy, telemetry rules, and downgrade paths. The
product advantage is that every device deployment is a compiled artifact
variant, not a one-off engineering project.

Core idea:

```text
.kolm artifact
  + target fleet description
  + policy and rights constraints
  + device capability probes
  + runtime candidates
  + K-Score proof
  + cost/latency/power budget
  -> target-specific bundle set
  -> physical or simulated validation
  -> rollout plan
  -> fleet drift monitor
  -> support matrix
```

### W1800: Device Fleet Taxonomy

Goal:

- Replace vague target labels with a precise taxonomy of device classes,
  operating modes, acceleration paths, and support obligations.

Build:

- Device classes:
  - browser
  - desktop app
  - iOS app
  - Android app
  - embedded Linux
  - Windows workstation
  - edge appliance
  - robot or kiosk
  - hospital workstation
  - air-gapped server
  - sovereign VPC node
  - GPU edge cluster
- Operating modes:
  - online
  - intermittently connected
  - offline
  - air-gapped
  - regulated local-only
  - cloud-assisted
  - human-review required
- Hardware axes:
  - CPU ISA
  - GPU family
  - NPU family
  - memory
  - storage
  - secure element
  - battery
  - thermal envelope
  - camera/mic permissions
  - browser API support
  - OS update channel

Smoke test:

- A target is described as "mobile" without OS, memory, accelerator, offline,
  and update constraints.
- Fail because the compiler cannot make an honest deployment plan.

Acceptance:

- "Any device" becomes a set of typed device claims.
- Account, CLI, registry, and sales collateral can show exact supported
  targets.
- Every artifact variant has a target fleet identity.

### W1801: Hardware Capability Probe

Goal:

- Measure the actual machine instead of relying on product names or marketing
  claims.

Build:

- Probe:
  - CPU features
  - available memory
  - sustained memory bandwidth estimate
  - GPU API availability
  - NPU delegate availability
  - WebGPU/WebNN/WASM SIMD availability
  - Core ML compute devices
  - Android accelerator availability
  - thermal headroom
  - battery or power mode
  - secure storage
  - filesystem quota
  - network policy
- Output:
  - capability vector
  - unsupported reasons
  - recommended runtime candidates
  - safe model size range
  - benchmark recipe

Smoke test:

- Device name says "NPU capable" but the runtime cannot access the NPU because
  OS permissions or delegate packages are missing.
- Fail NPU deployment eligibility.

Acceptance:

- Runtime planning uses actual capabilities.
- Support can diagnose deployment failures quickly.
- Device cohorts become measurable assets.

### W1802: Model Family Fit Solver

Goal:

- Choose the right artifact family for a device and task rather than forcing
  one model shape everywhere.

Build:

- Inputs:
  - task class
  - K-Score minimum
  - latency target
  - memory cap
  - privacy policy
  - offline requirement
  - device capability vector
  - update cadence
- Candidate families:
  - route-only artifact
  - prompt/policy artifact
  - small encoder
  - distilled decoder
  - adapter-tuned base
  - retrieval-assisted local model
  - cloud fallback hybrid
  - rules plus model verifier
- Solver objective:
  - maximize quality and privacy
  - minimize memory, power, latency, and operational support burden

Smoke test:

- A local decoder fits memory but misses K-Score while a hybrid route satisfies
  policy and quality.
- Fail if the compiler chooses local-only because it is cheaper.

Acceptance:

- Kolm can recommend the correct deployment family.
- Customers do not have to understand every runtime.
- The product avoids overpromising local replacement.

### W1803: Multi-Backend Export Compiler

Goal:

- Emit target-specific bundles for Core ML, LiteRT, ONNX Runtime Mobile,
  ExecuTorch, WebGPU/WebNN/WASM, IREE, TVM/MLC, TensorRT-LLM, and native edge
  services from one proof-carrying artifact.

Build:

- Backend adapters:
  - operator support map
  - dtype support map
  - quantization support map
  - dynamic shape support
  - KV cache support
  - streaming support
  - stateful model support
  - tokenizer packaging
  - schema validation hook
  - telemetry hook
  - revocation hook
- Output bundle:
  - runtime model file
  - tokenizer or preprocessor
  - postprocessor
  - manifest
  - proof subset
  - fallback route
