- `needs_live_certification`: regulated runtime claims need live deployment evidence.

Open proof:

- one browser proof packet;
- one iOS proof packet;
- one Android proof packet;
- one GPU server proof packet;
- one CPU/air-gap proof packet;
- one quantization-vs-K-Score report;
- one runtime drift/deopt demo.

### W4427 Implementation Handoff

Build sequence:

1. Define KolmTargetCompilerIR.
2. Define target capability fingerprint.
3. Define runtime proof packet.
4. Add dense fallback compiler path.
5. Add quantization planner with K-Score thresholds.
6. Add target conformance scenarios.
7. Add browser target probe.
8. Add mobile target probe.
9. Add server target probe.
10. Add runtime ABI and policy ABI.
11. Add autotuning sandbox.
12. Add drift sentinel and auto-deopt.
13. Add CLI and account UI readiness surfaces.
14. Add public target benchmark matrix.

First implementation target:

```text
support operations `.kolm` artifact compiled to browser WebGPU/WASM, Android LiteRT/QNN, iOS Core ML, CPU GGUF/ONNX, and GPU vLLM/TensorRT fallback with one proof packet per target.
```

### W4428 Ultimate Any-Device Runtime Objective

Ultimate invariant:

```text
Every `.kolm` artifact can be lowered to the best supported runtime for a named target only when Kolm can prove the chosen profile preserves behavior, rights, safety, cost, latency, and fallback guarantees for that target.
```

If Kolm executes this layer:

- "runs anywhere" becomes evidence-backed, not slogan-backed;
- quantization becomes failure-elastic and K-Score-aware;
- device support becomes testable;
- server runtime gets tied to governance;
- browser and mobile become real product surfaces;
- air-gapped deployment becomes credible;
- runtime profiles become marketplace trust objects;
- every optimization can deopt safely;
- Kolm owns behavior-aware AI compilation rather than format conversion.

This is the ultimate compilation tool: not ONNX export, not GGUF conversion, not TensorRT-LLM wrapping, but a proof-carrying compiler from owned AI behavior to the safest and most efficient target runtime.

## Autonomous Experimentation, Causal Optimization, And Roadmap Intelligence Layer

The research program now contains distillation science, K-Score governance, workload twins, artifact lifecycle, and any-device runtime compilation. The next frontier is deciding what to do next. A billion-dollar Kolm cannot depend on intuition or endless feature lists. It needs an optimization system that chooses experiments, build waves, labels, runtime profiles, route policies, pricing moves, and research bets based on expected causal impact on tracked metrics.

Google Vizier shows that black-box optimization services can tune complex systems at scale. Ax and BoTorch show adaptive experimentation and multi-objective Bayesian optimization can search expensive spaces efficiently. Optuna shows practical automated hyperparameter search. DoWhy and EconML show why observational correlations are not enough when product changes, routing changes, pricing changes, and label interventions are entangled. Evidently and Phoenix show that drift, traces, evals, and experiments need to be operationalized. Kolm should combine these ideas into an AI product optimization engine.

The invention is KolmExperimentGovernorIR:

```text
product graph + metric graph + workload twins + experiment lake + roadmap backlog
-> causal hypotheses
-> constrained multi-objective experiment planner
-> budget/risk/rights gate
-> experiment execution packet
-> K-Score and business outcome measurement
-> causal effect estimate
-> next best action
```

Core rule:

```text
Kolm should not merely record experiments. It should allocate scarce research, engineering, label, compute, and go-to-market capacity to the experiment with the highest expected verified metric movement.
```

### W4500 KolmExperimentGovernorIR Schema

Define the decision object.

Fields:

- `objective_set`: K-Score, cost, latency, safety, privacy, rights, device coverage, conversion, retention, ARR, margin, support load, and benchmark trust.
- `candidate_actions`: train, distill, route, label, compile, quantize, price, document, launch, deprecate, partner, publish, or reject.
- `constraints`: compute budget, label budget, engineering capacity, legal scope, customer risk, release window, and rights.
- `evidence_inputs`: workload twin results, production traces, K-Score receipts, customer interviews, sales funnel, support tickets, incident reports, and benchmark deltas.
- `causal_model`: assumptions, confounders, treatment, outcome, identification strategy, and sensitivity checks.
- `optimizer_state`: prior results, surrogate model, Pareto front, uncertainty, and acquisition function.
- `decision`: next experiment, expected value, risk, stop condition, owner, and proof required.

This IR makes research prioritization auditable.

### W4501 Metric Causal Graph

Build a causal graph of product metrics.

Nodes:

- capture volume;
- workload diversity;
- label quality;
- K-Score;
- route deferral;
- cloud spend;
- latency;
- runtime coverage;
- device success;
- trust proof;
- onboarding completion;
- activation;
- paid conversion;
- expansion;
- support load;
- gross margin;
- retention.

Edges should encode hypotheses:

- better workload twin coverage improves distillation success;
- higher K-Score confidence improves enterprise conversion;
- better device proof improves regulated buyer trust;
- lower latency improves activation only when quality is above floor;
- cheaper runtime hurts retention if safety incidents rise.

This prevents optimizing one metric while harming the system.

### W4502 Multi-Objective Product Utility Function

Kolm needs explicit utility.

Utility components:

- customer value;
- Kolm gross margin;
- safety risk reduction;
- privacy risk reduction;
- rights compliance;
- product differentiation;
- implementation speed;
- evidence quality;
- future optionality.

Do not collapse everything too early. Maintain a Pareto frontier:

- quality vs cost;
- speed vs safety;
- openness vs moat;
- short-term ARR vs long-term standard;
- customer-specific value vs reusable platform value.

Output:

- Pareto candidates;
- dominated candidates;
- recommended action with explanation.

### W4503 Experiment Portfolio Optimizer

Optimize portfolio, not only individual experiments.

Portfolio buckets:

- P0 customer-blocking fixes;
- K-Score calibration;
- distillation research;
- runtime/device proof;
- connector coverage;
- enterprise trust;
- sales conversion;
- public research;
- marketplace trust;
- platform debt.

Constraints:

- no bucket starves for more than one planning interval;
- P0 safety/privacy/legal work preempts speculative work;
- research bets must include kill criteria;
- go-to-market experiments must not overclaim readiness.

The system should recommend a balanced portfolio of bets.

### W4504 Causal Hypothesis Compiler

Turn vague ideas into testable hypotheses.

Bad:

```text
Improve onboarding.
```

Good:

```text
If the account overview shows route savings, K-Score confidence, and readiness closeout within 30 seconds of first capture, then activation-to-second-session will increase by 15 percent for technical buyers without increasing support tickets.
```

Hypothesis fields:

- treatment;
- target segment;
- outcome metric;
- guardrail metric;
- expected effect size;
- measurement window;
- confounders;
- minimum detectable effect;
- stop rule.

### W4505 Adaptive Experiment Planner

Use adaptive experimentation for expensive product and research search.

Search spaces:

- student architectures;
- loss recipes;
- quantization maps;
- route thresholds;
- scenario pack composition;
- label allocation;
- onboarding flows;
- pricing offers;
- documentation paths;
- sales proof assets.

Planner choices:

- random exploration;
- Bayesian optimization;
- multi-objective Bayesian optimization;
- bandits;
- successive halving;
- early stopping;
- constrained search;
- safe exploration.

Rule:

```text
Adaptive search must respect safety, privacy, rights, and customer-risk constraints before optimizing reward.
```

### W4506 Experiment Budget Market

Treat compute, labels, expert time, and engineering time as scarce capital.

Budgets:

- GPU hours;
- teacher model spend;
- human labels;
- expert labels;
- researcher time;
- engineer time;
- device-farm time;
- sales cycles;
- customer trust.

Each experiment estimates:

- expected metric move;
- uncertainty;
- cost;
- reversibility;
- risk;
- reuse value.

Decision:

```text
fund, defer, merge, split, kill, or require more evidence
```

### W4507 Label Value Optimizer

Not all labels are equal.

Select labels by:

- expected K-Score uncertainty reduction;
- expected distillation lift;
- route threshold improvement;
- customer ARR affected;
- safety severity;
- rarity;
- transfer potential;
- expert scarcity;
- cost.

Output:

- next examples to label;
- required rater type;
- expected value of information;
- whether synthetic data is enough;
- whether live expert review is required.

This connects active learning to product economics.

### W4508 Scenario Value Optimizer

Not all scenarios are worth building.

Rank scenarios by:

- risk mass;
- customer frequency;
- severity;
- transferability;
- benchmark gap;
- sales proof value;
- incident recurrence;
- K-Score uncertainty reduction;
- implementation effort.

Scenario backlog becomes optimized, not manually curated.

### W4509 Runtime Profile Search Governor

Runtime autotuning can waste huge time if unguided.

Governor inputs:

- target fingerprint;
- workload twin;
- K-Score thresholds;
- prior runtime profiles;
- device-farm queue;
- energy/cost target;
- release deadline.

Actions:

- test aggressive profile;
- test conservative profile;
- skip target;
- request more device evidence;
- choose dense fallback;
- deoptimize in production.

Metric:

```text
expected K-Score-preserving runtime utility per device-farm minute
```

### W4510 Distillation Experiment Search Governor

Govern training experiments.

Actions:

- no-train;
- collect more captures;
- request labels;
- change teacher council;
- change loss recipe;
- change architecture;
- change curriculum;
- change quantization co-training;
- stop run early;
- promote candidate.

Stop when:

- expected K-Score lift is below cost;
- safety axis regresses;
- rights scope insufficient;
- transfer uncertainty too high;
- cheaper routing dominates distillation.

### W4511 Route Policy Experiment Governor

Route policy is a continuous experiment.

Candidate treatments:

- local-first;
- teacher fallback;
- provider switch;
- risk-based fallback;
- confidence threshold;
- region allowlist;
- cache policy;
- human review threshold;
- deny policy.

Guardrails:

- critical fail rate;
- privacy leaks;
- rights violations;
- latency p95;
- customer complaints;
- gross margin.

Output:

- recommended route policy;
- uncertainty;
- segment scope;
- rollback rule.

### W4512 Pricing And Packaging Experiment Governor

Pricing should be tested with ethics and margin constraints.

Candidate experiments:

- free tier limit;
- Pro usage cap;
- Team seat/usage mix;
- enterprise platform fee;
- savings-based add-on;
- compute credits;
- marketplace private offer;
- pilot-to-contract conversion.

Guardrails:

- margin floor;
- customer trust;
- support cost;
- activation;
- conversion;
- expansion;
- churn.

Rule:

```text
Pricing experiments cannot hide readiness gaps or imply unproven savings.
```

### W4513 Documentation And Developer Experience Optimizer

Docs should be optimized by user outcome, not page count.

Metrics:

- time to first capture;
- time to first K-Score receipt;
- time to first `.kolm` artifact;
- time to first route swap;
- time to first device proof;
- support ticket rate;
- copy-paste failure rate;
- CLI error rate.

Experiments:

- quickstart order;
- code sample style;
- tutorial length;
- API reference grouping;
- docs search ranking;
- account UI next action;
- error message rewrite.

This connects docs directly to activation.

### W4514 Research Bet Evaluator

Evaluate research bets with portfolio logic.

Fields:

- scientific novelty;
- product leverage;
- moat leverage;
- publication likelihood;
- implementation cost;
- dependency risk;
- customer relevance;
- data availability;
- negative-result value.

Decision:

- publish-track;
- private-moat-track;
- prototype-only;
- kill;
- revisit after more data.

This prevents research theater.

### W4515 Competitive Response Simulator

Simulate competitor moves.

Competitor actions:

- provider lowers prices;
- cloud adds routing;
- open-source runtime improves;
- eval vendor copies K-Score surface;
- MLOps platform adds artifact registry;
- device vendor launches local model tooling;
- marketplace adds AI artifacts;
- legal ruling changes distillation risk.

Output:

- affected Kolm moats;
- counter-move;
- urgency;
- evidence needed;
- product copy changes.

### W4516 Customer Segment Optimizer

Choose customer segments by evidence.

Segment features:

- AI spend;
- repetitive task mass;
- regulation;
- device need;
- data rights clarity;
- engineering maturity;
- buying urgency;
- willingness to run capture;
- security burden;
- support cost.

Output:

- best ICP segment;
- rejected segment;
- onboarding requirements;
- proof asset needed;
- expected ARR and margin.

### W4517 Roadmap Wave Compiler

Turn the optimizer output into implementation waves.

Wave packet:

- wave id;
- objective;
- metric target;
- impacted product surfaces;
- files/modules likely affected;
- proof required;
- tests/gates;
- rollout plan;
- kill criteria;
- dependencies.

This makes implementation agents faster because research decisions become concrete build packets.

### W4518 Experiment Provenance Ledger

Every decision needs provenance.

Record:

- hypothesis;
- data used;
- exclusions;
- model/optimizer;
- decision;
- owner;
- result;
- causal estimate;
- uncertainty;
- follow-up.

This prevents repeating failed experiments and supports investor/customer proof.

### W4519 Causal Effect Estimation Layer

After experiments, estimate causal impact.

Methods:

- randomized A/B;
- switchback tests;
- difference-in-differences;
- synthetic controls;
- causal forests;
- doubly robust estimation;
- instrumental variables when needed;
- sensitivity analysis.

Use cases:

- did route policy reduce cost without hurting quality?
- did onboarding copy improve activation?
- did K-Score receipt improve sales conversion?
- did runtime compiler improve retention?
- did expert labels improve production transfer?

### W4520 Drift-To-Experiment Trigger

Drift should trigger experiments.

Drift sources:

- workload drift;
- provider model drift;
- connector drift;
- K-Score drift;
- runtime drift;
- price drift;
- buyer behavior drift;
- support ticket drift.

Actions:

- run replay;
- generate scenario;
- launch route experiment;
- request labels;
- deoptimize runtime;
- update docs;
- update pricing model.

### W4521 Autonomous Safe Exploration

Autonomy must be bounded.

Safe exploration constraints:

- no live side effects without approval;
- no high-risk customer rollout without canary;
- no safety/privacy/rights guardrail violation;
- no pricing manipulation outside approved bounds;
- no training on disallowed data;
- no production claim from uncalibrated simulation.

The governor can suggest. Promotion still follows product gates.

### W4522 Multi-Agent Implementation Planner

The roadmap has many parallel agents. The optimizer should decompose work.

Planner outputs:

- backend slice;
- frontend slice;
- docs slice;
- research slice;
- verification slice;
- disjoint write sets;
- dependency edges;
- merge risk;
- verification owner.

This directly supports the user's multi-bot workflow without collisions.

### W4523 Product Surface Coverage Optimizer

Every product surface should get attention.

Surfaces:

- public website;
- docs;
- CLI;
- API;
- account;
- registry;
- runtime;
- enterprise trust;
- marketplace;
- SDKs;
- onboarding;
- sales proof.

The optimizer flags:

- under-tested surface;
- under-documented surface;
- no screenshot proof;
- no API proof;
- no K-Score proof;
- no customer journey proof.

### W4524 Invention Search Engine

Use the experiment lake to propose inventions.

Inputs:

- repeated failure clusters;
- high-value unsolved scenarios;
- dominated runtime profiles;
- impossible customer asks;
- competitor gaps;
- research literature;
- support tickets;
- sales objections.

Outputs:

- invention hypothesis;
- why now;
- expected metric move;
- build path;
- proof required;
- publication/private boundary.

This makes invention systematic rather than dependent on occasional brainstorming.

### W4525 Optimization Failure Mode Library

Failure modes:

- optimizer overfits short-term ARR;
- optimizer ignores safety tails;
- Bayesian search exploits measurement bug;
- causal graph omits confounder;
- labels selected for cheapness not value;
- experiments collide across teams;
- public metric improves while customer metric worsens;
- optimizer recommends legally impossible action;
- drift trigger causes experiment spam.

Each failure mode needs:

- detector;
- guardrail;
- owner;
- kill switch.

### W4526 Product Graph Experimentation Readiness

Add experimentation readiness to the product graph.

Statuses:

- `implemented`: experiment ledger and manual wave packets exist.
- `needs_public_benchmark_data`: public experiment methodology not published.
- `needs_package_release`: optimizer service/package not released.
- `needs_live_certification`: causal claims need production experiment evidence.

Open proof:

- one K-Score calibration experiment;
- one distillation search experiment;
- one runtime autotuning experiment;
- one route policy experiment;
- one docs/onboarding experiment;
- one pricing/package experiment;
- one causal effect report.

### W4527 Implementation Handoff

Build sequence:

1. Define KolmExperimentGovernorIR.
2. Add experiment provenance ledger.
3. Add metric causal graph.
4. Add candidate action schema.
5. Add multi-objective utility model.
6. Add label value optimizer.
7. Add scenario value optimizer.
8. Add runtime profile search governor.
9. Add distillation search governor.
10. Add route policy governor.
11. Add causal effect report format.
12. Add roadmap wave compiler.
13. Add safe exploration gates.
14. Add product surface coverage optimizer.

First implementation target:

```text
choose between three next actions for the support operations sandbox: collect labels, run a distillation architecture search, or compile a device runtime profile, using expected K-Score lift per dollar and risk.
```

### W4528 Ultimate Experiment Governor Objective

Ultimate invariant:

```text
Kolm always knows the next highest-value experiment or build wave, why it is worth doing, what metric it should move, what risk it carries, and what evidence will prove whether it worked.
```

If Kolm executes this layer:

- research becomes compounding;
- implementation waves become higher signal;
- label spend improves;
- compute spend improves;
- runtime search improves;
- product copy becomes evidence-backed;
- pricing becomes safer;
- customer segments become clearer;
- every bot or engineer gets better task packets;
- the company stops mistaking motion for progress.

This is the operating system for the invention lab. It turns "research forever" into a machine that finds, funds, proves, kills, or ships the next best Kolm breakthrough.

## Ecosystem Standardization, Loader, SDK, And Network Effects Layer

The prior layers make Kolm technically stronger. This layer makes Kolm harder to
displace. A model compiler wins only if the artifact becomes something other
systems expect to install, verify, execute, inspect, compose, and govern. Docker
won because containers became an ecosystem object. Hugging Face won because
model repositories became an ecosystem object. MCP is spreading because tool
servers are becoming an ecosystem object. Kolm should make `.kolm` the ecosystem
object for production AI behavior.

The frontier insight is that `.kolm` should not compete with OCI, Sigstore,
SLSA, SPDX, CycloneDX, model cards, MLflow, Hugging Face, MCP, OpenAPI, or SDK
package managers. It should bind them. A `.kolm` artifact should be installable
like a package, attestable like a build, documented like a model card,
invokable like an API, composable like an MCP tool, governable like an enterprise
asset, and executable like a runtime profile.

### North Star

Any customer, partner, cloud, device runtime, registry, or agent framework can
discover a `.kolm` artifact, verify who built it, inspect what it can do,
understand what it is allowed to do, install the correct loader, execute it on a
supported target, and report back proof without private conversations with Kolm.

### W4600 KolmEcosystemArtifactIR Schema

Create `KolmEcosystemArtifactIR`, the portable ecosystem contract for `.kolm`
distribution.

Core fields:

- artifact digest;
- artifact semantic version;
- artifact capability class;
- task family;
- modality set;
- runtime profiles;
- loader requirements;
- policy requirements;
- provenance pointers;
- SBOM and AI BOM pointers;
- signature bundle pointers;
- model card pointers;
- evaluation card pointers;
- marketplace metadata;
- support and liability tier;
- deprecation policy;
- compatibility matrix.

This IR must be smaller than the internal product graph but rich enough for
external systems to reason about the artifact without needing Kolm internals.

Implementation handoff:

1. Define the schema in the research-to-product graph.
2. Generate JSON Schema.
3. Generate a human-readable spec page.
4. Add examples for API router, distilled student, edge student, guardrail, and
   vertical foundation student artifacts.
5. Reject marketplace listing if the IR is incomplete.

Metric impact:

- install conversion up;
- enterprise security review time down;
- integration support tickets down;
- third-party loader adoption up.

### W4601 Artifact Identity And Namespace Authority

The ecosystem needs names that cannot be hijacked.

Kolm should define an authoritative namespace model:

- public namespace;
- verified publisher namespace;
- enterprise private namespace;
- regulated namespace;
- partner namespace;
- deprecated namespace;
- reserved system namespace.

Each namespace must map to:

- registry identity;
- publisher identity;
- signing policy;
- entitlement policy;
- transfer policy;
- dispute policy;
- squatting prevention rules.

Dependency confusion is not only a package-manager problem. It becomes an AI
artifact problem when a customer installs `claims-redactor` from the wrong
registry or a partner publishes a confusingly similar artifact name.

Build design:

- registry signs namespace state;
- publisher signs artifact state;
- customer policy pins allowed registries and namespaces;
- resolver refuses ambiguous names;
- marketplace UI shows namespace proof.

Failure tests:

- public artifact shadows a private enterprise artifact;
- registry serves a valid digest from an unauthorized namespace;
- partner namespace changes ownership without explicit transfer receipt;
- artifact name uses homoglyph or confusing punctuation.

### W4602 Dual-Signature Registry Publication

A `.kolm` artifact should carry both publisher and registry proof.

Publisher signature proves:

- the builder identity;
- the release pipeline;
- the source commit or recipe input;
- the eval and policy packet;
- the artifact digest.

Registry countersignature proves:

- the artifact was accepted by the registry;
- the namespace policy allowed publication;
- the listing metadata matched the artifact digest;
- entitlement and marketplace state were attached;
- the publication timestamp and sequence number were recorded.

This closes a gap common in software ecosystems: a user can verify who built a
package, but not always which registry was authoritative for the namespace when
the package was installed.

Implementation handoff:

1. Define publisher signature envelope.
2. Define registry countersignature envelope.
3. Add resolver policy requiring both signatures for marketplace and regulated
   installs.
4. Add offline verification bundle for air-gapped use.

### W4603 OCI-Compatible Artifact Packaging

Kolm should make `.kolm` artifacts compatible with OCI distribution patterns
without pretending `.kolm` is just a container image.

Packaging layout:

- artifact layer;
- metadata layer;
- eval packet layer;
- policy layer;
- runtime profile layer;
- model card layer;
- SBOM or AI BOM layer;
- signature and attestation references.

Why:

- enterprises already trust OCI registries;
- cloud marketplaces already understand image-style distribution;
- Sigstore and Cosign can attach signatures and attestations;
- artifact referrers create a natural path for proof packets;
- existing registry infrastructure avoids building every distribution primitive
  from scratch.

Kolm-specific difference:

The manifest must describe behavior, policy, eval scope, and runtime
compatibility, not only files. A `.kolm` artifact is a behavioral package.

Failure tests:

- registry stores artifact but drops eval referrer;
- artifact installs without policy layer;
- runtime profile digest does not match manifest;
- registry garbage collection deletes proof attachments.

### W4604 Loader Compatibility Contract

Define a loader contract that every external runtime can implement.

Loader responsibilities:

- verify signatures;
- parse manifest;
- check runtime compatibility;
- enforce policy;
- load artifact payload;
- map invocation interface;
- emit telemetry and proof;
- fail closed on unsupported capability;
- report exact incompatibility reason.

Loader maturity levels:

- L0: inspect only;
- L1: verify and list capabilities;
- L2: execute local runtime profile;
- L3: enforce policy and telemetry;
- L4: support composition and rollback;
- L5: certified regulated execution.

This lets Kolm rank ecosystem support honestly. A runtime that can only inspect
metadata should not be marketed as execution support.

### W4605 SDK Conformance Test Suite

Every SDK should run the same conformance suite.

Required languages:

- Node;
- Python;
- Rust;
- C;
- TypeScript browser;
- Swift;
- Kotlin;
- React Native;
- CLI;
- MCP server adapter.

Conformance dimensions:

- artifact verification;
- manifest parsing;
- invocation;
- streaming;
- errors;
- policy refusal;
- telemetry;
- offline mode;
- upgrade path;
- compatibility reporting.

Implementation handoff:

1. Define language-neutral fixtures.
2. Define expected responses.
3. Generate per-SDK test harnesses.
4. Publish a public badge for SDK conformance.
5. Fail release if an SDK claims support without conformance evidence.

Metric impact:

- SDK bug rate down;
- developer activation up;
- docs drift down;
- support cost down.

### W4606 Package Manager Release Matrix

Kolm should treat package manager publication as a product surface, not release
cleanup.

Release channels:

- npm;
- PyPI;
- crates.io;
- Homebrew;
- winget;
- Docker or OCI;
- GitHub Releases;
- VS Code Marketplace;
- Swift Package Index;
- Maven Central;
- NuGet if .NET customers matter;
- MCP Registry for tool adapters.

Each release channel needs:

- signed artifact;
- provenance;
- version mapping;
- install command;
- smoke test;
- rollback policy;
- deprecation policy;
- ownership continuity;
- support contact;
- security disclosure path.

Do not claim channel readiness until install works from a clean machine and
provenance is visible.

### W4607 Trusted Publishing And Provenance Standard

Kolm should use OIDC-based trusted publishing wherever available.

Why:

- avoids long-lived package tokens;
- ties package to build identity;
- improves auditability;
- gives enterprise security teams a familiar verification story;
- creates a bridge from GitHub artifact attestations to package-manager
  provenance.

Required publication proof:

- source repository;
- workflow identity;
- commit digest;
- artifact digest;
- package digest;
- environment approval state;
- signer identity;
- registry acceptance.

Failure tests:

- package was built locally and uploaded with a personal token;
- package has no provenance statement;
- artifact digest does not match source build;
- workflow identity changes without release owner approval.

### W4608 AI BOM And Behavioral SBOM

SPDX and CycloneDX cover important software supply-chain facts, but `.kolm`
needs an AI-specific behavior bill of materials.

AI BOM fields:

- teacher models;
- student architecture;
- datasets and capture sources;
- synthetic data generators;
- eval suites;
- judge models;
- policy bundles;
- guardrails;
- redaction modules;
- runtime kernels;
- quantization settings;
- known limitations;
- license constraints;
- geography constraints.

Behavioral SBOM fields:

- task domains;
- unsupported tasks;
- refusal policy;
- escalation policy;
- confidence policy;
- route policy;
- privacy policy;
- audit policy.

This gives procurement and security teams a single file that answers: what is
inside this AI behavior and what can it do?

### W4609 Model Card, Eval Card, And Policy Card Fusion

Model cards are useful but insufficient for compiled AI artifacts.

Kolm should publish three cards per artifact:

- model card: model, data, architecture, intended use;
- eval card: benchmark, scenario, slice, regression, human review;
- policy card: allowed use, forbidden use, privacy, routing, escalation,
  retention, liability.

The marketplace listing should render a fused card, but the files remain
separate so security, legal, and ML teams can review the parts they own.

Failure tests:

- artifact has model card but no policy card;
- eval card claims benchmark without raw report pointer;
- card version does not match artifact version;
- marketplace renders stale card after artifact update.

### W4610 External Runtime Adapter Kit

Kolm should make external runtime support cheap.

Adapter kit contents:

- loader contract;
- minimal parser;
- verifier;
- policy evaluator;
- runtime profile interpreter;
- test fixtures;
- conformance runner;
- example adapter;
- certification checklist.

Target external adapters:

- llama.cpp;
- Ollama;
- Hugging Face Hub;
- vLLM;
- SGLang;
- ONNX Runtime GenAI;
- ExecuTorch;
- LiteRT;
- Core ML;
- WebGPU or WebNN browser runtime;
- LM Studio import path.

The adapter kit should make "supports `.kolm`" a low-effort feature for the
ecosystem.

### W4611 MCP Tool And Agent Artifact Bridge

MCP makes tool discovery standard, but tool execution still needs stronger
policy, provenance, and evaluation for enterprise use.

Kolm should bridge `.kolm` artifacts to MCP:

- artifact becomes MCP tool;
- manifest becomes tool description;
- policy card becomes permission scope;
- eval card becomes trust badge;
- runtime proof becomes invocation receipt;
- artifact version becomes tool version;
- route policy becomes model selection logic.

This lets agents call compiled, governed AI behavior instead of arbitrary
unverified tools.

Failure tests:

- MCP tool description overstates artifact capability;
- agent calls tool outside policy scope;
- tool version changes without artifact digest pin;
- receipt lacks artifact digest or policy version.

### W4612 OpenAPI And SDK Generation Contract

Kolm artifacts that expose API-like behavior should generate an OpenAPI
contract.

Generated contract should include:

- request schema;
- response schema;
- streaming events;
- error codes;
- confidence fields;
- provenance fields;
- policy refusal fields;
- audit receipt fields;
- rate-limit hints;
- example calls.

SDK generation then becomes deterministic. This reduces integration time and
prevents copy drift between docs, SDKs, and runtime behavior.

### W4613 Artifact Discovery Search Index

The registry needs search that understands AI behavior.

Index dimensions:

- task;
- industry;
- modality;
- language;
- runtime;
- device;
- eval score;
- K-Score;
- privacy tier;
- compliance scope;
- license;
- latency;
- cost;
- support tier;
- installation channel.

Discovery should answer:

- "best HIPAA-safe redactor for edge iOS";
- "low-latency support-ticket router for WebGPU";
- "auditable claims extractor for payer workflows";
- "tool-use agent policy bundle for finance."

This turns the marketplace into an intent engine, not a file listing.

### W4614 Compatibility Negotiation Protocol

Installation should negotiate compatibility before download.

Inputs:

- customer runtime;
- device capabilities;
- policy requirements;
- geography;
- entitlement;
- data class;
- latency target;
- offline requirement.

Outputs:

- compatible artifact digest;
- required loader;
- unsupported reasons;
- fallback artifact;
- upgrade recommendation;
- proof packet requirements.

This prevents bad installs and creates high-quality telemetry about what
customers are trying to run.

### W4615 Marketplace Trust Score

Create a marketplace trust score that is not a vanity metric.

Score dimensions:

- signature completeness;
- provenance completeness;
- eval coverage;
- policy coverage;
- runtime conformance;
- support responsiveness;
- incident history;
- compatibility breadth;
- documentation quality;
- customer-reported failures;
- security review status.

Important rule:

Trust score must show subscores. A high eval score cannot hide poor provenance,
and strong provenance cannot hide weak eval coverage.

### W4616 Enterprise Private Registry Bridge

Many enterprises will not install from a public registry.

Kolm needs a bridge pattern:

- public artifact metadata mirrored to private registry;
- private entitlements enforced;
- signatures preserved;
- registry countersignature added;
- private policy overlays attached;
- audit logs exported;
- air-gapped bundle supported.

Target environments:

- Artifactory;
- Nexus;
- private OCI registries;
- cloud artifact registries;
- S3-compatible object storage;
- GitHub Enterprise;
- self-hosted Kolm registry.

Metric impact:

- enterprise close rate up;
- security exceptions down;
- deployment time down.

### W4617 Ecosystem Governance Board

If `.kolm` becomes a standard, governance must not look like one vendor
rewriting rules whenever convenient.

Governance model:

- public spec versioning;
- compatibility policy;
- deprecation policy;
- security disclosure process;
- reference test suite;
- independent implementation path;
- partner advisory process;
- change proposal template;
- conformance badge rules.

Do not over-open too early:

- keep proprietary optimization data private;
- open the artifact and conformance contract;
- publish enough for third parties to build loaders;
- keep cloud optimizer, K-Score calibration data, and distillation telemetry as
  strategic advantages.

### W4618 Ecosystem Telemetry With Privacy Boundaries

Network effects require telemetry, but enterprise buyers require privacy.

Collect:

- install attempts;
- compatibility failures;
- runtime target;
- loader version;
- artifact class;
- failure code;
- opt-in benchmark reports;
- anonymized latency and cost bins.

Do not collect by default:

- prompts;
- outputs;
- customer data;
- private policy contents;
- regulated identifiers;
- secrets.

Privacy design:

- local aggregation;
- differential privacy option for broad metrics;
- enterprise opt-out;
- signed telemetry schema;
- audit-visible collection policy.

### W4619 Backward Compatibility And Deprecation Economics

Ecosystems die when upgrades are painful.

Kolm should define:

- semantic versioning rules for artifact behavior;
- runtime compatibility windows;
- loader minimum versions;
- policy schema migration;
- eval report migration;
- artifact deprecation notices;
- long-term support channels;
- regulated artifact freeze policy.

Deprecation should include:

- reason;
- affected customers;
- replacement artifact;
- migration path;
- deadline;
- risk if not upgraded;
- support contact.

### W4620 Ecosystem Abuse And Malware Defense

An AI artifact marketplace needs abuse controls.

Threats:

- malicious artifact payload;
- hidden exfiltration tool;
- prompt injection packaged as behavior;
- policy bypass;
- model extraction payload;
- poisoned eval card;
- fake provenance;
- namespace squatting;
- typosquatting;
- dependency confusion;
- malicious update.

Defenses:

- static artifact inspection;
- sandboxed execution;
- behavior fuzzing;
- policy linting;
- provenance verification;
- namespace review;
- malware scanning;
- marketplace quarantine;
- customer blast-radius analysis.

### W4621 Partner Certification Program

Partner ecosystems need a certification path.

Certification tracks:

- runtime loader certified;
- cloud deployment certified;
- device runtime certified;
- vertical artifact certified;
- connector certified;
- security integration certified;
- SI implementation certified.

Each track requires:

- conformance tests;
- documentation;
- support path;
- incident process;
- renewal cadence;
- compatibility matrix.

This lets Kolm scale implementation capacity without losing quality.

### W4622 Standard Library Of Artifact Types

Define a standard library of first-class artifact types.

Initial types:

- router artifact;
- distilled student artifact;
- edge student artifact;
- guardrail artifact;
- redaction artifact;
- eval artifact;
- policy artifact;
- agent tool artifact;
- RAG retriever artifact;
- multimodal extractor artifact;
- workflow composition artifact;
- vertical foundation student artifact.

Each type needs:

- required fields;
- optional fields;
- eval expectations;
- runtime compatibility expectations;
- policy expectations;
- marketplace category.

This prevents every artifact from becoming a custom one-off.

### W4623 Ecosystem Scorecard

Create a public ecosystem scorecard for `.kolm` readiness.

Scorecard categories:

- specification maturity;
- reference implementation maturity;
- loader count;
- SDK conformance;
- package release completeness;
- registry support;
- marketplace quality;
- partner integrations;
- security posture;
- compliance posture;
- docs quality;
- community examples.

This is not marketing. It is an internal operating dashboard that can become
public when strong enough.

### W4624 Ecosystem Flywheel Strategy

Network effects need deliberate sequencing.

Flywheel:

1. Ship best first-party artifacts.
2. Make them installable and verifiable.
3. Publish loader SDKs.
4. Certify two external runtimes.
5. Let enterprises mirror artifacts privately.
6. Launch partner certification.
7. Launch marketplace with strict trust score.
8. Publish conformance badge.
9. Encourage vertical partners to publish artifacts.
10. Turn artifact usage telemetry into better runtime profiles and K-Score
    calibration.

The flywheel is not "more artifacts." It is more verified artifacts running in
more places with more proof.

### W4625 Ecosystem Failure Mode Library

Track ecosystem-specific failure modes.

Failures:

- artifact installs but cannot execute;
- loader executes but ignores policy;
- marketplace listing claims unsupported runtime;
- private registry loses signatures;
- SDK parses old schema incorrectly;
- package manager release lags core release;
- third-party adapter forks behavior;
- customer pins vulnerable artifact;
- deprecation notice never reaches user;
- trust badge becomes stale.

Every failure should produce:

- reproduction;
- owner;
- affected artifact class;
- prevented-by control;
- test fixture;
- docs update.

### W4626 Product Graph Ecosystem Readiness

The product graph should expose ecosystem readiness separately from core
functionality.

Suggested readiness fields:

- `spec_ready`;
- `schema_ready`;
- `loader_ready`;
- `sdk_conformance_ready`;
- `package_channel_ready`;
- `registry_ready`;
- `marketplace_ready`;
- `partner_certification_ready`;
- `private_registry_ready`;
- `telemetry_privacy_ready`;
- `governance_ready`.

This prevents the company from claiming "standard" before external adoption and
conformance infrastructure exist.

### W4627 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmEcosystemArtifactIR`.
2. Emit JSON Schema and examples.
3. Add publisher and registry signature envelopes.
4. Package artifacts with OCI-compatible layout.
5. Build loader conformance fixtures.
6. Add SDK conformance test runners.
7. Build private registry mirror flow.
8. Create marketplace trust score.
9. Create partner certification checklist.
10. Expose ecosystem readiness in product graph.
11. Publish the external adapter kit.
12. Start with two runtime partners and one enterprise private registry pilot.

Do not start with a broad marketplace. Start with verified first-party artifacts
and a tight conformance story.

### W4628 Ultimate Ecosystem Objective

Ultimate objective: make `.kolm` the signed, governed, portable package format
for production AI behavior.

When this works:

- developers install AI behavior instead of copying prompts;
- enterprises verify AI behavior instead of trusting demos;
- runtimes support `.kolm` because customers ask for it;
- partners publish artifacts because trust and distribution are built in;
- marketplaces rank artifacts by proof, not hype;
- private registries preserve policy and provenance;
- Kolm becomes the neutral distribution and verification layer for compiled AI.

This is the path from product to standard. Distillation makes artifacts valuable.
The ecosystem makes them inevitable.

## Unified Three-Surface Product Kernel And Backend Architecture Layer

The product should not be explained as "one AI API" because that only covers one
surface. The true product is a backend system that turns production AI behavior
into owned, portable, measurable artifacts across three surfaces:

1. Model traffic ownership: capture, route, audit, and optimize external model
   calls.
2. Distillation and compilation: turn captured behavior and expert data into
   task-specific student artifacts.
3. Enterprise any-device deployment: push verified artifacts to clouds, private
   clusters, browsers, phones, edge hardware, and air-gapped environments.

The unifying category is not a gateway, compiler, registry, or MLOps platform by
itself. It is a behavior-to-artifact operating system. Every prompt, route,
trace, eval, policy, student, runtime profile, and deployment becomes part of a
single product kernel.

### North Star

A customer can start with messy production model calls and end with signed,
auditable, cheaper, faster, task-specific AI artifacts running anywhere, while
the backend keeps a continuous proof trail from source traffic to runtime
outcome.

### W4700 KolmProductKernelIR Schema

Define `KolmProductKernelIR`, the canonical backend object that links all three
product surfaces.

Core fields:

- organization;
- workspace;
- namespace;
- source traffic streams;
- capture policies;
- route policies;
- datasets;
- eval suites;
- distillation jobs;
- compile jobs;
- artifact versions;
- runtime profiles;
- deployment targets;
- telemetry streams;
- readiness states;
- proof packets;
- commercial metrics;
- compliance boundaries.

This IR should not be a database table. It should be a generated contract that
summarizes the state of the customer AI estate and lets every API, account page,
CLI command, and automation agent agree on what exists.

### W4701 Three-Surface Capability Contract

Every capability must declare which surface it serves.

Surface taxonomy:

- `traffic_control`: capture, route, save cost, audit, replay.
- `artifact_compilation`: distill, fine-tune, evaluate, quantize, sign.
- `anywhere_runtime`: deploy, verify, monitor, rollback, govern.

Rules:

- no public copy can describe one surface as the whole product;
- each page, API group, CLI command, and account module declares one primary
  surface and optional secondary surfaces;
- cross-surface workflows declare the handoff object;
- dashboards show missing setup per surface.

This prevents the product from feeling disorganized and prevents backend routes
from becoming unrelated features.

### W4702 Source Traffic Ingestion Plane

The first surface starts with real model traffic.

Backend objects:

- provider connection;
- API key scope;
- request capture policy;
- response capture policy;
- redaction policy;
- sampling policy;
- tenant boundary;
- replay eligibility;
- cost attribution;
- eval candidate flag.

Design principle:

The system should ingest enough behavioral evidence to improve the product while
never requiring a customer to expose secrets or regulated data by default.

Metrics:

- capture activation time;
- safe capture rate;
- replayable traffic percent;
- redaction false negative rate;
- cost attribution completeness.

### W4703 Route Policy Plane

Routing is not just provider selection. It is the production decision layer.

Route policy inputs:

- task class;
- confidence;
- cost budget;
- latency budget;
- risk tier;
- jurisdiction;
- customer entitlement;
- artifact availability;
- runtime availability;
- eval freshness;
- teacher fallback policy.

Route policy outputs:

- provider call;
- local artifact call;
- hybrid call;
- refusal;
- escalation;
- capture-for-training;
- label-request;
- experiment assignment.

This makes routing the bridge between traffic ownership and distillation.

### W4704 Artifact Candidate Discovery

The system should continuously detect when traffic is ready to become an
artifact.

Candidate signals:

- repeated task cluster;
- stable input schema;
- stable output schema;
- high provider cost;
- low entropy target behavior;
- strong eval coverage;
- human review availability;
- regulated repeatability need;
- customer deployment target known.

Output:

`ArtifactCandidate` with expected savings, expected quality risk, required
labels, required evals, recommended student family, and recommended runtime.

This turns passive gateway logs into a distillation pipeline.

### W4705 Distillation Job Plane

Distillation should be a backend job family with first-class state.

Job state:

- candidate source;
- teacher set;
- student search space;
- dataset version;
- eval suite;
- budget;
- safety constraints;
- privacy constraints;
- intermediate checkpoints;
- score trajectory;
- failure modes;
- promotion decision.

No distillation result should become a product artifact until the job plane can
explain what changed, why it is acceptable, and where it may fail.

### W4706 Compile Job Plane

Compilation is the conversion from model behavior to deployable artifact.

Compile state:

- model checkpoint;
- target runtime;
- quantization plan;
- kernel plan;
- memory plan;
- latency target;
- power target;
- policy bundle;
- verifier bundle;
- loader version;
- artifact digest.

Compilation should produce a runtime profile even when the model itself is not
yet promoted. Failed compile jobs become signals for architecture search and
runtime roadmap.

### W4707 Runtime Target Plane

The third surface needs a target graph.

Target classes:

- managed cloud;
- customer cloud;
- Kubernetes;
- serverless;
- browser;
- iOS;
- Android;
- Windows desktop;
- macOS desktop;
- Linux edge;
- GPU server;
- CPU-only server;
- air-gapped appliance.

Each target declares:

- supported artifact types;
- memory limits;
- accelerator;
- offline status;
- telemetry constraints;
- update channel;
- security boundary;
- rollback mechanism.

### W4708 Proof Packet Spine

Every surface must emit proof packets.

Packet types:

- capture proof;
- route decision proof;
- dataset proof;
- distillation proof;
- eval proof;
- compilation proof;
- signature proof;
- deployment proof;
- runtime proof;
- incident proof;
- rollback proof.

The proof packet spine is what makes the product enterprise-ready instead of a
collection of ML tools.

### W4709 Unified Readiness State Machine

Backend readiness should be consistent across account, API, CLI, docs, and
deployment checks.

Readiness states:

- `not_started`;
- `configured`;
- `capturing`;
- `candidate_ready`;
- `distilling`;
- `compiled`;
- `verified`;
- `deployed`;
- `monitored`;
- `optimizing`;
- `blocked_external`;
- `blocked_customer`;
- `blocked_policy`;
- `blocked_benchmark`.

Each state needs:

- owner;
- next action;
- proof required;
- blocking dependency;
- customer-visible copy;
- internal diagnostic.

### W4710 Account Experience Backend Contract

The post-auth account page should be powered by the product kernel, not custom
copy.

Account modules:

- traffic ownership status;
- provider and key status;
- capture quality;
- candidate artifacts;
- distillation jobs;
- compile jobs;
- runtime targets;
- storage readiness;
- compliance proof;
- cost and savings;
- next best action.

Backend rule:

Every account card should be generated from a contract with data, state, action,
empty-state, error-state, and proof links.

### W4711 CLI And TUI Parity Contract

Every account next action should have a CLI equivalent and, where useful, a TUI
view.

Parity fields:

- account action label;
- API route;
- CLI command;
- TUI view;
- docs route;
- expected output;
- error mapping;
- proof artifact.

This prevents the product from becoming a website-only experience while the
buyer expects operator-grade tooling.

### W4712 Product API Envelope

All product-surface APIs should use a consistent envelope.

Envelope fields:

- ok;
- request id;
- surface;
- journey;
- readiness;
- data;
- evidence paths;
- warnings;
- missing prerequisites;
- next actions;
- docs links.

This gives frontend, CLI, SDKs, and support tooling the same backend truth.

### W4713 Customer AI Estate Map

Kolm should maintain a live map of each customer's AI estate.

Nodes:

- provider accounts;
- model families;
- routes;
- prompts;
- tools;
- datasets;
- evals;
- artifacts;
- deployments;
- devices;
- teams;
- policies;
- incidents.

Edges:

- derived from;
- deployed to;
- evaluated by;
- governed by;
- owned by;
- depends on;
- replaces;
- falls back to.

This map becomes the basis for audits, optimization, migration, and expansion.

### W4714 Cost-To-Artifact Ledger

The backend should connect spend to artifact opportunities.

Ledger fields:

- source call cost;
- route savings;
- candidate savings;
- distillation cost;
- label cost;
- compile cost;
- runtime cost;
- deployment cost;
- break-even date;
- realized savings;
- quality risk.

This makes the product commercial outcome legible without reducing it to "save
40 percent."

### W4715 Quality-To-Artifact Ledger

Savings are not enough. Quality must be tracked through the pipeline.

Ledger fields:

- teacher quality;
- captured behavior quality;
- dataset quality;
- label quality;
- eval quality;
- student quality;
- runtime quality;
- production quality;
- human preference quality;
- compliance quality.

Each artifact should have a quality lineage that shows where risk entered and
where it was removed.

### W4716 Policy-To-Artifact Ledger

Policy should compile alongside behavior.

Policy sources:

- customer policy;
- legal policy;
- data policy;
- safety policy;
- geography policy;
- model license policy;
- retention policy;
- human approval policy.

Every artifact must say which policy version it carries and how runtime
enforcement is verified.

### W4717 Deployment Outcome Loop

Deployment is not the end of the product.

Runtime telemetry should feed:

- route policy updates;
- candidate discovery;
- distillation refresh;
- eval expansion;
- runtime profile tuning;
- incident response;
- customer ROI reporting;
- product roadmap.

The backend should never allow a deployed artifact to become invisible.

### W4718 Customer Migration Compiler

Many customers arrive with existing stacks.

Migration sources:

- raw OpenAI SDK calls;
- Anthropic calls;
- Gemini calls;
- LangChain;
- LlamaIndex;
- Vercel AI SDK;
- internal gateways;
- Ollama;
- LM Studio;
- fine-tuned hosted models;
- ad hoc scripts.

Compiler output:

- compatibility report;
- capture wrapper;
- route policy;
- candidate artifacts;
- risk list;
- staged rollout plan;
- rollback plan.

This is how the product turns chaos into a concrete first week.

### W4719 Product Surface Test Matrix

Every backend feature should be tested against the surface it claims to improve.

Matrix columns:

- surface;
- journey;
- API route;
- account module;
- CLI command;
- TUI view;
- docs page;
- proof path;
- fixture;
- smoke probe;
- failure mode.

No new feature should ship as "done" if it exists only in code and not in the
surface matrix.

### W4720 Backend-Owned Copy Contract

The backend should expose product copy primitives so frontend language stays
honest.

Copy primitives:

- surface name;
- outcome headline;
- readiness caveat;
- next action;
- proof label;
- metric label;
- unsupported reason;
- external blocker label.

This does not mean backend writes marketing. It means backend prevents marketing
from claiming unavailable states.

### W4721 First-Run Product Orchestrator

The first-run journey should be backend-directed.

Inputs:

- customer role;
- provider used;
- spend range;
- deployment target;
- compliance need;
- data sensitivity;
- existing code path;
- desired outcome.

Output:

- setup path;
- smallest proof;
- first capture;
- first candidate artifact;
- first ROI estimate;
- first deployment target;
- next action queue.

The product should not ask new users to understand Kolm's architecture before
receiving a concrete path.

### W4722 Enterprise Admin Product Kernel

Enterprise admins need a control plane.

Admin objects:

- teams;
- roles;
- entitlements;
- policies;
- namespaces;
- artifact approvals;
- data residency;
- provider credentials;
- deployment targets;
- audit exports;
- procurement evidence.

The admin surface should use the same product kernel, not a separate billing or
settings island.

### W4723 Multi-Tenant Isolation Model

The backend architecture must make tenancy explicit.

Isolation dimensions:

- organization;
- workspace;
- namespace;
- project;
- user;
- service account;
- artifact;
- dataset;
- eval suite;
- runtime target;
- storage bucket;
- audit log.

Each backend route should be able to state its tenant boundary and whether it
reads, writes, exports, or executes across that boundary.

### W4724 Backend Product Failure Mode Library

Track backend-specific product failures.

Failures:

- route works but account card has no state;
- account next action has no CLI equivalent;
- artifact exists but no proof packet;
- distillation job succeeds but no compile path;
- compile succeeds but no target compatibility;
- deployment succeeds but no runtime telemetry;
- copy claims shipped but readiness says blocked;
- API response lacks next action;
- docs route references stale state.

Every failure should create a product-surface regression test.

### W4725 Cross-Surface Analytics

Analytics should measure movement across the full journey.

Funnels:

- traffic captured to candidate artifact;
- candidate artifact to distillation job;
- distillation job to compiled artifact;
- compiled artifact to runtime deployment;
- deployment to monitored savings;
- monitored savings to team expansion;
- team expansion to enterprise contract.

This reveals whether the product is truly one platform or three disconnected
tools.

### W4726 Product Kernel Readiness API

Expose a backend endpoint that summarizes the product kernel.

Response sections:

- surfaces;
- journeys;
- customer estate;
- readiness;
- blockers;
- next actions;
- proofs;
- metrics;
- route groups;
- account modules;
- CLI/TUI parity;
- docs parity.

This gives the frontend, CLI, sales engineering, support, and implementation
agents one object to work from.

### W4727 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmProductKernelIR`.
2. Map existing routes and account modules to the three-surface contract.
3. Generate customer AI estate map from existing provider, capture, artifact,
   deployment, and billing data.
4. Add unified readiness state machine.
5. Add product API envelope fields where missing.
6. Add account module contracts.
7. Add CLI/TUI parity declarations.
8. Add cost, quality, and policy ledgers.
9. Add first-run orchestrator.
10. Add product surface test matrix.
11. Add backend-owned copy primitives.
12. Expose product kernel readiness API.

First implementation should be read-only. Once the product kernel is trusted,
write actions can attach to the same next-action model.

### W4728 Ultimate Product Kernel Objective

Ultimate objective: make Kolm feel like one inevitable product instead of a
bundle of powerful parts.

When the product kernel works:

- the homepage can explain all three surfaces without jargon;
- the account page shows exactly where the customer is;
- the CLI and TUI prove the same state;
- backend routes have clear ownership;
- artifacts have lineage from traffic to runtime;
- every feature has a product surface;
- every surface has proof;
- every proof has a next action;
- the company can build faster without becoming incoherent.

This is the structural answer to the product clarity problem. The website can
only be world-class if the backend truth underneath it is equally coherent.

## Synthetic Smoke-Test, Metamorphic Proof, And Simulation Harness Layer

The research backlog is too large to trust intuition. Each invention needs a
cheap pre-implementation proof path that catches impossible ideas before an
implementation agent spends days on code. This layer defines the simulated
environment Kolm should use to test inventions across routing, capture,
distillation, quantization, compilation, deployment, evaluation, governance,
pricing, and ecosystem distribution.

The key idea is to treat every invention as a falsifiable control system. It
must declare what metric it should move, what adversarial condition would break
it, what simulated fixture can approximate that condition, and what production
evidence is required before the claim graduates.

### North Star

No invention enters an implementation wave until it has a synthetic smoke test,
a metamorphic relation, a failure-mode fixture, a metric hypothesis, and a
promotion threshold.

### W4800 KolmSimHarnessIR Schema

Define `KolmSimHarnessIR`, the common contract for invention proof.

Fields:

- invention id;
- product surface;
- metric hypothesis;
- expected lift;
- guardrail metrics;
- synthetic fixture;
- real-world assumption;
- metamorphic relation;
- adversarial transformation;
- simulated control;
- simulated treatment;
- promotion threshold;
- failure threshold;
- production evidence required.

This IR becomes the pre-code bridge between research and implementation.

### W4801 Product Digital Twin Fixtures

Build synthetic fixtures that represent customer AI estates.

Fixture types:

- startup API gateway with high GPT spend;
- enterprise support center;
- payer claims workflow;
- legal contract extraction;
- mobile offline assistant;
- browser-only AI tool;
- air-gapped regulated deployment;
- multi-provider enterprise router;
- vertical foundation student deployment.

Each fixture includes:

- traces;
- costs;
- latency profiles;
- data sensitivity;
- route policies;
- eval suites;
- deployment targets;
- failure history.

### W4802 Routing Simulation Harness

Simulate route policies before deploying them.

Inputs:

- traffic mix;
- provider prices;
- latency distributions;
- failure rates;
- quality distributions;
- risk tiers;
- local artifact availability.

Outputs:

- expected savings;
- expected quality loss;
- fallback rate;
- escalation rate;
- customer-visible incident risk;
- confidence calibration drift.

Promotion rule:

Route policy cannot ship if savings come from sending high-risk traffic to an
unsupported provider or artifact.

### W4803 Capture Quality Simulator

Capture systems fail silently when the wrong traffic is captured or sensitive
fields leak.

Simulate:

- sampling bias;
- redaction leakage;
- provider-specific schema drift;
- duplicate calls;
- prompt template churn;
- teacher version changes;
- tenant boundary mistakes;
- low-value traffic capture.

Metrics:

- useful capture rate;
- privacy risk;
- replayability;
- downstream distillation value;
- label cost avoided.

### W4804 Distillation Search Simulator

Before running expensive jobs, simulate whether the search space is plausible.

Inputs:

- teacher ensemble;
- dataset size;
- task entropy;
- student family;
- label budget;
- eval strength;
- runtime target.

Outputs:

- expected K-Score;
- expected failure classes;
- expected compute cost;
- architecture shortlist;
- early-stop threshold.

The simulator does not replace training. It prevents obviously bad training
runs.

### W4805 Quantization Metamorphic Test Harness

Quantization must preserve behavior under task-relevant transformations.

Metamorphic relations:

- formatting changes should not change structured output;
- irrelevant context should not flip classification;
- equivalent units should preserve numeric answer;
- redacted identifiers should preserve policy decision;
- language-neutral fields should preserve extraction.

Failure signal:

If quantization changes behavior under a relation that the teacher and full
student preserve, the quantization plan is unsafe for that task.

### W4806 Compiler Target Simulator

Compile decisions should be tested against target constraints before runtime
work begins.

Simulated target variables:

- RAM;
- VRAM;
- CPU cores;
- accelerator type;
- battery envelope;
- network availability;
- storage limit;
- telemetry restriction;
- update channel.

Output:

- viable target list;
- required compression;
- likely bottleneck;
- fallback plan;
- unsupported reason.

### W4807 Runtime Load Simulator

Runtime inventions must survive realistic traffic.

Simulate:

- burst traffic;
- cold starts;
- long context;
- mixed task sizes;
- provider outages;
- local artifact cache misses;
- GPU memory fragmentation;
- mobile thermal throttling.

Guardrails:

- p95 and p99 latency;
- error rate;
- fallback correctness;
- memory ceiling;
- quality under degradation.

### W4808 K-Score Calibration Simulator

K-Score changes must be linked to human preference.

Simulate:

- noisy human raters;
- judge model drift;
- benchmark overfitting;
- slice imbalance;
- task mix shift;
- correlated metrics;
- adversarial score gaming.

Output:

- calibration confidence;
- required human labels;
- score uncertainty;
- fragile slices;
- next benchmark additions.

### W4809 Policy And Compliance Simulator

Governance features must fail closed.

Simulate:

- wrong region;
- missing BAA;
- forbidden model license;
- retention mismatch;
- minor data policy conflict;
- human approval required;
- audit export missing;
- unverified artifact.

Expected result:

The system refuses, escalates, or selects a compliant alternative with a clear
proof packet.

### W4810 Ecosystem Install Simulator

Marketplace and SDK inventions need install proofs.

Simulate:

- clean developer machine;
- locked-down enterprise machine;
- private registry mirror;
- air-gapped bundle;
- stale loader;
- missing signature;
- incompatible runtime;
- revoked artifact.

Metrics:

- time to first verified run;
- failed install rate;
- error clarity;
- security refusal correctness.

### W4811 Pricing And ROI Simulator

Pricing inventions should be tested before reaching customers.

Simulate:

- low spend startup;
- high spend enterprise;
- seasonal traffic;
- regulated buyer;
- marketplace buyer;
- annual commit buyer;
- savings-based plan buyer.

Outputs:

- gross margin;
- customer ROI;
- payback period;
- price fairness;
- expansion likelihood;
- support burden.

### W4812 Documentation And Onboarding Simulator

Docs can be simulated with task agents and fixture users.

Tasks:

- install CLI;
- capture first provider call;
- run first artifact;
- interpret readiness blocker;
- deploy to a target;
- verify proof packet;
- fix common error.

Metrics:

- steps to success;
- ambiguity count;
- dead-end count;
- command mismatch;
- concept load;
- support ticket probability.

### W4813 Account UX State Simulator

Post-auth UX must be tested as backend state.

Simulate:

- no provider connected;
- provider connected but no capture;
- capture with no candidates;
- candidate ready;
- distillation running;
- compile failed;
- deployment ready;
- storage missing;
- compliance blocked;
- enterprise approval pending.

The account page should always have one clear state and one useful next action.

### W4814 Failure Injection Catalog

Each invention should be tested with failures.

Failure classes:

- missing credential;
- expired key;
- provider outage;
- malformed trace;
- bad eval fixture;
- poisoned capture;
- low confidence;
- high latency;
- storage write failure;
- signature mismatch;
- stale product graph;
- tenant mismatch.

This catalog becomes a shared fixture library for implementation agents.

### W4815 Metamorphic Relation Library

Build a relation library by product surface.

Traffic control:

- equivalent request should route consistently;
- higher risk should not lower policy guard;
- cheaper provider cannot be selected if quality guard fails.

Artifact compilation:

- schema-preserving input change should preserve schema;
- irrelevant context should not change extraction;
- safe quantization should preserve calibrated decisions.

Anywhere runtime:

- same artifact digest should produce same governed behavior;
- unsupported target should fail with exact reason;
- rollback should restore prior behavior.

### W4816 Synthetic Data Governance

Synthetic fixtures can mislead.

Rules:

- synthetic data must state which real property it approximates;
- fixtures must include known blind spots;
- synthetic pass cannot become production claim;
- real benchmark is required for marketing;
- regulated fixtures need legal review if derived from real schemas.

This keeps the simulation harness honest.

### W4817 Experiment Interference Simulator

Kolm will run many experiments. Some will interfere.

Simulate:

- route policy experiment overlaps pricing experiment;
- distillation experiment changes runtime quality;
- docs experiment changes activation quality;
- benchmark experiment changes product claims;
- provider outage biases savings experiment.

Output:

- conflict graph;
- isolation recommendation;
- randomization unit;
- guardrail metrics;
- invalidated experiments.

### W4818 Causal Promotion Gate

Promotion needs more than metric movement.

Required proof:

- baseline;
- treatment;
- confounder list;
- randomization or causal adjustment;
- guardrail pass;
- slice analysis;
- failure-mode pass;
- rollback plan.

If causal evidence is weak, the invention can proceed only as an internal beta.

### W4819 Implementation Agent Packet Generator

The simulator should generate handoff packets.

Packet fields:

- invention objective;
- files likely involved;
- fixture paths;
- expected API shape;
- expected failure tests;
- metric to improve;
- guardrail tests;
- docs updates;
- non-goals;
- verification commands.

This reduces wasted implementation loops.

### W4820 Regression Memory

Every failed simulation should become memory.

Store:

- failed assumption;
- fixture;
- observed failure;
- root cause;
- corrected invariant;
- future test.

The invention lab gets smarter when it remembers why ideas failed.

### W4821 Frontier Claim Sandbox

Before publishing a claim, simulate the strongest counterexample.

Claim types:

- saves cost;
- improves quality;
- runs anywhere;
- audit-ready;
- standard;
- benchmark-leading;
- safer;
- faster.

Each claim needs:

- evidence object;
- boundary;
- counterexample fixture;
- allowed wording;
- forbidden wording.

### W4822 Simulated Customer Council

Synthetic customers should represent buyer constraints.

Personas:

- CTO;
- ML platform lead;
- security reviewer;
- compliance officer;
- finance buyer;
- mobile engineer;
- support operations leader;
- legal operations leader.

Each persona evaluates:

- whether the proof answers their concern;
- what blocker remains;
- what metric matters;
- what integration is missing.

### W4823 Benchmark Mutation Engine

Benchmarks should mutate to prevent overfitting.

Mutations:

- paraphrase;
- unit conversion;
- schema order;
- missing optional field;
- adversarial distractor;
- rare class injection;
- language variant;
- noisy OCR;
- long-context insertion.

Promotion rule:

An artifact that wins only on static fixtures is not robust enough.

### W4824 Simulated Production Replay

Replay is the bridge between synthetic and production.

Replay modes:

- offline captured traffic;
- redacted traffic;
- synthetic adjacent traffic;
- teacher replay;
- student replay;
- route policy replay;
- cost replay;
- incident replay.

Outputs:

- behavior delta;
- cost delta;
- latency delta;
- policy delta;
- failure delta.

### W4825 Proof Coverage Dashboard

Track proof coverage for each invention.

Coverage categories:

- synthetic fixture;
- metamorphic relation;
- adversarial fixture;
- causal estimate;
- runtime load;
- security failure;
- policy failure;
- install failure;
- human review;
- production pilot.

An invention with low proof coverage should not receive broad engineering
investment.

### W4826 Product Graph Simulation Readiness

Expose simulation readiness in the product graph.

Fields:

- `sim_fixture_ready`;
- `metamorphic_tests_ready`;
- `failure_injection_ready`;
- `causal_gate_ready`;
- `production_replay_ready`;
- `claim_boundary_ready`;
- `implementation_packet_ready`.

This makes research quality visible to implementation agents.

### W4827 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmSimHarnessIR`.
2. Create the product digital twin fixture format.
3. Add route simulation.
4. Add capture quality simulation.
5. Add distillation search simulation.
6. Add quantization metamorphic tests.
7. Add runtime load simulation.
8. Add K-Score calibration simulation.
9. Add policy/compliance simulation.
10. Add ecosystem install simulation.
11. Add implementation agent packet generator.
12. Add proof coverage dashboard.

First implementation should run on static fixtures and produce JSON reports. It
does not need live customer data to become useful.

### W4828 Ultimate Simulation Objective

Ultimate objective: make every Kolm invention cheaper to prove, faster to kill,
and safer to ship.

When this works:

- research ideas become testable objects;
- implementation agents receive sharper tickets;
- bad ideas die early;
- good ideas get metric evidence before code;
- frontend claims inherit honest boundaries;
- backend systems ship with failure fixtures;
- production pilots start from known risk;
- Kolm compounds faster than teams relying on intuition.

This is the lab bench for the billion-dollar product. The company should not
only invent better AI infrastructure; it should invent a better way to know
which AI infrastructure inventions deserve to exist.

## Distillation Research Program, Publication Moat, And Technical Leadership Layer

Kolm should not rely only on shipping product features. If the ambition is to
own the category, Kolm needs a research program that makes the market believe
Kolm defines what good distillation, compilation, and artifact quality mean. The
research should publish enough to create trust and attract talent, while keeping
the proprietary data flywheel, calibration infrastructure, runtime telemetry,
and optimization execution private.

This layer turns the roadmap idea of "hire 2-3 ML researchers" into an operating
model: research questions, data rights, experimental infrastructure, publication
strategy, open-source artifacts, patent/trade-secret boundaries, and product
integration loops.

### North Star

Kolm becomes the technical authority for production AI distillation: the company
that publishes the metric, builds the calibration dataset, proves the runtime
optimizations, and ships the artifacts customers actually deploy.

### W4900 Distillation Research Charter

Define a charter for the research group.

Mandate:

- improve task-specific student quality;
- reduce cost per successful task;
- make K-Score scientifically credible;
- prove any-device deployment tradeoffs;
- create public benchmarks without leaking customer data;
- translate findings into product waves.

Boundaries:

- publish methods and evaluation protocols;
- keep customer traces private;
- keep proprietary calibration data controlled;
- keep runtime telemetry advantage private;
- keep architecture search telemetry private unless deliberately released.

### W4901 Research Team Shape

Start with a focused team, not a broad lab.

Roles:

- distillation lead: teacher/student transfer, architecture search, curriculum;
- evaluation lead: K-Score, human preference calibration, benchmark validity;
- systems lead: quantization, runtime kernels, compiler, profiling;
- research engineer: reproducible pipelines, datasets, reports, release
  artifacts.

Avoid hiring only paper authors. The team must be able to ship product-grade
experiments and production artifacts.

### W4902 Publication Portfolio

Target a portfolio of papers that map to product surfaces.

Paper tracks:

- task-adaptive student architecture selection;
- confidence-calibrated hybrid local-cloud routing;
- cross-domain transfer via gradient and behavior aggregation;
- distillation-aware quantization for task-specific students;
- K-Score as a production artifact quality metric;
- proof-carrying AI artifacts for regulated deployment.

Each paper should produce:

- public method;
- public fixtures when safe;
- reproducibility packet;
- product wave mapping;
- claim boundaries;
- implementation follow-up.

### W4903 Task-Adaptive Student Architecture Selection Program

Research question:

Which student architecture is optimal for a task under quality, latency, memory,
privacy, and cost constraints?

Core method:

- classify task entropy;
- estimate schema complexity;
- measure teacher disagreement;
- estimate required reasoning depth;
- search student families;
- predict runtime feasibility;
- select architecture before full training.

Product output:

`StudentArchitectureRecommendation` with expected K-Score, cost, latency,
memory, and risk.

### W4904 Confidence-Calibrated Routing Research Program

Research question:

When should a request use local artifact, cloud teacher, hybrid ensemble, or
human escalation?

Core method:

- calibrate confidence by task slice;
- estimate expected utility;
- include abstention;
- model fallback cost;
- include policy risk;
- update from production outcomes.

Product output:

`ConfidenceRoutePolicy` that saves cost without silently increasing critical
failure rate.

### W4905 Cross-Domain Transfer Research Program

Research question:

Which patterns learned in one customer, domain, or task can safely improve
another without leaking data or overfitting?

Core method:

- represent behavior patterns without raw data;
- aggregate gradients or behavioral summaries;
- measure transfer risk;
- separate universal structure from domain-specific labels;
- require privacy and license checks before reuse.

Product output:

`TransferCandidate` with source domains, target domain, expected lift, privacy
boundary, and eval requirements.

### W4906 K-Score Mathematical Specification

K-Score needs a public mathematical spec.

Spec components:

- task success score;
- schema fidelity score;
- safety score;
- calibration score;
- latency score;
- cost score;
- robustness score;
- reproducibility score;
- auditability score;
- deployment fit score.

Each component needs:

- formula;
- valid input types;
- confidence interval;
- aggregation rule;
- failure override rule;
- known limitations.

Important rule:

K-Score cannot hide catastrophic failures behind averages.

### W4907 Open K-Score Calculator

Release an open calculator that competitors and customers can run.

Capabilities:

- read eval reports;
- compute component scores;
- compute uncertainty;
- render slice breakdown;
- compare artifacts;
- reject incomplete evidence;
- output JSON and human report.

Strategic reason:

Open the calculator, not the proprietary calibration data. That creates
credibility while preserving the data moat.

### W4908 K-Score Calibration Dataset Program

Maintain a calibration dataset that maps K-Score to human preference and
production outcomes.

Dataset tiers:

- public synthetic fixtures;
- public human preference fixtures;
- private customer-opt-in anonymized fixtures;
- regulated domain expert fixtures;
- runtime outcome fixtures.

Quarterly refresh:

- add new task slices;
- retire overfit fixtures;
- publish changes;
- track score drift;
- report correlation and failure cases.

### W4909 Human Preference Correlation Study

K-Score must prove correlation with humans.

Study design:

- pairwise artifact comparisons;
- task-specific human raters;
- expert raters for regulated domains;
- blind evaluation;
- bootstrap confidence intervals;
- slice-level analysis;
- comparison to BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, exact match, LLM-as-judge, and raw cost
  metrics.

Claim boundary:

Publish where K-Score works, where it fails, and what it is not intended to
measure.

### W4910 Distillation-Aware Quantization Research

Distilled models may have different activation distributions, confidence
profiles, and redundancy patterns than foundation teachers.

Research targets:

- activation-aware precision assignment;
- task-sensitive outlier detection;
- confidence-preserving quantization;
- slice-specific calibration;
- layer redundancy detection;
- runtime deoptimization triggers.

Product output:

`DistillationAwareQuantizationPlan` with precision map, expected quality loss,
runtime gain, and rollback threshold.

### W4911 Task-Specific Attention Optimization

Task-specific students often learn narrower attention patterns.

Research targets:

- attention sparsity detection;
- prompt template locality;
- retrieval-conditioned attention;
- structured-output attention constraints;
- KV retention policy;
- sequence truncation safety.

Product output:

`TaskAttentionProfile` used by compilers and runtimes to reduce memory and
latency without breaking task behavior.

### W4912 Dynamic Layer Skipping Research

Some student layers may be redundant for narrow tasks.

Research method:

- measure layer contribution by slice;
- test early exit thresholds;
- calibrate confidence after skipped layers;
- detect hard examples;
- fall back to full depth;
- track production drift.

Product output:

`DynamicDepthPolicy` with safe exit conditions, expected speedup, and quality
guardrails.

### W4913 Research Data Governance

The research program must not contaminate customer trust.

Rules:

- customer data opt-in is explicit;
- regulated data is isolated;
- public datasets are license-checked;
- synthetic data lineage is recorded;
- benchmark data has source rights;
- model outputs are treated as governed data;
- deletion requests propagate to research stores.

Every research run should include data-rights metadata.

### W4914 Proprietary Data Moat Boundary

Define what stays private.

Private assets:

- production trace distributions;
- architecture search outcomes;
- failure-mode frequency;
- human label economics;
- runtime device telemetry;
- calibration residuals;
- customer-specific task clusters;
- pricing and ROI response curves.

Public assets:

- metric formula;
- selected fixtures;
- calculator;
- paper methods;
- conformance tests;
- artifact format.

This balance builds credibility without giving away the compounding engine.

### W4915 Research-To-Product Promotion Gate

No paper should remain a PDF.

Promotion requirements:

- method maps to product object;
- product object has API owner;
