- simulation fixture passes;
- eval suite exists;
- implementation wave exists;
- claim boundary exists;
- docs path exists;
- customer pilot path exists.

This keeps research from becoming disconnected prestige work.

### W4916 Reproducibility Packet Standard

Every publication should ship a reproducibility packet.

Packet contents:

- method summary;
- public fixtures;
- environment;
- seeds;
- model identifiers;
- dataset cards;
- eval scripts;
- raw reports;
- negative results;
- known limitations.

The packet should be complete enough for trust, but scrubbed of proprietary or
customer-confidential data.

### W4917 Negative Results Library

Negative results are moat material.

Track:

- failed architecture searches;
- failed quantization plans;
- failed routing policies;
- benchmark overfitting;
- evaluator drift;
- transfer failures;
- runtime regressions.

Use:

- avoid repeated work;
- train experiment governor;
- improve simulator;
- sharpen papers;
- improve implementation packets.

### W4918 Patent And Trade Secret Strategy

Not every invention should be published.

Publish:

- metric definitions;
- high-level methods;
- reproducibility fixtures;
- conformance standards.

Patent or keep secret:

- architecture search heuristics from proprietary telemetry;
- runtime autotuning policies;
- calibration residual models;
- cross-customer transfer filters;
- commercial optimization policies;
- device-specific compiler heuristics.

Decision rule:

If disclosure helps standard adoption more than it helps competitors copy the
execution engine, publish. Otherwise protect.

### W4919 Research Benchmark Governance

Benchmarks must not become vanity leaderboards.

Governance:

- public task definitions;
- hidden holdout where appropriate;
- freshness policy;
- anti-overfitting mutations;
- third-party submission rules;
- conflict-of-interest disclosure;
- raw report retention;
- benchmark retirement process.

K-Score credibility depends on benchmark governance as much as formulas.

### W4920 Research Infrastructure Cost Control

The research group should model compute spend like product spend.

Controls:

- budget per hypothesis;
- early stopping;
- spot/preemptible policy;
- artifact reuse;
- dataset reuse;
- experiment deduplication;
- carbon and energy reporting;
- expected value per experiment.

This prevents "research forever" from becoming an uncontrolled GPU sink.

### W4921 Research Talent Magnet

The research program should attract people who want both papers and deployed
impact.

Talent signals:

- public benchmark;
- open calculator;
- high-quality technical blog;
- reproducibility packets;
- conference workshops;
- advisory board;
- clear product impact;
- access to unique production telemetry under strict governance.

The message:

Come to Kolm if you want your distillation research to run in production, not
only on benchmark tables.

### W4922 Academic And Industry Collaboration Model

Collaborations should be structured.

Collaboration types:

- public benchmark partnership;
- domain expert evaluation;
- runtime partner study;
- university sponsored project;
- standards working group;
- customer opt-in case study.

Each collaboration needs:

- data agreement;
- publication agreement;
- IP boundary;
- confidentiality boundary;
- artifact ownership;
- timeline;
- success metric.

### W4923 Technical Blog And Demo Cadence

Research needs a publishing rhythm.

Cadence:

- monthly technical note;
- quarterly benchmark update;
- semiannual major research report;
- conference paper submissions;
- reproducible demos;
- failure postmortems when useful.

Each post should answer:

- what problem exists in production;
- what Kolm invented;
- what evidence supports it;
- how it maps to product;
- what remains unsolved.

### W4924 Competitive Measurement Yardstick

If K-Score becomes the yardstick, competitors are measured on Kolm's terms.

Yardstick design:

- compare model gateways;
- compare fine-tuning alternatives;
- compare MLOps platforms;
- compare local runtime stacks;
- compare proprietary copilots;
- compare agent frameworks.

Metrics:

- cost per successful task;
- reproducibility;
- deployment fit;
- auditability;
- human preference;
- policy compliance;
- runtime portability.

Do not compare only raw model quality. Kolm wins by measuring production fit.

### W4925 Research Claim Review Board

Before publication or marketing, claims need review.

Reviewers:

- research;
- engineering;
- product;
- legal;
- security;
- customer-facing lead.

Required checks:

- evidence exists;
- claim boundary is explicit;
- benchmark is fair;
- confidential data is absent;
- competitor claims are sourced;
- product availability is accurate;
- future work is not phrased as shipped.

### W4926 Product Graph Research Readiness

Expose research readiness as a product graph dimension.

Fields:

- `paper_ready`;
- `calculator_ready`;
- `benchmark_ready`;
- `calibration_ready`;
- `runtime_study_ready`;
- `publication_claim_reviewed`;
- `product_wave_linked`;
- `data_rights_verified`;
- `reproducibility_packet_ready`.

This keeps technical leadership tied to actual product truth.

### W4927 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define research charter.
2. Define K-Score mathematical spec.
3. Build open K-Score calculator.
4. Create calibration dataset governance.
5. Create human preference study design.
6. Define distillation-aware quantization study.
7. Define task-adaptive architecture search study.
8. Define confidence-calibrated routing study.
9. Define reproducibility packet standard.
10. Define publication claim review.
11. Link research outputs to product graph and roadmap waves.
12. Recruit the first focused research pod.

First proof:

Publish one rigorous technical note with an open fixture, a K-Score calculator
prototype, and one product artifact case study.

### W4928 Ultimate Research Leadership Objective

Ultimate objective: make Kolm the company that defines and proves production
distillation quality.

When this works:

- K-Score becomes a credible yardstick;
- distillation papers create authority;
- runtime studies create systems credibility;
- open tools create trust;
- proprietary telemetry creates defensibility;
- implementation waves inherit research evidence;
- customers believe Kolm has the deepest technical answer;
- competitors are forced to argue inside Kolm's measurement frame.

This is how research becomes revenue, trust, talent, and category power without
turning the company into an academic lab disconnected from shipped product.

## Information-Theoretic Artifact Sufficiency And Minimum Student Layer

Kolm's deepest scientific opportunity is to stop treating distillation as "make
a smaller model imitate a bigger model" and instead treat it as finding the
minimum sufficient artifact for a production behavior. Information bottleneck,
rate-distortion theory, PAC-Bayes compression, minimum description length, and
sample compression all point to the same product question: what is the smallest
object that preserves the behavior the customer actually needs, with measurable
risk?

This layer turns that question into product architecture. It should tell Kolm
when to distill, what to distill, how small the student can be, what information
must be preserved, what information must be discarded, and which failure modes
prove the artifact is under-specified.

### North Star

Every `.kolm` artifact is the smallest deployable behavior object that meets a
task's quality, safety, policy, latency, memory, cost, and audit constraints.

### W5000 KolmSufficiencyIR Schema

Define `KolmSufficiencyIR`, the contract for minimum sufficient artifacts.

Fields:

- task definition;
- input distribution;
- output distribution;
- relevant variables;
- nuisance variables;
- invariances;
- forbidden shortcuts;
- teacher behavior;
- student behavior;
- distortion metric;
- retained information estimate;
- discarded information estimate;
- compression ratio;
- risk envelope;
- deployment constraints;
- proof packet.

This IR is the bridge between mathematical compression and production artifact
readiness.

### W5001 Behavior Relevance Graph

A student should preserve task-relevant behavior, not incidental teacher style.

Build a graph with:

- inputs;
- latent task variables;
- policy variables;
- schema variables;
- user intent;
- domain facts;
- nuisance variables;
- output fields;
- failure classes.

Edges show which input properties should causally affect which output
properties. If a variable has no valid path to the output, the student should
learn to ignore it.

Example:

A claims redactor should preserve diagnosis category sensitivity and identifier
type, but ignore physician writing style unless it affects redaction risk.

### W5002 Task-Specific Distortion Metric

Rate-distortion needs a distortion function. Kolm should learn or define one
per task.

Distortion components:

- semantic error;
- schema error;
- safety error;
- policy error;
- numeric error;
- calibration error;
- latency penalty;
- memory penalty;
- audit penalty.

Critical rule:

Some distortions are infinite. A privacy leak, policy bypass, or regulated
decision error cannot be averaged away by cheap latency.

### W5003 Minimum Viable Student Estimator

Before training, estimate the smallest plausible student.

Inputs:

- task entropy;
- output entropy;
- schema complexity;
- teacher disagreement;
- required context length;
- label noise;
- policy complexity;
- runtime target.

Outputs:

- minimum parameter class;
- minimum precision;
- minimum context;
- minimum eval coverage;
- expected irreducible error;
- recommended student family.

This prevents overbuilding students and prevents underpowered students from
wasting training runs.

### W5004 Distillability Classifier

Not every workload should be distilled.

Classify workloads:

- highly distillable repeated behavior;
- moderately distillable with more labels;
- route-only optimization candidate;
- retrieval or tool candidate;
- human approval candidate;
- not distillable under current constraints.

Signals:

- output stability;
- task repetition;
- teacher agreement;
- low long-tail risk;
- low novelty demand;
- stable schema;
- deployment target need.

This is a core commercial control. Selling distillation when routing is the
right answer destroys trust.

### W5005 Nuisance Information Eraser

The student should discard information that increases privacy risk or
overfitting without improving task performance.

Nuisance classes:

- user identity;
- writing style;
- irrelevant demographics;
- provider-specific phrasing;
- date artifacts;
- prompt scaffolding;
- accidental identifiers;
- dataset source fingerprints.

Method:

- adversarial probes detect retained nuisance variables;
- contrastive pairs define invariances;
- student training penalizes nuisance predictability;
- artifact proof reports residual nuisance leakage.

### W5006 Invariance Contract Compiler

Every task needs explicit invariances.

Invariance examples:

- whitespace should not change structured extraction;
- synonymous labels should not change class;
- identifier formatting should not change redaction;
- irrelevant paragraph order should not change decision;
- language variant should preserve meaning where supported;
- device runtime should not change policy outcome.

The compiler should produce metamorphic tests from these invariances and fail
artifact promotion if they break.

### W5007 Shortcut And Spurious Feature Detector

Students may learn shortcuts.

Detect:

- source watermark reliance;
- prompt template reliance;
- demographic proxy reliance;
- provider-style reliance;
- output length reliance;
- lexical artifact reliance;
- timestamp leakage;
- synthetic-data marker reliance.

Proof:

Counterfactual interventions remove or alter the suspected shortcut and measure
whether the artifact still performs.

### W5008 Sufficient Dataset Selector

More data is not always better.

The selector should choose the smallest dataset that spans the behavior.

Selection criteria:

- coverage of task modes;
- coverage of rare failures;
- teacher disagreement diversity;
- policy boundary coverage;
- runtime target relevance;
- label confidence;
- privacy risk;
- rights clearance.

Output:

`SufficientDatasetPlan` with included examples, excluded examples, marginal
value, and known gaps.

### W5009 Label Value Under Sufficiency

Labeling should target uncertainty in the sufficient representation, not random
examples.

High-value labels:

- boundary cases;
- high teacher disagreement;
- rare but costly failures;
- policy edges;
- slices with high calibration error;
- examples that collapse multiple candidate students;
- examples that disprove shortcuts.

This lets Kolm spend label budget where it changes artifact sufficiency.

### W5010 Teacher Information Audit

Teachers contain more information than the task needs.

Audit:

- which teacher behaviors are useful;
- which are style;
- which are unsafe;
- which are unavailable under license;
- which are too expensive to preserve;
- which require runtime fallback.

The audit should produce a preserve/discard/escalate map before student
training.

### W5011 Student Bottleneck Probe

After training, probe what information the student retained.

Probe dimensions:

- task variables;
- nuisance variables;
- policy variables;
- schema variables;
- sensitive variables;
- shortcut variables;
- deployment variables.

Goal:

Retain task and policy variables. Discard nuisance and forbidden variables.
Expose residual leakage in the artifact proof.

### W5012 Compression-Risk Frontier

For each task, generate a compression-risk curve.

Axes:

- artifact size;
- memory;
- latency;
- cost;
- K-Score;
- critical failure rate;
- calibration error;
- policy failure rate.

Decision:

Choose the knee point that delivers meaningful cost and latency gains without
crossing task-specific risk thresholds.

### W5013 Minimum Description Artifact Ledger

Use an MDL-like ledger for artifacts.

Ledger terms:

- model description length;
- data description length;
- policy description length;
- runtime profile length;
- exception list length;
- proof packet length.

Insight:

If the exception list grows faster than the student shrinks, the artifact is not
actually simple. It is hiding complexity in patches.

### W5014 PAC-Bayes-Inspired Evidence Packet

Do not overclaim formal guarantees, but use PAC-Bayes and compression evidence
as proof signals.

Packet fields:

- prior source;
- compression method;
- quantization method;
- posterior artifact;
- empirical risk;
- complexity term;
- bound estimate where valid;
- assumptions;
- invalidating conditions.

This gives sophisticated buyers a stronger scientific story without pretending
every deployment has theorem-level safety.

### W5015 Task Entropy Dashboard

Expose task entropy to product decisions.

Metrics:

- input diversity;
- output diversity;
- teacher disagreement;
- schema branching;
- policy branching;
- long-tail density;
- novelty rate;
- failure-class entropy.

Low entropy tasks are distillation candidates. High entropy tasks may need
routing, retrieval, tools, or human review instead.

### W5016 Artifact Under-Specification Detector

An artifact is under-specified when it passes current evals but lacks enough
behavioral constraints.

Signals:

- high performance on static fixtures;
- poor metamorphic robustness;
- high slice variance;
- high sensitivity to prompt wording;
- unstable confidence;
- hidden shortcut reliance;
- no evals for deployment target.

Output:

`UnderSpecificationReport` with missing invariances, missing slices, and
recommended data collection.

### W5017 Artifact Over-Specification Detector

An artifact is over-specified when it preserves too much.

Signals:

- memorized identifiers;
- style imitation;
- larger than needed;
- slower than target;
- retained sensitive features;
- poor cross-template generalization;
- unnecessary reasoning trace reproduction.

Output:

`OverSpecificationReport` with removal candidates and privacy/cost gains.

### W5018 Sufficient Runtime Profile

The minimum sufficient artifact includes runtime, not just weights.

Runtime sufficiency fields:

- precision needed;
- context length needed;
- KV cache policy;
- early exit policy;
- fallback policy;
- hardware requirement;
- telemetry requirement;
- deoptimization trigger.

This prevents a model from being "sufficient" in a notebook but insufficient on
the target device.

### W5019 Sufficient Policy Profile

Policy is part of sufficiency.

The artifact must preserve:

- refusal rules;
- escalation rules;
- data-handling rules;
- geography rules;
- retention rules;
- human approval rules;
- license constraints.

If the student preserves task output but loses policy behavior, it is not
sufficient.

### W5020 Cross-Task Shared Sufficient Features

Some features are reusable across tasks.

Discover:

- shared schema features;
- shared redaction features;
- shared routing confidence features;
- shared document layout features;
- shared domain terminology;
- shared policy boundaries.

Use:

- initialize new students;
- reduce label demand;
- improve vertical foundation students;
- build private feature libraries without sharing raw customer data.

### W5021 Distillation Stop Rule

Kolm needs a stop rule.

Stop when:

- compression-risk frontier has plateaued;
- additional labels do not reduce uncertainty;
- smaller students fail invariance tests;
- bigger students do not improve critical slices;
- runtime target is satisfied;
- policy sufficiency is proven;
- commercial break-even is reached.

This prevents endless training loops.

### W5022 Do-Not-Distill Rule

Sometimes the best product answer is no distillation.

Do not distill when:

- task novelty is too high;
- teacher disagreement is unresolved;
- policy risk is high;
- eval coverage is weak;
- customer volume is too low;
- retrieval solves the problem better;
- tool execution is required;
- human review is mandatory;
- runtime target cannot support a safe student.

The product should recommend route optimization, retrieval, tools, or workflow
controls instead.

### W5023 Sufficiency-Aware Pricing

Pricing should reflect artifact sufficiency.

Commercial model:

- low-complexity repeated tasks price on savings;
- high-complexity regulated tasks price on proof and assurance;
- cross-task reusable features create expansion value;
- do-not-distill recommendations build trust and reduce churn.

This makes pricing honest and defensible.

### W5024 Sufficiency Failure Mode Library

Track failures:

- student preserves style but loses policy;
- student preserves common cases but fails rare costly case;
- quantization preserves accuracy but breaks confidence;
- artifact compresses weights but grows exception patches;
- dataset appears large but misses boundary modes;
- K-Score average hides under-specified slice.

Each failure becomes a fixture and a research note.

### W5025 Sufficiency Benchmark Suite

Create benchmarks that test minimum sufficiency.

Benchmark families:

- invariance preservation;
- nuisance removal;
- shortcut resistance;
- rare boundary behavior;
- compression-risk frontier;
- policy preservation;
- runtime sufficiency;
- cross-template generalization.

This benchmark is more aligned with Kolm than generic leaderboard tasks.

### W5026 Product Graph Sufficiency Readiness

Expose sufficiency readiness.

Fields:

- `distillability_classified`;
- `distortion_metric_ready`;
- `invariance_contract_ready`;
- `nuisance_probe_ready`;
- `compression_risk_curve_ready`;
- `runtime_sufficiency_ready`;
- `policy_sufficiency_ready`;
- `do_not_distill_reviewed`.

This lets account, CLI, and backend automation explain why an artifact is or is
not ready.

### W5027 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmSufficiencyIR`.
2. Add behavior relevance graph.
3. Add task-specific distortion metric format.
4. Add distillability classifier.
5. Add invariance contract compiler.
6. Add nuisance and shortcut probes.
7. Add sufficient dataset selector.
8. Add compression-risk curve generator.
9. Add under- and over-specification reports.
10. Add do-not-distill rule.
11. Add sufficiency readiness to product graph.
12. Add sufficiency benchmark fixtures.

First implementation target:

Use the support operations sandbox to compare a routed teacher, a small student,
a larger student, and a retrieval/tool workflow. Produce a report that explains
which option is minimum sufficient and why.

### W5028 Ultimate Sufficiency Objective

Ultimate objective: make Kolm the company that knows the minimum AI artifact
required for a task.

When this works:

- distillation becomes a principled decision, not a default;
- students get smaller without losing what matters;
- privacy improves because nuisance information is removed;
- runtime deployment gets easier;
- customers trust recommendations because Kolm sometimes says "do not distill";
- K-Score becomes tied to task sufficiency, not generic model quality;
- the product moat shifts from "we trained a model" to "we know exactly what
  behavior must be preserved and can prove it."

This is the scientific core of owned AI. Kolm should not merely compress models.
It should compile the minimum sufficient behavior object.

## Three-Surface Website, Narrative, And Product Experience Contract Layer

The website cannot be world-class if the product story is framed around only
one surface. "One AI API" is true for traffic control, but it does not explain
compiling and distilling top models or deploying enterprise models to any
device. The public experience should therefore be built from the same
three-surface product kernel as the backend: control model traffic, compile
owned artifacts, and run them anywhere.

This layer is not frontend code. It is the product narrative contract that the
frontend can implement without inventing copy, visual hierarchy, or page
structure from scratch.

### North Star

In five seconds, a technical buyer understands that Kolm turns production AI
workloads into signed, measured, portable artifacts that reduce cost, preserve
quality, and run across providers, clouds, and devices.

### W5100 SiteNarrativeIR Schema

Define `SiteNarrativeIR`, a structured source for public messaging.

Fields:

- audience;
- product surface;
- primary outcome;
- proof artifact;
- demo path;
- metric;
- CTA;
- trust evidence;
- readiness caveat;
- forbidden claim;
- page owner.

Every page should map to this IR so copy, SEO, screenshots, docs, and account
flows do not drift.

### W5101 Three-Surface Hero Contract

The hero should answer:

- what is this;
- who is it for;
- why now;
- what proof can I try;
- where do I go next.

Recommended hero concept:

```text
Compile production AI into signed artifacts that run anywhere.
Capture real model traffic, distill the repeated work, and deploy verified
.kolm artifacts across clouds, devices, and auditors.
```

This covers all three surfaces without pretending traffic routing is the whole
company.

CTA split:

- primary: run the live artifact demo;
- secondary: view product architecture;
- enterprise: talk to sales or view trust packet.

### W5102 Above-The-Fold Proof Stack

The first viewport should include proof, not paragraphs.

Required elements:

- one-line category statement;
- three product-surface tabs or columns;
- live `.kolm` artifact runner entry point;
- compact code before/after;
- metric proof row;
- trust proof row;
- enterprise CTA.

Avoid:

- abstract slogans;
- giant decorative media;
- video-only explanation;
- long paragraphs;
- claiming every workload runs locally.

### W5103 Surface-Specific Value Propositions

Each product surface needs its own outcome statement.

Traffic control:

```text
Cut provider waste and capture the workloads worth owning.
```

Artifact compilation:

```text
Distill repeated AI work into signed `.kolm` artifacts with task-scoped K-Score.
```

Anywhere runtime:

```text
Run verified artifacts on cloud, browser, mobile, edge, or air-gapped targets.
```

The homepage should show all three as one system, then route to deeper pages for
each.

### W5104 Navigation Architecture

Navigation should be smaller and more operational.

Top-level nav:

- Product;
- Platform;
- Docs;
- Pricing;
- Enterprise;
- Customers or Proof.

Product dropdown:

- Control model traffic;
- Compile artifacts;
- Run anywhere;
- Evaluate with K-Score;
- Govern and audit.

Rules:

- no duplicate labels;
- no underline state inconsistency;
- active state must be visible without color-only dependence;
- mobile nav must avoid nested overload;
- every nav item should answer a user intent.

### W5105 Visual System Contract

Kolm should feel like enterprise AI infrastructure, not a generic AI startup.

Visual principles:

- dense but calm;
- high contrast;
- restrained motion;
- precise grid;
- technical but not terminal-only;
- artifact/proof motif instead of vague gradients;
- diagrams over stock imagery;
- screenshots over abstractions;
- consistent icon stroke and radius.

Avoid:

- many surface colors competing;
- excessive rounded cards;
- decorative blur blobs;
- paragraphs inside every card;
- inconsistent button radii;
- nav underlines that collide with active states.

### W5106 Typography Contract

Typography should support scanning.

Rules:

- hero headline: short and literal;
- section headlines: outcome-first;
- body copy: two sentences max per block where possible;
- cards: one claim, one proof;
- monospace only for artifacts, routes, code, and receipts;
- no viewport-width font scaling;
- no dense all-caps labels except tiny metadata.

The product is complex. Typography must reduce complexity, not perform it.

### W5107 Color And Theme Contract

The brand system should work in dark and light modes.

Requirements:

- define semantic tokens for background, surface, border, text, muted text,
  accent, success, warning, danger, and proof;
- do not rely on color alone;
- maintain WCAG 2.2 contrast targets;
- make focus states visible in both themes;
- validate video/demo elements in both themes;
- avoid one-note purple/blue or dark-slate dominance.

The visual signature should come from proof artifacts, diagrams, and product
specificity, not from decorative gradients.

### W5108 Button And CTA Contract

Buttons should encode intent hierarchy.

Button types:

- primary action;
- secondary navigation;
- enterprise contact;
- destructive or dangerous;
- icon-only utility with tooltip.

Rules:

- one primary CTA per section;
- disabled state explains why;
- loading state reserves width;
- hover, focus, active, and selected states are consistent;
- buttons never wrap awkwardly on mobile;
- enterprise plan never routes to fake self-serve checkout.

### W5109 Demo Widget Contract

The live demo must create the aha moment.

Demo flow:

1. Paste an OpenAI-style call or choose sample.
2. Kolm shows captured task and cost.
3. Kolm proposes artifact candidate.
4. User clicks compile sample.
5. Demo emits `.kolm` artifact summary.
6. User sees two-line swap.
7. User sees K-Score, cost, and runtime target.

Demo constraints:

- no secret required;
- no live credential entry in public demo;
- accessible keyboard path;
- light and dark mode support;
- failure state that teaches.

### W5110 Video Explainer Contract

The video should not be a decorative hero asset.

90-second structure:

- 0-10 seconds: the problem, repeated AI workloads are expensive and unowned;
- 10-25 seconds: capture real calls;
- 25-45 seconds: compile/distill into `.kolm`;
- 45-65 seconds: deploy anywhere;
- 65-80 seconds: audit and K-Score proof;
- 80-90 seconds: live demo CTA.

Fallback:

- transcript;
- poster frame;
- light/dark rendering;
- reduced-motion-safe alternative.

### W5111 Code Comparison Contract

The before/after code block should be concrete.

Before:

- raw provider SDK call;
- model name;
- prompt;
- cost and lock-in shown.

After:

- capture or route through Kolm;
- `.kolm` artifact invocation;
- two-line swap;
- receipt or proof output.

Rules:

- no huge snippets;
- syntax highlighting readable in both themes;
- copy buttons clear;
- mobile scroll contained;
- code demonstrates all three surfaces over the page, not only routing.

### W5112 ROI Calculator Contract

The ROI calculator should be a sales tool, not a toy.

Inputs:

- monthly model spend;
- task repeatability;
- latency sensitivity;
- quality risk tier;
- deployment target;
- human review cost;
- compliance need.

Outputs:

- routing savings;
- distillation savings;
- runtime savings;
- label and compile cost;
- break-even estimate;
- confidence range;
- caveat if not enough data.

Do not show precise savings when assumptions are weak.

### W5113 Proof And Social Trust Contract

If customer logos are unavailable, use proof cards.

Proof card types:

- benchmark proof;
- artifact proof;
- deployment proof;
- compliance-control proof;
- partner proof;
- design partner quote;
- open-source/research proof.

Every proof card needs:

- source;
- date;
- scope;
- limitation;
- link or artifact id.

No vague "trusted by innovators" copy.

### W5114 Page Taxonomy

Public pages should map to buyer intent.

Required pages:

- homepage;
- product overview;
- traffic control;
- artifact compilation;
- any-device runtime;
- K-Score/evaluation;
- governance/audit;
- pricing;
- enterprise;
- docs;
- API reference;
- SDKs;
- quickstart;
- examples;
- security/trust;
- case studies or proof library.

Each page needs one primary job, one CTA, and one proof object.

### W5115 Docs Information Architecture

Docs should get users to value fast.

Docs structure:

- Start: install, capture first call, run sample artifact.
- Concepts: `.kolm`, K-Score, proof packets, runtime profiles.
- Guides: traffic control, distillation, deployment, governance.
- References: CLI, API, SDKs, config, artifact schema.
- Examples: provider migration, claims redactor, support router, mobile edge.
- Operations: storage, secrets, SSO, observability, rollback.

Each guide should end with:

- expected output;
- failure states;
- next action;
- related account page;
- CLI equivalent.

### W5116 SEO And Search Intent Contract

SEO should target concrete production problems.

Keyword clusters:

- AI API cost optimization;
- AI model routing;
- LLM distillation for production;
- AI model portability;
- deploy LLM to edge devices;
- auditable AI outputs;
- AI vendor lock-in;
- AI evaluation metrics;
- local-cloud hybrid inference;
- signed AI artifacts.

Page metadata should be concise, literal, and unique. Titles should not include
mojibake, duplicated brand text, or vague slogans.

### W5117 Accessibility Contract

Minimum:

- WCAG 2.2 AA target;
- visible focus;
- keyboard navigation;
- sufficient contrast;
- semantic headings;
- skip link;
- reduced motion;
- alt text for meaningful visuals;
- no hover-only mega-nav;
- form labels and errors.

This is not optional polish. Enterprise buyers include accessibility in vendor
review.

### W5118 Performance Contract

Marketing pages should feel fast.

Targets:

- good Core Web Vitals;
- reserved media dimensions;
- no layout shift from demos or videos;
- optimized images;
- lazy below-fold media;
- minimal third-party scripts;
- fast theme switching;
- static rendering where possible.

Heavy product demos should progressively load after the initial message is
usable.

### W5119 Account Experience Contract

Post-auth account should show the product matrix.

Account modules:

- provider traffic connected;
- spend and route savings;
- capture quality;
- artifact candidates;
- distillation jobs;
- K-Score and evals;
- runtime targets;
- storage readiness;
- compliance proof;
- team/admin;
- next best action.

Do not bury product depth behind settings pages. The account overview should be
the operational product map.

### W5120 Enterprise Buying Contract

Enterprise pages must be explicit.

Required content:

- contact sales CTA;
- trust packet;
- deployment models;
- data residency;
- SSO/SCIM;
- audit logs;
- procurement docs;
- BAA/DPA status;
- support/SLA;
- private registry;
- air-gapped option;
- custom pricing.

Do not show enterprise as a self-serve checkout if sales review is required.

### W5121 Pricing Contract

Pricing should match product value.

Plan communication:

- Free: try and prototype.
- Pro: individual/team production start.
- Team: collaboration, shared artifacts, higher limits.
- Enterprise: governance, private deployment, custom procurement.

Pricing page should separate:

- traffic control value;
- distillation/compile value;
- any-device runtime value;
- support and governance value.

### W5122 Use-Case Landing Contract

Use-case pages should be concrete.

Recommended first use cases:

- support automation;
- claims and healthcare documents;
- legal extraction;
- fintech compliance;
- mobile/offline assistant;
- enterprise AI gateway migration.

Each page should include:

- starting workflow;
- what Kolm captures;
- what artifact is produced;
- where it runs;
- expected metric movement;
- proof requirement.

### W5123 Content Engine Contract

Content should create qualified demand.

Pillar articles:

- true cost of GPT-4-class models in production;
- how to make AI workloads portable;
- when to distill and when not to;
- AI vendor lock-in risk;
- K-Score methodology;
- deploying AI to edge devices;
- auditable AI artifacts for regulated teams.

Each article should end in a product path, not generic thought leadership.

### W5124 Visual Asset Contract

Assets should show the actual product.

Preferred assets:

- interface screenshots;
- artifact proof packet diagrams;
- architecture diagrams;
- route flow diagrams;
- runtime target matrix;
- K-Score reports;
- code diffs;
- deployment maps.

Avoid:

- generic abstract AI images;
- dark blurred dashboards;
- unplayable video blocks;
- decorative 3D with no product meaning.

### W5125 Product Demo Page Contract

The demo page should support multiple buyer questions.

Demo modes:

- traffic capture demo;
- artifact compile demo;
- any-device runtime demo;
- K-Score evaluation demo;
- governance proof demo.

Each mode should share one UI shell but show a different product surface. This
keeps coherence while proving breadth.

### W5126 Website Failure Mode Library

Track website-specific failures.

Failures:

- hero only explains traffic routing;
- CTA promises unavailable self-serve enterprise;
- video invisible in light mode;
- nav active state inconsistent;
- page has paragraphs but no proof;
- docs command does not match CLI;
- pricing page conflicts with API;
- screenshots pass but message is unclear;
- demo loads but teaches nothing.

Each failure should become a screenshot and copy regression checklist item.

### W5127 Implementation Handoff

Frontend handoff order:

1. Define `SiteNarrativeIR`.
2. Rewrite hero around the three-surface product.
3. Reduce nav to buyer intent.
4. Build proof-first above-the-fold layout.
5. Implement live demo widget with samples.
6. Add side-by-side code comparison.
7. Add ROI calculator with caveats.
8. Build product-surface pages.
9. Align docs IA.
10. Audit accessibility, performance, dark/light, mobile, and copy clarity.
11. Run five-second comprehension tests.
12. Re-screenshot every edited route.

The handoff should be executed by the frontend owner, but the narrative contract
belongs in the product truth spine.

### W5128 Ultimate Website Objective

Ultimate objective: the website should make Kolm feel like the obvious
infrastructure layer for companies moving from rented model calls to owned AI
artifacts.

When this works:

- the hero explains all three surfaces;
- demos create immediate proof;
- docs get developers to value;
- pricing matches reality;
- enterprise buying feels credible;
- visual design feels precise and serious;
- every page has one job;
- every claim has proof;
- every CTA maps to a real product path.

The website should not wrap content in a generic design. The design should make
the product's actual structure impossible to miss.

## Continual Artifact Evolution, Non-Regression, And Model-Merging Layer

Kolm should not treat a `.kolm` artifact as a one-time compile. Production AI
workloads drift: providers change model behavior, user requests shift, edge
targets evolve, policies update, pricing changes, and new failure modes appear.
The product must therefore support continuous artifact evolution without
catastrophic forgetting, hidden regressions, or uncontrolled model sprawl.

The frontier opportunity is to combine continual learning, replay, task vectors,
model merging, LoRA composition, EWC-style stabilization, K-Score regression
gates, and artifact lifecycle governance into one production system. The result
is not "fine-tune again." It is versioned behavioral evolution with proof.

### North Star

Every `.kolm` artifact can improve from new traffic while preserving the
behaviors, policies, runtime guarantees, and audit receipts that made the prior
version safe to deploy.

### W5200 KolmArtifactEvolutionIR Schema

Define `KolmArtifactEvolutionIR`, the state object for artifact evolution.

Fields:

- artifact id;
- previous version;
- proposed version;
- traffic delta;
- drift signals;
- new failure clusters;
- replay set;
- protected behaviors;
- new behaviors;
- forbidden changes;
- update method;
- merge method;
- regression suite;
- K-Score delta;
- rollback trigger;
- rollout plan;
- proof packet.

This IR makes artifact evolution auditable instead of ad hoc retraining.

### W5201 Protected Behavior Set

Every artifact version should declare what must not regress.

Protected behavior classes:

- critical task outputs;
- policy refusals;
- schema guarantees;
- privacy redactions;
- calibration boundaries;
- latency budgets;
- runtime compatibility;
- audit output format;
- customer-specific invariances.

Protected behaviors become replay fixtures and promotion gates for every update.

### W5202 Evolution Trigger Classifier

Not every signal should trigger retraining.

Triggers:

- distribution drift;
- recurring failure cluster;
- provider teacher update;
- policy change;
- schema change;
- new deployment target;
- latency regression;
- cost opportunity;
- customer escalation;
- benchmark freshness decay.

Classifier outputs:

- no action;
- eval refresh;
- route policy update;
- dataset update;
- LoRA patch;
- student retrain;
- model merge;
- runtime recompile;
- human review.

### W5203 Continual Distillation Queue

Continuous background distillation needs queue discipline.

Queue priority:

- severity of failure;
- customer impact;
- expected cost savings;
- expected K-Score lift;
- label availability;
- runtime feasibility;
- policy risk;
- compute budget;
- rollback complexity.

This prevents background jobs from optimizing easy but low-value tasks while
critical failures wait.

### W5204 Replay Memory Design

Replay is the simplest defense against forgetting, but it can become a privacy
and storage risk.

Replay memory types:

- public fixtures;
- synthetic fixtures;
- customer-owned redacted fixtures;
- protected boundary cases;
- high-severity incidents;
- policy refusal cases;
- runtime edge cases;
- old-version outputs.

Rules:

- store minimum necessary examples;
- retain rights metadata;
- hash and sign replay sets;
- expose replay coverage;
- allow customer deletion and replacement.

### W5205 Elastic Behavior Consolidation

Borrow the spirit of Elastic Weight Consolidation, but apply it to behavior and
artifact proof.

Consolidation signals:

- parameters important to protected behaviors;
- activations important to protected behaviors;
- task vectors important to protected behaviors;
- runtime profile settings important to protected behaviors;
- policy rules important to protected behaviors.

Training should penalize changes that damage protected behavior unless an
explicit migration approves the change.

### W5206 Task Vector Ledger

Task vectors should become first-class artifact metadata.

Ledger fields:

- base model;
- task vector digest;
- source dataset;
- task scope;
- eval scope;
- known interactions;
- compatible merges;
- incompatible merges;
- quantization compatibility;
- runtime target compatibility.

This turns model merging from a lab trick into a governed product primitive.

### W5207 LoRA Patch Registry

Small behavior updates should not always require full artifact rebuilds.

Patch registry fields:

- patch id;
- target artifact version;
- LoRA or adapter digest;
- behavior objective;
- protected behavior tests;
- K-Score delta;
- runtime overhead;
- merge eligibility;
- expiration policy.

Patch rule:

A patch can ship only if the base artifact plus patch has its own proof packet.

### W5208 Model Merge Planner

Model merging can combine capabilities without inference-time ensembles, but it
can also create interference.

Planner inputs:

- candidate checkpoints;
- task vectors;
- protected behavior set;
- conflict map;
- sign conflict rate;
- layer interference;
- eval coverage;
- runtime target.

Planner outputs:

- merge method;
- weights;
- excluded vectors;
- expected interference;
- required regression suite;
- rollback plan.

### W5209 Interference Detector

Detect when two updates conflict before promotion.

Interference types:

- sign conflict;
- layer conflict;
- feature conflict;
- policy conflict;
- calibration conflict;
- runtime conflict;
- license conflict;
- customer-specific behavior conflict.

Interference should block merge until resolved or isolated.

### W5210 Task Vector Quantization

Task vectors can be large. Quantizing them may make storage and transfer
practical.

Use cases:

- private customer patch libraries;
- edge artifact patch delivery;
- marketplace behavior packs;
- rollback-friendly updates;
- low-bandwidth deployment.

Guardrails:

- quantized task vector must preserve protected behavior;
- precision choices need K-Score and slice proof;
- lossy patches require rollback threshold.

### W5211 Artifact Branching And Merge Strategy

Artifacts need branch discipline similar to software.

Branches:

- stable;
- canary;
- customer-specific;
- regulated frozen;
- experimental;
- runtime-specific;
- policy-specific.

Merge rules:

- stable accepts only fully proven changes;
- regulated accepts only reviewed changes;
- customer-specific branches declare divergence;
- experimental branches cannot silently become stable.

### W5212 Non-Regression K-Score Gate

K-Score must compare versions, not only absolute quality.

Gate dimensions:

- total K-Score delta;
- critical slice delta;
- protected behavior delta;
- calibration delta;
- latency delta;
- cost delta;
- policy delta;
- runtime compatibility delta.

Promotion rule:

No positive average score can hide a critical protected-behavior regression.

### W5213 Artifact Memory Consolidation Window

Updates should pass a consolidation window before broad rollout.

Window checks:

- canary traffic;
- replay suite;
- long-tail slice eval;
- policy eval;
- runtime telemetry;
- customer feedback;
- incident watch;
- rollback rehearsal.

This prevents immediate promotion from masking delayed regressions.

### W5214 Drift-To-Patch Loop

Production drift should become targeted patches.

Loop:

1. Detect drift.
2. Cluster affected traffic.
3. Identify protected behavior overlap.
4. Generate candidate patch.
5. Run replay and metamorphic tests.
6. Canary patched artifact.
7. Promote or rollback.
8. Store negative result if failed.

This is continuous improvement with memory.

### W5215 Customer-Specific Evolution Boundaries

Customers need different evolution rules.

Boundary settings:

- allow automatic patch;
- require approval;
- freeze artifact;
- allow only policy updates;
- allow only runtime recompiles;
- prohibit cross-customer transfer;
- require local-only replay.

These settings should be visible in enterprise account controls.

### W5216 Version Diff For Behavior

Artifact diffs should compare behavior, not only metadata.

Diff sections:

- added behaviors;
- removed behaviors;
- changed outputs;
- changed refusals;
- changed confidence;
- changed runtime targets;
- changed policy;
- changed cost and latency;
- changed proof coverage.

This is the AI equivalent of a software release diff.

### W5217 Rollback-Safe Evolution

Every update needs rollback.

Rollback proof:

- prior artifact still available;
- prior runtime profile still compatible;
- prior policy still valid;
- data migration reversible or not needed;
- route policy can repoint;
- cache invalidation handled;
- audit trail preserved.

No evolution without rollback unless the customer explicitly accepts an
irreversible migration.

### W5218 Continual Learning Benchmark Suite

Build benchmarks for artifact evolution.

Benchmark tasks:

- learn new slice without forgetting old slice;
- update policy without changing allowed behavior;
- add deployment target without quality loss;
- merge two task vectors without interference;
- patch rare failure without damaging common case;
- quantize patch without calibration loss.

Metrics:

- forward transfer;
- backward transfer;
- forgetting rate;
- interference rate;
- protected behavior pass rate;
- rollback success.

### W5219 Evolution Cost Optimizer

Artifact updates have cost.

Choose among:

- route update;
- prompt/policy update;
- LoRA patch;
- task vector merge;
- full retrain;
- runtime recompile;
- human workflow change.

Optimization objective:

Maximize expected K-Score and customer value per compute, label, risk, and
deployment cost.

### W5220 Evolution Incident Postmortem

Every bad update should create a postmortem.

Postmortem fields:

- changed artifact;
- trigger;
- missed regression;
- affected customers;
- failed gate;
- rollback time;
- fixture added;
- process change;
- customer communication.

This makes the evolution system safer over time.

### W5221 Artifact Evolution Marketplace Policy

Marketplace artifacts need stricter update rules.

Rules:

- seller cannot silently weaken behavior;
- buyers see diff before major update;
- regulated buyers can pin versions;
- critical security patches can be expedited;
- trust score incorporates update quality;
- abandoned artifacts are flagged.

Marketplace trust depends on predictable evolution.

### W5222 Cross-Customer Learning Without Cross-Customer Leakage

Evolution can learn from many customers only with strong boundaries.

Allowed sharing:

- aggregate failure taxonomy;
- synthetic fixtures;
- public benchmark updates;
- anonymized task vector statistics if permitted;
- non-identifying runtime profiles.

Disallowed by default:

- raw prompts;
- raw outputs;
- customer-specific labels;
- policy documents;
- private artifacts.

### W5223 Evolution-Aware Runtime Selection

Runtime choice affects update cadence.

Examples:

- browser runtime may need smaller patches;
- air-gapped runtime needs offline bundles;
- mobile runtime needs battery-safe update windows;
- regulated runtime needs approval gates;
- cloud runtime can canary faster.

The evolution plan should be target-aware.

### W5224 Evolution Failure Mode Library

Track failures:

- patch fixes rare case and breaks common case;
- merge improves average and damages protected slice;
- policy update changes output schema;
- task vector quantization changes calibration;
- canary traffic unrepresentative;
- rollback artifact unavailable;
- customer-specific branch drifts too far from stable.

Each failure becomes a replay fixture and gate.

### W5225 Product Graph Evolution Readiness

Expose evolution readiness.

Fields:

- `protected_behavior_ready`;
- `replay_memory_ready`;
- `drift_trigger_ready`;
- `patch_registry_ready`;
- `merge_planner_ready`;
- `non_regression_gate_ready`;
- `rollback_ready`;
- `customer_approval_ready`.

This keeps account and CLI honest about whether background improvement is safe.

### W5226 Evolution Governance Board

High-risk updates require review.

Review dimensions:

- customer impact;
- regulated domain;
- policy change;
- protected behavior change;
- data rights;
- rollback confidence;
- marketplace impact;
- public claim impact.

Small low-risk patches can be automated; high-risk changes need human approval.

### W5227 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmArtifactEvolutionIR`.
2. Add protected behavior sets.
3. Add drift trigger classifier.
4. Add replay memory format.
5. Add task vector ledger.
6. Add LoRA patch registry.
7. Add model merge planner.
8. Add interference detector.
9. Add version behavior diff.
10. Add non-regression K-Score gate.
11. Add rollback-safe evolution proof.
12. Expose evolution readiness in product graph.

First implementation target:

Take one artifact with a known failure cluster, generate a LoRA-style patch or
small retrain, run protected replay, produce a behavior diff, then canary and
rollback in simulation.

### W5228 Ultimate Evolution Objective

Ultimate objective: make `.kolm` artifacts improve continuously without losing
the trust that made customers deploy them.

When this works:

- artifacts evolve from production traffic;
- customers control update boundaries;
- protected behavior does not regress silently;
- task vectors become reusable product assets;
- patches are cheaper than full retrains;
- model merging becomes governed, not artisanal;
- marketplace artifacts remain trustworthy over time;
- Kolm compounds every customer lesson into safer future artifacts.

This is how Kolm turns deployed artifacts from static deliverables into living,
governed AI assets.

## Cross-Lingual, Locale, And International Artifact Compiler Layer

Kolm's enterprise wedge should not stop at English-language AI workloads. Global
companies run support, claims, legal, finance, healthcare, and operations across
languages, scripts, dialects, jurisdictions, currencies, and cultural norms.
Cross-lingual distillation should not mean "translate the prompt and hope." It
should mean compiling locale-aware `.kolm` artifacts with language-specific
K-Score, policy, evaluation, runtime, and audit guarantees.

The opportunity is to turn internationalization into a technical moat: every
new language, locale, and jurisdiction adds calibrated artifacts, evaluation
slices, translation risk data, and deployment evidence that competitors cannot
reconstruct from generic multilingual models.

### North Star

Kolm can capture a production AI workload in one language, identify which parts
transfer across languages, compile safe locale-specific artifacts, and prove
per-language quality, policy, cost, and deployment readiness.

### W5300 KolmLocaleArtifactIR Schema

Define `KolmLocaleArtifactIR`, the contract for multilingual artifacts.

Fields:

- source language;
- target language;
- locale;
- script;
- dialect;
- region;
- jurisdiction;
- currency;
- date/time format;
- units;
- task family;
- translation path;
- native training path;
- per-language eval suite;
- policy overlays;
- runtime target;
- fallback language;
- human review requirement;
- proof packet.

This makes language and locale first-class artifact dimensions.

### W5301 Language Coverage Matrix

Build a coverage matrix for every artifact.

Rows:

- language;
- locale;
- script;
- direction;
- task;
- available labels;
- available evals;
- teacher quality;
- student quality;
- human reviewer availability;
- regulatory status.

Columns:

- capture-ready;
- route-ready;
- distill-ready;
- compile-ready;
- deploy-ready;
- audit-ready.

This prevents vague claims like "multilingual support" without evidence.

### W5302 Per-Language K-Score

K-Score must be per-language and per-locale.

Language-specific axes:

- semantic fidelity;
- structured output fidelity;
- terminology fidelity;
- locale formatting;
- cultural appropriateness;
- toxicity/safety;
- code-switching robustness;
- low-resource uncertainty;
- human preference calibration.

No global K-Score average should hide a weak language.

### W5303 Translation-Versus-Native Decision Engine

For each task, decide whether to translate, train native, route to teacher, or
use human review.

Options:

- translate input to high-resource language;
- translate output back;
- train multilingual student;
- train locale-specific student;
- route to multilingual teacher;
- use retrieval in source language;
- require human review.

Decision factors:

- semantic risk;
- regulatory risk;
- terminology density;
- label availability;
- teacher quality;
- runtime target;
- cost;
- latency;
- customer preference.

### W5304 Cross-Lingual Transfer Safety Gate

Cross-lingual transfer can silently break policy and meaning.

Gate checks:

- terminology preservation;
- negation preservation;
- numerical preservation;
- entity preservation;
- date/currency/unit preservation;
- legal phrase preservation;
- consent and privacy term preservation;
- refusal policy preservation.

If transfer fails any high-risk check, use native training or human review.

### W5305 Locale Policy Overlay Compiler

Policy differs by region and language.

Locale policy overlays include:

- data residency;
- consent language;
- retention rules;
- regulated domain terms;
- local privacy rules;
- medical/legal disclaimers;
- age/minor rules;
- financial advice rules;
- human approval rules.

The artifact should bind policy overlay to locale and runtime target.

### W5306 Terminology Memory And Glossary Compiler

Enterprise multilingual work depends on terminology.

Compile:

- customer glossary;
- industry glossary;
- forbidden translations;
- preferred terms;
- abbreviations;
- entity aliases;
- product names;
- legal terms;
- payer/provider terms.

The glossary becomes part of the artifact proof and eval suite.

### W5307 Code-Switching And Mixed-Language Robustness

Production traffic often mixes languages.

Test:

- English plus Spanish support tickets;
- local language plus English product terms;
- romanized non-Latin text;
- OCR text with mixed scripts;
- names and addresses in original script;
- code snippets inside non-English request.

Artifacts should declare whether code-switching is supported, unsupported, or
requires route fallback.

### W5308 Low-Resource Language Strategy

Low-resource languages need different economics.

Strategies:

- teacher route with human review;
- translation bridge;
- active learning;
- community/expert labels;
- synthetic augmentation;
- multilingual transfer;
- narrow task-specific student;
- abstention on low confidence.

Metric:

Do not optimize only average multilingual score. Track the cost and risk of
supporting the next language.

### W5309 Script And Tokenization Risk Gate

Different scripts create runtime and quality risks.

Risks:

- token inflation;
- poor tokenizer segmentation;
- OCR errors;
- bidirectional text;
- accent/diacritic handling;
- normalization differences;
- homoglyph attacks;
- punctuation differences;
- whitespace-free languages.

The compiler should estimate token cost, latency, and quality risk by script.

### W5310 Locale Formatting Verifier

Structured output must respect locale.

Verify:

- dates;
- times;
- addresses;
- phone numbers;
- currencies;
- decimals;
- measurements;
- names;
- legal identifiers;
- medical codes.

Formatting errors can break downstream workflows even when language looks
correct.

### W5311 Cross-Lingual Retrieval And Evidence Grounding

RAG and evidence tasks need language-aware grounding.

Modes:

- retrieve in source language;
- translate query then retrieve;
- retrieve multilingual embeddings;
- translate evidence;
- answer in requested language;
- cite original-language evidence.

Proof packet should state which mode was used and whether evidence was
translated.

### W5312 Multilingual Human Review Network

Human review is part of the product for high-risk languages.

Reviewer metadata:

- language;
- dialect;
- domain expertise;
- jurisdiction expertise;
- certification;
- review latency;
- conflict of interest;
- calibration history.

Use reviewers for calibration, high-risk decisions, and benchmark creation.

### W5313 Per-Language Calibration Dataset

Maintain calibration data by language.

Dataset types:

- synthetic fixtures;
- human-translated fixtures;
- native-written fixtures;
- customer opt-in fixtures;
- domain expert fixtures;
- adversarial fixtures.

Each dataset should record whether it was translated or native, because
translated benchmarks can hide real production variance.

### W5314 Cross-Lingual Artifact Diff

Compare artifact behavior across languages.

Diff dimensions:

- intent preservation;
- output schema;
- refusal policy;
- entity extraction;
- terminology;
- confidence;
- cost;
- latency;
- runtime target;
- audit fields.

This identifies languages that need native students or extra labels.

### W5315 International Pricing And ROI Model

Pricing and ROI differ by region.

Inputs:

- local model provider availability;
- cloud region cost;
- language token inflation;
- human review cost;
- support volume;
- compliance cost;
- currency;
- purchasing power;
- marketplace channel.

Output:

- region-specific ROI range;
- break-even estimate;
- confidence interval;
- caveats.

### W5316 International Deployment Target Matrix

Global deployments need region and device support.

Matrix dimensions:

- country/region;
- cloud availability;
- data residency;
- edge target;
- mobile OS share;
- browser support;
- GPU availability;
- NPU support;
- offline requirement;
- network quality.

This prevents "run anywhere" from hiding region-specific blockers.

### W5317 Multilingual Safety And Toxicity Gate

Safety classifiers often fail unevenly across languages.

Gate checks:

- hate/harassment;
- self-harm;
- sexual content;
- medical/legal risk;
- political sensitivity;
- culturally specific slurs;
- translated policy mismatch;
- jailbreak phrasing in low-resource language.

Artifacts should carry per-language safety confidence, not a global pass/fail.

### W5318 Cross-Lingual Structured Output Contracts

Structured outputs should be language-neutral where possible.

Design:

- stable field names;
- locale-specific values where needed;
- explicit language tags;
- original text spans;
- normalized values;
- translation confidence;
- human review flags.

This lets downstream systems work globally without brittle free text.

### W5319 Locale-Aware Account And Docs Experience

International product experience needs localized operational clarity.

Account should show:

- enabled languages;
- unsupported languages;
- per-language K-Score;
- reviewer availability;
- locale policies;
- translation path;
- deployment region;
- next action.

Docs should distinguish translated docs from product support in that language.

### W5320 International Compliance Map

Global AI deployment touches local law.

Track:

- GDPR;
- EU AI Act;
- HIPAA-adjacent healthcare workflows;
- country privacy rules;
- financial services rules;
- data export restrictions;
- local model-hosting restrictions;
- language-specific disclosure requirements.

This map should feed route policy and artifact deployment eligibility.

### W5321 Cross-Lingual Benchmark Suite

Build KolmBench multilingual.

Benchmark families:

- structured extraction;
- classification;
- redaction;
- support routing;
- legal clause extraction;
- healthcare admin;
- RAG answer grounding;
- code-switching;
- locale formatting;
- safety refusal.

Report per-language and per-task results. Do not publish only macro averages.

### W5322 Language Expansion Prioritizer

Prioritize languages by product value.

Inputs:

- customer demand;
- traffic volume;
- revenue opportunity;
- risk;
- data availability;
- reviewer availability;
- model quality;
- deployment feasibility;
- regulatory pressure.

Output:

- next language wave;
- required labels;
- required reviewers;
- expected ROI;
- readiness blocker.

### W5323 Multilingual Marketplace Trust

Marketplace artifacts need language badges.

Badges:

- language inspected;
- language executable;
- language K-Score calibrated;
- language human-reviewed;
- language regulated-ready;
- language unsupported.

This prevents buyers from assuming English proof applies globally.

### W5324 Cross-Lingual Failure Mode Library

Track failures:

- translated fixture hides native phrasing;
- low-resource tokenizer inflates cost;
- legal term translated incorrectly;
- date format corrupts structured output;
- safety refusal fails in dialect;
- code-switching breaks route policy;
- artifact passes English but fails Spanish/French/Japanese slice.

Each failure becomes a benchmark fixture.

### W5325 Product Graph Locale Readiness

Expose locale readiness.

Fields:

- `language_capture_ready`;
- `language_eval_ready`;
- `language_kscore_ready`;
- `locale_policy_ready`;
- `terminology_ready`;
- `reviewer_ready`;
- `translation_path_ready`;
- `native_student_ready`;
- `deployment_region_ready`.

This keeps international claims honest.

### W5326 International GTM Contract

International expansion should follow readiness.

Launch requirements:

- localized website page;
- localized docs;
- local compliance note;
- supported provider/region;
- at least one language-calibrated artifact;
- sales/support path;
- incident escalation path.

Do not launch a country page if product support is only aspirational.

### W5327 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmLocaleArtifactIR`.
2. Add language coverage matrix.
3. Add per-language K-Score.
4. Add translation-versus-native decision engine.
5. Add terminology compiler.
6. Add code-switching tests.
7. Add locale formatting verifier.
8. Add cross-lingual artifact diff.
9. Add multilingual safety gate.
10. Add multilingual benchmark suite.
11. Add locale readiness to product graph.
12. Pilot with English plus two high-demand languages and one low-resource
    language.

First proof:

Take one structured extraction artifact, evaluate English, Spanish, and Japanese
or another non-Latin-script language, report per-language K-Score, formatting
failures, token cost, and required human-review paths.

### W5328 Ultimate Global Artifact Objective

Ultimate objective: make Kolm the company that can safely globalize owned AI
workloads without pretending translation equals product support.

When this works:

- customers know which languages are truly supported;
- artifacts carry locale-aware proof;
- K-Score becomes language-specific;
- translation and native distillation are chosen deliberately;
- global deployment is tied to compliance and runtime reality;
- low-resource languages become a product roadmap, not a hidden failure mode;
- every new language expands Kolm's data and evaluation moat.

This is how Kolm turns international expansion into a technical advantage
instead of a marketing checkbox.

## Typed Output, Schema-Constrained Decoding, And Workflow Contract Layer

Many production AI failures are not failures of intelligence. They are failures
of shape: invalid JSON, missing fields, unsafe enum values, wrong units, stale
schema versions, ambiguous evidence spans, or outputs that humans can read but
software cannot trust. If Kolm wants `.kolm` artifacts to run enterprise
workflows, structured output must be a compiled guarantee, not a prompt style.

This layer turns W809 structured output validation into a full product
architecture. It connects JSON Schema, Zod/Pydantic-style validation,
grammar-constrained decoding, provider structured outputs, SGLang/LMQL/Guidance
style constraints, runtime validators, artifact proof packets, SDK generation,
and downstream workflow compatibility.

### North Star

Every `.kolm` artifact that emits structured data has a typed contract, a
constraint plan, a validator, an error-repair path, a schema migration story,
and proof that the contract holds across providers, students, runtimes,
languages, and deployment targets.

### W5400 KolmTypedOutputIR Schema

Define `KolmTypedOutputIR`, the canonical contract for structured AI outputs.

Fields:

- schema id;
- schema version;
- JSON Schema or equivalent;
- language bindings;
- provider constraint support;
- runtime constraint support;
- validator;
- repair strategy;
- refusal shape;
- confidence fields;
- provenance fields;
- evidence spans;
- migration policy;
- compatibility level;
- downstream workflow owner.

This IR makes structured output a product object, not a hidden parser.

### W5401 Schema Capability Matrix

Different providers and runtimes support different schema subsets.

Matrix dimensions:

- required fields;
- optional fields;
- enums;
- nested objects;
- arrays;
- unions;
- `anyOf`/`oneOf`;
- regex patterns;
- numeric ranges;
- string formats;
- recursion;
- EBNF grammar;
- reasoning/final-answer separation.

The compiler should choose the strongest enforceable constraint for each target
and emit warnings when the schema must be weakened.

### W5402 Constraint Plan Compiler

For every artifact, compile a constraint plan.

Constraint modes:

- strict provider structured output;
- local grammar-constrained decoding;
- regex-constrained decoding;
- EBNF-constrained decoding;
- prompt plus validator;
- validator plus repair;
- validator plus human review;
- refusal if no safe constraint exists.

The plan should state which constraints are enforced during generation and which
are checked after generation.

### W5403 Typed Refusal Contract

Refusals should be structured too.

Refusal fields:

- refusal code;
- policy reason;
- confidence;
- evidence pointer if allowed;
- safe next action;
- human escalation flag;
- retry eligibility;
- audit id.

Without typed refusals, downstream workflows cannot tell the difference between
model failure, policy refusal, missing data, and infrastructure error.

### W5404 Evidence Span Contract

Enterprise structured outputs need evidence.

Each field can include:

- source document id;
- source span;
- page number;
- bounding box;
- confidence;
- extraction method;
- transformation method;
- human review state.

This is especially important for healthcare, legal, finance, claims, and audit
workflows.

### W5405 Schema-Aware Distillation

Students should learn schema behavior explicitly.

Training signals:

- field-level loss;
- enum loss;
- missing-field penalty;
- invalid-format penalty;
- evidence-span alignment;
- refusal-shape preservation;
- repair cost;
- downstream workflow success.

Do not train only on serialized JSON strings. Train on field semantics and
contract success.

### W5406 Grammar-Constrained Runtime Selection

Runtime choice affects structured output guarantees.

Runtime requirements:

- supports JSON schema or grammar constraints;
- exposes token masking behavior;
- handles tokenizer/schema edge cases;
- separates reasoning from final structured answer when needed;
- reports constraint failures;
- supports streaming partial structures where safe.

If a runtime cannot enforce or validate the contract, it should not be eligible
for that artifact.

### W5407 Provider Structured Output Adapter

Providers differ in structured output behavior.

Adapter responsibilities:

- normalize provider schema subset;
- reject unsupported schema features;
- map provider errors;
- test adversarial constraint prompts;
- compare provider output to local validator;
- record provider structured output reliability.

This prevents Kolm from assuming "JSON mode" means production-grade schema
compliance.

### W5408 Multi-Language Type Binding Generator

Structured artifacts should generate type bindings.

Targets:

- TypeScript;
- Python/Pydantic;
- Rust;
- Go;
- Swift;
- Kotlin;
- OpenAPI;
- JSON Schema;
- SQL or warehouse schema where appropriate.

Generated bindings reduce integration errors and make docs executable.

### W5409 Schema Versioning And Migration

Schemas evolve.

Version policy:

- additive optional fields are minor changes;
- required field changes are major changes;
- enum removal is breaking;
- semantic meaning changes are breaking;
- evidence-span changes may be breaking;
- refusal-shape changes may be breaking.

Migration artifacts:

- old schema;
- new schema;
- transformer;
- compatibility tests;
- downstream owner approval;
- deprecation window.

### W5410 Partial Output And Streaming Contract

Streaming structured output is useful but dangerous.

Rules:

- partial output must be marked partial;
- downstream systems cannot act on incomplete required fields;
- field-level streaming requires stable field boundaries;
- repair may happen before finalization;
- audit records final validated object.

This matters for low-latency agent and workflow integrations.

### W5411 Schema Repair Engine

When output fails validation, repair should be bounded.

Repair strategies:

- deterministic coercion;
- unit normalization;
- enum mapping;
- field extraction from evidence spans;
- model repair with original output and validation errors;
- human review.

Repair limits:

- no silent fabrication;
- no policy bypass;
- no evidence-free correction for high-risk fields;
- no infinite retry loop.

### W5412 Field-Level Confidence And Calibration

One confidence score for the whole JSON object is too weak.

Confidence should attach to:

- each field;
- each evidence span;
- each transformed value;
- each inferred value;
- each normalized unit;
- each refusal.

K-Score should include field-level calibration, not only object validity.

### W5413 Schema-Aware K-Score Axis

Add a K-Score axis for typed output.

Components:

- syntax validity;
- schema validity;
- semantic field correctness;
- evidence support;
- calibration;
- downstream workflow success;
- repair rate;
- refusal correctness.

Invalid JSON is a zero for syntax. Valid JSON with wrong content is still a
failure.

### W5414 Adversarial Schema Test Suite

Structured output should be attacked.

Test attacks:

- ask model to omit required field;
- ask model to use invalid enum;
- ask model to inject extra fields;
- ask model to output prose;
- ask model to use wrong unit;
- ask model to invent evidence;
- ask model to exploit nested schema ambiguity;
- ask model to break streaming boundaries.

Every provider and runtime should run the same tests.

### W5415 Schema Fuzzing And Property Tests

Generate schema fuzz tests.

Fuzz dimensions:

- nesting depth;
- array length;
- optional fields;
- unicode;
- long strings;
- numeric extremes;
- date formats;
- union branches;
- locale formatting;
- malicious strings.

The artifact should fail safely when the schema or input creates ambiguity.

### W5416 Downstream Workflow Simulator

Structured output exists to drive workflows.

Simulate:

- CRM update;
- claims adjudication support;
- legal clause table;
- support ticket routing;
- audit export;
- billing attribution;
- device deployment plan.

Measure:

- workflow success;
- manual correction rate;
- invalid object rate;
- field-level harm risk;
- integration latency.

### W5417 Typed Tool-Use Contract

Agent tools need typed inputs and outputs.

Tool contract fields:

- input schema;
- output schema;
- side effect level;
- permission scope;
- idempotency;
- retry policy;
- audit fields;
- compensating action.

This binds structured output to agent governance and prevents free-form tool
calls from becoming hidden integration debt.

### W5418 Human Review UI Contract

Humans need field-level review, not blob review.

Review UI should show:

- field value;
- evidence span;
- confidence;
- validation status;
- suggested repair;
- policy warning;
- downstream impact.

Human edits become calibration data and schema improvement signals.

### W5419 Schema Drift Detector

Detect when production inputs or outputs drift from the schema.

Drift signals:

- new enum values;
- rising optional-field usage;
- missing required fields;
- evidence span mismatch;
- repair rate increase;
- human edit cluster;
- downstream rejection.

Drift can trigger schema migration, student update, or route fallback.

### W5420 Typed Artifact Marketplace Badge

Marketplace artifacts should display typed-output maturity.

Badges:

- schema declared;
- constrained decoding supported;
- validator included;
- field confidence calibrated;
- evidence spans included;
- migration policy declared;
- adversarial schema tests passed.

Buyers should know whether an artifact can safely drive software workflows.

### W5421 Schema Rights And Data Handling

Schemas can encode sensitive business logic.

Protect:

- proprietary field definitions;
- customer workflow structure;
- regulated categories;
- internal codes;
- evidence mappings;
- downstream system names.

Artifact sharing should distinguish public schema, private schema, and
customer-specific schema overlays.

### W5422 Cross-Provider Structured Output Bakeoff

Run a provider bakeoff for structured output.

Metrics:

- schema subset support;
- syntax validity;
- semantic correctness;
- adversarial compliance;
- latency;
- cost;
- streaming support;
- repair rate;
- error clarity.

This becomes route policy data. Some providers may be cheaper but unsafe for
typed workflows.

### W5423 Typed Output Failure Mode Library

Track failures:

- valid JSON with wrong field;
- missing evidence;
- enum hallucination;
- silent unit conversion error;
- schema version mismatch;
- provider accepts unsupported schema feature;
- repair fabricates value;
- downstream workflow acts on partial object.

Each failure becomes a fixture and K-Score regression test.

### W5424 Product Graph Typed Readiness

Expose typed-output readiness.

Fields:

- `schema_declared`;
- `constraint_plan_ready`;
- `validator_ready`;
- `repair_ready`;
- `field_confidence_ready`;
- `evidence_spans_ready`;
- `migration_ready`;
- `downstream_sim_ready`;
- `adversarial_schema_tests_ready`.

This prevents account, docs, and marketing from claiming workflow readiness
without typed proof.

### W5425 Developer Experience For Typed Artifacts

Developers should not hand-roll parsers.

DX requirements:

- generated types;
- examples;
- validation errors with field paths;
- fixture generator;
- sample invalid outputs;
- migration guide;
- SDK helpers;
- copy-paste-safe code snippets.

The faster a developer trusts the type contract, the faster Kolm activates.

### W5426 Typed Output Economics

Structured output has direct ROI.

Value drivers:

- less manual correction;
- fewer workflow failures;
- lower retry cost;
- safer provider routing;
- faster integration;
- lower compliance review;
- fewer support tickets.

ROI calculator should include invalid-output cost for structured workflows.

### W5427 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `KolmTypedOutputIR`.
2. Add schema capability matrix.
3. Add constraint plan compiler.
4. Add typed refusal contract.
5. Add evidence span contract.
6. Add provider structured output adapters.
7. Add multi-language type binding generator.
8. Add schema repair engine.
9. Add field-level confidence.
10. Add adversarial schema test suite.
11. Add downstream workflow simulator.
12. Add typed readiness to product graph.

First proof:

Take a claims extraction artifact and compile one schema to provider structured
output, local grammar-constrained decoding, validator-only fallback, TypeScript
types, Python/Pydantic types, and a downstream workflow simulator. Report
validity, semantic accuracy, repair rate, and workflow success.

### W5428 Ultimate Typed Artifact Objective

Ultimate objective: make `.kolm` artifacts safe to call from software, not just
useful to read.

When this works:

- structured outputs become compiled guarantees;
- developers stop writing brittle regex repair code;
- workflows can act on AI output with field-level confidence;
- provider routing knows which models can satisfy which schemas;
- distillation optimizes contract success, not only text similarity;
- K-Score measures typed workflow reliability;
- enterprise buyers can audit every field back to evidence.

This is how Kolm turns AI responses into dependable software interfaces.

## Enterprise Process Mining, Task Graph, And Business Outcome Compiler Layer

Thesis:

- Kolm should not only compile model calls. It should compile the business
  process around those calls.
- The winning enterprise product is the system that can answer: which process is
  this AI task part of, what outcome does it change, which steps are bottlenecks,
  which exceptions create risk, and what exact artifact should replace repeated
  expensive model behavior?
- Process mining gives Kolm the missing enterprise substrate: event logs, cases,
  object-centric flows, conformance, bottlenecks, variants, and process drift.
- Kolm's unique move is to join process mining with model traffic, distillation,
  typed outputs, runtime profiles, and signed evidence.

Why this is a 100x product lever:

- CFOs do not buy "faster completions." They buy lower cycle time, fewer
  escalations, fewer rework loops, higher first-pass yield, and lower cost per
  completed case.
- CTOs do not want another model observability panel. They want a way to turn
  repeated AI-assisted work into owned, reliable process machinery.
- Business owners do not care that a student model is 4-bit. They care that
  refund approvals, prior auth reviews, support escalations, claims redactions,
  invoice matching, and onboarding checks finish faster with fewer errors.
- Kolm can become the first AI compiler that binds artifacts to business process
  outcomes, not just model benchmarks.

Frontier synthesis:

- XES standardizes event logs for process mining.
- OCEL captures object-centric event logs where one event touches multiple
  objects, such as an order, customer, shipment, invoice, and refund.
- BPMN describes intended process models.
- PM4Py and commercial process-intelligence platforms show discovery,
  conformance, and bottleneck analysis.
- OpenTelemetry and AI gateway traces show model-level behavior.
- Kolm should fuse these into a `ProcessArtifactIR`: actual process traces plus
  AI calls plus evidence plus economics plus candidate artifacts.

New primitive:

```text
ProcessArtifactIR:
  process_id
  process_version
  tenant_boundary
  event_log_refs
  object_types
  case_linking_policy
  ai_call_nodes
  human_task_nodes
  system_task_nodes
  decision_nodes
  exception_variants
  bpmn_contract
  conformance_rules
  sla_contract
  risk_contract
  cost_contract
  candidate_artifacts
  distillation_curriculum
  runtime_policy
  replay_fixtures
  proof_packet
```

### W5500 Process Event Ingest Contract

Objective:

- Create a canonical ingest contract for XES, OCEL, OpenTelemetry traces, CRM
  events, ticket events, EHR events, ERP events, queue events, and AI gateway
  calls.

Build:

- Add `ProcessEventV1` with `event_id`, `timestamp`, `activity`, `actor`,
  `system`, `case_id`, `object_refs`, `ai_call_ref`, `cost`, `latency`,
  `risk_class`, `payload_schema`, `redaction_state`, and `source_hash`.
- Normalize vendor logs without preserving raw sensitive values unless the
  tenant explicitly enables an evidence vault.
- Hash source rows and retain reversible pointers only inside the tenant trust
  boundary.

Smoke simulation:

- Input: Zendesk ticket created, GPT support answer, human edit, Salesforce case
  closure.
- Expected output: one linked process case with four events, one AI call node,
  one human correction node, and no leaked raw customer text in the default log.

Metric lift:

- Increases captured process coverage.
- Reduces integration ambiguity.
- Makes ROI measurement possible per actual business process.

### W5501 Case And Object Linker

Objective:

- Link AI calls to the right business case and objects, even when the trace has
  no clean case id.

Build:

- Use deterministic joins first: ticket id, claim id, invoice id, order id,
  patient encounter id, thread id, workflow id.
