### W6227 Assurance Case Readiness Gate

Readiness states:

- `assurance_case_ir_ready`
- `evidence_registry_ready`
- `claim_decomposition_ready`
- `defeater_tracking_ready`
- `operating_context_ready`
- `review_workflow_ready`
- `export_ready`
- `claim_gate_ready`
- `auditor_portal_ready`

Claim rules:

- Do not claim assurance without an approved assurance case.
- Do not claim safety case without safety-specific hazards, mitigations, and
  review evidence.
- Do not claim external assurance without external reviewer evidence.
- Do not hide open defeaters.

### W6228 Ultimate Assurance Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should compile proof into arguments buyers can trust.
- The best enterprise product is not only a pile of tests, reports, and
  receipts. It is a structured assurance system that turns evidence into
  scoped, reviewable, auditable claims with explicit limitations and open risks.
- This is how Kolm can sell high-stakes owned AI without sounding like generic
  AI governance software.

## Incentive-Compatible Marketplace, Data Economy, And Anti-Gaming Layer

Thesis:

- If Kolm succeeds, it will become an economy: tenants contribute private
  failures, experts label edge cases, artifact publishers sell packs, benchmark
  submitters chase leaderboard rank, reviewers approve assurance cases, and
  customers buy outcomes. Every economy gets gamed.
- Kolm should design incentives before the marketplace scales. Quality should
  pay more than volume, marginal risk reduction should pay more than redundant
  examples, honest uncertainty should pay more than confident overclaiming, and
  exploit reports should pay more than leaderboard theater.
- Mechanism design, peer prediction, Shapley-style contribution valuation, and
  reputation systems become product infrastructure when tied to K-Score,
  process ROI, assurance cases, benchmark anti-gaming, and artifact admission.

New primitive:

```text
IncentiveMechanismReceipt:
  mechanism_id
  participant_class
  contribution_type
  value_metric
  marginal_value_estimate
  quality_adjustment
  anti_gaming_checks
  sybil_resistance
  payout_rule
  reputation_delta
  dispute_window
  clawback_policy
  audit_refs
```

### W6300 Incentive Mechanism Registry

Objective:

- Register every economic rule that rewards or penalizes behavior in Kolm.

Mechanisms:

- data contribution bounty
- rare failure bounty
- label payment
- expert review payment
- benchmark prize
- artifact marketplace revenue share
- exploit report bounty
- assurance review credit
- tenant consortium credit
- savings-based success fee

Smoke simulation:

- A bounty pays for raw example count.
- Registry rejects it because it rewards volume without marginal value or
  quality adjustment.

### W6301 Marginal Value Estimator

Objective:

- Pay for contribution value, not contribution size.

Build:

- Estimate marginal lift to K-Score, risk reduction, calibration coverage,
  process ROI, subgroup coverage, red-team coverage, or assurance confidence.
- Use approximate Shapley, influence, counterfactual ablation, or holdout impact
  depending on cost.

Smoke simulation:

- 10,000 duplicate easy support tickets add no K-Score lift.
- Estimator assigns near-zero value despite high volume.

### W6302 Data Contribution Shapley Ledger

Objective:

- Track which data contributions improved artifacts.

Build:

- Record contribution id, source rights, privacy scope, affected artifact,
  estimated marginal value, uncertainty, payout, and revocation impact.
- Never expose raw tenant data to other tenants.

Smoke simulation:

- A tenant wants credit for failure cases that improved a vertical pack.
- Ledger shows aggregate marginal contribution without leaking examples.

### W6303 Peer-Prediction Label Incentives

Objective:

- Reward expert labels when ground truth is sparse.

Build:

- Use agreement, calibration examples, spot-checked gold labels, disagreement
  value, and peer-prediction-inspired scoring to reward effort and honesty.
- Penalize correlated low-effort labeling.

Smoke simulation:

- Reviewers agree by copying each other quickly.
- Mechanism detects low-effort correlation and withholds high-quality bonus.

### W6304 Rare Failure Bounty Market

Objective:

- Incentivize discovery of high-value failures.

Build:

- Pay by severity, novelty, reproducibility, blast radius, fixability, and
  downstream risk reduction.
- Deduplicate submissions by failure fingerprint.

Smoke simulation:

- Ten users submit same jailbreak.
- First reproducible report earns bounty; duplicates get small validation
  credit only.

### W6305 Benchmark Anti-Gaming Market Rules

Objective:

- Make leaderboard games expensive and visible.

Build:

- Use private holdouts, rotating tasks, contamination checks, submission limits,
  anomaly detection, provenance requirements, and post-hoc audits.
- Penalize benchmark-specific overfitting with reputation loss.

Smoke simulation:

- Artifact improves public score but fails private drift and red-team holdouts.
- Leaderboard suppresses rank and marks gaming risk.

### W6306 Artifact Seller Reputation Model

Objective:

- Make marketplace trust dynamic and evidence-based.

Signals:

- verified installs
- incident rate
- open defeaters
- stale evidence
- support response
- refunds
- security reports
- compatibility breaks
- customer outcome lift
- assurance review status

Smoke simulation:

- Seller has many downloads but poor incident response.
- Reputation falls despite popularity.

### W6307 Sybil Resistance And Identity Tiers

Objective:

- Prevent fake identities from manipulating bounties, reviews, and ratings.

Build:

- Identity tiers: anonymous, verified email, organization-verified,
  payment-verified, enterprise-verified, external-auditor-verified.
- Higher-risk actions require stronger identity.

Smoke simulation:

- New accounts rate an artifact highly after bounty dispute.
- Sybil detector reduces weight and opens review.

### W6308 Collusion Detector

Objective:

- Detect coordinated manipulation.

Patterns:

- reciprocal reviews
- circular purchases
- shared IP/device/account clusters
- synchronized benchmark submissions
- suspicious labeling agreement
- artifact fork rating rings
- bounty farming

Smoke simulation:

- Three publishers review each other's artifacts and downvote a competitor.
- Detector flags collusion and quarantines ratings.

### W6309 Payout Clawback And Dispute Engine

Objective:

- Recover rewards from fraudulent or harmful contributions.

Build:

- Define dispute window, evidence requirements, appeal flow, clawback triggers,
  seller reserve, and customer notification.

Smoke simulation:

- Dataset contribution later found to include restricted data.
- Ledger marks dependent payouts clawback-eligible and flags affected artifacts.

### W6310 Outcome-Based Pricing Guard

Objective:

- Make savings-based pricing incentive-compatible.

Build:

- Use causal receipts, finance-approved baselines, guardrail metrics, exclusions,
  cap/floor, audit window, and dispute process.
- Prevent Kolm from profiting by lowering quality or increasing abstention.

Smoke simulation:

- Spend falls because artifact refuses many cases.
- Pricing guard blocks success fee because completion and review load degraded.

### W6311 Tenant Consortium Credit System

Objective:

- Reward tenants who safely contribute aggregate learning.

Build:

- Credits based on marginal value of aggregate failure signatures, not raw data.
- Require privacy, rights, and clean-room compliance before credit.

Smoke simulation:

- Tenant contributes noisy aggregate that does not improve pack.
- Credit is low and explanation is visible.

### W6312 Expert Reviewer Quality Score

Objective:

- Rank reviewers by impact, not throughput.

Build:

- Measure calibration accuracy, disagreement usefulness, downstream K-Score
  lift, severe-failure catch rate, timeliness, and domain scope.

Smoke simulation:

- Reviewer approves many easy cases quickly but misses rare severe failures.
- Quality score stays low.

### W6313 Assurance Reviewer Incentives

Objective:

- Reward reviewers for finding real defeaters.

Build:

- Pay more for valid defeaters, evidence gaps, overbroad claims, stale proof,
  and safety hazards than for rubber-stamping.
- Track false objections and review latency.

Smoke simulation:

- Reviewer approves every case without comments.
- System gives no high-trust reviewer status.

### W6314 Artifact Marketplace Escrow

Objective:

- Protect buyers and sellers.

Build:

- Hold revenue share until install, smoke test, support window, and incident
  window pass.
- Release faster for sellers with strong reputation and low incident rate.

Smoke simulation:

- Artifact installs but fails customer's claimed runtime.
- Escrow holds payout and opens dispute.

### W6315 Reputation-Weighted Discovery

Objective:

- Rank artifacts by expected buyer value, not marketing.

Build:

- Discovery score combines evidence, fit, compatibility, price, reputation,
  assurance status, incidents, freshness, and tenant objective.
- Do not let paid placement override safety-critical warnings.

Smoke simulation:

- Sponsored artifact has weak assurance for healthcare.
- Discovery suppresses it for healthcare tenants.

### W6316 Mechanism Stress Simulator

Objective:

- Simulate strategic behavior before launching incentives.

Agents:

- honest contributor
- spam contributor
- colluding sellers
- benchmark overfitter
- bounty farmer
- low-effort labeler
- malicious artifact publisher
- enterprise buyer
- auditor

Smoke simulation:

- New bounty rewards examples with high severity tags.
- Simulator predicts severity inflation and recommends adjudication gates.

### W6317 Moral Hazard Detector

Objective:

- Detect when incentives cause risky behavior.

Examples:

- success fee encourages under-escalation
- label bounty encourages fabricated edge cases
- review payment encourages slow reviews
- seller revenue encourages broad unsupported claims
- benchmark prizes encourage overfitting

Smoke simulation:

- Seller broadens supported claims after ranking starts rewarding install count.
- Moral hazard detector requires stronger claim gate.

### W6318 Adverse Selection Defense

Objective:

- Prevent low-quality artifacts from dominating because buyers cannot inspect
  quality.

Build:

- Require evidence bundles, trial smoke tests, assurance cases, incident history,
  compatibility proofs, and refund/escrow terms.

Smoke simulation:

- New artifact has aggressive claims but no proof.
- Marketplace puts it in unverified tier, not default recommendations.

### W6319 Incentive-Compatible Data Deletion

Objective:

- Support deletion rights without creating dishonest contribution claims.

Build:

- If data is removed, recompute affected contribution credit where practical.
- Mark downstream packs as no-longer-benefiting or dependency-retained depending
  on legal and technical state.

Smoke simulation:

- Contributor requests deletion after receiving credit.
- Ledger updates credit and dependent artifact proof scope.

### W6320 Anti-Capture Governance

Objective:

- Prevent large participants from capturing standards, benchmarks, or
  marketplace policy.

Build:

- Cap influence by identity tier, buyer volume, contribution volume, and
  governance role.
- Require conflict disclosures and independent review for policy changes.

Smoke simulation:

- Major seller proposes benchmark weights favoring its artifact.
- Governance requires conflict review and independent validation.

### W6321 Reputation Portability And Exit

Objective:

- Let participants export reputation evidence without enabling forgery.

Build:

- Signed reputation statements include scope, time, evidence references,
  incidents, and limitations.
- Export does not reveal private buyer data.

Smoke simulation:

- Seller claims perfect reputation outside Kolm.
- Verifier shows scope-limited reputation and unresolved incidents.

### W6322 Market Manipulation Red-Team

Objective:

- Attack the economy directly.

Attacks:

- fake installs
- fake reviews
- benchmark laundering
- data poisoning for bounty
- collusive labels
- escrow evasion
- reputation reset
- dispute flooding
- evidence forgery

Smoke simulation:

- Fake installs inflate marketplace rank.
- Red-team expects discovery score to ignore unverified installs.

### W6323 Incentive K-Score Axis

Objective:

- Score whether the platform rewards the right behavior.

Components:

- marginal value alignment
- anti-gaming robustness
- sybil resistance
- review quality
- dispute fairness
- evidence quality
- buyer outcome alignment
- privacy-rights compliance

Smoke simulation:

- Marketplace grows revenue while incidents rise.
- Incentive K-Score fails even if ARR improves.

### W6324 Incentive Transparency Dashboard

Objective:

- Show participants why they earned or lost credit.

UX:

- value estimate
- evidence used
- uncertainty
- quality adjustment
- anti-gaming flags
- dispute status
- reputation delta
- payout state

Smoke simulation:

- Contributor sees a payout but not why it changed.
- Dashboard fails transparency requirement.

### W6325 Regulatory And Tax Export

Objective:

- Make incentives auditable for finance and regulators.

Build:

- Export payout ledger, identity tier, tax form state, clawbacks, disputes,
  cross-border restrictions, and evidence references.

Smoke simulation:

- Bounty payout crosses jurisdiction threshold.
- Export includes required tax and sanctions checks.

5. Add peer-prediction label scoring.

7. Add sybil and collusion detectors.
8. Add escrow and dispute engine.
9. Add mechanism stress simulator.
10. Add incentive claim/readiness gate.

### W6327 Incentive Readiness Gate

Readiness states:

- `mechanism_registry_ready`
- `marginal_value_ready`
- `contribution_ledger_ready`
- `label_incentives_ready`
- `seller_reputation_ready`
- `sybil_resistance_ready`
- `collusion_detection_ready`
- `escrow_dispute_ready`
- `stress_simulator_ready`

Claim rules:

- Do not claim fair payouts without marginal value estimation and dispute flow.
- Do not claim marketplace trust without seller reputation, sybil resistance,
  and incident history.
- Do not claim benchmark integrity without anti-gaming controls.
- Do not claim consortium value sharing without privacy and rights proof.

### W6328 Ultimate Incentive Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make the economics of owned AI align with the truth.
- Contributors should be rewarded for real marginal improvement, reviewers for
  finding real risk, sellers for durable customer outcomes, tenants for
  privacy-safe aggregate value, and Kolm for causal value delivered.
- The moat is not only technical. It is an incentive-compatible ecosystem where
  gaming is expensive, honest proof is valuable, and the best artifacts rise
  because they create verified outcomes.

## Synthetic Data Ecology, Model-Collapse Defense, And Diversity Preservation Layer

Thesis:

- Synthetic data is a power tool and a failure mode. It can fill rare cases,
  generate adversarial variants, rebalance domains, protect privacy, and improve
  distillation. It can also cause recursive model collapse, diversity loss,
  benchmark contamination, hidden teacher bias amplification, and false
  confidence.
- Kolm should treat synthetic data as an ecology with food chains, predators,
  invasive species, genetic diversity, lineage, and collapse risk. Every
  synthetic row needs ancestry, purpose, generator, validation, diversity score,
  collapse-risk score, and promotion boundary.
- The product advantage is not "generate more examples." It is "generate the
  smallest synthetic set that improves a measured failure without erasing real
  distribution tails."

New primitive:

```text
SyntheticEcologyReceipt:
  dataset_id
  synthetic_row_ids
  generator_artifacts
  human_anchor_refs
  real_data_anchor_ratio
  ancestry_depth
  diversity_metrics
  tail_coverage_metrics
  collapse_risk_score
  contamination_scan
  validation_protocol
  promotion_scope
  retirement_policy
```

### W6400 Synthetic Ancestry Ledger

Objective:

- Track the ancestry of every synthetic example.

Build:

- Store generator model, prompt, source examples, transformations, teacher
  outputs, human edits, validation results, and descendant datasets.
- Distinguish human-authored, human-edited synthetic, model-generated, model
  paraphrased, adversarially generated, and simulator-generated examples.

Smoke simulation:

- A synthetic extraction row is generated from another synthetic row and then
  paraphrased.
- Ledger records ancestry depth and marks it higher collapse risk than a
  human-anchored row.

### W6401 Real-Data Anchor Ratio

Objective:

- Prevent synthetic data from overwhelming real distribution anchors.

Build:

- Each dataset declares minimum real anchor ratio by task, tail slice, risk
  class, language, and vertical.
- Synthetic examples can augment but not replace required anchor mass.

Smoke simulation:

- Dataset has 95 percent synthetic easy cases and 5 percent real hard cases.
- Gate rejects training because tail anchors are too thin.

### W6402 Recursive Synthetic Depth Limit

Objective:

- Bound how many generations away a synthetic example can be from real evidence.

Build:

- Compute ancestry depth and generator chain.
- Allow deeper chains only for red-team probes or simulator tests, not for
  decision-grade training unless validated by real/human anchors.

Smoke simulation:

- Row generated from row generated from row generated from teacher output enters
  distillation.
- Depth limit blocks it from main training but allows it in adversarial eval.

### W6403 Tail Distribution Preservation Monitor

Objective:

- Detect when synthetic augmentation erases rare modes.

Build:

- Track rare entity types, languages, jurisdictions, failure modes, policy
  edges, schema edge cases, and human disagreement clusters.
- Compare tail mass before and after synthetic mixing.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic data improves average invoice extraction but removes rare vendor
  formats.
- Monitor blocks promotion.

### W6404 Diversity Collapse Score

Objective:

- Measure whether synthetic data reduces useful diversity.

Metrics:

- lexical diversity
- semantic cluster diversity
- schema path diversity
- evidence-source diversity
- process-variant diversity
- label entropy
- error-mode diversity
- embedding tail coverage

Smoke simulation:

- Generated support answers all share the same structure and policy phrasing.
- Diversity score fails despite high judge score.

### W6405 Synthetic Source Rights Gate

Objective:

- Ensure synthetic examples inherit source rights correctly.

Build:

- If synthetic data derives from restricted source, it carries derivative
  restrictions unless legal policy says otherwise.
- Track license, tenant boundary, consent, deletion, and marketplace export
  permissions.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic contract clauses derive from customer confidential contracts.
- Gate blocks public benchmark and marketplace pack use.

### W6406 Generator Bias Fingerprint

Objective:

- Measure bias introduced by the synthetic generator.

Build:

- Compare generator output against real anchors and alternative generators by
  style, label distribution, refusal pattern, protected-class proxy, language,
  and domain-specific reasoning.

Smoke simulation:

- Teacher overuses "needs review" for one jurisdiction.
- Bias fingerprint prevents synthetic rows from training that bias into student.

### W6407 Multi-Generator Council

Objective:

- Avoid one generator creating a monoculture.

Build:

- Use multiple generators, simulators, retrieval sources, and human reviewers.
- Require disagreement analysis and consensus only where appropriate.

Smoke simulation:

- One teacher generates all rare fraud examples.
- Council requires a second generator or human adjudication before training use.

### W6408 Synthetic Row Fitness Test

Objective:

- Validate each synthetic row against its intended purpose.

Fitness tests:

- schema validity
- evidence consistency
- rights validity
- tail novelty
- no benchmark contamination
- human plausibility
- adversarial usefulness
- process replay usefulness

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic row is novel but impossible under tenant ontology.
- Fitness test rejects it.

### W6409 Collapse-Aware Mixing Policy

Objective:

- Decide how much synthetic data can enter training.

Build:

- Mix by task, risk, tail slice, ancestry depth, validation strength, and real
  anchor ratio.
- Use synthetic data more aggressively for red-team and less for core factual
  behavior.

Smoke simulation:

- Redaction rare-case generation wants 70 percent synthetic.
- Policy allows it for eval stress set but limits train set until real anchors
  increase.

### W6410 Synthetic Data Quarantine

Objective:

- Keep experimental synthetic data from leaking into production training.

Build:

- Quarantine rows until ancestry, rights, contamination, fitness, diversity, and
  validation checks pass.
- Require explicit promotion receipt.

Smoke simulation:

- Developer exports simulator rows into distillation data manually.
- Quarantine scanner flags unpromoted synthetic rows.

### W6411 Synthetic Benchmark Firewall

Objective:

- Prevent synthetic training rows from contaminating evals.

Build:

- Track row ancestry against public and private benchmarks.
- Detect paraphrase, semantic equivalent, schema equivalent, and generated
  variants.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic benchmark-like row enters training after paraphrase.
- Firewall blocks decision-grade score.

### W6412 Human Anchor Acquisition Planner

Objective:

- Acquire real or expert-labeled anchors where synthetic uncertainty is high.

Build:

- Identify tail slices with high synthetic ratio, high value, high uncertainty,
  or high collapse risk.
- Recommend expert labels, customer examples, simulator validation, or withheld
  deployment.

Smoke simulation:

- Rare oncology prior-auth cases are all synthetic.
- Planner requests real expert-labeled anchors before local automation.

### W6413 Synthetic-To-Real Transfer Test

Objective:

- Prove synthetic improvements transfer to real data.

Build:

- Evaluate on real holdout, fresh customer sample, human adjudication, and
  process replay.
- Mark synthetic-only lift as experimental until transfer is proven.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic augmentation improves synthetic eval but not fresh real cases.
- Transfer test blocks production claim.

### W6414 Synthetic Diversity Active Learning

Objective:

- Generate synthetic examples only where diversity gaps matter.

Build:

- Use coverage gaps, failure clusters, process variants, ontology edges, and
  underrepresented languages to propose generation targets.
- Stop generation when marginal diversity value falls.

Smoke simulation:

- Generator keeps producing more common support refunds.
- Active learning redirects to rare billing dispute escalation cases.

### W6415 Synthetic Label Noise Estimator

Objective:

- Estimate noise introduced by generated labels.

Build:

- Compare teacher label, student prediction, human label, simulator truth, and
  ontology constraints.
- Store uncertainty and downweight noisy synthetic labels.

Smoke simulation:

- Teacher labels ambiguous contract clause inconsistently.
- Noise estimator routes those rows to human review or excludes them.

### W6416 Synthetic Style Decontaminator

Objective:

- Prevent students from learning generator style instead of task behavior.

Build:

- Randomize style, strip boilerplate, use style adversaries, and evaluate
  behavior under style perturbation.
- Penalize dependence on synthetic phrasing.

Smoke simulation:

- Student succeeds only on synthetic phrasing.
- Decontaminator generates real-style variants and fails promotion until robust.

### W6417 Synthetic Safety Boundary

Objective:

- Separate synthetic data for safety testing from synthetic data for training.

Build:

- Red-team prompts, exploit variants, and unsafe examples may belong in eval or
  refusal training but not in normal behavior imitation.
- Track allowed use per row.

Smoke simulation:

- Jailbreak examples are accidentally used as positive answer training.
- Boundary blocks the training job.

### W6418 Synthetic Data Watermark And Origin Tag

Objective:

- Mark synthetic examples so future systems do not treat them as real anchors.

Build:

- Add origin tags, hashes, watermarks where applicable, and detached provenance.
- Preserve tags through export, marketplace packs, and consortium learning.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic row exported to partner without origin tag.
- Gate blocks export.

### W6419 Synthetic Collapse Simulator

Objective:

- Simulate recursive training over generations before launch.

Build:

- Repeatedly train or approximate train/eval loops with increasing synthetic
  ratios.
- Measure diversity, tail coverage, K-Score, memorization, and error-mode
  collapse across generations.

Smoke simulation:

- Three generations of synthetic augmentation improve average score but collapse
  rare jurisdiction coverage.
- Simulator blocks recurrent generation policy.

### W6420 Synthetic Ecology Dashboard

Objective:

- Make dataset ecology visible.

UX:

- real anchor ratio
- synthetic ratio
- ancestry depth
- diversity score
- tail coverage
- collapse risk
- rights restrictions
- benchmark contamination
- transfer proof

Smoke simulation:

- Account says dataset is train-ready without showing synthetic ratio.
- Dashboard fails readiness.

### W6421 Synthetic Data Retirement Policy

Objective:

- Retire synthetic rows that become harmful, stale, or redundant.

Build:

- Retire by low marginal value, source rights change, generator bug, policy
  drift, tail overrepresentation, contamination, or collapse risk.

Smoke simulation:

- Old synthetic rows encode outdated policy.
- Retirement policy removes them and triggers affected artifact recalibration.

### W6422 Synthetic Consortium Learning Guard

Objective:

- Prevent cross-tenant synthetic data from laundering private facts.

Build:

- Aggregate only patterns, not examples, unless rights and privacy allow.
- Run memorization, uniqueness, and reidentification probes on synthetic rows.

Smoke simulation:

- Synthetic example contains rare tenant-specific phrase.
- Guard blocks consortium export.

### W6423 Collapse-Resistant K-Score Axis

Objective:

- Add model-collapse resistance to quality scoring.

Components:

- tail preservation
- real-anchor dependency
- diversity preservation
- transfer to real holdout
- ancestry depth
- recursive-generation stability
- synthetic label noise
- benchmark firewall status

Smoke simulation:

- Artifact improves average K-Score but loses rare tail behavior.
- Collapse-resistant axis blocks promotion.

### W6424 Synthetic Claim Gate

Objective:

- Prevent overclaiming synthetic-data wins.

Rules:

- "Improved by synthetic data" requires real holdout transfer.
- "Privacy-safe synthetic" requires memorization and rights probes.
- "Rare-case coverage" requires tail metrics, not example count.
- "No model collapse risk" is forbidden; say risk measured and bounded.

Smoke simulation:

- Blog claims synthetic data solved rare claims cases using synthetic-only eval.
- Gate blocks claim.

### W6425 Synthetic Data Marketplace Badge

Objective:

- Let users trust synthetic dataset packs.

Badges:

- ancestry-tracked
- real-anchor-validated
- rights-scoped
- transfer-proven
- collapse-risk-measured
- benchmark-firewalled
- human-reviewed
- consortium-safe

Smoke simulation:

- Dataset pack has generated examples but no real transfer proof.
- Marketplace shows synthetic-experimental, not production-grade.

### W6426 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Add `SyntheticEcologyReceipt`.
2. Add synthetic ancestry ledger.
3. Add real-anchor ratio gate.
4. Add diversity and tail monitors.
5. Add synthetic rights gate.
6. Add row fitness tests.
7. Add collapse-aware mixing policy.
8. Add benchmark firewall.
9. Add synthetic-to-real transfer tests.
10. Add collapse-resistant K-Score axis and claim gate.

Suggested implementation files:

```text
src/synthetic-ecology-receipt.js
src/synthetic-ancestry-ledger.js
src/real-anchor-ratio-gate.js
src/synthetic-diversity-monitor.js
src/synthetic-rights-gate.js
src/synthetic-row-fitness.js
src/collapse-aware-mixing-policy.js
src/synthetic-benchmark-firewall.js
src/synthetic-transfer-test.js
src/synthetic-claim-gate.js
tests/wave6400-synthetic-ancestry-ledger.test.js
tests/wave6401-real-anchor-ratio-gate.test.js
tests/wave6402-collapse-aware-mixing.test.js
tests/wave6403-synthetic-transfer-test.test.js
```

### W6427 Synthetic Ecology Readiness Gate

Readiness states:

- `ancestry_ledger_ready`
- `real_anchor_ratio_ready`
- `diversity_monitor_ready`
- `collapse_risk_ready`
- `rights_gate_ready`
- `fitness_tests_ready`
- `benchmark_firewall_ready`
- `transfer_test_ready`
- `synthetic_claim_gate_ready`

Claim rules:

- Do not train decision-grade artifacts on synthetic rows without ancestry,
  rights, fitness, and transfer checks.
- Do not claim rare-case coverage without tail preservation metrics.
- Do not claim synthetic privacy without memorization and reidentification
  probes.
- Do not hide synthetic ratio in benchmark or product claims.

### W6428 Ultimate Synthetic Ecology Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make synthetic data safe enough to compound without poisoning the
  future.
- The strongest system is not one that generates the most rows. It is one that
  preserves real-world tails, knows every row's ancestry, prevents recursive
  collapse, proves transfer to real holdouts, respects rights, and retires
  synthetic data when it stops helping.
- This turns synthetic data from a cheap shortcut into a controlled scientific
  instrument for distillation.

## Machine Unlearning, Deletion Propagation, And Revocable Artifact Compiler Layer

This layer converts deletion from a legal support ticket into a product primitive.

Machine unlearning research is converging on a hard truth: deleting a row is not
the same as deleting its influence. SISA-style training reduces retraining cost
by limiting where each record can influence parameters. Certified data removal
defines a stronger target: the post-removal model should be difficult to
distinguish from a model trained without the deleted data. Modern LLM unlearning
benchmarks such as TOFU show why this is still unsolved: many baselines can
reduce surface recall while failing the deeper "as if never learned" standard.
Policy work also warns that unlearning is not a universal solution for
generative-AI behavior, copyright, or safety. Kolm should use this reality as an
advantage: make every deletion claim scoped, measured, and receipt-backed.

Kolm's product problem is harder than ordinary model unlearning because Kolm
creates derivatives. A captured call can influence a teacher council decision, a
student architecture, a synthetic rare-case row, a K-Score calibration bin, a
runtime profile, a marketplace artifact, a customer audit export, and an edge
device cache. The best product is therefore not just a model-level unlearner. It
is a derivative-influence compiler that can answer:

```text
Given a removal request, which artifacts, students, indexes, evals, synthetic
descendants, receipts, deployments, marketplace packages, and claims are still
valid, which must be rebuilt, which can be approximately unlearned, and which
must be revoked?
```

New primitive: `UnlearningReceipt`.

Required fields:

- `request_id`: stable deletion, revocation, poisoning, or legal-hold request.
- `request_basis`: privacy deletion, contractual deletion, copyright dispute,
  tenant offboarding, poisoning emergency, data correction, or safety removal.
- `subject_scope`: user, tenant, dataset row, document span, synthetic lineage,
  feature, label, concept, route, marketplace package, or derived artifact.
- `source_records`: canonical IDs and hashes of the affected captures, files,
  annotations, eval rows, synthetic rows, embeddings, and caches.
- `influence_graph`: artifacts, students, teachers, indexes, evals, calibration
  sets, receipts, output logs, and deployments reachable from the source.
- `method`: exact rebuild, SISA shard/slice replay, approximate gradient
  unlearning, model edit, retrieval/index deletion, synthetic taint retirement,
  output suppression, or full revocation.
- `proof_target`: exact, certified, approximate, behavioral suppression, index
  deletion, or claim revocation.
- `verification_suite`: membership-inference probes, targeted recall probes,
  TOFU-style forget/retain checks, K-Score regression checks, canary prompts,
  retrieval probes, output-diff probes, and downstream task holdouts.
- `residual_risk`: what may still remain, what was not measured, and what cannot
  be claimed.
- `supersedes`: prior artifacts, receipts, benchmark reports, marketplace
  listings, or public claims invalidated by the request.
- `legal_hold_conflicts`: records that cannot be destroyed because another
  obligation requires retention, with policy owner and expiry.
- `edge_sync_status`: which offline devices or customer deployments have pulled
  the revocation manifest.
- `claim_scope`: a public-safe statement such as "retrieval and derived artifact
  influence removed; foundation teacher weights not modified."

Strategic insight:

- OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Meta can talk about model-level safety and
  policy. Kolm can own artifact-level revocability.
- Enterprises do not only ask, "Can the base model forget?" They ask, "Which of
  our production artifacts, audits, devices, indexes, and reports are now tainted
  and what do I do by Friday?"
- A deletion compiler becomes a buying trigger for regulated customers because
  it converts privacy, copyright, poisoning, and tenant-offboarding events into a
  bounded operational plan.

### W6500 Unlearning Request Contract

Build an unlearning intake contract that separates request basis from technical
action.

Request classes:

- Privacy deletion: a data subject or customer contract requires removal.
- Copyright or license revocation: content was later disputed or license scope
  changed.
- Poisoning emergency: a discovered malicious capture must be removed before it
  spreads through distillation.
- Correction: a record is false and must be replaced, not merely hidden.
- Tenant offboarding: all tenant-derived artifacts must be revoked or exported
  according to the contract.
- Safety deprecation: a behavior, tool call, or policy decision is no longer
  acceptable.

Why this matters:

- A privacy deletion may allow legal-hold retention of audit metadata.
- A copyright dispute may require output suppression while preserving internal
  evidence.
- A poisoning emergency requires blast-radius-first scheduling.
- A correction should produce a superseding artifact rather than silently
  degrading quality.

Implementation contract:

- The request object must be immutable after creation.
- Every field must be hashable and signable.
- Every downstream action must point back to the request.
- The product must never show "deleted" without specifying whether it means raw
  data deletion, index deletion, approximate unlearning, exact rebuild, output
  suppression, or artifact revocation.

### W6501 Data Influence Graph

Invent the Kolm Data Influence Graph.

Nodes:

- capture event
- source document
- annotation
- synthetic row
- teacher completion
- distillation batch
- student checkpoint
- K-Score run
- calibration bin
- benchmark report
- embedding vector
- retrieval index segment
- runtime profile
- compiled .kolm artifact
- marketplace package
- deployment target
- audit export
- generated output receipt

Edges:

- `trained_from`
- `distilled_from`
- `evaluated_by`
- `calibrated_by`
- `synthetically_derived_from`
- `embedded_into`
- `cached_in`
- `served_by`
- `marketed_as`
- `superseded_by`
- `revoked_by`

This graph is the core invention because it turns deletion into graph
compilation. SISA constrains influence inside training. Kolm should constrain
influence across the full artifact lifecycle.

Data structure:

```text
InfluenceEdge {
  source_hash
  target_hash
  edge_type
  strength_estimate
  proof_level
  created_at
  retention_class
  unlearning_policy
}
```

Scoring:

- `direct_influence`: the source was in the training/eval/index set.
- `derived_influence`: the source produced a synthetic row, model answer, or
  calibration target.
- `behavioral_influence`: the source changed outputs but no exact derivative can
  be localized.
- `claim_influence`: the source supported a metric or marketing statement.

### W6502 Artifact Impact Classifier

The impact classifier maps each affected graph node to an action:

- no action
- metadata correction
- raw deletion
- index tombstone
- cache purge
- exact shard replay
- approximate unlearning
- eval recalculation
- calibration recalibration
- artifact rebuild
- output receipt supersession
- marketplace delisting
- full revocation

Decision features:

- regulatory basis
- model size
- artifact criticality
- influence strength
- proof target
- affected tenant count
- edge/offline deployment count
- available checkpoints
- SISA shard availability
- cost of full rebuild
- residual K-Score risk
- contractual SLA

Product output:

```text
delete request D-1842
affected artifacts: 7
exact rebuild: 2
approximate unlearning: 1
index tombstone: 3
marketplace delisting: 1
estimated completion: 3h 12m
residual claim: "raw and retrieval influence removed; base teacher unchanged"
```

### W6503 Exact Rebuild Path

Exact rebuild remains the gold standard for small enough artifacts.

Kolm should keep an exact rebuild path for:

- critical regulated artifacts
- medical, insurance, and financial decision support
- customer-specific students with bounded datasets
- artifacts in legal dispute
- benchmark or certification evidence
- high-risk marketplace packages

Build requirement:

- The artifact build recipe must be deterministic enough to rebuild from the
  retained allowed set.
- All random seeds, package versions, teacher model versions, prompts, eval
  versions, and calibration splits must be captured.
- The rebuild must produce a new signed artifact and mark the old one
  superseded, not mutate history.

Why this is commercially powerful:

- Competitors can claim "we removed the data." Kolm can produce a before/after
  artifact chain, a rebuild manifest, and a measurable delta.

### W6504 SISA And Slice-Aware Training Strategy

SISA research shows that sharding, isolating, slicing, and aggregating can reduce
unlearning cost by localizing data influence. Kolm should adapt that idea for
distillation.

Kolm-specific SISA design:

- shard by tenant, route, task family, language, risk class, and data-rights
  class
- slice by time and curriculum stage
- checkpoint after each slice
- train student ensembles or adapter banks instead of one monolithic student
  when revocability matters
- keep a shard/slice manifest inside the .kolm artifact

Distillation-specific improvement:

- Separate "teacher behavior capture" shards from "student compression" shards.
- If a removal request only affects a teacher trace used for calibration, do not
  retrain the full student until impact exceeds threshold.
- If it affects a rare-case curriculum slice, regenerate that slice and replay
  only downstream student phases.

Tradeoff:

- SISA can cost some accuracy or training efficiency.
- Kolm should expose "revocability level" as an artifact build option:
  `max_quality`, `balanced`, `revocable`, `regulated`.

### W6505 Approximate Unlearning Planner

Approximate unlearning is necessary when full retraining is impossible, but it
must never be marketed as exact.

Candidate methods:

- gradient ascent on forget set with retain regularization
- noisy SGD with certified or approximate guarantees when assumptions match
- influence-function updates for convex or near-linear heads
- model editing for localized factual associations
- adapter replacement for modular students
- LoRA delta subtraction when training deltas are well isolated
- output suppression for behavior-level emergency mitigation

Planner output:

- method selected
- assumptions required
- expected utility loss
- expected forget strength
- verification suite
- fallback if verification fails

Decision rule:

```text
if exact_rebuild_cost <= SLA_budget and risk >= regulated:
  exact rebuild
elif SISA_slice_available:
  slice replay
elif modular_adapter_available:
  adapter replacement
elif verification_target == behavior_suppression:
  approximate unlearning plus output guard
else:
  revoke artifact
```

### W6506 Embedding And Retrieval Index Deletion

For many enterprise AI systems, the fastest and most legally meaningful removal
is not model-weight unlearning. It is retrieval/index deletion.

Kolm should treat indexes as first-class artifacts:

- vector embedding ID
- source document hash
- chunk hash
- retriever version
- index segment
- cache residency
- downstream RAG artifact dependency
- last query time
- delete tombstone

Deletion steps:

1. Tombstone source chunks.
2. Remove vectors from active index.
3. Rebuild affected index segments if the ANN backend cannot guarantee hard
   deletion.
4. Purge reranker caches and prompt caches.
5. Re-run retrieval probes that previously returned the target material.
6. Supersede outputs that cited or copied the removed content.

Metric:

- `retrieval_residue_rate`: percentage of deletion probes that still retrieve
  target or near-target content.

### W6507 Synthetic Descendant Taint

Synthetic data makes deletion harder because the source may no longer be visible
in the derived row.

Rules:

- Every synthetic row must carry ancestry to source examples, generator model,
  prompt, seed, and fitness test.
- If a source is removed, descendants are not automatically safe.
- A descendant can be retained only if it passes independence tests and the
  request basis allows transformation retention.
- Synthetic rows that preserve unique phrases, rare facts, or private
  attributes are tainted.

New metric:

- `synthetic_taint_escape_rate`: percentage of removed-source facts still
  recoverable from synthetic descendants.

Product moat:

- Most AI systems cannot answer whether synthetic training data inherited a
  deleted record. Kolm can because W6400 ancestry and this layer become one
  compiler.

### W6508 Calibration And Evaluation Set Deletion

If deleted data appears in holdouts, K-Score itself may become tainted.

Actions:

- remove or replace affected eval rows
- recalculate K-Score baselines
- invalidate benchmark reports that used the deleted rows
- mark public claims superseded when confidence intervals change
- preserve old reports only as audit history with revoked status

Important distinction:

- A model can be valid while its score is invalid.
- A score can be valid while a marketing claim is invalid.
- A benchmark can be reproducible while no longer legally usable.

### W6509 Output And Receipt Supersession

Production outputs are derivatives too.

Kolm should not pretend it can erase outputs already delivered to customers.
Instead, it should support receipt supersession:

- original output receipt
- affected source or artifact
- new policy status
- customer notification class
- replacement output if available
- downstream system webhook
- audit export

For regulated users, this creates operational clarity:

- "These 312 outputs were produced using artifact A before deletion request
  D-1842."
- "42 outputs copied target content and require customer notification."
- "270 outputs are unaffected under current probes."

### W6510 Unlearning Verification Probe

Unlearning without verification is theater.

Probe classes:

- membership inference regression
- canary extraction
- target completion recall
- paraphrase recall
- entity association recall
- TOFU-style forget-set question answering
- retain-set utility
- neighbor concept utility
- RAG retrieval probe
- synthetic descendant probe
- output-diff probe
- K-Score regression

Every `UnlearningReceipt` must include:

- pre-unlearning score
- post-unlearning score
- threshold
- confidence interval
- failed probes
- unmeasured surfaces

Product copy rule:

- Do not say "forgotten" unless the receipt says what was tested and what
  remains untested.

### W6511 Membership Inference Regression

Membership inference is not sufficient proof of forgetting, but it is a required
privacy regression.

Kolm should run membership probes before and after:

- raw model logits where available
- teacher/student output likelihood
- embedding-neighbor leakage
- prompt-cache hits
- synthetic descendant similarity
- per-tenant student adapters

Metric:

- `membership_advantage_delta`: reduction in membership inference advantage on
  deleted data, measured against retain and nonmember controls.

Claim scope:

- "Membership risk reduced under these probes" is honest.
- "Data is impossible to recover" is not honest unless proven under a stronger
  formal framework.

### W6512 Residual Memorization Scanner

Generative models can stop answering direct prompts while still leaking through
paraphrases, fragments, or role-play.

The residual scanner should generate:

- direct recall prompts
- partial quote completions
- paraphrased entity prompts
- multilingual prompts
- tool-use prompts
- jailbreak-style prompts
- retrieval-augmented prompts
- neighboring concept prompts

The scanner must separate:

- target recall
- benign world knowledge
- neighboring-topic degradation
- refusal overblocking
- hallucinated replacement knowledge

Metric:

- `residual_memorization_area`: integrated leakage across direct, paraphrase,
  multilingual, and adversarial probes.

### W6513 Legal Hold Conflict Resolver

Deletion requests often conflict with audit, abuse, billing, security, or
litigation retention obligations.

Build a resolver that can produce:

- destroy raw content
- retain hashed metadata
- retain encrypted escrow
- retain audit-only record
- suppress training and serving use
- delay destruction until legal hold expires
- export evidence to customer

The product should show:

```text
raw content: destroyed
training use: revoked
serving use: revoked
audit metadata: retained until 2027-05-01 under legal hold L-17
```

This prevents vague compliance claims and reduces enterprise legal friction.

### W6514 Tenant-Scoped Unlearning SLA

Unlearning must have service levels by risk and artifact class.

Example tiers:

- `index_delete`: minutes
- `cache_purge`: minutes
- `receipt_supersession`: hours
- `adapter_unlearn`: hours
- `SISA_slice_replay`: hours to days
- `exact_rebuild`: days
- `foundation_teacher`: not controlled by Kolm; route to provider or suppress
  downstream use

Account UX should show:

- pending requests
- affected artifacts
- ETA
- proof target
- verification status
- residual risk
- required customer action

### W6515 Marketplace Pack Revocation

If Kolm has an artifact marketplace, unlearning must extend to packages already
downloaded.

Required features:

- revocation manifest
- transparency log entry
- package version supersession
- customer notification webhook
- offline grace period
- signature status change
- compatibility migration path

Marketplace status vocabulary:

- `active`
- `deprecated`
- `superseded`
- `revoked`
- `legal_hold`
- `do_not_train`
- `do_not_serve`

Hard rule:

- A revoked marketplace pack must fail verification by default unless a customer
  explicitly runs in historical/audit mode.

### W6516 Federated And Consortium Unlearning

Federated distillation and consortium learning require distributed deletion.

Protocol:

1. Issue signed unlearning request.
2. Identify participating clients or consortium members.
3. Compute local influence reports without exposing raw data.
4. Run local index deletion, adapter replay, or slice replay.
5. Return signed proof summaries.
6. Aggregate proof without leaking member-private data.
7. Update global artifact receipt and consortium ledger.

Difficult cases:

- a member is offline
- a member refuses deletion
- a member has legal hold
- a member cannot reveal whether it had the record
- a derived global model cannot localize influence

Kolm advantage:

- The product can provide a consortium "unlearning completeness score" instead
  of pretending distributed deletion is binary.

### W6517 Edge And Offline Revocation Sync

Enterprise models run on phones, laptops, medical devices, robots, and
air-gapped deployments. Revocation must reach them.

Build:

- compact revocation manifests
- delta bundles for edge artifacts
- signed tombstones
- offline grace policies
- audit mode for stale devices
- device check-in receipt
- blocked artifact execution after critical revocation

User-facing state:

- "Artifact A was revoked because D-1842 affected its rare-case curriculum."
- "142 of 149 devices have applied the revocation."
- "7 offline devices are past grace period and must stop serving."

### W6518 Unlearning K-Score Axis

K-Score should gain an unlearning axis.

Subscores:

- forget-set suppression
- retain-set utility
- neighboring-domain stability
- membership-risk reduction
- retrieval residue
- synthetic descendant residue
- calibration integrity
- artifact supersession completeness
- edge sync completeness
- claim-scope honesty

The score should not reward overforgetting. A model that refuses all related
questions may look safe but destroy utility. The axis must penalize both
underforgetting and collateral damage.

Formula sketch:

```text
UnlearningScore =
  ForgetStrength
  * RetainUtility
  * NeighborStability
  * ProofCoverage
  * ClaimHonesty
  - ResidualLeakagePenalty
  - CollateralDamagePenalty
```

### W6519 Cost And Blast-Radius Planner

Deletion has cost. The product should make that cost visible before action.

Planner outputs:

- affected artifacts
- affected customers
- exact rebuild cost
- approximate unlearning cost
- serving downtime
- K-Score risk
- legal risk
- marketplace risk
- edge sync risk
- recommended path

Optimization target:

```text
minimize total risk-adjusted cost
subject to proof target, SLA, regulatory basis, and customer contract.
```

This turns unlearning into an executive decision surface instead of an
engineering scramble.

### W6520 Unlearning Audit Export

Auditors need evidence, not dashboards.

Export package:

- request
- policy basis
- influence graph summary
- affected artifacts
- actions taken
- verification probes
- pre/post metrics
- residual risks
- superseded claims
- legal-hold conflicts
- signed receipt chain
- human approvals

Formats:

- JSON
- CSV
- PDF
- SIEM event stream
- OpenTelemetry event
- governance API envelope

### W6521 Account Readiness Surface

Post-auth account should show a readiness contract:

- "Can you delete raw captured data?"
- "Can you delete vectors and RAG chunks?"
- "Can you revoke compiled artifacts?"
- "Can you exact-rebuild regulated artifacts?"
- "Can you approximate-unlearn large students?"
- "Can you verify forget/retain behavior?"
- "Can you sync revocation to devices?"
- "Can you produce auditor-ready receipts?"

Each capability should be tagged:

- shipped
- implemented
- requires package release
- requires customer config
- requires external provider
- benchmark-scoped
- not available

### W6522 Poisoned-Data Emergency Unlearning

Poisoning response needs a faster path than ordinary privacy deletion.

Emergency flow:

1. Quarantine source and descendants.
2. Freeze affected artifacts.
3. Stop marketplace downloads.
4. Disable routes using affected students.
5. Purge indexes and caches.
6. Run blast-radius scan.
7. Rebuild or revoke artifacts.
8. Publish internal incident receipt.

Priority:

- safety and containment first
- exact proof second
- customer communication third

The product should distinguish "contained" from "fully unlearned."

### W6523 Model Edit Versus Retrain Decision

Knowledge editing, concept erasure, behavioral suppression, and unlearning are
not the same thing.

Decision table:

- Use model editing for localized factual correction when legal deletion is not
  the basis.
- Use output suppression for emergency safety behavior while planning a rebuild.
- Use exact rebuild when a regulated deletion claim must be strong.
- Use SISA replay when a revocable training structure exists.
- Use approximate unlearning when the proof target permits residual risk and
  verification passes.
- Use revocation when no honest method can satisfy the claim.

This avoids the most dangerous product failure: claiming legal deletion when the
system only changed visible behavior.

### W6524 Privacy And Utility Tradeoff Ledger

Every unlearning action trades privacy, utility, cost, and time.

Ledger columns:

- method
- proof strength
- privacy risk reduction
- retain utility
- collateral damage
- latency impact
- cost
- wall-clock time
- customer-visible downtime
- rollback possibility
- auditor acceptability

This ledger becomes proprietary data. Over thousands of requests, Kolm learns
which methods work for which artifact types, model sizes, domains, and deletion
bases. That becomes a moat competitors cannot clone from papers.

### W6525 Unlearning Benchmark Suite

Kolm should publish an unlearning benchmark suite, but keep enterprise
operational telemetry private.

Public benchmark tasks:

- TOFU-style fictitious profile forgetting
- private document span deletion
- RAG index deletion
- synthetic descendant taint removal
- artifact exact rebuild
- SISA slice replay
- membership inference regression
- retain-set utility
- edge revocation sync
- output receipt supersession

Metrics:

- forget strength
- retain utility
- collateral damage
- residual retrieval rate
- membership advantage
- synthetic residue
- rebuild cost
- proof coverage
- claim honesty

Public position:

- "Kolm does not claim universal forgetting. Kolm measures and receipts the
  exact deletion surface each artifact can support."

### W6526 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Add `UnlearningReceipt` schema to the research/product contract.
2. Add influence graph edges to capture, distillation, eval, synthetic data,
   index, artifact, and deployment records.
3. Add artifact action classifier.
4. Add retrieval/index deletion proof first because it is easiest to verify.
5. Add output receipt supersession.
6. Add exact rebuild and SISA manifests for new regulated artifacts.
7. Add approximate unlearning planner only after verification probes exist.
8. Add account readiness and audit export.
9. Add marketplace revocation manifest.
10. Add edge/offline revocation sync.

Implementation agent deliverables:

- JSON schema for `UnlearningReceipt`.
- `GET /v1/unlearning/readiness`.
- `POST /v1/unlearning/requests`.
- `GET /v1/unlearning/requests/:id`.
- `POST /v1/unlearning/requests/:id/plan`.
- `POST /v1/unlearning/requests/:id/execute`.
- `GET /v1/unlearning/requests/:id/receipt`.
- CLI: `kolm unlearn plan`, `kolm unlearn execute`,
  `kolm unlearn verify`, `kolm unlearn export`.
- Account page: Unlearning readiness, open requests, proof status, residual
  risk, and affected artifacts.
- Test suite: deletion graph, index purge, synthetic taint, receipt
  supersession, membership probes, and claim-scope audit.

### W6527 Unlearning Readiness Gate

This layer must add a hard verifier before public claims.

Verifier requirements:

- every artifact has a declared unlearning policy
- every source record has at least one influence edge or explicit exemption
- every synthetic row has ancestry
- every index segment supports deletion or rebuild
- every marketplace package has a revocation path
- every output receipt can be superseded
- every approximate unlearning claim has forget/retain probes
- every exact deletion claim has rebuild evidence
- every legal-hold conflict is visible
- every user-facing claim names the proof level

Failing examples:

- "Data deleted" with no proof target.
- "Model forgot" with no retain-set utility test.
- "Compliant deletion" when only the vector index was purged.
- "Revoked artifact" while edge devices can still run it silently.

### W6528 Ultimate Unlearning Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make AI artifacts revocable by design.
- The product should not promise magical forgetting. It should prove, with a
  signed receipt, which influence paths were removed, rebuilt, suppressed,
  superseded, or revoked.
- The strongest enterprise position is: "When data rights change, Kolm tells you
  every artifact, eval, index, output, device, and claim affected by that change,
  then produces a verifiable closeout plan."

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  tenant = hospital-a
  deletion request = remove patient p-1842 oncology note
  affected graph:
    1 capture event
    4 chunks
    4 embeddings
    2 synthetic rare-case rows
    1 redaction eval row
    1 student adapter
    1 .kolm artifact
    37 output receipts
expected plan:
  purge 4 vectors
  retire 2 synthetic rows
  recalc redaction K-Score
  replay adapter slice
  supersede 1 artifact
  notify owners of 3 copied-output receipts
  mark 34 receipts unaffected by probes
  emit UnlearningReceipt with residual risk
pass condition:
  retrieval_residue_rate = 0
  target_completion_recall below threshold
  retain K-Score delta within tolerance
  all affected outputs classified
  artifact verification rejects old package outside audit mode
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it turns a painful governance problem
into a defensible artifact compiler advantage: competitors can offer deletion
forms, while Kolm can offer deletion impact compilation, proof, and revocation.

## Insurable AI, Warranty, Reserve, And Risk-Transfer Compiler Layer

This layer converts Kolm's proof spine into a commercial risk product.

Enterprise AI adoption is increasingly constrained by liability, regulatory
uncertainty, coverage gaps, model errors, privacy leakage, hallucinations,
biased outputs, agentic failures, and copyright exposure. AI-specific insurance
providers now exist because ordinary cyber, technology E&O, professional
liability, and general liability policies do not cleanly cover generative and
agentic AI risk. NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and continuous-assurance research
all point in the same direction: buyers need ongoing evidence, not one-time
certificates. Kolm already has the raw ingredients: K-Score, route receipts,
assurance cases, artifact lineage, unlearning receipts, runtime profiles, eval
history, policy scopes, and output receipts.

The invention is `RiskTransferCompiler`: a system that turns measurable artifact
risk into warranty scope, customer SLA language, internal reserve levels,
insurance-underwriting packets, and risk-adjusted pricing.

Core thesis:

- If Kolm can measure artifact risk better than insurers, procurement teams, and
  competitors, Kolm can sell not just software but confidence.
- The next frontier is not "AI is safe." It is "This exact artifact, for this
  exact use condition, has this measured loss envelope, this excluded behavior,
  this monitoring plan, this reserve, this warranty scope, and this escalation
  path."
- That converts K-Score from a quality metric into an economic instrument.

New primitive: `RiskTransferReceipt`.

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `use_case`
- `tenant_scope`
- `risk_class`
- `covered_failure_modes`
- `excluded_failure_modes`
- `k_score_state`
- `conformal_risk_state`
- `assurance_case_id`
- `incident_history`
- `unlearning_readiness`
- `runtime_profile`
- `human_review_policy`
- `monitoring_obligations`
- `warranty_scope`
- `service_credit_scope`
- `reserve_estimate`
- `insurance_packet_hash`
- `evidence_freshness`
- `claim_language`

Commercial unlock:

- Free and Pro tiers show risk status but provide no warranty.
- Team can get artifact-scoped service credits for platform failures.
- Enterprise can get negotiated warranty scope, risk-transfer packets, and
  insurer-ready evidence.
- Regulated verticals can pay for artifact risk underwriting as a premium
  feature.

### W6600 RiskTransferIR Schema

Define a neutral intermediate representation for artifact risk transfer.

Fields:

- artifact behavior boundary
- intended use
- prohibited use
- user population
- jurisdiction
- decision impact
- automation level
- human review requirement
- model/provider stack
- data category
- output type
- K-Score card
- risk-control envelope
- monitoring controls
- customer obligations
- Kolm obligations
- excluded risks
- covered risks
- evidence freshness

The IR must be generated from product evidence, not sales copy.

Acceptance:

- A risk-transfer packet cannot exist without a signed artifact receipt.
- A warranty cannot cover a failure mode that is not measured.
- A service credit cannot cover an outcome the customer controls.

### W6601 Failure-Mode Loss Taxonomy

Translate AI failures into loss categories.

Loss categories:

- incorrect factual output
- schema-invalid output
- citation unsupported output
- privacy leakage
- IP or license exposure
- discriminatory or unfair output
- agent failed to escalate
- tool action exceeded authority
- medical/financial/legal high-impact error
- safety policy violation
- customer misconfiguration
- provider outage
- model drift
- stale evidence or stale retrieval
- revocation not applied
- marketplace artifact defect

For each loss category, define:

- detectability
- preventability
- customer dependency
- Kolm dependency
- third-party provider dependency
- likely severity
- required evidence
- insurability
- exclusion language

Why this matters:

- Insurance and warranty fail when failure modes are vague.
- Kolm can map every product proof to a financial consequence.

### W6602 Artifact Loss Envelope

Create a loss envelope for each artifact.

Inputs:

- K-Score by slice
- critical-fail rate
- conformal risk upper bound
- observed production incidents
- human-review volume
- route fallback rate
- provider drift history
- deployment surface
- data rights risk
- unlearning readiness
- output receipt volume

Outputs:

- expected annual incident count
- severe incident bound
- maximum credible loss
- uncertainty interval
- required reserve
- monitoring frequency
- warranty eligibility

Formula sketch:

```text
ExpectedArtifactLoss =
  sum_over_failure_modes(
    exposure_volume
    * calibrated_failure_probability
    * severity_distribution
    * control_effectiveness
    * evidence_freshness_discount
  )
```

### W6603 Covered-Use Boundary Compiler

Warranty depends on use.

The same artifact can be covered for:

- summarizing support tickets
- extracting invoice fields
- triaging low-risk claims

and excluded for:

- autonomous denial of insurance coverage
- diagnosis
- legal advice
- lending decision
- employment decision

Build a compiler that emits:

- covered use
- excluded use
- human-review threshold
- required configuration
- prohibited prompts or tools
- required logging
- required monitoring
- invalidators

This should be machine-readable so runtime can refuse out-of-scope use.

### W6604 Warranty Scope Generator

Generate warranty language from measured evidence.

Do not generate broad warranties. Generate artifact-specific terms:

- "Kolm warrants the artifact will meet the attached schema validity threshold
  under the covered test distribution."
- "Kolm does not warrant medical correctness, legal sufficiency, or autonomous
  consumer-impact decisions."
- "Warranty is void if customer disables logging, human review, or policy
  routing."
- "Warranty expires when evidence freshness is older than the stated threshold."

This is stronger than a generic legal disclaimer because it ties warranty to
runtime controls.

### W6605 Risk-Adjusted Pricing

Pricing should account for both value and risk.

Inputs:

- cost savings
- workflow value
- artifact loss envelope
- required monitoring
- human-review support
- indemnity or warranty scope
- private deployment needs
- compliance export needs
- insurer packet depth

Output:

- base subscription
- usage fee
- premium risk fee
- reserve allocation
- coverage eligibility
- discount for stronger controls

Examples:

- Strong human review reduces risk premium.
- Better unlearning readiness reduces privacy reserve.
- Higher K-Score confidence lowers warranty reserve.
- Customer disables logs, risk fee increases or coverage becomes unavailable.

### W6606 Internal Reserve Engine

Do not sell warranty without reserves.

Reserve engine:

- estimates expected claims
- tracks warranty exposure by artifact and tenant
- reserves against severe but plausible incidents
- flags correlated risk concentration
- blocks overbroad warranty language
- informs pricing and enterprise contract approvals

Correlated risk examples:

- same teacher version used across many artifacts
- same prompt-injection defense failure
- same RAG connector bug
- same marketplace package installed by many tenants
- same provider model drift event

This prevents a startup-killing promise.

### W6607 Insurance Underwriting Packet

Generate an insurer-ready packet.

Packet contents:

- artifact inventory
- use-case boundaries
- K-Score reports
- assurance cases
- incident history
- privacy controls
- security controls
- unlearning readiness
- human oversight
- route policy
- deployment topology
- provider dependencies
- monitoring evidence
- excluded high-risk uses
- loss envelope

Kolm should not become an insurance carrier by default. Kolm should become the
best evidence engine for carriers, brokers, customers, and internal risk owners.

### W6608 Coverage Gap Detector

Customers often assume existing insurance covers AI failures.

Build a detector that maps their usage to likely coverage questions:

- Is this cyber, E&O, media liability, professional liability, product liability,
  or AI-specific coverage?
- Are generative AI exclusions present?
- Are agentic tool actions excluded?
- Are IP/copyright risks covered?
- Are privacy leaks covered?
- Are regulatory fines covered?
- Are high-impact decisions excluded?

Output:

- not legal advice
- coverage questions to ask broker
- evidence packet needed
- recommended artifact controls

### W6609 Service Credit Contract

Service credits should be tied to product-controlled failures.

Eligible:

- uptime breach
- verified platform routing bug
- verified schema compiler bug
- verified artifact signing/verification outage
- verified account/export unavailability

Usually not eligible:

- customer prompt misuse
- unsupported use case
- disabled human review
- third-party provider behavior outside contracted route
- customer BYOC infrastructure failure

This keeps support honest and prevents unbounded liability.

### W6610 Outcome Warranty Eligibility Gate

Some artifacts can support stronger commitments.

Eligibility requirements:

- stable task distribution
- high K-Score with narrow confidence interval
- low critical-fail rate
- conformal or selective-risk envelope
- human review for high-impact decisions
- fresh evals
- live monitoring
- unlearning readiness
- incident response path
- no unresolved legal or source-rights gate

If any requirement fails, product copy must say "monitored" or "measured," not
"warranted."

### W6611 Human Oversight Discount

Human review is not just a governance checkbox. It is a risk-control variable.

Risk reduction depends on:

- reviewer skill
- review latency
- review coverage
- alert quality
- fatigue
- override rate
- disagreement rate
- training freshness
- escalation authority

Kolm should compute a human oversight discount only when reviewer reliability is
measured. Otherwise, "human in the loop" is not a meaningful risk control.

### W6612 Agentic Tool Liability Boundary

Agentic systems create a special liability problem because outputs become
actions.

Boundary fields:

- tool authority
- dollar limit
- reversible action flag
- external notification flag
- approval requirement
- audit evidence
- rollback path
- customer-owned credential scope
- Kolm-owned policy scope

Warranty rule:

- Covered outputs and covered tool actions must be separate.
- A text answer can be low risk while the associated tool action is high risk.

### W6613 Provider Dependency Pass-Through

Kolm routes to teachers and providers. Risk must flow through contracts.

Track:

- provider terms
- provider SLA
- data retention terms
- output indemnity if any
- model-change notice
- eval freshness after provider update
- geographic processing
- subprocessor coverage

Customer packet:

- which risks Kolm controls
- which risks provider controls
- which risks customer controls
- which risks are excluded

This prevents Kolm from absorbing unpriced provider risk.

### W6614 Correlated Model Risk Monitor

Insurance markets fear correlated losses. AI artifacts share teachers,
datasets, kernels, routes, and policies.

Monitor:

- teacher concentration
- provider concentration
- vertical concentration
- prompt template concentration
- artifact lineage concentration
- marketplace package concentration
- runtime kernel concentration
- jurisdiction concentration

Output:

- correlated exposure score
- reserve increase
- rollout throttle
- mandatory canary
- customer notice if needed

### W6615 Incident-To-Premium Feedback Loop

Incidents should update risk pricing.

Signals:

- near misses
- support escalations
- failed eval slices
- output supersessions
- unlearning requests
- customer overrides
- provider drift
- legal claims
- red-team findings

The loop must be careful:

- Do not punish customers for reporting incidents.
- Discount for stronger controls after remediation.
- Keep pricing explainable.
- Separate internal reserve from customer-facing price where needed.

### W6616 Risk Transfer K-Score Axis

Add an economic-risk axis to K-Score.

Subscores:

- evidence freshness
- loss envelope confidence
- control effectiveness
- human oversight quality
- incident history
- unlearning readiness
- provider dependency risk
- correlated exposure
- warranty eligibility
- claim-scope honesty

This lets buyers compare artifacts by business risk, not just technical quality.

### W6617 Broker And Carrier API

Kolm can expose evidence to brokers and insurers with customer permission.

API shape:

- list artifacts
- get risk-transfer packet
- get evidence hashes
- get loss envelope summary
- get monitoring status
- get incident summary
- get exclusions
- get controls
- subscribe to evidence freshness events

Privacy rule:

- Never expose raw prompts, customer data, or private outputs to a carrier by
  default. Share summaries, hashes, controls, and customer-approved evidence.

### W6618 Board And CFO Risk Report

Executives need a financial view.

Report:

- AI use cases in production
- expected savings
- expected risk
- control maturity
- residual exposure
- insurance gaps
- warranty exposure
- reserve estimate
- incidents and near misses
- upcoming evidence expirations

This makes Kolm valuable above engineering.

### W6619 Artifact Bonding And Escrow

For high-value marketplace artifacts, create a bonding model.

Options:

- seller posts reserve
- Kolm withholds marketplace revenue share
- artifact requires proof bond for regulated listings
- customers can see whether a package is bonded
- claims can draw from bond under defined conditions

This makes marketplace trust financially real.

### W6620 Coverage-Safe Copy Gate

Marketing must not create accidental warranty.

Gate phrases:

- "guaranteed"
- "insured"
- "covered"
- "no liability"
- "certified safe"
- "compliant"
- "never hallucinates"
- "autonomous decisions"

Replacement pattern:

- state proof target
- state use boundary
- state measured threshold
- state exclusions
- state freshness

Example:

```text
Instead of: Guaranteed safe claims automation.
Use: Claims extraction artifact measured at 99.2 percent schema validity on the
attached benchmark, with human-review required for denial, pricing, and
coverage-impacting decisions.
```

### W6621 Risk Transfer Simulator

Simulate warranty exposure before selling it.

Inputs:

- monthly task volume
- K-Score by slice
- critical failure probability
- severity distribution
- human-review catch rate
- provider drift probability
- monitoring delay
- customer control compliance
- correlated exposure

Outputs:

- expected claims
- p95 loss
- p99 loss
- required reserve
- warranty eligibility
- recommended exclusions
- recommended price

Simulation pass condition:

- p99 loss is below reserve plus insurance-backed limit.
- coverage language excludes unmeasured failure modes.
- monitoring catches drift before loss envelope exceeds threshold.

### W6622 Risk Transfer Product Surface

Create a post-auth enterprise view:

- artifact risk status
- covered use
- excluded use
- warranty eligibility
- reserve status
- insurer packet status
- monitoring obligations
- evidence freshness
- incident count
- renewal blockers

UX principle:

- Do not hide exclusions in legal PDFs. Show them at the same level as the
  artifact's quality and cost metrics.

### W6623 Vertical Warranty Templates

Templates:

- support deflection
- invoice extraction
- claims intake
- code review assistance
- medical admin coding support
- legal research assistance
- agentic back-office workflow

Each template must define:

- covered output type
- prohibited use
- human review requirement
- data rights requirement
- eval suite
- monitoring period
- warranty language
- exclusions

### W6624 Litigation And Claim Evidence Vault

When an incident becomes a claim, evidence must be immutable.

Vault contents:

- original artifact
- route receipt
- output receipt
- policy state
- user action
- tool calls
- logs
- eval freshness
- provider state
- customer configuration
- incident timeline
- remediation

Access:

- legal hold
- customer export
- insurer packet
- auditor packet
- internal root-cause analysis

### W6625 Risk Pooling Across Similar Artifacts

Kolm can learn risk across artifacts.

Pool by:

- task class
- vertical
- runtime profile
- provider stack
- human-review policy
- data category
- control maturity
- K-Score band

Use:

- better pricing priors
- better reserve estimates
- better underwriting packets
- better product recommendations

Moat:

- Competitors can copy features. They cannot copy longitudinal loss and near-miss
  data from thousands of artifact deployments.

### W6626 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `RiskTransferReceipt`.
2. Add loss taxonomy.
3. Add artifact loss envelope computation from existing K-Score and reliability
   data.
4. Add covered-use boundary compiler.
5. Add coverage-safe copy gate.
6. Add service-credit and warranty eligibility rules.
7. Add reserve simulator.
8. Add insurer packet export.
9. Add account enterprise risk view.
10. Add broker/carrier API after customer permission controls exist.

Implementation agent deliverables:

- `docs/risk-transfer-contract.md`
- `src/risk-transfer-kernel.js`
- `src/artifact-loss-envelope.js`
- `src/coverage-safe-copy.js`
- `src/warranty-eligibility.js`
- `src/insurance-packet.js`
- `public/account/risk-transfer.html`
- `GET /v1/risk-transfer/readiness`
- `GET /v1/risk-transfer/artifacts/:id`
- `POST /v1/risk-transfer/simulate`
- `kolm risk-transfer packet --artifact <id> --json`
- tests for claim gating, warranty eligibility, packet generation, reserve
  simulation, and stale evidence invalidation

### W6627 Risk Transfer Readiness Gate

Verifier requirements:

- every warranty phrase maps to explicit coverage
- every coverage scope maps to measured failure modes
- every measured failure mode has freshness
- every artifact has exclusions
- every service credit maps to a platform-controlled failure
- every reserve estimate has assumptions
- every insurer packet has source evidence hashes
- every customer-facing statement distinguishes insurance, warranty, SLA, and
  monitoring

Fail if:

- "insured" appears without carrier, coverage, limit, and policy status.
- "guaranteed" appears without proof scope and exclusions.
- "safe" appears without risk class and monitoring state.
- "enterprise-ready" hides insurance or liability gaps.

### W6628 Ultimate Risk Transfer Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make AI risk financially legible.
- Buyers should know the measured risk, covered use, exclusions, reserves,
  insurance-readiness, and monitoring obligations for every artifact.
- The best possible product does not merely reduce AI risk. It prices risk,
  transfers risk where appropriate, blocks unpriced risk, and turns evidence into
  a revenue lever.

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  artifact = claims-intake.kolm
  volume = 1,000,000 tasks/year
  use = extraction and triage only
  excluded = denial, pricing, cancellation
  k_score = 0.943
  critical_fail_rate = 0.0018
  human_review_catch_rate = 0.91
  evidence_freshness = 12 days
expected:
  warranty eligible for schema validity and platform routing
  not warranty eligible for coverage decisions
  reserve estimate produced
  insurer packet produced without raw customer data
  copy gate blocks "guaranteed claims automation"
pass:
  covered use is machine-readable
  exclusions are visible
  p99 loss is within reserve plus coverage limit
  stale evidence invalidates warranty eligibility
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it moves Kolm from AI tooling to AI
risk infrastructure. A buyer choosing between "AI gateway," "fine-tuning
platform," and "Kolm" should see that Kolm is the only one that can compile a
task into an artifact, prove it, revoke it, monitor it, and make its residual
risk financially usable.

## Cyber-Physical, Real-Time, And Safety-Critical Runtime Compiler Layer

This layer keeps "run anywhere" honest when anywhere includes machines that can
hurt people, stop factories, misroute vehicles, or break regulated devices.

Phones, laptops, browsers, servers, and GPUs mostly fail as software systems.
Robots, vehicles, medical devices, drones, industrial controllers, kiosks,
warehouses, and field hardware fail as cyber-physical systems. The difference is
not just latency. It is deadlines, actuation authority, safety cases,
degradation modes, environmental sensors, operator override, communications
quality, hardware faults, and certification evidence. Functional safety
standards such as IEC 61508 and ISO 26262 are built around the idea that safety
depends on correctly performing safety functions under defined integrity levels
and lifecycle controls. UL 4600 emphasizes safety arguments for autonomous
products. ROS 2 and DDS expose deadline, liveliness, lifespan, and QoS concepts
because real-world systems cannot treat messages as best-effort text.

Kolm should not market every compiled artifact as safe for cyber-physical
control. Kolm should build a `CyberPhysicalSafetyCompiler`: a runtime and
evidence layer that decides whether an artifact can observe, advise, recommend,
or actuate, and under which deadline, fallback, monitor, and safety-envelope
conditions.

New primitive: `PhysicalSafetyReceipt`.

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `deployment_target`
- `physical_domain`
- `authority_level`: observe, advise, recommend, approve, command, actuate
- `control_loop_role`
- `deadline_budget`
- `deadline_miss_policy`
- `sensor_trust_model`
- `actuator_limits`
- `safe_state`
- `fallback_controller`
- `human_override_path`
- `runtime_qos`
- `environment_envelope`
- `simulation_evidence`
- `physical_test_evidence`
- `safety_case_links`
- `certification_scope`
- `unsupported_use`

Commercial thesis:

- Most AI vendors will say "edge deployment" and stop at quantization.
