- Kolm can say: "This artifact is observe-only in industrial control, advisory
  in medical workflow, command-capable only behind a certified controller, and
  blocked from actuation when deadline or sensor trust fails."
- That makes Kolm credible for manufacturing, logistics, healthcare operations,
  robotics, aerospace suppliers, automotive suppliers, and physical security.

### W6700 CyberPhysicalIR Schema

Define an IR for cyber-physical deployment.

Fields:

- physical domain
- hazard class
- authority level
- loop frequency
- maximum latency
- jitter tolerance
- deadline miss action
- safety monitor
- fallback controller
- operator interface
- sensor sources
- actuator boundary
- communication QoS
- environmental assumptions
- certification mapping
- evidence freshness

Acceptance:

- No artifact can actuate without an explicit authority level.
- No real-time claim can exist without a deadline budget.
- No safety-critical claim can exist without safe-state behavior.

### W6701 Authority Level Compiler

Separate observation from action.

Authority levels:

- `observe`: read signals only
- `summarize`: report state
- `advise`: recommend action to human
- `approve`: gate action proposed by another system
- `command`: send bounded command
- `actuate`: directly control physical actuator

Rules:

- Default to observe.
- Upgrade authority only with evidence.
- Require stricter gates for irreversible or high-energy actions.
- Require human override for high-impact actions unless a certified controller
  owns final safety.

This prevents an assistant from becoming a hidden controller.

### W6702 Deadline And Jitter Budget

Real-time systems fail when answers arrive late.

Compile:

- deadline
- p95 latency
- p99 latency
- worst-case execution estimate
- jitter envelope
- cold-start behavior
- queue policy
- priority
- deadline miss action

Policy:

- If deadline is missed, do not answer late into a physical control loop.
- Emit safe-state, stale-output, or human-handoff signal.
- Log deadline misses as safety events, not only performance events.

### W6703 Safe-State Planner

Every cyber-physical artifact needs a safe state.

Examples:

- robot slows or stops
- vehicle hands control to certified stack
- industrial line pauses
- medical workflow routes to clinician
- drone returns home or hovers
- kiosk disables payment or unlock action

The safe state must be domain-specific and tested. "Retry" is not a safety
state.

### W6704 Fallback Controller Contract

Kolm artifacts should rarely be the final safety controller.

Contract:

- primary AI artifact
- independent safety monitor
- certified fallback controller when available
- deterministic guard layer
- emergency stop path
- human override
- health signal

The artifact can optimize normal operation, but the fallback controller owns
bounded safety behavior when confidence, deadline, sensor trust, or runtime
health fails.

### W6705 Sensor Trust And Perception Boundary

Perception failures are not ordinary model errors.

Track:

- sensor type
- calibration state
- occlusion
- timestamp skew
- confidence
- adversarial risk
- lighting/weather/thermal envelope
- missing data
- out-of-distribution signal

Rule:

- If sensor trust is below threshold, AI output authority must downgrade.

This binds multimodal artifacts to physical evidence quality.

### W6706 Actuator Limit Compiler

Bound what the artifact can command.

Limits:

- max speed
- max force
- max temperature
- max voltage/current
- max dosage or rate where applicable
- allowed workspace
- allowed time window
- allowed tool set
- reversible-only flag
- dollar or material limit

The runtime should validate commands before they reach actuators.

### W6707 QoS And Liveliness Receipt

ROS 2 and DDS-style systems expose deadline, liveliness, lifespan, reliability,
durability, and history settings because message behavior changes safety.

Receipt fields:

- topic or channel
- reliability mode
- deadline
- liveliness lease
- lifespan
- history depth
- packet loss tolerance
- stale message behavior
- incompatibility behavior

Kolm should compile these settings from artifact risk, not ask operators to hand
tune them.

### W6708 Simulation-To-Physical Gap

Simulation is necessary and insufficient.

Track:

- simulator version
- scenario coverage
- randomized domain parameters
- physical device class
- real sensor logs
- sim/real delta
- known unmodeled effects
- pass/fail divergence

Gate:

- A simulation-only artifact cannot claim physical deployment readiness.
- Physical validation can be scoped to device family, environment, and authority
  level.

### W6709 Hazard Analysis Binder

Bind artifact behavior to hazard analysis.

Hazard fields:

- hazard
- cause
- consequence
- severity
- exposure
- controllability
- mitigation
- verification
- residual risk

This does not make Kolm a functional-safety certifier. It makes Kolm evidence
usable by safety engineers.

### W6710 Safety Case Integration

Connect this layer to the existing assurance-case layer.

Each physical deployment should produce:

- claim
- context
- assumptions
- evidence
- argument
- counterexamples
- residual risks
- reviewer approval

Physical safety cases must include deadline, safe-state, sensor trust, actuator
limits, and fallback evidence.

### W6711 Human Override And Operator UX Contract

Human override must be real.

Measure:

- override discoverability
- time to override
- operator workload
- alert fatigue
- false alarm rate
- authority clarity
- audit capture

A button that the operator cannot find during a deadline is not a control.

### W6712 Degraded-Mode Compiler

Define behavior under degradation:

- low battery
- overheating
- sensor loss
- network loss
- provider unavailable
- model confidence low
- deadline misses
- storage full
- revoked artifact
- stale calibration

Output states:

- normal
- reduced performance
- observe-only
- human-review-only
- safe-state
- shutdown

### W6713 Physical Incident Replay

Cyber-physical incidents require replay across time.

Replay packet:

- sensor inputs
- timestamps
- route decisions
- model outputs
- tool commands
- actuator commands
- QoS events
- deadline misses
- operator actions
- fallback transitions
- environment state

This lets support, safety, insurer, and customer teams reconstruct what happened
without guessing.

### W6714 Certification Scope Guard

Never imply certification from evidence.

Guard states:

- tested
- validated
- safety-case-ready
- certification-supporting
- certified by named body
- out of scope

Copy rule:

- "Aligned to IEC 61508 evidence needs" is not "IEC 61508 certified."
- "Supports ISO 26262 work products" is not "automotive safety certified."
- "UL 4600-style safety argument packet" is not a UL certification.

### W6715 Real-Time Runtime Profile

Add a runtime profile for hard and soft real-time:

- CPU isolation
- memory lock
- no dynamic allocation in critical path when required
- bounded queue
- priority
- watchdog
- deadline timer
- deterministic fallback
- logging without blocking control loop

This profile may reject normal web/server runtimes.

### W6716 Safety-Critical Quantization Gate

Quantization can change behavior. In safety-critical contexts, aggressive
compression must be scoped.

Gate:

- no quantization on safety-critical heads unless validated
- compare pre/post quantization by hazard slice
- test rare physical scenarios
- measure calibration drift
- block deployment if safe-state classifier changes

This ties DAQ and device compilation to physical safety.

### W6717 Tool And Robot Action Firewall

Agent tools and robot actions need a firewall.

The firewall checks:

- authority level
- command bounds
- stale context
- sensor trust
- operator approval
- route risk
- deadline status
- revocation status
- safety monitor status

If any check fails, it emits safe-state or approval-required.

### W6718 Physical K-Score Axis

Add a physical deployment K-Score axis.

Subscores:

- deadline compliance
- jitter stability
- safe-state correctness
- sensor-trust handling
- actuator-bound compliance
- fallback transition correctness
- human override effectiveness
- simulation-to-physical transfer
- incident replay completeness
- certification-scope honesty

This makes "runs on device" meaningful.

### W6719 Fleet Safety Rollout

Physical deployments need staged rollout.

Stages:

- simulator only
- hardware-in-the-loop
- shadow observe
- advisory mode
- bounded command
- limited fleet
- expanded fleet
- full deployment

Promotion requires:

- no severe incidents
- deadline pass
- K-Score pass
- operator approval
- fallback pass
- incident replay ready

### W6720 Offline Physical Revocation

Offline robots and devices must receive revocations.

Policies:

- critical revocation stops actuation after grace period
- stale devices downgrade authority
- signed revocation manifests
- local audit log
- manual service workflow

This connects W6500 unlearning/revocation to physical safety.

### W6721 Industrial Control Boundary

Industrial PLC/SCADA environments have special constraints:

- deterministic controllers
- strict change windows
- safety instrumented systems
- network segmentation
- vendor qualification
- maintenance windows
- operator procedures

Kolm's default role should be advisory or monitoring unless a customer-specific
safety case upgrades authority.

### W6722 Medical Device Boundary

Medical devices require strict scope.

Rules:

- distinguish administrative workflow from device control
- do not claim clinical decision autonomy without validation
- require clinician review for high-impact outputs
- log evidence for audits
- preserve patient safety over automation

Artifact authority should default to summarize, extract, or route, not actuate.

### W6723 Vehicle And Mobility Boundary

Vehicle systems need a conservative boundary.

Kolm can support:

- documentation
- log analysis
- scenario generation
- simulation evaluation
- fleet incident triage
- offline model evaluation

Kolm should not claim autonomous driving control unless the safety case,
certification scope, hardware, and fallback controller are explicit.

### W6724 Cyber-Physical Red Team

Red-team beyond text:

- sensor spoofing
- delayed messages
- stale frames
- dropped packets
- command injection
- actuator saturation
- operator confusion
- deadline miss storms
- revocation race
- fallback failure

The red team should produce physical safety receipts, not only prompt-injection
reports.

### W6725 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `CyberPhysicalIR`.
2. Define `PhysicalSafetyReceipt`.
3. Add authority-level checks.
4. Add deadline and safe-state policy.
5. Add actuator limit firewall.
6. Add QoS/liveliness receipt.
7. Add simulation-to-physical evidence binder.
8. Add safety-case integration.
9. Add account readiness surface.
10. Add red-team scenarios.

Implementation agent deliverables:

- `docs/cyber-physical-safety-contract.md`
- `src/cyber-physical-kernel.js`
- `src/physical-safety-receipt.js`
- `src/actuator-action-firewall.js`
- `src/realtime-runtime-profile.js`
- `src/physical-incident-replay.js`
- `GET /v1/physical-safety/readiness`
- `POST /v1/physical-safety/validate`
- `kolm physical-safety validate --artifact <id> --target <profile> --json`
- tests for authority downgrade, deadline miss, stale sensor, actuator limit,
  safe-state, revocation, and certification-scope claim gating

### W6726 Safety Claim Gate

Verifier requirements:

- every physical artifact declares authority level
- every authority above observe has a safe-state
- every real-time claim has deadline evidence
- every actuator command has bounds
- every simulation claim states whether physical validation exists
- every certification phrase names actual certification status
- every offline device has revocation policy
- every physical incident can be replayed

Fail if:

- "safe for robots" appears without domain and authority scope.
- "real-time" appears without deadline and jitter evidence.
- "certified" appears without named certification and scope.
- "edge-ready" hides physical safety limitations.

### W6727 Physical Safety Product Surface

Account UI should expose:

- target device
- authority level
- safety state
- deadline health
- sensor trust
- fallback status
- revocation status
- last physical validation
- incident replay availability
- unsupported uses

CLI should expose:

```text
kolm physical-safety validate --artifact warehouse-router.kolm --target robot-kiosk --json
kolm physical-safety receipt --artifact warehouse-router.kolm --json
```

### W6728 Ultimate Cyber-Physical Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make "run anywhere" mean "run only within proven authority,
  deadline, safety, and fallback boundaries."
- The product should expand from software inference to real-world deployment
  without pretending text-model confidence is physical safety.
- The strongest claim is: "Kolm can compile enterprise AI artifacts for devices
  and physical systems with explicit authority limits, safe states, deadline
  receipts, incident replay, and certification-scope honesty."

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  artifact = warehouse-pick-route.kolm
  target = mobile-robot-advisory
  loop = 10 Hz
  deadline = 50 ms
  authority = advise
  fallback = certified navigation stack
  sensor_trust = low due to occlusion
expected:
  authority downgrades to observe
  no actuator command emitted
  safe-state recommendation emitted
  QoS deadline receipt records state
  incident replay captures sensor skew and downgrade reason
pass:
  late answer is not applied
  stale sensor blocks command
  certification copy says safety-case-supporting, not certified
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it opens the any-device surface to
industries that cannot buy generic "edge AI." It gives Kolm a credible path into
physical operations while blocking the overclaims that destroy trust.

## Human Factors, Trust Calibration, And Operator Cognition Compiler Layer

This layer makes human use part of the artifact contract.

Better models do not automatically create better decisions. Human-AI interaction
research keeps showing the same operational failure: people overtrust automation
when it sounds confident, undertrust it after salient errors, ignore it when it
interrupts their workflow, rubber-stamp it when overloaded, or misunderstand
what confidence and explanations mean. Microsoft's human-AI guidelines, Google's
PAIR Guidebook, NASA workload methods, and trust-calibration research all point
to the same product requirement: an AI system must shape the user's mental model
and reliance behavior, not just return an answer.

Kolm's advantage is that every `.kolm` artifact already has route receipts,
K-Score, confidence, policy, evals, traces, failure modes, and deployment
context. That evidence can be compiled into a human-factors contract for each
operator role.

New primitive: `TrustCalibrationReceipt`.

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `operator_role`
- `decision_context`
- `automation_authority`
- `human_responsibility`
- `confidence_display_policy`
- `explanation_policy`
- `uncertainty_language`
- `failure_mode_training`
- `review_workload`
- `attention_budget`
- `override_path`
- `feedback_path`
- `trust_calibration_score`
- `overreliance_risk`
- `underreliance_risk`
- `reviewer_fatigue_risk`
- `handoff_quality`
- `mental_model_check`
- `ui_claim_scope`

Core thesis:

- Kolm should not sell "human in the loop" as a checkbox.
- Kolm should compile the right human-AI team for each workflow: what the AI
  should show, when it should be silent, when it should ask, when it should
  defer, how much friction to add, and how to teach the operator what the
  artifact can and cannot do.
- This becomes a moat because production trace data reveals how humans actually
  rely on artifacts, not how designers hoped they would.

### W6800 HumanFactorsIR Schema

Define an IR for human-AI work.

Fields:

- operator role
- decision risk
- time pressure
- reversibility
- information asymmetry
- domain expertise
- task frequency
- automation authority
- required attention
- workload estimate
- review cost
- explanation need
- confidence display need
- feedback granularity
- override mechanism
- escalation mechanism
- training requirement
- mental model target

Acceptance:

- No workflow can claim "human review" without a defined human task.
- No confidence score can be shown without a display policy.
- No high-risk automation can ship without an override and escalation path.

### W6801 Operator Mental Model Contract

Users need an accurate mental model of the artifact.

The contract should answer:

- What does the artifact do?
- What does it not do?
- What data does it use?
- When does it adapt?
- What does feedback change?
- What does confidence mean?
- What failures should the operator expect?
- What should the operator do when uncertain?

Bad pattern:

- "AI assistant for claims."

Good pattern:

- "Extracts claim fields, highlights missing documents, cites source spans, and
  requires adjuster approval before any coverage-impacting action."

### W6802 Confidence Display Compiler

Confidence can help or harm.

Compiler choices:

- hide confidence when it would create false precision
- show calibrated probability for expert users
- show qualitative confidence bands for general users
- show confidence only with evidence anchors
- show uncertainty reason, not just a number
- show confidence history for repeated workflows

Rules:

- Do not show model confidence if it is not calibrated for the task.
- Do not show a percentage for subjective generation quality.
- Do not show a green badge when the output needs human judgment.

### W6803 Explanation Dose Controller

Explanations have dosage.

Too little explanation creates blind trust. Too much creates fatigue or spurious
justification. The controller chooses:

- no explanation
- evidence span
- short rationale
- counterexample
- uncertainty reason
- feature contribution
- policy rule
- audit trace
- full receipt

Dose depends on:

- risk
- novelty
- disagreement
- confidence
- operator expertise
- prior error history
- time pressure

### W6804 Overtrust Sentinel

Detect overtrust.

Signals:

- operator approves too quickly
- operator never opens evidence
- operator ignores low confidence
- operator accepts known-bad canary
- operator approves outside scope
- override rate collapses after accuracy streak
- review time decreases while risk increases

Actions:

- add friction
- require evidence view
- route to second review
- retrain operator
- downgrade automation authority
- inject calibration examples

### W6805 Undertrust Sentinel

Detect undertrust.

Signals:

- operator rejects high-evidence outputs
- operator duplicates manual work
- operator disables useful automation
- review time stays high on low-risk tasks
- feedback says "do not trust AI" despite strong evidence

Actions:

- improve explanation
- show historical precision by task slice
- expose comparison to manual baseline
- reduce noisy alerts
- train with validated examples
- allow low-risk automation after repeated wins

### W6806 Reviewer Fatigue Budget

Human review has a capacity limit.

Track:

- review volume
- average review time
- interruption rate
- false alarm rate
- shift length
- queue pressure
- severity mix
- reviewer reliability
- missed-error rate

Policy:

- Do not increase review volume without measuring review quality.
- Escalation should prioritize high-risk uncertainty, not random samples only.
- A fatigued human is not a safety control.

### W6807 Automation Bias Drill

Build calibration drills into enterprise onboarding.

Drill types:

- obvious correct AI
- subtle wrong AI
- low-confidence correct AI
- high-confidence wrong AI
- missing evidence
- stale evidence
- policy conflict
- out-of-scope request

Measure:

- detection rate
- correction quality
- time to challenge
- trust adjustment
- post-drill review behavior

This turns training into a measurable product feature.

### W6808 Role-Specific UX Compiler

Different roles need different surfaces.

Roles:

- frontline operator
- reviewer
- manager
- auditor
- admin
- developer
- risk officer
- legal counsel
- executive

Examples:

- Operator needs next action and evidence.
- Auditor needs receipt chain.
- Risk officer needs unresolved failure modes.
- Executive needs trend, exposure, and ROI.
- Developer needs reproduction command.

The same artifact should render different evidence for each role without
changing the underlying truth.

### W6809 Reversibility And Friction Policy

Friction should depend on reversibility.

Low friction:

- reversible draft
- internal summary
- low-risk extraction
- sandbox output

High friction:

- money movement
- patient-impacting action
- coverage denial
- legal filing
- external email
- actuator command

The policy compiles decision reversibility into approval steps.

### W6810 Mental Workload Measurement

Use workload as a product metric.

Measurements:

- NASA-TLX-style subjective workload
- review time
- context switches
- evidence clicks
- deferrals
- escalations
- error corrections
- queue backlog
- post-task confidence

K-Score should not improve by shifting hidden cognitive cost onto users.

### W6811 Explanation Integrity Gate

Explanations can become persuasive fiction.

Gate:

- explanation must cite actual evidence or policy
- explanation cannot invent causal reasons
- feature attribution must match supported method
- rationale must distinguish model reasoning from post-hoc summary
- uncertainty reason must be grounded in measurable state

If explanation integrity fails, show evidence without claiming "why."

### W6812 Feedback Control Contract

User feedback must have visible effects.

Feedback fields:

- feedback target
- feedback type
- who gave it
- expected use
- privacy scope
- training eligibility
- effect timing
- confirmation

Good UX:

- "This correction will update the claim-field extractor after review."

Bad UX:

- "Thanks for your feedback" with no visible consequence.

### W6813 Trust Decay And Recovery Model

Trust changes after errors.

Track:

- trust before incident
- incident severity
- explanation quality
- recovery action
- subsequent reliance
- recurrence

Use:

- adjust disclosure
- schedule training
- increase evidence surfacing
- temporarily lower automation authority

Do not assume trust resets after a fix.

### W6814 Team-Level Cognition Map

AI workflows are often team workflows.

Track:

- handoffs
- shared context
- conflicting edits
- role ambiguity
- escalation loops
- duplicated reviews
- final accountability

Kolm should make the human team topology visible, because a technically good
artifact can fail if accountability is unclear.

### W6815 Operator Error Taxonomy

Create an error taxonomy for human-AI work:

- accepted wrong output
- rejected right output
- ignored warning
- missed evidence
- misunderstood confidence
- used out-of-scope artifact
- failed to escalate
- overrode policy incorrectly
- duplicate manual work
- delayed decision
- wrong feedback label

Every error should map to product remediation, not blame.

### W6816 Human Review Sampling Compiler

Sampling should be risk-aware.

Sample more when:

- model confidence is low
- domain is high risk
- operator is new
- artifact recently changed
- provider drift occurred
- incident cluster exists
- subgroup performance is uncertain

Sample less when:

- task is low risk
- evidence is strong
- reviewer quality is stable
- distribution is familiar

### W6817 Trust Calibration K-Score Axis

Add a human-team axis:

- overtrust rate
- undertrust rate
- review quality
- workload cost
- explanation integrity
- override effectiveness
- feedback usefulness
- mental model accuracy
- role clarity
- handoff quality

This prevents "automation improved quality" claims that ignore human error.

### W6818 UI Copy Scope Gate

The UI itself can overclaim.

Block:

- "approved by AI"
- "the AI knows"
- "guaranteed correct"
- "human verified" when only routed to queue
- "confidence 96%" when not calibrated
- "explain why" when explanation is post-hoc guess

Require:

- capability
- limitation
- evidence state
- human responsibility
- next action

### W6819 Calibrated Autonomy Ladder

Autonomy should increase gradually.

Stages:

- suggestion only
- suggestion plus evidence
- draft action
- one-click human approve
- batched low-risk approve
- exception-only review
- autonomous within bounded policy

Promotion requires:

- low overtrust
- low undertrust
- stable workload
- fresh K-Score
- clear accountability
- incident-free window

### W6820 Human Factors Experiment Harness

Run human-subject-inspired product experiments without pretending they are
clinical trials.

Experiments:

- confidence display A/B
- explanation dose
- evidence ordering
- friction level
- alert wording
- escalation threshold
- training drill
- review queue design

Measure:

- decision accuracy
- review time
- reliance calibration
- workload
- correction quality
- retention
- user confidence

### W6821 Accessibility And Inclusive Cognition

Trust calibration must work for different users.

Support:

- low vision
- color blindness
- motor constraints
- cognitive load sensitivity
- non-native language
- neurodiversity
- low domain expertise
- high domain expertise

Do not encode safety only in color, tiny badges, or jargon.

### W6822 Emotional Tone And Anthropomorphism Guard

Overly human tone can distort trust.

Rules:

- do not imply feelings
- do not imply certainty through warmth
- do not apologize as if the system understands harm
- use direct operational language
- distinguish assistant voice from evidence

Kolm should sound like a precise system, not a charming coworker, in regulated
contexts.

### W6823 Managerial Override Governance

Managers can distort calibration by pressuring throughput.

Track:

- override pressure
- quota pressure
- review-time targets
- ignored escalations
- blocked feedback
- retaliation risk

Governance:

- high-risk overrides require reason
- throughput targets cannot suppress safety gates
- escalations are protected events

### W6824 Human-AI Incident Review

Incident reviews should include human factors.

Questions:

- Did UI imply more confidence than evidence supported?
- Was the operator overloaded?
- Was warning visible?
- Was explanation misleading?
- Was escalation practical?
- Did policy and accountability conflict?
- Did team handoff lose context?

Output:

- UI fix
- policy fix
- training fix
- artifact fix
- staffing fix
- claim-scope fix

### W6825 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `HumanFactorsIR`.
2. Define `TrustCalibrationReceipt`.
3. Add role-specific evidence rendering.
4. Add confidence display policy.
5. Add explanation integrity gate.
6. Add overtrust and undertrust sentinels.
7. Add reviewer fatigue budget.
8. Add feedback control contract.
9. Add trust-calibration K-Score axis.
10. Add account operator-cognition surface.

Implementation agent deliverables:

- `docs/human-factors-contract.md`
- `src/human-factors-kernel.js`
- `src/trust-calibration-receipt.js`
- `src/confidence-display-policy.js`
- `src/explanation-integrity-gate.js`
- `src/reviewer-fatigue-budget.js`
- `GET /v1/human-factors/readiness`
- `POST /v1/human-factors/receipt`
- `kolm human-factors audit --artifact <id> --role <role> --json`
- tests for confidence overclaim, explanation integrity, review fatigue,
  overtrust sentinel, feedback scope, and autonomy promotion

### W6826 Human Factors Readiness Gate

Verifier requirements:

- every human-review claim has role, task, and authority
- every confidence display is calibrated or hidden
- every explanation has evidence or is labeled post-hoc
- every high-risk workflow has override and escalation
- every reviewer workload is measured
- every autonomy increase has calibration evidence
- every feedback path states how feedback is used
- every UI claim names human responsibility

Fail if:

- "human in the loop" appears without a real human task.
- "confidence" appears without calibration status.
- "explain" appears without explanation integrity.
- "autonomous" appears without authority, risk, and fallback gates.

### W6827 Operator Product Surface

Post-auth account should include:

- operator role map
- automation authority ladder
- review workload
- overtrust/undertrust sentinels
- confidence display status
- explanation integrity status
- feedback-loop status
- training drills
- human-factors incidents

This surface should be dense and operational, not motivational. Operators need
to know what to do next.

### W6828 Ultimate Human Factors Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should optimize the human-AI team, not just the model.
- Every artifact should compile into a role-specific operator experience that
  calibrates trust, protects attention, supports correction, and makes human
  responsibility explicit.
- The strongest product claim is: "Kolm knows when to automate, when to advise,
  when to interrupt, when to explain, when to defer, and when the human review
  process itself has stopped being a valid safety control."

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  artifact = insurance-claims-triage.kolm
  role = adjuster
  task = missing-document triage
  confidence = calibrated but low
  workload = high
  action = coverage-impacting
expected:
  no autonomous action
  evidence-first explanation
  human responsibility shown
  second review required
  workload risk logged
  confidence shown as "low evidence" not "62 percent"
pass:
  operator cannot rubber-stamp without opening evidence
  feedback path explains how correction will be used
  UI copy does not imply AI approval
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it prevents the quiet failure mode
that destroys enterprise AI: technically strong artifacts used by humans who
trust them at the wrong time, ignore them at the wrong time, or misunderstand
their role.

## Regulated Model Risk, Independent Validation, And Challenger Governance Compiler Layer

This layer makes Kolm credible inside banks, insurers, lenders, capital-markets
firms, and any enterprise that already operates model risk management.

Financial regulators have spent years defining model risk as the risk of adverse
consequences from incorrect or misused model outputs. SR 11-7 and OCC 2011-12
emphasize development, implementation, use, validation, governance, policies,
controls, effective challenge, limitations, and ongoing monitoring. The PRA
SS1/23 model-risk principles add modern expectations around model tiering,
dynamic recalibration, vendor documentation, post-model adjustments, and
escalation. EBA model validation work highlights harmonized governance for
internal models and model-validation practices.

Kolm should not treat these as compliance afterthoughts. It should compile every
`.kolm` artifact into a model-risk object: inventory entry, tier, owner,
developer evidence, independent validation packet, challenger comparison,
limitations register, use approval, monitoring plan, change log, and retirement
path.

New primitive: `ModelRiskReceipt`.

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `model_inventory_id`
- `model_owner`
- `business_owner`
- `validator`
- `risk_tier`
- `intended_use`
- `prohibited_use`
- `model_definition`
- `data_sources`
- `development_evidence`
- `conceptual_soundness`
- `implementation_verification`
- `independent_validation`
- `challenger_results`
- `limitations`
- `assumptions`
- `post_model_adjustments`
- `approval_status`
- `monitoring_plan`
- `change_control`
- `retirement_plan`

Strategic thesis:

- Banks and insurers do not buy "AI compiler" unless it fits their existing
  model-governance operating model.
- Kolm can win by making distillation artifacts easier to validate than ordinary
  black-box model deployments.
- The killer feature is not only better models. It is a model-risk packet that
  lets first line, second line, audit, and supervisors understand what the
  artifact is, why it is fit for use, how it can fail, and who approved it.

### W6900 ModelRiskIR Schema

Define a regulated model-risk IR.

Fields:

- model inventory entry
- artifact lineage
- use case
- business process
- risk tier
- materiality
- decision impact
- model owner
- business owner
- validation owner
- development packet
- validation packet
- monitoring packet
- limitation register
- approval workflow
- change-control workflow

Acceptance:

- No regulated artifact can be "production approved" without an inventory ID.
- No high-risk artifact can be approved by its developer alone.
- No artifact can be used outside its approved use boundary.

### W6901 Model Inventory Compiler

Compile every artifact into a model inventory entry.

Inventory fields:

- name
- version
- owner
- developer
- validator
- business use
- data inputs
- output
- downstream decisions
- tier
- status
- approval date
- next review date
- dependencies
- vendor/provider components
- retirement date

The inventory should update automatically when `.kolm` artifacts are compiled,
superseded, revoked, or deployed.

### W6902 Risk Tiering Engine

Tier models by impact, complexity, usage, and substitutability.

Signals:

- financial exposure
- consumer impact
- regulatory reporting
- automation authority
- model complexity
- data sensitivity
- output reversibility
- volume
- reliance
- fallback availability
- monitoring maturity

Tier output:

- low
- moderate
- high
- critical

Tier drives validation depth, approval level, monitoring frequency, and change
control.

### W6903 Development Evidence Packet

Model development evidence should include:

- problem statement
- intended use
- training data
- excluded data
- feature or prompt design
- teacher sources
- distillation process
- architecture selection
- benchmark results
- limitations
- assumptions
- known failure modes
- implementation notes

Kolm advantage:

- Distillation captures and receipts make development evidence more complete
  than manually written model documents.

### W6904 Conceptual Soundness Review

Conceptual soundness asks whether the model approach makes sense.

Review:

- objective aligns with business use
- data represents the target population
- features or prompts are justified
- assumptions are explicit
- loss function matches risk
- K-Score axes match business harm
- omitted-variable and proxy risks are reviewed
- model limitations are documented

For LLM artifacts, conceptual soundness must include:

- prompt and tool boundary
- teacher selection
- distillation target
- retrieval scope
- calibration method
- abstention policy

### W6905 Implementation Verification

A valid model can fail through bad implementation.

Verify:

- artifact hash
- runtime version
- route policy
- provider versions
- schema enforcement
- feature preprocessing
- prompt template
- retrieval index
- tokenizer
- quantization profile
- deployment environment

This ties model risk to Kolm's proof-carrying artifact architecture.

### W6906 Independent Validation Workflow

Independent validation must be independent enough to challenge the model.

Workflow:

- assign validator not in build chain
- provide evidence packet
- rerun tests
- review conceptual soundness
- review implementation verification
- challenge assumptions
- test limitations
- approve, approve with conditions, reject, or require remediation

Kolm should make independent validation a workflow object, not a PDF upload.

### W6907 Effective Challenge Ledger

SR-style model governance depends on effective challenge.

Ledger fields:

- challenge question
- challenger
- evidence reviewed
- model-owner response
- decision
- remediation
- due date
- residual risk
- approval condition

Do not allow unresolved P0 challenges to hide behind "approved."

### W6908 Challenger Model Harness

Compare artifacts against challengers.

Challengers:

- current production process
- manual baseline
- frontier teacher
- alternative student
- rules engine
- simpler model
- different provider
- previous artifact version

Metrics:

- K-Score
- error severity
- fairness
- latency
- cost
- stability
- explainability
- human workload
- drift

Acceptance:

- A complex artifact should not be promoted if a simpler challenger performs
  similarly with lower model risk.

### W6909 Limitations Register

Every artifact needs a limitations register.

Fields:

- limitation
- affected population
- affected input type
- severity
- detection method
- mitigation
- owner
- review date
- user-facing disclosure

Examples:

- weak on handwritten invoices
- no guarantee for non-English contracts
- low evidence for rare cancer treatment codes
- provider drift after teacher update
- high false positives on short support tickets

### W6910 Assumption And Dependency Register

Assumptions must be explicit.

Track:

- input distribution
- provider behavior
- data freshness
- retrieval freshness
- schema stability
- business process stability
- human review availability
- regulatory use boundary
- hardware/runtime target

If an assumption breaks, monitoring should mark the model review-required.

### W6911 Post-Model Adjustment Governance

Business teams often adjust model outputs.

Post-model adjustments:

- threshold override
- policy overlay
- manual business rule
- risk add-on
- suppression list
- fallback route
- human review escalation

Governance:

- reason
- owner
- expected effect
- validation
- monitoring
- expiry

This prevents silent spreadsheets from becoming the real model.

### W6912 Model Change Control

Distillation artifacts change frequently unless controlled.

Change categories:

- patch
- minor model update
- major model update
- data update
- teacher update
- K-Score update
- route policy update
- runtime update
- limitation update
- emergency change

Each category should trigger the right validation depth.

### W6913 Ongoing Monitoring Plan

Monitoring must map to model risk.

Metrics:

- K-Score drift
- population stability
- data quality
- output distribution
- override rate
- exception rate
- human review fail rate
- provider drift
- incident rate
- limitation hits
- challenger gap

Monitoring cadence depends on risk tier.

### W6914 Outcomes Analysis

Regulated model risk needs outcomes analysis.

Compare:

- predicted outcome
- realized outcome
- manual baseline
- challenger baseline
- subgroup outcome
- exception outcome
- override outcome

For generative artifacts, realized outcomes may include:

- correction required
- downstream process success
- customer complaint
- audit finding
- incident
- business KPI movement

### W6915 Vendor And Provider Model Risk

Vendor components create opacity.

Track:

- provider model
- provider terms
- provider change notice
- vendor documentation
- data retention
- output rights
- model update cadence
- outage history
- validation limitations

Kolm should generate vendor-risk addenda for provider-backed artifacts.

### W6916 Use Approval Boundary

Model approval is use-specific.

The same artifact can be approved for:

- extracting fields
- summarizing evidence
- drafting internal notes

and rejected for:

- making credit decisions
- denying claims
- issuing legal advice
- sending external notices

Runtime should enforce approved use boundaries.

### W6917 Model Retirement And Decommissioning

Models need retirement paths.

Retirement triggers:

- better challenger
- stale evidence
- unresolved limitation
- provider removal
- unlearning revocation
- business process change
- regulatory change
- unacceptable incident

Decommissioning must preserve audit evidence while stopping production use.

### W6918 Model Risk K-Score Axis

Add model-risk axis:

- inventory completeness
- validation independence
- conceptual soundness
- implementation verification
- limitation completeness
- monitoring coverage
- challenger strength
- assumption freshness
- change-control integrity
- use-boundary enforcement

This turns MRM from paperwork into a measurable product property.

### W6919 Validator Workbench

Build a validator-focused product surface.

Needs:

- evidence packet
- rerun commands
- challenger comparison
- limitation editor
- assumption editor
- effective challenge ledger
- approval conditions
- monitoring preview
- export packet

This is distinct from developer UX and account admin UX.

### W6920 Audit And Supervisor Export

Export:

- inventory
- model document
- validation report
- approval history
- change log
- monitoring reports
- limitations
- assumptions
- incidents
- challenger results
- use boundary

Formats:

- PDF
- JSON
- CSV
- secure evidence room

### W6921 First-Line Second-Line Separation

Model governance depends on roles.

First line:

- owns use case
- develops or configures artifact
- monitors operations
- remediates issues

Second line:

- validates
- challenges
- sets policy
- approves conditions

Third line:

- audits the program

Kolm should encode role separation in workflow permissions.

### W6922 Dynamic Recalibration Governance

Some artifacts recalibrate from new data.

Governance:

- what can recalibrate
- trigger
- data source
- validation depth
- approval requirement
- rollback
- monitoring
- disclosure

No dynamic recalibration should silently change a regulated model's approved
behavior.

### W6923 Model Adjustment And Overlay Simulator

Simulate overlays before approval.

Inputs:

- threshold changes
- policy overlays
- PMAs
- human review changes
- fallback changes

Outputs:

- K-Score effect
- fairness effect
- cost effect
- false positive/negative effect
- workload effect
- limitation effect

### W6924 Issue And Remediation Tracker

Model-risk findings need lifecycle state:

- identified
- accepted
- remediating
- validated
- overdue
- waived
- closed

Waivers must include expiry, owner, and residual risk.

### W6925 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define `ModelRiskReceipt`.
2. Add model inventory generation.
3. Add risk tiering.
4. Add development evidence packet.
5. Add independent validation workflow.
6. Add effective challenge ledger.
7. Add challenger harness.
8. Add limitations and assumptions registers.
9. Add monitoring plan generator.
10. Add validator workbench and export.

Implementation agent deliverables:

- `docs/model-risk-contract.md`
- `src/model-risk-kernel.js`
- `src/model-inventory.js`
- `src/model-validation-workflow.js`
- `src/challenger-harness.js`
- `src/limitations-register.js`
- `GET /v1/model-risk/readiness`
- `POST /v1/model-risk/inventory`
- `GET /v1/model-risk/artifacts/:id`
- `POST /v1/model-risk/artifacts/:id/validate`
- `kolm model-risk packet --artifact <id> --json`
- tests for role separation, challenge blocking, use-boundary enforcement,
  limitation completeness, and stale monitoring invalidation

### W6926 Model Risk Readiness Gate

Verifier requirements:

- every regulated artifact has inventory
- every high-tier artifact has independent validation
- every approval has use boundary
- every limitation has owner and mitigation
- every assumption has monitoring or review date
- every challenger comparison is current enough for tier
- every change maps to validation depth
- every unresolved critical challenge blocks approval

Fail if:

- "approved" appears without owner, validator, and use boundary.
- "validated" appears when developer self-approved.
- "model risk ready" appears without limitations register.
- "production" appears while monitoring is stale.

### W6927 Regulated Model Risk Product Surface

Account UI should expose:

- model inventory
- risk tier
- owner
- validator
- approval status
- next review
- unresolved challenges
- monitoring freshness
- limitations
- assumptions
- approved uses
- prohibited uses

CLI:

```text
kolm model-risk packet --artifact claims-triage.kolm --json
kolm model-risk validate --artifact claims-triage.kolm --validator second-line --json
```

### W6928 Ultimate Model Risk Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should make each `.kolm` artifact easier to govern than a spreadsheet,
  vendor black box, prompt chain, or generic fine-tuned model.
- The product should compile AI behavior into an inventory entry, validation
  packet, effective challenge workflow, limitations register, monitoring plan,
  and approved use boundary.
- The strongest claim is: "Kolm gives regulated firms an AI artifact that first
  line can operate, second line can validate, third line can audit, and
  supervisors can understand."

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  artifact = credit-memo-summarizer.kolm
  use = draft internal credit memo
  prohibited = approve credit, set risk rating
  tier = high
  challenger = manual analyst baseline
expected:
  inventory entry created
  independent validation required
  use boundary blocks credit approval
  limitations include stale financial statements
  challenger comparison included
  monitoring plan requires quarterly outcome analysis
pass:
  developer cannot self-approve
  unresolved P0 challenge blocks production
  export packet includes assumptions, limitations, and monitoring cadence
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it makes Kolm compatible with the
operating model of the most valuable regulated buyers. It turns model risk from
a sales blocker into a product surface and a defensible moat.

## AI Incident Intelligence, Near-Miss Exchange, And Safety Telemetry Network Layer

Frontier gap:

- Public AI incident databases and risk repositories are becoming the memory
  layer for AI governance, but they are mostly external reference material.
- Security programs have CVE, KEV, EPSS, SIEM, SOAR, threat intel, and
  post-incident regression discipline. Production AI still lacks the same
  operational loop for model failures, agent failures, eval gaps, route policy
  gaps, prompt injection, data leakage, bias regressions, hallucinated actions,
  unsafe autonomy, cost collapse, and provider availability collapse.
- Most AI vendors treat incidents as support tickets. Kolm should treat every
  incident and near miss as compiler input.

Kolm invention: `IncidentIntelligenceReceipt`.

This signed, privacy-scoped record turns a failure into product learning. It
connects public incident feeds, private customer incidents, near-miss telemetry,
red-team findings, provider outages, model drift signals, marketplace
advisories, route policy patches, eval pack patches, model risk updates,
insurance reserve updates, customer notices, and regression tests.

The product goal is not a dashboard that says "something happened." The product
goal is a compiler that turns any serious failure into a normalized taxonomy
entry, a deidentified reproduction fixture, an affected artifact graph, a route
or runtime policy patch, an eval and red-team update, a customer remediation
workflow, a disclosure decision, a recurrence watch, and a signed proof packet
that the lesson was applied.

This creates a compound moat. Competitors can read public incident databases,
but they cannot observe Kolm's private cross-customer near misses, artifact
lineage, route behavior, runtime telemetry, eval deltas, remediation outcomes,
and enterprise postmortems. The more artifacts Kolm runs, the better its
incident immune system becomes.

`IncidentIntelligenceReceipt` fields:

- `incident_id`
- `source_type`
- `source_reference`
- `severity`
- `confidence`
- `affected_artifacts`
- `affected_routes`
- `affected_runtime_targets`
- `affected_customers_scope`
- `failure_mode`
- `taxonomy_crosswalk`
- `root_cause_graph`
- `evidence_hashes`
- `privacy_scope`
- `deidentification_method`
- `reproduction_fixture`
- `regression_tests`
- `route_policy_patch`
- `eval_pack_patch`
- `red_team_scenario_patch`
- `marketplace_advisory`
- `customer_notice`
- `risk_transfer_update`
- `model_risk_update`
- `disclosure_status`
- `owner`
- `time_to_detect`
- `time_to_mitigate`
- `recurrence_watch`
- `learning_actions`
- `verification_status`

### W7000 IncidentIntelligenceIR Schema

Build an incident intermediate representation that can carry both public and
private incident evidence without leaking customer data.

Core objects:

- `IncidentCase`
- `NearMiss`
- `FailureMode`
- `IncidentEvidence`
- `AffectedArtifact`
- `AffectedRoute`
- `AffectedRuntime`
- `RemediationAction`
- `DisclosureDecision`
- `RegressionFixture`
- `IncidentIntelligenceReceipt`

Required design constraint:

- raw evidence is never required for global learning
- customer-specific identifiers remain tenant-scoped
- deidentified signatures can cross tenant boundaries
- every remediation references concrete artifact or route IDs
- every incident is replayable or explicitly marked non-replayable

Implementation handoff:

- Create `src/incident-intelligence.js`.
- Add `docs/incident-intelligence-ir.md`.
- Add `public/incident-intelligence.html`.
- Add `GET /v1/incidents/taxonomy`.
- Add `GET /v1/incidents/readiness`.
- Add `POST /v1/incidents/intake`.
- Add `GET /v1/incidents/:id/receipt`.

Acceptance:

- one AI Incident Database entry normalizes into the IR
- one private near miss normalizes with customer data removed
- receipt hashing is stable
- missing replay evidence blocks "resolved" status

### W7001 NearMissReceipt

Near misses are more valuable than incidents because they arrive before public
harm. Kolm should capture blocked unsafe actions, rejected route decisions,
output validation failures, prompt-injection attempts, confidence collapse,
judge disagreement, retrieval contradiction, provider timeout cascades,
device-runtime fallback, and operator override.

`NearMissReceipt` fields:

- `near_miss_id`
- `trigger`
- `blocked_action`
- `why_blocked`
- `artifact_id`
- `route_id`
- `input_signature`
- `output_signature`
- `policy_signature`
- `tenant_scope`
- `deidentified_pattern`
- `should_promote_to_incident`
- `recommended_eval`
- `recommended_policy_patch`

Product metric:

- increase pre-harm detection rate
- decrease repeat near misses
- decrease P0 incident count
- increase regression suite coverage per artifact

### W7002 Incident Intake And Normalization

Build a multi-source intake pipeline.

Sources:

- customer support escalation
- account UI incident form
- CLI report command
- webhook from monitoring stack
- red-team finding
- provider status outage
- public AI Incident Database item
- OECD AI Incidents Monitor item
- AVID vulnerability entry
- MITRE ATLAS technique
- MIT AI Risk Repository risk entry

Normalization steps:

1. classify source and trust level
2. extract actor, model, route, tool, artifact, runtime, data class, and harm
3. map to Kolm failure taxonomy
4. assign severity and confidence
5. detect customer data
6. redact or tokenize evidence
7. link to affected artifacts
8. create remediation backlog
9. generate receipt

Synthetic smoke test:

```text
input:
  public incident = prompt injection caused unintended tool call
  private telemetry = blocked tool call in tenant A
expected:
  both map to tool-use / prompt-injection / unsafe-action
  tenant A evidence stays private
  global deidentified pattern is shareable
pass:
  global eval patch can be generated without customer data
```

### W7003 Failure Taxonomy Crosswalk

Kolm should not invent an isolated taxonomy. It should crosswalk among Kolm
artifact failure modes, AI Incident Database classifications, OECD incident and
hazard terms, AVID vulnerability categories, MIT AI Risk Repository domains,
MITRE ATLAS techniques, NIST AI RMF risk functions, internal K-Score axes,
insurance underwriting risk classes, and model risk management issue
categories.

Crosswalk output:

- `kolm_failure_mode`
- `external_taxonomy_refs`
- `k_score_axes`
- `affected_controls`
- `recommended_eval_types`
- `recommended_runtime_policies`
- `required_disclosure_review`

Acceptance:

- prompt injection maps to security, autonomy, tool-use, privacy, and
  operational controls
- biased credit summaries map to fairness, model risk, validation, affected
  population, and use-boundary controls
- provider outages map to routing, resilience, availability, cost, and SLA
  controls

### W7004 Privacy-Preserving Evidence Redaction

Incident learning fails if customers cannot share evidence. Kolm needs an
evidence redaction compiler.

Capabilities:

- detect secrets, PII, PHI, PCI, tenant names, contract IDs, IP addresses, and
  proprietary prompts
- replace sensitive values with stable typed placeholders
- preserve causal structure
- preserve token length bands when needed for model reproduction
- attach a privacy budget for cross-tenant learning
- produce evidence hashes so private records can be verified later

Example:

```text
raw:
  "Patient Jane Doe, MRN 419..., tool called deny_claim()"
deidentified:
  "Patient [PERSON_1], [MRN_1], tool called [HIGH_IMPACT_ACTION_1]()"
preserved:
  action class, data class, policy failure, output structure
removed:
  identity, account, tenant, clinical details not required for replay
```

Acceptance:

- no raw customer value appears in global incident graph
- replay still reproduces the policy failure
- redaction receipt is attached to the incident receipt

### W7005 Root Cause Graph

Every incident should produce a graph, not a flat label.

Node types:

- `Artifact`
- `TeacherModel`
- `StudentModel`
- `Prompt`
- `Tool`
- `Retriever`
- `Policy`
- `Runtime`
- `Provider`
- `Dataset`
- `Operator`
- `UI`
- `Deployment`
- `Eval`
- `Control`

Edge types:

- `caused`
- `amplified`
- `masked`
- `detected_by`
- `missed_by`
- `mitigated_by`
- `requires_patch`
- `requires_validation`

Root cause graph value:

- prevents "fixed by prompt tweak" theater
- distinguishes model error from route error from policy error
- shows whether the same weakness appears in other artifacts
- gives enterprise buyers a real postmortem packet

### W7006 Blast Radius Compiler

When one artifact fails, Kolm should immediately ask where else the same pattern
can occur.

Blast radius inputs:

- shared teacher model
- shared student architecture
- shared prompt template
- shared tool schema
- shared retriever corpus
- shared policy pack
- shared runtime target
- shared route strategy
- shared eval gap
- shared customer vertical

Outputs:

- impacted artifacts
- potentially impacted artifacts
- no-impact proof
- required temporary controls
- remediation order
- customer-specific notice list

Metric:

- reduce time from incident discovery to affected-artifact inventory from days
  to minutes

### W7007 Reproduction Fixture Builder

The moat is not "we logged the incident." The moat is "we can replay the
incident in a privacy-safe harness."

Fixture kinds:

- prompt replay
- tool-call replay
- retrieval replay
- route replay
- model-output replay
- device-runtime replay
- multimodal replay
- operator-decision replay

Fixture fields:

- minimum context
- redacted input
- expected unsafe output or unsafe action
- policy that should catch it
- original model/runtime metadata
- deterministic seeds where available
- confidence thresholds
- pass/fail oracle

Acceptance:

- a customer incident can become a regression test in the same day
- the test can run without customer raw data
- failure cannot be marked closed without fixture pass or documented exception

### W7008 Regression Test Generator

Incident fixtures should auto-expand into a targeted regression pack.

Expansion strategies:

- lexical perturbations
- prompt-injection variants
- tool argument mutations
- retrieval contradiction variants
- language variants
- format variants
- latency and timeout variants
- device-memory pressure variants
- multi-turn variants
- operator override variants

Hard rule:

- Generated tests cannot change the core harm class. They should test
  robustness around the same failure, not create noisy unrelated red-team spam.

Metric:

- increase incident-to-regression conversion rate to 100 percent for P0/P1
  events
- reduce recurrence rate for the same failure mode

### W7009 Route Policy Hotpatch Planner

Many AI incidents should be mitigated before retraining or recompilation
finishes.

Hotpatch options:

- force human review
- disable tool class
- pin safer provider
- raise confidence threshold
- require validator model
- degrade to extractive answer
- require citation
- route to cloud teacher
- route to deterministic local model
- block runtime target
- add rate limit
- add safety precheck

Planner output:

- proposed patch
- affected routes
- expected quality, cost, and latency impact
- rollback plan
- expiration
- validation required

This makes Kolm operationally superior to static model deployment: incidents
can alter route policy immediately while longer remediation runs.

### W7010 Eval Pack Patch Compiler

Every incident should update K-Score, not just support notes.

Eval patch contents:

- incident fixture
- generated regression variants
- target artifacts
- K-Score axis mapping
- threshold change
- historical backtest
- pass/fail owner

Examples:

- unsupported citation incident adds citation-fidelity axis cases
- prompt-injection incident adds tool-isolation cases
- biased summary incident adds protected-class counterfactual cases
- outage incident adds route-resilience cases
- device fallback incident adds local-runtime compatibility cases

Acceptance:

- incident closure requires eval patch review
- future artifacts inherit relevant eval patches by taxonomy match

### W7011 Marketplace Advisory System

If artifacts become packages, incidents need advisories.

Advisory fields:

- advisory ID
- affected artifact versions
- affected runtime targets
- severity
- exploitability
- harm type
- patched versions
- mitigations
- proof status
- disclosure date
- credit
- related external incidents

This gives `.kolm` a security ecosystem:

- enterprise scanners can flag vulnerable artifacts
- marketplace trust increases
- customers can subscribe to advisories
- partners can build compliance workflows

### W7012 Customer Notice And Remediation Workflow

Enterprise buyers care about what they need to do next.

Notice types:

- informational
- mitigation available
- action required
- service degradation
- regulated disclosure review
- postmortem available

The workflow should generate affected resources, business impact, mitigation
steps, validation commands, expected downtime or quality tradeoff, support
contact, evidence packet link, executive summary, and technical appendix.

Acceptance:

- customer notice never exposes other tenants
- customer can verify their own remediation state
- account UI shows open, mitigated, and closed incident posture

### W7013 External Incident Monitor Ingestion

Kolm should continuously ingest external risk signals.

Feeds to support:

- AI Incident Database
- OECD AI Incidents Monitor
- AVID AI Vulnerability Database
- MIT AI Risk Repository
- MITRE ATLAS
- provider status pages
- security advisories for model-serving dependencies
- open-source model card updates
- package vulnerability feeds for runtime dependencies

Ingestion outputs:

- normalized external incident record
- relevance score to Kolm artifacts
- suggested eval patches
- suggested marketplace advisories
- suggested customer notices
- suggested research backlog

Metric:

- external incident to internal relevance decision under 24 hours

### W7014 Incident Similarity Search

Build a similarity engine over incidents, near misses, eval failures, route
failures, and customer escalations.

Signals:

- taxonomy overlap
- root cause graph overlap
- prompt structure
- tool schema
- route policy
- model family
- runtime target
- data class
- harm type
- remediation success

Output:

- similar past incidents
- similar near misses
- previous fixes
- recurrence risk
- regression tests already available
- missing controls

Moat:

- Kolm accumulates the world's most operationally useful memory of production
  AI failure patterns.

### W7015 Near-Miss Pattern Miner

The highest leverage product is a pattern miner that finds incident precursors.

Examples:

- repeated validator disagreement before a hallucination incident
- rising fallback rate before provider outage impact
- repeated prompt-injection attempts against a new tool schema
- rising override rate by human operators
- increasing output validation failures after model version change
- rising cost anomalies before route collapse

The miner should generate pre-incident warnings, suggested capture filters,
suggested eval patches, suggested policy threshold changes, and a recommended
owner.

Metric:

- increase warnings that become useful mitigations
- decrease alerts with no owner or no recommended action

### W7016 Incident K-Score Axis

Add incident intelligence to K-Score.

New axes:

- `incident_replay_coverage`
- `near_miss_detection_rate`
- `failure_mode_recurrence`
- `external_incident_relevance`
- `remediation_latency`
- `disclosure_readiness`
- `blast_radius_confidence`
- `policy_hotpatch_effectiveness`

This makes K-Score more than quality. It becomes an operational resilience score
for production AI artifacts.

Acceptance:

- artifact cards show incident readiness
- high-risk artifacts require replay coverage before enterprise sale
- K-Score can explain whether quality is high but incident readiness is low

### W7017 Red-Team Scenario Feed

Incident intelligence should directly feed red-team generation.

Pipeline:

1. normalize incident
2. redact evidence
3. isolate failure class
4. generate scenario template
5. produce domain variants
6. bind expected policy response
7. run against artifacts
8. attach results to receipt

Example:

- A public incident about an agent leaking secrets through a tool call becomes a
  reusable red-team pack for all Kolm tool-using artifacts.

### W7018 Provider And Model Drift Incident Linker

External model and provider changes can cause incidents without local code
changes.

Track provider model version changes, pricing changes, latency changes, outage
windows, safety policy changes, tokenizer changes, context-window behavior
changes, output-format changes, and deprecation notices.

Incident receipt should say:

- caused by Kolm artifact change
- caused by provider or model change
- caused by route policy
- caused by customer config
- cause unknown

This is critical for enterprise trust and for not accepting liability for
external drift without evidence.

### W7019 Risk Transfer And Reserve Update

Incidents should update insurance and reserve models.

Inputs:

- incident severity
- recurrence risk
- affected customers
- contractual exposure
- mitigation speed
- evidence quality
- control maturity
- external comparables

Outputs:

- updated risk tier
- reserve adjustment
- policy exclusion check
- warranty eligibility update
- underwriting note
- customer premium implication if applicable

This ties W6600 risk-transfer receipts to actual operational data.

### W7020 Model Risk And Validation Update

For regulated buyers, incidents must update model risk records.

Outputs:

- limitations register update
- model inventory status update
- validation issue
- effective challenge item
- use-boundary revision
- monitoring threshold change
- independent validation request
- management action plan

This ties W6900 model risk receipts to post-production reality.

### W7021 Public/Private Disclosure Policy

Kolm needs a disclosure compiler because not every incident should be public,
but every serious incident needs governance.

Decision inputs:

- customer confidentiality
- regulatory threshold
- marketplace artifact impact
- public exploitability
- cross-customer relevance
- active mitigation availability
- legal review
- insurance review
- partner obligations

Disclosure states:

- private tenant notice
- private cross-tenant advisory
- marketplace advisory
- coordinated vulnerability disclosure
- public postmortem
- regulator packet

Acceptance:

- disclosure decision is signed
- rationale is auditable
- customer data is never leaked through disclosure metadata

### W7022 Consortium Safety Learning

Kolm can create a privacy-preserving safety consortium for production AI
incidents.

Mechanism:

- share deidentified incident signatures
- share taxonomy mappings
- share eval fixtures when safe
- share mitigations and advisories
- keep raw evidence tenant-local
- attach cryptographic receipts

Value:

- every participant learns from more incidents than they personally suffer
- Kolm becomes the infrastructure layer for AI safety operations
- cross-company incident learning becomes a network effect

### W7023 Incident Freshness And Recurrence Watch

Closed incidents should not disappear.

Watch rules:

- rerun replay fixtures on model updates
- rerun replay fixtures on route changes
- rerun replay fixtures on runtime changes
- reopen if similar near misses rise
- reopen if external incident similarity rises
- expire mitigations only after evidence

Account UI should show open incidents, mitigated incidents, recurrence watch,
stale fixes, and artifacts with low replay coverage.

### W7024 Account Incident Intelligence Surface

Post-auth account pages should expose this as an operational surface.

Views:

- incident posture overview
- artifact incident history
- near-miss trends
- open mitigations
- recurrence watch
- customer notices
- evidence export
- external incidents relevant to my artifacts
- advisory subscriptions

UX rules:

- no fear-mongering copy
- every alert has an owner and action
- severity labels are consistent
- hidden raw evidence never appears in global views
- "resolved" requires receipt proof

### W7025 Implementation Handoff

Backend build order:

1. incident IR and receipt hashing
2. taxonomy crosswalk file
3. redaction and evidence hashing
4. intake API
5. fixture builder
6. regression generator
7. route hotpatch planner
8. eval patch compiler
9. account API
10. marketplace advisory API
11. external feed importer
12. K-Score incident axes

Suggested files:

- `src/incident-intelligence.js`
- `src/incident-taxonomy.js`
- `src/incident-redaction.js`
- `src/incident-fixtures.js`
- `src/incident-routing.js`
- `src/incident-advisories.js`
- `docs/incident-intelligence-ir.md`
- `docs/incident-taxonomy-crosswalk.json`
- `public/incident-intelligence.html`
- `tests/wave7000-incident-intelligence.test.js`

No implementation should claim automated disclosure. First version should
generate disclosure recommendations and require human/legal approval.

### W7026 Incident Readiness Gate

Add a readiness gate so this cannot become marketing prose.

Gate checks:

- incident IR schema exists
- taxonomy crosswalk exists
- one public incident fixture exists
- one private near-miss fixture exists
- redaction removes raw identifiers
- route hotpatch planner produces reversible patch
- eval patch compiler writes regression plan
- account API returns incident posture
- public copy does not claim incident network effects until data exists

CLI:

```text
kolm incidents readiness --json
kolm incidents intake --source aiid --id <id> --json
kolm incidents replay --incident <id> --artifact <artifact> --json
kolm incidents advisory --artifact <artifact> --json
```

### W7027 Incident Intelligence Product Surface

This becomes a fourth product lens across the three core surfaces:

1. One AI API: route incidents, provider drift, outage resilience, tool-call
   safety, and policy hotpatching.
2. Compile and distill top models: incident-derived evals, red-team packs,
   K-Score incident axes, and replay gates before artifact release.
3. Enterprise models down to any device: runtime-specific failures, device
   fallback incidents, offline replay, and regulated postmortem packets.

Positioning:

- "Kolm turns AI failures into signed regression tests."
- "Kolm gives every artifact an incident memory."
- "Kolm learns from near misses before they become customer harm."

Avoid:

- "AI safety solved"
- "guaranteed no incidents"
- "certified incident-free"

### W7028 Ultimate Incident Intelligence Objective

Ultimate objective:

- Kolm should become the immune system for production AI.
- Every customer incident, near miss, public incident, provider outage,
  vulnerability advisory, red-team finding, and model-risk issue should compile
  into a safer artifact graph.
- The result should be a product loop competitors cannot copy without the same
  artifact network, telemetry, eval corpus, and remediation history.

Synthetic smoke-test scenario:

```text
input:
  external = AI Incident Database item about prompt injection causing unsafe tool use
  private = tenant near miss where validator blocked a similar tool call
  artifact = benefits-agent.kolm
  route = local student unless confidence < 0.82, then teacher
  tool = approve_claim()
expected:
  normalized taxonomy maps to prompt-injection / tool-use / unsafe-action
  private evidence is deidentified
  affected artifact graph includes benefits-agent.kolm and shared tool schema
  route hotpatch requires human review for approve_claim()
  eval patch adds prompt-injection tool isolation fixtures
  K-Score incident axis updates replay coverage
  marketplace advisory remains private unless shared artifact is affected
  model risk packet adds limitation and monitoring update
  recurrence watch starts
pass:
  no raw customer data leaves tenant scope
  replay fixture reproduces the unsafe-action risk
  incident cannot close until regression passes
  account UI can show owner, mitigation, and proof without leaking evidence
```

This is a 100x product multiplier because it converts the unavoidable reality
of AI incidents into a compounding operational advantage. The best AI company
will not be the company claiming zero failures. It will be the company that
learns fastest, proves what it learned, prevents recurrence, and lets customers
audit that loop.

## AI Procurement, Third-Party Risk, And Sales-Cycle Compression Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

- The best AI infrastructure company can still lose if enterprise procurement
  takes six months, security review stalls, legal cannot verify claims, or
  third-party risk teams receive generic answers.
- AI buyers increasingly need evidence across security, privacy, model risk,
  data rights, incident handling, supply chain, subprocessors, residency,
  business continuity, and responsible AI governance.
- Existing GRC and vendor-risk tools manage questionnaires. They do not compile
  live artifact evidence into buyer-specific trust packets.

Kolm invention: `ThirdPartyRiskReceipt`.

This receipt turns `.kolm` artifact evidence into procurement-ready answers:

- SIG and custom vendor questionnaires
- CSA CAIQ and Cloud Controls Matrix mappings
- NIST AI RMF and NIST SP 800-161 mappings
- ISO/IEC 42001 and ISO/IEC 27001 control mappings
- SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR, DPA, BAA, and subprocessor evidence
- artifact-specific K-Score and incident readiness evidence
- model risk and independent validation packets
- supply-chain attestations and SBOM links
- customer-specific deployment boundary and data-flow diagrams

The product goal is to make buying Kolm easier than buying a generic AI vendor.
Kolm should not merely answer security questionnaires. It should produce a
buyer-specific evidence room where every answer links to a receipt, artifact,
control, owner, timestamp, and freshness status.

### W7100 ProcurementEvidenceIR Schema

Create an intermediate representation for procurement evidence.

Core objects:

- `BuyerProfile`
- `QuestionnaireItem`
- `ControlMapping`
- `EvidenceArtifact`
- `AnswerDraft`
- `ReviewerDecision`
- `FreshnessStatus`
- `RiskException`
- `ThirdPartyRiskReceipt`

Required fields:

- `buyer_id`
- `industry`
- `region`
- `frameworks_requested`
- `data_classes`
- `deployment_model`
- `artifact_ids`
- `questionnaire_source`
- `control_refs`
- `answer`
- `evidence_refs`
- `owner`
- `review_status`
- `expiration`
- `cannot_claim`

Acceptance:

- no answer can cite a capability without an evidence reference
- expired evidence marks the answer stale
- unsupported claims become explicit exceptions, not hidden prose

### W7101 Questionnaire Intake Parser

Enterprise buyers send messy inputs:

- spreadsheets
- PDFs
- portals
- free-form emails
- SIG questionnaires
- CAIQ questionnaires
- custom security addenda
- privacy impact assessments
- AI governance addenda

Kolm should parse these into `QuestionnaireItem` records with:

- requested framework
- control domain
- answer type
- evidence required
- risk owner
- buyer deadline
- confidence

Metric:

- reduce procurement intake triage from days to minutes

### W7102 Evidence Room Compiler

Compile a buyer-specific evidence room.

Evidence rooms should include:

- security overview
- privacy overview
- data-flow diagram
- subprocessor list
- residency and retention map
- artifact proof packet
- K-Score report
- incident readiness report
- model risk report
- compliance status
- open exceptions
- freshness dashboard

Hard rule:

- evidence room must distinguish implemented controls, audited controls,
  certified controls, external package releases, and benchmark-backed claims.

### W7103 Control Crosswalk Engine

Map Kolm controls to buyer frameworks.

Crosswalk targets:

- Shared Assessments SIG domains
- CSA CAIQ and Cloud Controls Matrix
- NIST SP 800-161 supply-chain controls
- NIST AI RMF functions
- ISO/IEC 42001 management-system controls
- ISO/IEC 27001 security controls
- SOC 2 trust services criteria
- HIPAA safeguards
- GDPR processor obligations
- EU AI Act evidence themes

Output:

- control reference
- Kolm capability
- evidence path
- owner
- status
- freshness
- gap

### W7104 Answer Provenance And Citation Binder

Every questionnaire answer should cite proof.

Binder inputs:

- product graph
- readiness closeout ledger
- artifact receipt
- incident receipt
- model risk receipt
- SBOM and SLSA provenance
- policy document
- audit evidence
- account readiness state

Binder output:

- answer text
- citation list
- evidence hashes
- stale evidence warnings
- cannot-claim list

This prevents the sales team from overclaiming and gives buyers auditable
answers.

### W7105 Dynamic Trust Center Generator

Static trust pages become stale. Kolm should generate a trust center from live
receipts.

Sections:

- security
- privacy
- AI governance
- subprocessors
- data residency
- incident response
- uptime and resilience
- artifact provenance
- model risk
- benchmark scope
- certifications and open gaps

Buyer mode:

- public trust center shows general posture
- NDA mode shows customer-specific packets
- regulator mode shows traceable evidence exports

### W7106 AI-Specific Security Questionnaire Pack

Generic security questionnaires miss AI risk. Kolm should ship an AI-specific
questionnaire answer pack.

Domains:

- prompt injection
- tool-use safety
- model data leakage
- training data rights
- output validation
- eval methodology
- human oversight
- incident replay
- route policy
- model and provider drift
- artifact signing
- device deployment

This becomes a sales asset and a buyer education tool.

### W7107 Buyer Risk Scoring

Not all buyers ask the same risk questions.

Score buyer requirements by:

- industry
- data class
- deployment model
- user population
- external action authority
- regulatory exposure
- procurement maturity
- incident disclosure expectation
- model risk governance maturity

Output:

- required evidence
- optional evidence
- likely blocker
- likely legal issue
- likely security issue
- next best packet

Metric:

- increase security-review pass rate
- decrease unanswered follow-up questions

### W7108 Procurement Readiness Gate

Before a sales-led enterprise launch, Kolm should compute procurement readiness.

Gate checks:

- trust center current
- subprocessors current
- DPA and BAA templates current
- incident response packet current
- security architecture current
- AI governance packet current
- customer deployment boundary documented
- artifact proof packet available
- open readiness gaps disclosed
- package and certification claims scoped

CLI:

```text
kolm procurement readiness --buyer healthcare-enterprise --json
kolm procurement packet --buyer acme --artifact claims-triage.kolm --json
```

### W7109 Security Review Copilot

Build a workflow for internal security and legal review.

States:

- draft answer
- evidence attached
- owner review
- legal review
- security review
- approved
- buyer sent
- stale
- superseded

Rules:

- high-risk answers require reviewer approval
- new claims require evidence
- certification claims require certificate evidence
- unsupported claims route to exception workflow

### W7110 Exception And Gap Ledger

Enterprise buyers respect honest gaps more than vague claims.

Gap ledger fields:

- gap ID
- buyer impact
- current mitigation
- target close date
- owner
- evidence required
- workaround
- buyer-visible language
- cannot-claim text

This ties directly to product readiness closeout and prevents hidden debt from
turning into sales churn.

### W7111 Subprocessor And Data-Flow Compiler

AI stacks involve many third parties. Kolm should compile data flows per buyer.

Data-flow nodes:

- Kolm service
- model provider
- object storage
- observability provider
- email provider
- payment provider
- support system
- customer cloud
- device runtime

For each node:

- data class
- region
- retention
- encryption
- transfer basis
- subprocessor
- opt-out or BYOC alternative

Acceptance:

- buyer can see exactly where their data goes
- BYOC and local-only modes produce different diagrams

### W7112 Supply-Chain Assurance Packet

Procurement teams increasingly ask about software supply chain.

Packet contents:

- SBOM
- signed release provenance
- build workflow summary
- dependency risk
- container provenance
- artifact signing
- vulnerability response
- third-party model dependency disclosure
- runtime dependency disclosure

This links Kolm artifact trust to broader enterprise software trust.

### W7113 AI Management System Packet

ISO/IEC 42001 makes AI governance legible to buyers. Kolm should produce an AI
management-system packet even before formal certification.

Packet sections:

- AI policy
- intended use
- risk assessment
- impact assessment
- lifecycle controls
- monitoring
- incident response
- human oversight
- supplier management
- improvement loop

Hard rule:

- packet says "aligned evidence" unless an actual certificate exists

### W7114 Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring

Vendor risk does not end at signature.

Monitor:

- control freshness
- subprocessor changes
- incident changes
- dependency vulnerabilities
- benchmark freshness
- package release status
- certification status
- cloud provider posture
- model provider changes
- customer-specific deployment drift

Notify:

- customer owner
- Kolm owner
- legal/security owner
- account team

### W7115 Procurement Metrics Engine

Track the sales-cycle metrics that matter.

Metrics:

- days in security review
- questionnaire completion time
- first-pass acceptance rate
- follow-up questions per buyer
- answers without evidence
- stale evidence count
- exception count
- legal redlines avoided
- time to enterprise approval
- proof packet reuse rate

These metrics should feed product priorities. If buyers repeatedly block on the
same missing proof, that proof becomes a product wave.

### W7116 Buyer-Specific Artifact Proof Packet

An enterprise buyer should not receive generic proof. They should receive proof
for the exact artifact, data boundary, and deployment mode they will use.

Packet contents:

- artifact hash
- artifact purpose
- data classes
- K-Score report
- eval coverage
- incident readiness
- route policy
- runtime targets
- monitoring plan
- limitations
- owner and escalation path

### W7117 Legal Clause Evidence Mapper

Contracts include clauses that need technical evidence.

Map clauses to proof:

- audit rights
- data deletion
