Design teams around goals, not only functions.

For each workflow, specify:

- human owner
- artifact owner
- AI assistant role
- human review role
- escalation role
- audit role
- exception owner
- manager accountability

The design should support Microsoft-style "agent boss" realities without
turning uncontrolled agents into invisible labor.

### W8104 SOP Diff Compiler

Every artifact deployment changes operating procedure.

Compile SOP diffs:

- pre-AI procedure
- AI-assisted procedure
- human review step
- escalation step
- evidence capture step
- rollback step
- exception handling
- customer communication
- compliance note

Do not let an AI deployment go live while the SOP still describes the old
manual process.

### W8105 Training Plan Compiler

Generate role-specific training plans.

Training modules:

- what Kolm artifact does
- what it cannot do
- how to read K-Score
- how to approve/reject output
- how to report failure
- how to run rollback
- how to export evidence
- how to protect data
- how to avoid overreliance
- how to escalate edge cases

Attach completion evidence to the workforce receipt.

### W8106 Overreliance And Deskilling Monitor

Track when users stop exercising critical judgment.

Signals:

- approval without review
- declining edit quality
- rare-case failure misses
- reduced skill practice
- increased downstream corrections
- concentration of expertise in fewer people
- reviewer disagreement collapse

Mitigations:

- mandatory spot checks
- refresher tasks
- hidden challenge cases
- rotating human review
- temporary automation downgrade

### W8107 Worker Feedback And Contestability

Workers need a safe way to challenge AI workflow changes.

Capture:

- confusing output
- wrong automation boundary
- increased workload
- missing context
- unsafe recommendation
- customer harm concern
- training gap
- accessibility issue
- labor policy concern

Feedback should feed product, SOP, training, and risk updates.

### W8108 Labor Value Model

Calculate labor value without reducing people to cost.

Model:

- time saved
- rework avoided
- review load shifted
- cycle time improved
- customer response time
- quality improvement
- error reduction
- employee burden reduction
- overtime reduction
- skill growth

Include confidence intervals and exclusion notes. Do not claim labor savings
when work merely shifted to hidden review queues.

### W8109 Review Capacity Reallocation

If artifacts reduce routine work, reallocate humans to high-value judgment.

Planner outputs:

- reduced routine queue hours
- increased exception queue hours
- training hours
- quality-review hours
- customer-facing time
- governance review time
- innovation capacity

This turns automation from headcount fear into capacity strategy.

### W8110 Workforce Risk Register

Every deployment should have workforce risks.

Risks:

- displacement risk
- deskilling
- overreliance
- surveillance perception
- unfair performance scoring
- accessibility exclusion
- language/localization mismatch
- role ambiguity
- increased pace pressure
- union/works council concern
- customer-facing accountability confusion

Each risk needs owner, mitigation, review date, and evidence.

### W8111 HRIS And Identity Integration Boundary

Integrate carefully with HR systems.

Allowed:

- role/job profile ids
- training completion
- team/cost-center mapping
- certification status
- access role mapping

High-risk:

- individual productivity scoring
- disciplinary use
- sensitive demographic inference
- disability inference
- covert surveillance

Kolm should default away from individual surveillance metrics.

### W8112 Fairness And Inclusion Workforce Gate

AI workflow changes can affect groups unevenly.

Check:

- language accessibility
- disability accessibility
- shift/region effects
- contractor/employee differences
- junior/senior skill impacts
- gender/race/age proxy risks where legally and ethically reviewable
- training access parity

Output:

- inclusion risk score
- mitigation plan
- required review

### W8113 Manager Operating Cadence

Managers need a cadence, not a dashboard dump.

Cadence:

- weekly adoption review
- monthly value review
- quarterly skill review
- incident review
- training review
- SOP review
- employee feedback review

Each cadence should have a compact receipt and action list.

### W8114 Workforce Digital Twin

Extend workload twins to workforce twins.

Simulate:

- workload volume
- task routing
- review queues
- training availability
- skill constraints
- role coverage
- vacation/shift constraints
- escalation load
- adoption curves
- incident load

Output:

- bottleneck forecast
- training bottleneck
- review capacity risk
- adoption risk
- automation boundary recommendation

### W8115 Human-Agent Responsibility Matrix

Every workflow needs responsibility clarity.

Matrix:

- who drafts
- who decides
- who approves
- who communicates
- who audits
- who fixes
- who owns incident response
- who owns model/artifact update
- who owns customer harm

Never let "the AI did it" become an accountability gap.

### W8116 Works Council, Union, And Labor Review Packet

Some regions and industries require formal labor review.

Packet:

- what changes
- what data is collected
- what is not collected
- role impact
- training plan
- human review boundary
- surveillance exclusions
- grievance/contestability path
- rollback plan
- worker feedback process

This supports EU and multinational enterprise adoption without hiding labor
impact.

### W8117 Job Architecture And Career Path Update

AI changes career paths.

Generate:

- revised role description
- new skill requirements
- new certification path
- progression options
- tasks removed
- tasks added
- tasks elevated
- mentoring needs

Example:
support agent becomes artifact supervisor plus escalation specialist, not only
"fewer support agents."

### W8118 Safe Productivity Measurement

Measure workflow productivity without harmful surveillance.

Prefer:

- team-level cycle time
- queue throughput
- quality score
- rework rate
- customer outcome
- review capacity

Avoid by default:

- individual keystroke timing
- covert prompt monitoring
- unmanaged manager rankings
- AI-generated disciplinary conclusions

This matters for trust and legal defensibility.

### W8119 Workforce Claim Scope Guard

Block public or sales claims like:

- "replace your team"
- "automate 80 percent of jobs"
- "no human review needed"
- "AI employees"
- "guaranteed headcount reduction"

Allow scoped claims:

- "reduce repeated frontier-model calls"
- "move routine review into owned artifacts"
- "reallocate reviewers to exceptions"
- "measure time saved with confidence intervals"
- "keep human approval where risk requires it"

### W8120 Learning Content Artifactization

Training itself should become a Kolm artifact.

Compile:

- onboarding lesson
- scenario drill
- failure example
- role-specific quiz
- SOP walkthrough
- evidence export lesson
- accessibility mode lesson

Score:

- comprehension
- safe use
- escalation accuracy
- overreliance detection

### W8121 Shadow AI To Workforce Conversion

When shadow AI is discovered, convert not only technology but work practice.

Steps:

1. identify informal AI task
2. map role and skill
3. determine data/risk scope
4. create approved artifact or route
5. update SOP
6. train users
7. deprecate unsafe tool
8. monitor adoption

This connects W7300 shadow AI discovery to workforce transformation.

### W8122 Executive Workforce Packet

CHRO/CIO/CFO packet:

- affected workflows
- task exposure map
- augmentation vs automation summary
- training investment
- labor value model
- inclusion and accessibility risks
- workforce feedback
- SOP status
- adoption trajectory
- open decisions

This makes Kolm a board-level workforce transformation system.

### W8123 Workforce Partner Certification

Extend W7700 partner roles:

- Kolm Workforce Transformation Lead
- Kolm SOP Migration Specialist
- Kolm AI Training Designer
- Kolm Human-Agent Operations Analyst
- Kolm Labor Risk Reviewer

Labs:

- map task to skills
- compile SOP diff
- design training plan
- detect deskilling risk
- create works council packet
- configure safe productivity metrics

### W8124 Vertical Workforce Playbooks

Create playbooks by vertical:

- healthcare quality/admin
- insurance claims
- bank operations
- legal review
- contact center
- software support
- manufacturing quality
- public-sector case work
- education feedback

Each playbook includes task exposure, human review boundaries, training, SOP
diffs, and labor-risk notes.

### W8125 Workforce Data Minimization

Workforce data is sensitive.

Default:

- team-level metrics
- role-level task maps
- aggregated feedback
- consented training records

Avoid:

- individual hidden scoring
- sensitive demographic inference
- raw employee prompts in cross-tenant learning
- manager-only black-box risk scores

### W8126 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. WorkTaskIR.
2. Role exposure classifier.
3. Skills graph binder.
4. SOP diff compiler.
5. Training plan compiler.
6. Workforce risk register.
7. Human-agent responsibility matrix.
8. Workforce digital twin.
9. Executive workforce packet.
10. Partner certification labs.

First scope:
one support workflow, one claims/document workflow, one developer workflow, and
one account/admin workflow.

### W8127 Workforce Transformation Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

```text
input:
  WorkTaskIR
  artifact receipts
  skills graph
  SOP diff
  training plan
  workforce risk register
  adoption telemetry
checks:
  task exposure is role-level, not job-erasure rhetoric
  automation boundary explicit
  human review boundary explicit
  training plan attached
  SOP updated
  workforce feedback path exists
  safe productivity metrics configured
  displacement and deskilling risks reviewed
  labor/works-council scope noted where relevant
  claims avoid guaranteed headcount reduction
fail:
  AI deployment changes work but no SOP changes
  labor savings claim ignores hidden review work
  individual surveillance metric enabled by default
  workers have no contestability channel
  managers cannot explain responsibility matrix
pass:
  signed WorkTransformationReceipt
```

### W8128 Ultimate Workforce Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm becomes the product that lets enterprises convert repeated AI work into
owned artifacts while redesigning human work responsibly. Every deployment
should say what changed, who owns what, what skills are needed, how workers are
trained, how value is measured, and where automation is not allowed.

The strategic position:
Competitors will sell "AI agents" and "automation." Kolm should sell owned AI
with a workforce proof layer. That gives buyers the confidence to scale because
the product does not pretend people vanish; it shows how people, artifacts, and
controls operate together.

## Sovereign AI, Export-Control, And National-Security Boundary Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:
Kolm already has data-residency, compliance, air-gapped, privacy, CUI, and
trust evidence concepts scattered across the roadmap. That is not enough for
government, defense, critical infrastructure, global enterprise, and national
cloud buyers. The hardest frontier is sovereignty as an executable product
boundary: who may access model weights, which compute can train or serve, which
countries and entities are allowed, which government markings apply, which
cloud regions satisfy contract scope, which data can cross borders, which
artifacts can be exported, and which claims are blocked until official
certification or counsel review exists.

The invention:
Build the Kolm Sovereign Boundary Compiler. It turns export-control rules,
sanctions lists, CUI markings, FedRAMP/CMMC/GovCloud constraints, model-weight
controls, data residency, key sovereignty, compute locality, provider contract
terms, and customer national-security policy into an executable route,
training, artifact, and deployment boundary.

Core primitive:

```text
SovereignBoundaryReceipt =
  artifact_hash
  tenant_id
  jurisdiction_scope
  entity_screening_snapshot
  user_nationality_scope
  data_classification_marks
  model_weight_classification
  compute_location_policy
  provider_allowlist
  region_allowlist
  export_control_flags
  sanctions_screening_flags
  CUI_or_classified_boundary
  FedRAMP_CMMC_path
  key_sovereignty_policy
  air_gap_transfer_manifest
  counsel_review_status
  blocked_routes
  allowed_routes
  evidence_packet_hash
  signature
```

Why it can 100x the product:

- It unlocks government, defense, aerospace, regulated industry, and sovereign
  cloud buyers.
- It prevents international expansion from becoming a compliance minefield.
- It makes `.kolm` artifacts more controllable than raw model weights or
  provider APIs.
- It gives enterprises proof that routing, distillation, device deployment, and
  artifact export respect geopolitical and contractual boundaries.
- It creates a moat in boring but decisive procurement: most AI startups cannot
  answer export-control, CUI, GovCloud, CMMC, and region-sovereignty questions
  with executable evidence.

Strategic rule:
Kolm must not give legal advice or claim FedRAMP/CMMC/export-control approval
without official evidence. It should provide rule-scoped enforcement, evidence
packets, exception workflows, and counsel review hooks.

### W8200 SovereignPolicyIR Schema

Create a canonical sovereignty policy.

Fields:

- tenant jurisdiction
- customer legal entity
- user nationality or residency scope where lawful and required
- data categories
- CUI marks
- export-control category
- sanctions-screening status
- allowed regions
- prohibited regions
- allowed providers
- prohibited providers
- compute hardware constraints
- model-weight handling class
- key custody rule
- air-gap rule
- counsel review rule
- exception authority
- audit retention rule

This IR should be compiled into route policy, distillation policy, artifact
export policy, runtime deployment policy, and account UI readiness.

### W8201 Entity, End-User, And End-Use Screening Binder

Export controls and sanctions care about who, where, and why.

Bind:

- customer entity
- parent entity
- beneficial owner where available
- user domain
- billing country
- access country
- deployment country
- end-use statement
- end-user statement
- prohibited-party screening snapshot
- exception or license reference

Output:

- allow
- block
- require counsel review
- require license evidence
- require limited mode

Do not allow self-serve enterprise deployment into restricted scopes without
screening and review.

### W8202 Model Weight Classification

Model weights and distilled artifacts may have different control profiles.

Classify:

- open-weight public model
- closed-weight provider model
- customer-private student
- distilled behavior artifact
- adapter/LoRA
- quantized edge artifact
- robotics/action artifact
- evaluation-only artifact
- synthetic benchmark artifact

Track:

- training compute evidence
- source model lineage
- rights receipts
- exportability
- transfer restrictions
- publication status
- derived-artifact restrictions

This makes exportability explicit instead of implied.

### W8203 Compute Locality And Remote Access Guard

Compute access can be cross-border even when data stays still.

Guard:

- training location
- inference location
- admin location
- support location
- remote GPU access
- notebook access
- CI runner location
- storage replication
- log replication
- model-weight cache location

Block or require review when restricted users can remotely access restricted
compute, weights, or regulated data.

### W8204 Provider And Region Allowlist Compiler

Compile provider/region constraints.

Inputs:

- FedRAMP marketplace status
- GovCloud or national cloud status
- Azure Government compliance offering
- AWS GovCloud region eligibility
- contract data residency
- DPA/BAA/ITAR/EAR terms
- customer-approved subprocessor list
- cloud provider AI service terms

Output:

- allowed providers
- allowed regions
- blocked providers
- blocked regions
- required customer acknowledgement
- missing evidence

### W8205 CUI Boundary Compiler

Controlled Unclassified Information needs specific handling.

Compile:

- CUI category
- marking
- dissemination controls
- NIST SP 800-171/172 requirement scope
- CMMC contract scope
- enclave boundary
- export packet restrictions
- evidence retention
- incident reporting path

Fail closed if a route, artifact, log, or support workflow would move CUI into
an unapproved boundary.

### W8206 FedRAMP And CMMC Path Planner

Create a path planner for public-sector deployment.

Modes:

- no federal information
- federal information in customer environment
- CUI in non-federal system
- FedRAMP-certified dependency required
- DoD CMMC contractor scope
- agency ATO path
- air-gapped/offline path

For each mode, produce:

- required controls
- missing evidence
- customer responsibility
- Kolm responsibility
- cloud-provider dependency
- assessor dependency
- claim wording

### W8207 GovCloud And National Cloud Deployment Pack

Generate deployment packs for:

- AWS GovCloud
- Azure Government
- Google Assured Workloads or equivalent where applicable
- customer-owned sovereign cloud
- on-prem classified-like enclave
- air-gapped appliance

Each pack includes:

- region list
- service allowlist
- unsupported feature list
- identity boundary
- logging boundary
- key boundary
- object storage boundary
- support access boundary
- export/import path

### W8208 Key Sovereignty And Customer-Held Signing

Sovereign buyers often require customer control of keys.

Support:

- customer-held signing keys
- HSM-backed signing
- external key manager
- offline signing ceremony
- tenant-controlled revocation
- key rotation receipt
- support access without key disclosure

No artifact should be called sovereign if Kolm can silently sign, decrypt, or
export it without customer-controlled policy.

### W8209 Air-Gapped Transfer Manifest

Air-gapped deployments need controlled transfer.

Manifest:

- artifact hashes
- dependency hashes
- runtime bundle hashes
- license files
- evidence packet
- malware scan result
- SBOM
- signature chain
- expiry/freshness date
- import operator
- export operator
- removable media id
- receiving enclave id

Every air-gap import/export should be replayable without exposing secrets.

### W8210 Sovereign Distillation Policy

Distillation can accidentally export knowledge.

Policy dimensions:

- teacher provider location
- teacher model lineage
- student artifact destination
- source data residency
- rights scope
- cross-tenant aggregate permission
- model-weight export class
- evaluation data classification
- human reviewer location

Block:

- restricted data to foreign teacher
- restricted student to unapproved region
- reviewer outside allowed jurisdiction
- aggregate learning across incompatible sovereignty rings

### W8211 Sovereignty Rings

Define learning rings.

Rings:

- tenant-only
- legal-entity group
- country
- economic bloc
- allied government group
- public/open
- blocked

A signal may move outward only when rights, privacy, export, and customer policy
all allow it. This is the sovereignty version of privacy-preserving learning.

### W8212 National-Security Route Policy

Route policy should include national-security constraints.

Route inputs:

- user location
- data class
- model class
- provider class
- compute region
- route purpose
- incident level
- emergency exception

Route outputs:

- local artifact
- sovereign cloud artifact
- government cloud provider
- human review in jurisdiction
- block
- counsel review

### W8213 Sanctions And Restricted Geography Guard

Guard self-serve access.

Signals:

- billing country
- IP/geolocation
- user domain
- customer entity
- payment instrument country
- support contact location
- VPN/proxy risk
- export destination

Output should be policy-scoped and counsel-reviewed. Do not over-collect
personal data beyond what the policy requires.

### W8214 Counsel Review Workflow

Some decisions require legal review.

Workflow:

- policy hit
- impacted artifact/route
- blocked action
- evidence packet
- counsel assignment
- decision
- conditions
- expiry
- appeal or escalation
- audit log

Counsel decisions should be executable policy, not email threads.

### W8215 Sovereign Claim Scope Guard

Block claims like:

- "FedRAMP certified"
- "CMMC compliant"
- "ITAR compliant"
- "export-control approved"
- "sovereign AI ready"
- "government certified"

unless evidence exists.

Allow:

- "FedRAMP path evidence export"
- "GovCloud deployment pack"
- "CUI boundary controls"
- "customer-held key option"
- "export-control review workflow"
- "air-gapped transfer manifest"

### W8216 Sovereign Marketplace Class

Marketplace listings should expose sovereign scope.

Fields:

- supported jurisdictions
- provider/region constraints
- CUI support status
- FedRAMP/CMMC path status
- customer-held key support
- air-gap support
- export-control exclusions
- official certifications
- open review gates

This prevents sovereign-marketplace overclaiming.

### W8217 Exportable Evidence Packet

Export packets themselves can be sensitive.

Packet controls:

- redaction by recipient
- jurisdiction filtering
- CUI marking preservation
- classified-like exclusion
- cryptographic proof without content
- watermarking
- expiry
- recipient access log

Auditors should get enough evidence without receiving forbidden data.

### W8218 Sovereign Incident Response

Incidents differ by jurisdiction and data class.

Incident routing:

- local security team
- customer counsel
- government contracting officer
- regulator
- provider security team
- export-control officer
- works council or labor reviewer if workforce data involved

Attach:

- boundary receipt
- affected data class
- affected jurisdictions
- support-access log
- containment actions

### W8219 Restricted Support Access Mode

Support is often the hidden sovereignty leak.

Modes:

- no Kolm access
- metadata-only access
- screen-share without data export
- customer-run diagnostics
- jurisdiction-bound support
- cleared/support-approved personnel
- break-glass with signed reason

Every support action should produce a receipt.

### W8220 Policy Drift Monitor

Export and sovereign rules change.

Monitor:

- BIS/EAR updates
- Entity List changes
- sanctions updates
- EU dual-use updates
- FedRAMP terminology/status changes
- CMMC rules
- NIST CUI guidance
- cloud-provider compliance status
- customer contract changes

Output:

- impacted tenants
- impacted artifacts
- impacted route policies
- required claim changes
- required counsel reviews

### W8221 Sovereign Developer Experience

Developers need local feedback.

Commands and docs should eventually answer:

- why a route is blocked
- which boundary was violated
- what evidence is missing
- which region/provider is allowed
- whether a local artifact is permitted
- what counsel review is required
- how to generate an air-gap package

No developer should discover sovereignty rules only after production deploy.

### W8222 Sovereign Test Fixtures

Create simulation fixtures:

- CUI route to non-approved provider
- GovCloud-only customer using public region
- customer-held key missing
- restricted entity self-serve signup
- model-weight export blocked
- foreign support admin tries access
- air-gap package has stale signature
- sanctions country mismatch
- FedRAMP claim without marketplace evidence

Every fixture should prove fail-closed behavior.

### W8223 International Product Matrix

International expansion needs a product matrix.

For each country/region:

- supported provider regions
- allowed product surfaces
- export restrictions
- data residency requirements
- customer-held key support
- support model
- public claims
- sales approval
- partner requirements

This matrix should drive website copy, sales approvals, and account readiness.

### W8224 Sovereign AI Account Surface

Post-auth account should show:

- jurisdiction scope
- allowed regions
- blocked regions
- provider readiness
- CUI/FedRAMP/CMMC path status
- key sovereignty status
- air-gap bundle status
- counsel review queue
- policy drift alerts
- support access mode
- claim-scope exceptions

This should be dense and operational, not marketing.

### W8225 Sovereign Partner Certification

Extend partner certification.

Roles:

- Kolm Sovereign Deployment Architect
- Kolm Government Cloud Specialist
- Kolm CUI Boundary Specialist
- Kolm Air-Gapped Operations Specialist
- Kolm Export-Control Workflow Specialist

Labs:

- build GovCloud pack
- block unsupported region
- generate air-gap manifest
- process counsel review
- export redacted evidence packet
- configure restricted support mode

### W8226 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. SovereignPolicyIR.
2. Entity/end-use screening binder.
3. Provider/region allowlist compiler.
4. CUI boundary compiler.
5. Key sovereignty policy.
6. Air-gapped transfer manifest.
7. Sovereign route policy.
8. Counsel review workflow.
9. Policy drift monitor.
10. Account readiness surface.

First scope:
one GovCloud-style deployment, one CUI boundary simulation, one air-gapped
bundle, one customer-held-key flow, one blocked-region fixture.

### W8227 Sovereign Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

```text
input:
  SovereignPolicyIR
  tenant profile
  artifact receipts
  provider compliance evidence
  entity screening snapshot
  CUI markings
  key policy
  route policy
checks:
  jurisdiction explicit
  provider/region allowlist enforced
  model-weight class explicit
  CUI boundary cannot route to public provider
  key custody rule enforced
  support access mode explicit
  counsel review triggers when required
  FedRAMP/CMMC claims scoped to evidence
  air-gap package has signed manifest
  policy drift updates claim scope
fail:
  restricted artifact export allowed
  unsupported GovCloud claim
  foreign support access without policy
  customer-held-key claim while Kolm can silently sign
  self-serve bypasses screening
pass:
  signed SovereignBoundaryReceipt
```

### W8228 Ultimate Sovereign AI Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm becomes the way enterprises and governments deploy owned AI across
jurisdictions without losing control of data, keys, model weights, compute,
support access, or public claims. Every artifact, route, distillation run,
runtime target, and evidence packet has an executable sovereignty boundary.

The strategic position:
Global AI will fragment across law, geopolitics, data residency, compute
control, and national-security concerns. Kolm should make fragmentation
manageable by compiling it into signed artifact policy instead of forcing
customers to choose between innovation and sovereign control.

## Hardware Root Of Trust, Firmware Attestation, And Device Identity Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

- Kolm already has layers for tiny artifacts, omnidevice deployment, crypto
  agility, secure updates, confidential compute, robotics, and sovereign AI.
  Those layers still need one harder primitive: proof that the physical or
  virtual device executing an artifact is the intended device, booted through
  the intended chain, running approved firmware, using approved keys, and still
  eligible to make decisions under the artifact's safety and compliance scope.
- The AI market talks about "run anywhere" as a packaging problem. For regulated
  and physical-world buyers, it is a trust problem. A hospital tablet, factory
  gateway, defense laptop, retail scanner, ambulance wearable, autonomous robot,
  vehicle ECU, or offline inspection device can become a liability if Kolm
  cannot prove hardware identity, measured boot state, update lineage, rollback
  status, and operator approval.
- The breakthrough is to compile device trust into the `.kolm` artifact itself:
  not as a generic MDM integration, not as a checklist, and not as a vague
  "secure boot supported" label, but as an executable device-trust contract that
  gates artifact execution, routing, distillation, output receipts, incident
  response, and marketplace claims.

Kolm invention: `DeviceTrustReceipt`.

This receipt binds an AI artifact to a device trust chain:

- artifact hash and policy hash
- device class and serial or privacy-preserving device identity
- hardware root-of-trust class
- attestation key provenance
- measured boot chain
- firmware and bootloader versions
- secure boot state
- rollback counter and anti-rollback policy
- update framework and signed metadata state
- device posture signals
- operator approval credential
- sensor and actuator trust scope
- offline-attestation package
- quarantine and remediation state
- evidence freshness timestamp
- verifier and appraisal policy version
- claim-scope result

This layer should make Kolm the trust compiler for owned AI on real devices.
The customer should be able to ask: "Which artifacts can this device run right
now, why, under which evidence, and what changes would invalidate that answer?"

### W8300 DeviceTrustIR Schema

Create an intermediate representation for hardware-rooted artifact execution.

Core objects:

- `DeviceClass`
- `DeviceIdentity`
- `RootOfTrustProfile`
- `BootMeasurement`
- `FirmwareManifest`
- `UpdateChannel`
- `RollbackPolicy`
- `AttestationEvidence`
- `AttestationResult`
- `PostureSignal`
- `OperatorCredential`
- `TrustAppraisalPolicy`
- `DeviceTrustReceipt`

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `artifact_hash`
- `artifact_policy_hash`
- `tenant_id`
- `device_id`
- `device_class`
- `root_of_trust_type`
- `attestation_key_id`
- `attestation_key_issuer`
- `measurement_log_hash`
- `bootloader_hash`
- `kernel_or_rt_hash`
- `firmware_version`
- `firmware_sbom_hash`
- `secure_boot_state`
- `rollback_counter`
- `update_metadata_hash`
- `appraisal_policy_version`
- `freshness_nonce`
- `attestation_timestamp`
- `trust_state`
- `allowed_actions`
- `claim_scope`

Acceptance:

- a device cannot claim artifact eligibility without a trust-state result
- every trust-state result must cite the appraisal policy that produced it
- every receipt must distinguish hardware-rooted proof, software-only proof,
  MDM posture, and self-reported inventory
- offline receipts must include freshness limits and replay controls

### W8301 Hardware Capability And Root-Of-Trust Ledger

Build a ledger of device trust capabilities.

Classes:

- TPM 2.0 desktop, laptop, server, kiosk, and gateway
- DICE-style constrained device identity
- secure element or StrongBox-style mobile key storage
- TEE-backed device attestation
- UEFI secure boot device
- MCU with secure boot only
- MCU with secure boot plus hardware unique key
- vehicle or robotics ECU with Uptane-style update metadata
- confidential VM with cloud attestation
- software-only development profile

Ledger fields:

- hardware root available
- key attestation available
- measured boot available
- secure boot available
- rollback counter available
- firmware recovery available
- update metadata format
- attestation evidence format
- privacy-preserving identity mode
- physical tamper signal support
- remote wipe or quarantine support
- offline-verification capability

Claim rule:

- "hardware-rooted" is not a marketing synonym for "agent installed."
  Hardware-rooted means the receipt includes a verifiable root-of-trust path.

### W8302 Secure And Measured Boot Binder

Bind `.kolm` artifact eligibility to boot state.

Boot evidence:

- firmware measurement
- bootloader measurement
- kernel or RTOS measurement
- initramfs or rootfs measurement
- driver/module measurement where available
- application runtime measurement
- model/runtime loader measurement
- policy engine measurement

Use measured boot for appraisal and secure boot for prevention.

Appraisal examples:

- approved firmware and bootloader -> allow artifact execution
- secure boot disabled -> allow only low-risk demo artifacts
- unknown boot measurement -> require quarantine or human review
- stale boot measurement -> require fresh attestation
- debug boot flag active -> block regulated artifact

Smoke simulation:

```text
device:
  secure_boot_state: disabled
  measured_boot_chain: bootloader_ok, kernel_unknown
artifact:
  allowed_device_trust: hardware_rooted_measured_boot
result:
  trust_state: blocked
  reason: kernel measurement missing from allowlist
  next_action: re-attest after firmware repair
```

### W8303 TPM, Secure Element, And Key Attestation Binder

Bind keys to devices instead of trusting arbitrary uploaded public keys.

Evidence to capture:

- attestation certificate chain
- attestation key issuer
- key usage restrictions
- hardware-backed or software-backed key class
- secure element or TPM policy
- nonce binding
- exportability
- rollback resistance
- attestation root rotation status

Use cases:

- a factory gateway signs artifact execution receipts only with a TPM-bound key
- a mobile nurse device signs PHI redaction receipts with hardware-backed key
- a field robot signs action receipts only if device key attestation is current
- a defense laptop runs sovereign artifacts only if key provenance matches
  tenant policy

Claim rule:

- do not treat a long-lived device API key as device identity. Device identity
  must be rooted in attested key provenance or explicitly marked software-only.

### W8304 DICE-Style Device Identity Chain

Constrained devices often cannot carry full TPM semantics.

Compile DICE-style layers into a smaller receipt:

- unique device secret or compound device identifier
- first mutable code measurement
- firmware layer measurement
- application layer measurement
- configuration measurement
- attestation certificate or token
- verifier policy result

Why this matters:

- tiny and embedded devices are the easiest path to "run anywhere"
- they are also the easiest place to lose device accountability
- DICE-style chaining lets Kolm treat constrained devices as first-class
  artifact targets without pretending they are servers

Acceptance:

- device identity must change when critical firmware or configuration changes
- artifact policy must state which layer changes invalidate eligibility
- manufacturing personalization must be captured as evidence, not tribal memory

### W8305 Artifact-To-Firmware Binding

Tie artifact behavior to firmware behavior.

Binding fields:

- artifact hash
- runtime loader hash
- firmware manifest hash
- model runtime hash
- feature pipeline hash
- sensor driver hash
- policy engine hash
- update bundle hash
- rollback image hash

Failure case:

- A compliant model runs on a gateway whose sensor driver changed scale units.
  The model is technically installed, but the behavior is unproven. The
  firmware binding should mark the artifact review-required.

100x product effect:

- Kolm becomes the only AI artifact system that can explain not just "which
  model ran" but "which device stack made that model's input and output mean
  the same thing as the evidence claims."

### W8306 OTA Metadata And Update Channel Binder

Treat update frameworks as part of AI trust.

Track:

- update framework class
- signed root metadata
- targets metadata
- snapshot metadata
- timestamp metadata
- director or fleet-target metadata
- image digest
- expiration time
- staged rollout cohort
- rollback target
- update failure state
- recovery partition state

Compile:

- TUF-style metadata for artifact update channels
- Uptane-style director logic for vehicle/robot/fleet-specific targets
- RAUC or Mender style device update receipts where applicable

Claim rule:

- no artifact should claim field reliability if updates can be rolled back,
  frozen, mixed, replayed, or targeted to the wrong device without detection.

### W8307 Rollback And Anti-Rollback Policy

Rollback is a model risk problem.

Track:

- firmware rollback counter
- model rollback counter
- policy rollback counter
- evaluator rollback counter
- update channel metadata version
- emergency rollback exception
- tenant approval
- safety downgrade analysis

Rules:

- rollback to a vulnerable firmware blocks high-risk artifact execution
- rollback to an older model is allowed only if the risk register says why
- rollback to an older policy requires signed tenant approval
- emergency rollback receipts must expire

Smoke simulation:

```text
incoming:
  firmware_version: 4.1.2
  rollback_counter: 18
policy:
  minimum_counter: 21
result:
  block regulated workloads
  allow diagnostics
  require staged repair update
```

### W8308 Device Posture Verifier

Hardware proof is necessary but not sufficient.

Posture signals:

- disk encryption
- OS patch level
- EDR or MDM state
- jailbreak/root status
- debug flags
- USB debug state
- network profile
- location class
- clock quality
- operator session age
- certificate freshness
- sensor calibration state

Important distinction:

- hardware attestation proves something about a trust chain
- posture proves operational readiness
- neither should be silently substituted for the other

Kolm should output a combined trust result:

```text
trust_state:
  hardware: verified
  boot: verified
  firmware: stale
  posture: degraded
  artifact_scope: diagnostics_only
```

### W8309 Offline And Air-Gapped Attestation Bundle

Many important deployments cannot call a cloud verifier.

Bundle contents:

- device evidence token
- measurement allowlist
- appraisal policy
- timestamp or freshness statement
- monotonic counter if available
- artifact policy
- verifier signature
- operator approval signature
- expiration
- replay protection

Flow:

```text
prepare -> transfer policy -> collect evidence -> appraise locally ->
sign DeviceTrustReceipt -> run artifact -> export receipt later
```

This extends sovereign and air-gapped modes down to device trust instead of
stopping at file transfer.

### W8310 Fleet Quarantine And Trust Decay

Trust should decay when evidence ages.

Decay triggers:

- stale attestation
- expired update metadata
- missing heartbeat
- firmware CVE
- unexpected measurement
- rollback event
- operator credential revoked
- sensor calibration expired
- physical tamper signal
- geography mismatch

States:

- `trusted`
- `trusted_with_warning`
- `diagnostics_only`
- `quarantined`
- `repair_required`
- `decommissioned`

Product surface:

- account overview should show device trust counts
- API should expose why a device moved state
- CLI/TUI should eventually show local proof paths
- marketplace listings should state required trust class

### W8311 Field Repair And Provisioning Ceremony

Trust breaks during repair if the process is not explicit.

Provisioning ceremony:

- receive device
- verify old identity
- record reason for repair
- rotate or preserve device key according to policy
- install firmware
- verify secure boot and rollback counter
- run conformance fixture
- bind operator credential
- sign new DeviceTrustReceipt
- update fleet ledger

Repair classes:

- battery/service only
- sensor replacement
- mainboard replacement
- secure element replacement
- firmware reflash
- full reprovision

Hard rule:

- after root-of-trust replacement, old identity must not silently inherit the
  same risk class unless the tenant signs that policy.

### W8312 Key Rotation And Compromise Recovery

Device keys will rotate or fail.

Recovery plans:

- routine key rotation
- attestation root rotation
- suspected key compromise
- manufacturing key exposure
- secure element failure
- tenant CA migration
- device sale or transfer
- decommission wipe

Receipts should record:

- old key
- new key
- rotation reason
- authorization
- affected artifacts
- invalidated output receipts
- remediation deadline

This connects crypto-agility to device identity rather than treating keys as
abstract software secrets.

### W8313 WebAuthn And FIDO Operator Approval

Human approval should also be hardware-backed.

Use cases:

- approve high-risk artifact deployment
- approve device reprovisioning
- approve emergency rollback
- approve export-controlled artifact transfer
- approve robot action envelope expansion
- approve PHI workflow on a new device

Policy:

- prefer phishing-resistant WebAuthn/FIDO approvals for regulated operators
- bind approval to exact artifact, device, action, and timestamp
- distinguish synced passkey, device-bound credential, and enterprise attested
  credential where policy requires that distinction

Receipt field:

- `operator_approval_attestation_scope`

Claim rule:

- do not call a workflow "strongly approved" if approval is only email click or
  SMS code for a high-risk device action.

### W8314 Mobile And Edge Secure Element Policy

Phones and tablets are critical AI endpoints.

Policy inputs:

- hardware-backed keystore availability
- StrongBox or secure element support where applicable
- OS integrity signal
- app signing state
- biometric or device credential policy
- offline mode risk
- MDM profile
- PHI/PII class
- local model storage encryption

Artifact decisions:

- allow local inference
- require online teacher route
- require redaction before local storage
- allow diagnostics only
- require re-enrollment

This lets Kolm sell to clinical, field-service, retail, and public-sector teams
that need device-level proof without forcing every endpoint into a server model.

### W8315 Robotics And Physical Action Root Of Trust

Robotics and physical systems need a stronger gate.

Bind:

- robot controller identity
- safety controller identity
- firmware and ROS/DDS graph measurement
- sensor calibration
- actuator limit policy
- emergency stop state
- operator credential
- physical environment class
- action envelope

Rules:

- no physical action beyond advisory mode if root-of-trust is unknown
- no expanded action envelope after firmware update without revalidation
- no autonomous operation if safety-controller evidence is stale

This is the device-trust counterpart to the embodied AI layer.

### W8316 Runtime Attestation To K-Score Scope

K-Score should depend on execution context.

Add axes:

- device trust conformance
- boot chain conformance
- firmware freshness
- update-channel integrity
- rollback safety
- operator approval strength
- evidence freshness
- offline replay resistance

Example:

- The same artifact can have K-Score 0.91 on an attested gateway and 0.66 on an
  unknown device, because the artifact behavior is less provable in the second
  context.

This makes K-Score a deployability metric, not only a model-quality metric.

### W8317 Firmware SBOM And Vulnerability Binding

AI artifact trust inherits firmware risk.

Track:

- firmware SBOM
- bootloader components
- kernel/RTOS components
- driver components
- model runtime components
- cryptographic libraries
- update client
- vulnerability scan result
- exploitability status
- remediation plan

Rules:

- critical firmware CVE can reduce artifact eligibility
- vulnerable update client can block field rollout
- vulnerable model runtime can invalidate output receipts after disclosure

This joins SBOM, model BOM, eval BOM, and firmware BOM into one evidence graph.

### W8318 Device Trust Account Surface

Post-auth UX should answer:

- which devices can run which artifacts
- which devices are blocked and why
- what evidence is stale
- which trust class each device has
- which update channel each device uses
- which operator approvals are pending
- which firmware vulnerabilities affect artifacts
- which marketplace artifacts require unsupported trust classes

Good UX:

- fleet status table with trust state
- device detail receipt viewer
- "why blocked" explanation
- repair/provisioning checklist
- safe next action
- no hidden acronyms without drilldown

### W8319 Device Trust CLI And TUI Proof Commands

Research-only command targets for implementation handoff:

```text
kolm devices trust --json
kolm devices attest --device <id> --artifact <id> --json
kolm devices verify-receipt receipt.json --offline --json
kolm devices quarantine --device <id> --reason firmware-cve --json
kolm devices repair-plan --device <id> --json
kolm tui --views device-trust
```

The CLI must never print private attestation keys or device secrets. It should
print evidence hashes, issuers, status, and next actions.

### W8320 Device Trust Partner Certification Lab

Create a certification track for partners.

Roles:

- Kolm Device Trust Integrator
- Kolm Embedded Artifact Engineer
- Kolm Fleet Attestation Specialist
- Kolm Field Repair Specialist
- Kolm Robotics Trust Specialist
- Kolm Sovereign Device Specialist

Lab exercises:

- provision a TPM-backed gateway
- provision a DICE-style MCU target
- configure signed OTA metadata
- simulate rollback attack
- simulate firmware CVE
- run offline attestation
- repair and reprovision a device
- produce customer evidence packet

Partners become a multiplier because device trust is too operationally detailed
to scale only through product UI.

### W8321 Insurance, Procurement, And Sovereignty Coupling

Device trust improves non-technical buying metrics.

Insurance:

- lower ambiguity in incident causality
- stronger evidence for device-state at loss time
- clearer separation between operator error, firmware flaw, and model flaw

Procurement:

- buyers can require minimum trust class
- RFPs can ask for device evidence export
- suppliers can prove field-update governance

Sovereignty:

- restricted artifacts can require approved device classes
- export-control workflows can block untrusted endpoints
- air-gapped deployments can produce local receipts

This turns device trust into revenue enablement, not just security overhead.

### W8322 Device Trust Marketplace Class

Marketplace listings should include required device trust.

Fields:

- minimum trust class
- supported root-of-trust profiles
- required update framework
- offline support
- supported device classes
- prohibited debug states
- sensor trust requirements
- operator approval requirements
- firmware SBOM requirement
- conformance fixtures

Buyer benefit:

- no one buys a "runs on edge" artifact only to discover that it requires a
  hardware trust class their fleet cannot support.

### W8323 Counterfeit And Hardware Substitution Detection

Trust chain must detect substitutions.

Threats:

- counterfeit gateway
- swapped secure element
- cloned device API token
- emulated device identity
- supply-chain replacement
- lab device substituted for field device
- compromised repair vendor

Signals:

- attestation issuer mismatch
- device identity collision
- impossible location/history
- measurement drift after repair
- hardware capability mismatch
- serial inventory conflict
- operator approval anomaly

Output:

- quarantine
- require re-enrollment
- notify tenant
- preserve forensic packet

### W8324 Supply Chain And Manufacturing Evidence

Device trust starts before deployment.

Manufacturing/provisioning evidence:

- supplier identity
- batch identifier
- hardware root provisioning ceremony
- attestation CA
- firmware installed at factory
- secure boot keys
- test fixture result
- shipping chain-of-custody
- receiving attestation
- first-boot measurement

Why it matters:

- if manufacturing evidence is missing, the device may still be usable, but
  Kolm must scope the claim honestly.

### W8325 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Create fixtures before implementation.

Fixtures:

- good TPM gateway with current firmware
- gateway with secure boot disabled
- DICE MCU with firmware measurement change
- mobile device with hardware-backed key but stale OS
- robot with stale safety-controller evidence
- OTA metadata rollback attempt
- update metadata expiry
- attestation replay
- repair with secure element replacement
- counterfeit identity collision

Expected outputs:

- allow
- diagnostics only
- quarantine
- repair required
- re-attest
- operator approval required

This is the simulated environment the implementation agent should build first.

### W8326 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. DeviceTrustIR.
2. hardware capability/root-of-trust ledger.
3. attestation evidence parser abstraction.
4. measured boot and secure boot binder.
5. update metadata binder.
6. rollback and anti-rollback policy.
7. trust appraisal policy engine.
8. DeviceTrustReceipt signer/verifier.
9. offline attestation bundle.
10. device trust account/API/CLI/TUI surfaces.
11. simulated acceptance fixtures.
12. claim-scope gate for hardware-rooted language.

First implementation scope:

- one TPM-style gateway fixture
- one DICE-style constrained device fixture
- one mobile hardware-backed key fixture
- one OTA rollback fixture
- one offline receipt verifier
- one account readiness panel
- one claim gate that blocks "hardware-rooted" without evidence

Suggested implementation files:

```text
src/device-trust-ir.js
src/device-root-ledger.js
src/device-attestation-evidence.js
src/device-boot-binder.js
src/device-update-binder.js
src/device-trust-appraisal.js
src/device-trust-receipt.js
src/device-trust-claim-gate.js
docs/device-trust-fixtures.md
test/fixtures/device-trust/*.json
tests/wave8300-device-trust-ir.test.js
tests/wave8306-device-update-binder.test.js
tests/wave8325-device-trust-simulated-acceptance.test.js
```

No implementation agent should start by integrating a vendor SDK. Start by
defining the receipt, policy, fixtures, and claim rules, then plug vendor
attesters into that contract.

### W8327 Device Trust Readiness Gate

Readiness check:

```text
input:
  artifact policy
  device identity
  root-of-trust ledger entry
  attestation evidence
  boot measurements
  firmware manifest
  update metadata
  rollback counters
  posture signals
  operator approval
checks:
  root_of_trust_class explicit
  attestation freshness within policy
  measured boot allowlisted
  secure boot state compatible
  firmware version allowed
  firmware SBOM risk accepted
  update metadata current
  rollback counter not below minimum
  operator approval sufficient
  offline replay blocked
  claim scope honest
fail:
  software-only device claims hardware-rooted trust
  stale evidence runs regulated artifact
  rollback attack accepted
  update metadata expired
  debug flag ignored
  operator approval not bound to action
pass:
  signed DeviceTrustReceipt
```

### W8328 Ultimate Device Trust Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should make every AI action answerable to a device trust chain. A `.kolm`
artifact should not merely say "I can run on this device." It should say: "This
specific device, in this measured state, under this firmware and update chain,
with this operator approval and evidence freshness, may run these parts of the
artifact and produce these claims."

The strategic position:
The next frontier is not only better models. It is credible AI operation across
messy physical fleets. Enterprises will not trust owned AI at scale if model
receipts stop at the API boundary. Kolm can own the missing layer: compiling
model behavior, firmware reality, hardware identity, operator approval, update
lineage, and audit evidence into one executable trust contract.

## Heterogeneous Accelerator, Memory-Wall, And Silicon Co-Design Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

- Kolm already has strong layers for runtime portability, any-device
  compilation, quantization, TinyML, carbon-aware economics, and device trust.
  The missing frontier is deeper: the next 100x gains will come from compiling
  artifact behavior against the real physics of accelerators, memory systems,
  interconnects, chiplets, NPUs, GPUs, CPUs, FPGAs, analog/in-memory compute,
  and neuromorphic systems.
- Current model-serving stacks optimize for common GPU paths. Edge runtimes
  optimize for packaged mobile and NPU targets. ML compiler projects provide
  reusable lowering infrastructure. None of them understands Kolm's contract:
  task-specific K-Score, distillation retention, abstention policy, data rights,
  device trust, route fallback, carbon/cost policy, and audit receipts.
- The breakthrough is to make hardware fit a first-class artifact property.
  A `.kolm` artifact should know which parts of itself are memory-bound,
  bandwidth-bound, launch-bound, token-bound, sensor-bound, privacy-bound,
  numerically fragile, or event-driven, then select or synthesize the best
  target plan with evidence.

Kolm invention: `SiliconFitReceipt`.

This receipt proves that an artifact was profiled, lowered, tuned, and accepted
for a specific hardware class:

- artifact hash
- task and traffic shape
- model architecture and active subgraph
- operator set
- precision ladder
- tensor shape distribution
- KV/cache profile
- memory movement profile
- accelerator target
- backend compiler and version
- runtime engine and version
- kernel plan
- layout plan
- quantization and sparsity plan
- thermal and power envelope
- hardware trust class
- benchmark fixtures
- K-Score retention under target
- failure/fallback plan
- claim scope

The product goal is to make Kolm the compiler that tells an AI company where
its model behavior should live: NVIDIA GPU, AMD GPU, Intel CPU/GPU/NPU, Apple
Neural Engine/Core ML, Qualcomm Hexagon NPU, browser WebNN/WebGPU, CXL-backed
memory pool, FPGA prototype, analog/in-memory accelerator, or neuromorphic
event processor.

### W8400 SiliconFitIR Schema

Create an IR for artifact-to-hardware fit.

Core objects:

- `WorkloadShape`
- `OperatorCapability`
- `PrecisionPlan`
- `MemoryMotionGraph`
- `CachePlan`
- `BackendLoweringPlan`
- `KernelTunePlan`
- `AcceleratorTarget`
- `HardwareTrustScope`
- `ConformanceBenchmark`
- `FallbackPlan`
- `SiliconFitReceipt`

Required fields:

- `artifact_id`
- `artifact_hash`
- `task_class`
- `traffic_distribution_hash`
- `input_shape_distribution`
- `output_shape_distribution`
- `active_graph_hash`
- `operator_set`
- `dtype_set`
- `precision_plan_hash`
- `memory_motion_hash`
- `target_hardware_class`
- `backend_compiler`
- `runtime_engine`
- `kernel_plan_hash`
- `layout_plan_hash`
- `benchmark_fixture_hash`
- `retention_score`
- `latency_profile`
- `cost_profile`
- `energy_profile`
- `thermal_profile`
- `fallback_policy`
- `claim_scope`

Acceptance:

- no "optimized for X" claim without target, backend, workload, and fixture
- every performance claim has a functional unit and confidence band
- every lowering plan records numerical risk and K-Score retention
- hardware fit cannot bypass privacy, sovereign, device-trust, or safety gates

### W8401 Workload Shape Profiler

Hardware fit starts with shape, not vendor preference.

Profile:

- prompt length distribution
- output length distribution
- batch and concurrency shape
- schema branching
- tool-call frequency
- embedding dimension
- retrieval fanout
- multimodal tensor size
- sensor stream cadence
- context reuse
- prefill/decode split
- KV cache growth
- sparsity pattern
- early-exit opportunity
- abstention and fallback rate

Output:

```text
WorkloadShape:
  prefill_heavy: true
  decode_heavy: false
  long_context_tail: p95=48000
  batchability: low
  context_reuse: high
  memory_pressure: high
  event_driven: false
```

This prevents cargo-cult hardware selection.

### W8402 Memory-Wall And Data-Motion Ledger

The memory wall is often the product wall.

Track:

- HBM reads and writes
- CPU DRAM reads and writes
- PCIe or interconnect movement
- CXL memory access
- KV cache residency
- activation checkpoint movement
- tensor layout conversions
- host/device synchronization
- kernel launch overhead
- page faults or cache misses
- quantization/dequantization traffic
- remote storage reads

Ledger output:

- bytes moved per successful task
- bytes moved per accepted token
- memory bandwidth saturation
- memory capacity margin
- cache hit rate
- layout conversion waste
- dominant movement edge

Claim rule:

- do not call a model "compute optimized" if it is actually dominated by memory
  movement, context transfer, or KV residency.

### W8403 Operator, Dtype, And Backend Capability Matrix

Every backend has a truth table.

Matrix dimensions:

- operator support
- dynamic shape support
- sequence length support
- attention variant support
- sparse operator support
- KV cache format
- quantization format
- FP8, FP6, FP4, INT8, INT4, binary/ternary support
- custom kernel path
- graph capture support
- runtime fallback
- browser or mobile availability
- hardware security compatibility

Backends:

- CUDA/TensorRT-LLM
- ROCm/HIP
- OpenVINO
- Core ML
- Qualcomm AI Hub/QNN
- WebNN/WebGPU
- ONNX Runtime execution providers
- IREE/MLIR
- TVM Relax/TensorIR
- MLC LLM
- vendor NPU stacks
- FPGA/RTL prototype path
- analog/in-memory research path
- neuromorphic research path

Hard rule:

- unsupported operator fallback must be visible and priced. Silent fallback is
  a product defect.

### W8404 HBM, CXL, And Memory Pool Planner

Some artifacts fail because weights or KV state do not fit the right memory.

Plan:

- HBM capacity
- HBM bandwidth
- host DRAM pressure
- CXL memory tier
- CXL latency class
- memory pooling eligibility
- tensor placement
- KV placement
- retrieval cache placement
- paging risk
- batch admission limit
- prefill/decode split

Use CXL only when workload tolerance permits it.

Rules:

- hot decode KV stays close to compute unless evidence says otherwise
- cold retrieval or inactive context may live in slower tier
- CXL placement cannot create hidden p99 latency violations
- memory-pool savings must include tail latency and failure-domain risk

### W8405 Multi-Backend Portfolio Compiler

One artifact may need several backend plans.

Portfolio:

- GPU high-throughput path
- CPU fallback path
- NPU/mobile low-power path
- browser local path
- edge gateway path
- CXL-backed long-context path
- batch/offline path
- confidential/sovereign path

The compiler should output a route portfolio:

```text
artifact:
  routine_short_context -> local NPU
  long_context_retrieval -> GPU plus HBM plan
  PHI workflow -> sovereign GPU or local device
  outage mode -> CPU small student
  browser demo -> WebNN/WebGPU constrained student
```

This turns hardware heterogeneity into product reliability instead of support
chaos.

### W8406 Vendor Backend Adapter Contracts

Adapters should be contract-bound, not ad hoc.

Adapter contract:

- supported models and architectures
- supported operators
- supported dtypes
- supported sequence lengths
- graph compile constraints
- runtime initialization cost
- cold start cost
- memory requirements
- tuning options
- deterministic/reproducible modes
- telemetry emitted
- known failure modes
- license and redistribution constraints

First vendor paths:

- NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM
- AMD ROCm
- Intel OpenVINO
- Qualcomm AI Hub/QNN
- Apple Core ML
- ONNX Runtime execution providers
- WebNN/WebGPU
- IREE
- TVM Relax

Acceptance:

- an adapter is not "ready" until it can emit a SiliconFitReceipt for a fixture
  and fail honestly when unsupported.

### W8407 FPGA, ASIC, And Chiplet Target Model

Kolm should prepare for custom hardware without pretending to own silicon.

Target model fields:

- compute array type
- on-chip SRAM
- external memory
- interconnect
- chiplet packaging class
- UCIe or package interconnect assumptions
- CXL attachment
- supported dtypes
- supported sparsity
- activation function support
- host runtime
- firmware/update model
- power envelope
- manufacturing maturity

Use cases:

- enterprise appliance partner wants a Kolm edge box
- hardware startup wants `.kolm` conformance badge
- defense customer wants air-gapped accelerator evaluation
- cloud provider wants artifact-aware accelerator scheduling

Kolm does not need to build the chip first. It needs the contract that tells
which chip designs would actually improve artifact outcomes.

### W8408 Kernel Autotuning With Receipts

Autotuning can create hidden regressions.

Tune:

- matmul kernels
- attention kernels
- KV cache kernels
- quantize/dequantize kernels
- layout transforms
- sampling kernels
- embedding lookup
- convolution/sensor kernels
- sparse kernels
- sequence packing

Receipt fields:

- candidate space
- hardware target
- input distribution
- selected kernel
- measured latency
- measured variance
- numerical delta
- K-Score delta
- thermal state
- rejected candidates
- replay command

Rule:

- tuned kernel promotion requires both performance improvement and behavior
  retention under the artifact's own evaluation contract.

### W8409 Sparsity And Event-Driven Execution Planner

Distilled models often create structure that hardware can exploit.

Signals:

- activation sparsity
- attention sparsity
- token-level early stop
- layer skip opportunity
- expert sparsity
- event-triggered inference
- sensor-triggered wake
- tool-call branch sparsity
- schema branch sparsity

Planner output:

- dense baseline
- static sparse plan
- dynamic sparse plan
- event-driven plan
- fallback dense path
- correctness guard

Neuromorphic and event-driven accelerators become realistic for narrow,
high-value workloads when the artifact can prove event structure.

### W8410 Neuromorphic Target Path

Neuromorphic systems are not generic LLM servers.

Candidate workloads:

- event streams
- anomaly detection
- sensor fusion
- robotics reflex gating
- scheduling/optimization
- sparse temporal classification
- low-latency always-on monitoring
- wake policy

Compile:

- event encoding
- spike or event graph
- temporal window
- sparse connectivity
- state update policy
- energy model
- fallback dense verifier

Claim rule:

- do not market neuromorphic support for general text generation. Market it for
  event-driven sub-artifacts only when fixtures prove utility and fallback.

### W8411 Analog And In-Memory Compute Tolerance Planner

Analog/in-memory compute trades exactness for energy and locality.

Risk model:

- device noise
- drift
- nonlinearity
- write variability
- quantization limits
- tile capacity
- ADC/DAC overhead
- calibration cost
- retraining requirement
- aging

Kolm fit:

- small stable subgraphs
- tolerant classification
- embedding transforms
- sensor and speech workloads
- approximate reranking
- ensemble member acceleration

Acceptance:

- analog plan must include error envelope and K-Score retention
- regulated decisions need dense verifier or abstention path
- no analog acceleration claim without workload-specific tolerance proof

### W8412 Precision Ladder And Numeric Risk Compiler

Precision is a policy, not a checkbox.

Ladder:

- FP32 reference
- BF16/FP16
- FP8
- FP6
- FP4
- INT8
- INT4
- binary/ternary research path
- mixed precision by layer
- mixed precision by token phase
- mixed precision by risk class

Risk fields:

- sensitive layers
- sensitive tokens
- unstable logits
- schema boundary risk
- safety-class output risk
- numeric drift
- calibration loss
- distribution shift risk

Rule:

- low precision can be more aggressive for low-risk repetitive tasks than for
  high-risk regulated outputs. The artifact owns that distinction.

### W8413 Layout, Fusion, And Memory-Planning Compiler

Data layout can dominate performance.

Compile:

- tensor layout
- KV cache layout
- packed sequence format
- page size
- tile shape
- fusion plan
- operator scheduling
- memory arena
- activation reuse
- DMA/transfer plan
- host-device boundary

Smoke simulation:

```text
before:
  attention path has 7 layout conversions and 2 host synchronizations
after:
  fused path has 1 layout conversion and no host sync
accept:
  p95 latency improves 28 percent
  K-Score unchanged within confidence interval
```

### W8414 Thermal, Power, And Bandwidth Co-Optimizer

Peak throughput can be the wrong objective.

Optimize for:

- sustained throughput
- p95/p99 latency
- thermal throttling onset
- battery drain
- power cap compliance
- memory bandwidth saturation
- fan/noise limit for edge appliance
- mobile UX
- carbon and energy budget

The scheduler should know when a slower model is better because it avoids
thermal collapse, battery drain, or bandwidth contention.

### W8415 Hardware Procurement And Capacity Advisor

Turn hardware fit into buying leverage.

Inputs:

- workload shapes
- artifact portfolio
- retention requirements
- latency targets
- privacy/sovereignty rules
- expected growth
- support burden
- energy price
- depreciation window
- vendor lock-in risk

Output:

- buy GPU cluster
- rent managed GPU
- use CPU/NPU edge
- use BYOC
- use CXL memory tier
- use appliance partner
- do not buy yet; distill first

This prevents customers from buying hardware before Kolm has proven whether
distillation or routing can delete the need.

### W8416 Accelerator Conformance Benchmark Harness

Every target needs a conformance harness.

Fixtures:

- representative prompts
- long-context tail
- schema outputs
- tool-call traces
- multimodal tensors
- sensor streams
- safety cases
- privacy cases
- adversarial cases
- numerical sensitivity cases
- thermal soak
- memory pressure

Outputs:

- pass/fail
- latency bands
- throughput bands
- energy bands
- K-Score retention
- failure mode
- unsupported operator list
- fallback behavior

No conformance, no target badge.

### W8417 Reproducible Performance Claim Gate

Performance claims are easy to game.

Require:

- exact artifact version
- backend version
- hardware SKU
- driver version
- firmware version where relevant
- workload fixture
- batch/concurrency
- sequence lengths
- measurement window
- warmup policy
- thermal state
- power state
- confidence interval
- baseline

Blocked claims:

- "up to 10x" without fixture
- "NPU optimized" without backend receipt
- "runs on any device" without capability ledger
- "lower power" without functional unit
- "near teacher quality" without K-Score retention

### W8418 Hardware Marketplace Class

Marketplace should expose hardware fit.

Fields:

- supported accelerator classes
- required memory
- required dtypes
- backend compiler
- runtime engine
- conformance fixtures
- K-Score retention by target
- latency/energy/cost bands
- unsupported modes
- fallback path
- device trust requirements
- carbon/thermal caveats

Buyer result:

- an enterprise can filter artifacts by actual fleet and accelerator class,
  not by vague "edge" or "GPU" labels.

### W8419 Telemetry Feedback For Hardware Co-Design

Kolm can build a proprietary hardware intelligence moat.

Private telemetry:

- workload shape
- operator hot spots
- memory bottlenecks
- unsupported fallback
- K-Score deltas by target
- thermal throttling
- quantization sensitivity
- runtime crash signature
- driver incompatibility
- user-visible latency pain

Privacy:

- no raw prompts by default
- aggregate by artifact class and hardware class
- tenant opt-in for hardware partner sharing
- preserve sovereignty and rights constraints

Moat:

- thousands of real artifact runs reveal what hardware should be built next.

### W8420 Silicon Partner Program

Create a partner program for hardware vendors.

Partner outputs:

- target capability file
- backend adapter
- conformance results
- performance receipts
- known limitations
- customer deployment guide
- support escalation path
- benchmark reproduction packet

Partner classes:

- GPU provider
- NPU/mobile provider
- edge appliance vendor
- CXL memory vendor
- FPGA/ASIC startup
- analog/in-memory research partner
- neuromorphic research partner

Kolm should become the buyer-facing proof layer that hardware vendors use to
show real enterprise AI value.

### W8421 Trust, Sovereignty, And Confidential Hardware Placement

Hardware fit cannot ignore trust boundaries.

Placement must consider:

- device trust class
- confidential compute support
- sovereign region
- tenant key policy
- export-control class
- firmware attestation
- support access mode
- operator approval

Example:

- fastest accelerator is rejected because firmware trust is unknown
- lower-carbon region is rejected because sovereignty policy blocks it
- NPU local path wins because raw data cannot leave device
- confidential GPU path wins for regulated batch distillation

The compiler must optimize under policy, not after policy.

### W8422 Hardware Failure And Fallback Planner

Accelerators fail in boring ways.

Failure modes:

- driver crash
- unsupported op
- memory fragmentation
- thermal throttle
- GPU eviction
- spot interruption
- NPU compiler failure
- browser backend unavailable
- firmware update changes performance
- CXL memory tier degraded
- analog calibration drift

Fallback outputs:

- retry same backend
- route to lower precision
- route to CPU small student
- route to cloud teacher
- route to human review
- block high-risk action
- open repair ticket

The artifact should own fallback behavior instead of relying on platform glue.

### W8423 Hardware-Aware K-Score Axes

Add K-Score axes for hardware fit:

- retention under target backend
- numerical stability
- memory conformance
- p95/p99 latency conformance
- energy per accepted task
- thermal stability
- unsupported fallback rate
- target reproducibility
- device trust compatibility
- hardware failure recovery

This makes "best model" inseparable from "best deployed artifact."

### W8424 Account, API, CLI, And TUI Surface Contract

Eventually expose hardware fit everywhere.

Account:

- artifact hardware fit matrix
- unsupported target explanations
- performance receipts
- procurement advisor
- partner badges

API:

- target capability list
- fit evaluation endpoint
- conformance report endpoint
- receipt verifier endpoint

CLI/TUI:

```text
kolm hardware targets --json
kolm hardware fit artifact.kolm --target nvidia-h100 --json
kolm hardware fit artifact.kolm --target qualcomm-npu --json
kolm hardware conformance receipt.json --json
kolm hardware procure --workload traffic.json --json
```

Copy rule:

- public website should say "hardware-fit receipts" only after implementation
  exists. Until then it is a roadmap invention.

### W8425 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Build fixtures before integrations.

Synthetic targets:

- HBM-rich GPU
- VRAM-limited consumer GPU
- ROCm GPU
- CPU-only fallback
- mobile NPU
- browser WebNN backend
- CXL memory tier
- FPGA prototype
- analog approximate tile
- neuromorphic event processor

Synthetic workloads:

- short structured extraction
- long-context retrieval
- tool-call agent
- PHI redaction
- sensor anomaly
- batch distillation
- multimodal document parse

Expected decisions:

- accept target
- reject target
- accept with fallback
- accept diagnostics only
- require distillation first
- require quantization repair
- require hardware purchase

### W8426 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. SiliconFitIR.
2. workload shape profiler.
3. operator/dtype/backend capability matrix.
4. memory-motion ledger.
5. first GPU/CPU/NPU backend adapters as static capability files.
6. conformance benchmark harness.
7. precision ladder and numeric risk evaluator.
8. kernel/autotune receipt format.
9. hardware-aware K-Score axes.
10. fit planner and fallback planner.
11. account/API/CLI/TUI hardware-fit surfaces.
12. partner certification packet.
13. claim gate for hardware-performance language.

First implementation scope:

- one NVIDIA GPU fixture
- one CPU fallback fixture
- one mobile/NPU fixture
- one browser/WebNN fixture
- one unsupported-op failure
- one long-context memory-wall fixture
- one quantization retention fixture

Suggested implementation files:

```text
src/silicon-fit-ir.js
src/workload-shape-profiler.js
src/hardware-capability-matrix.js
src/memory-motion-ledger.js
src/backend-fit-planner.js
src/silicon-fit-receipt.js
src/hardware-claim-gate.js
docs/hardware-fit-fixtures.md
test/fixtures/hardware-fit/*.json
tests/wave8400-silicon-fit-ir.test.js
tests/wave8416-accelerator-conformance.test.js
tests/wave8425-hardware-fit-acceptance.test.js
```

Implementation principle:
do not start with vendor demos. Start with target-independent evidence. Vendor
backends are plugins into the receipt contract.

### W8427 Silicon Fit Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

```text
input:
  artifact
  workload shape
  target capability
  backend compiler
  runtime engine
  operator matrix
  memory-motion profile
  precision plan
  conformance fixture
  fallback plan
checks:
  target explicit
  backend version explicit
  operator support complete or fallback priced
  memory capacity and bandwidth within plan
  K-Score retention within policy
  latency/energy/cost claim has functional unit
  numerical risk accepted
  device trust and sovereignty gates preserved
  failure fallback defined
fail:
  unsupported op silently falls back
  benchmark omits workload shape
  low precision changes regulated output
  hardware claim lacks target receipt
  target plan violates privacy or sovereignty
pass:
  signed SiliconFitReceipt
```

### W8428 Ultimate Hardware Co-Design Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should turn hardware choice from guesswork into artifact evidence. The
best product is not "we support many runtimes." The best product is "for this
workflow, under this policy, this artifact is provably best on this hardware
class, with this precision, this memory plan, this fallback, and this retained
K-Score."

Strategic position:
AI companies are entering a hardware-fragmented era: GPUs, NPUs, local devices,
browser accelerators, CXL memory, chiplets, analog prototypes, neuromorphic
research systems, and sovereign appliances will all coexist. Kolm can own the
neutral compiler layer that converts model behavior into hardware truth. That
would make `.kolm` artifacts valuable to software teams, hardware vendors,
cloud providers, enterprises, and regulators at the same time.

## Constraint Solver, Decision Intelligence, And Quantum-Inspired Planning Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

- Kolm now has many powerful knobs: capture, routing, distillation, teacher
  council, active learning, quantization, hardware fit, device trust,
  sovereignty, human review, marketplace release, procurement, incident
  response, and customer adoption. If every subsystem chooses locally, the
  product becomes a bag of heuristics. The next frontier is one decision
  compiler that chooses the globally valid action under hard constraints and
  measurable uncertainty.
- Existing layers include route optimization, Bayesian experiments, SMT rights
  checks, economics planning, and hardware fit. They still need a shared
  optimization substrate that can express "do not train on this data," "do not
  route PHI here," "do not exceed budget," "do not violate latency," "do not
  claim support without proof," "do not overuse human reviewers," and "do not
  buy hardware before distillation value is known" in one auditable plan.
- The breakthrough is not "use OR-Tools" or "use quantum annealing." The
  breakthrough is to compile Kolm product choices into a typed optimization
  problem with hard constraints, soft objectives, proof obligations, fallback
  modes, and decision receipts. Classical solvers own most production cases.
  Quantum-inspired or annealing-style methods become research backends for
  large combinatorial portfolio problems only when they beat classical baselines
  on replayable fixtures.

Kolm invention: `DecisionOptimalityReceipt`.

This receipt proves that a product action was selected by a scoped solver:

- decision context
- candidate action set
- hard constraints
- soft objectives
- uncertainty model
- solver backend
- baseline policy
- selected action
- rejected actions
- infeasible constraints
- optimality gap or proof status
- sensitivity analysis
- expected metric deltas
- policy and rights evidence
- fallback plan
- replay fixture
- claim scope

Product goal:
Kolm should be able to answer why it chose "route locally," "ask teacher,"
"distill," "collect labels," "buy GPU," "use NPU," "defer batch," "human
review," "block," "rollback," "ship," or "do nothing" with a signed, replayable
decision packet.

### W8500 DecisionIR Schema

Define a common IR for product decisions.

Core objects:

- `DecisionContext`
- `CandidateAction`
- `HardConstraint`
- `SoftObjective`
- `UncertaintyModel`
- `SolverBackend`
- `BaselinePolicy`
- `DecisionResult`
- `OptimalityEvidence`
- `SensitivityReport`
- `DecisionOptimalityReceipt`

Required fields:

- `decision_id`
- `tenant_id`
- `artifact_id`
- `decision_class`
- `candidate_set_hash`
- `constraint_set_hash`
- `objective_set_hash`
- `evidence_snapshot_hash`
- `solver_backend`
- `solver_version`
- `time_budget_ms`
- `selected_action`
- `rejected_actions`
- `infeasible_constraints`
- `objective_value`
- `optimality_gap`
- `uncertainty_interval`
- `baseline_comparison`
- `policy_scope`
- `replay_fixture_hash`
- `claim_scope`

Acceptance:

- no high-impact automated decision without a DecisionIR record
- every hard constraint must be machine-checkable or explicitly manual-review
- every solver result must preserve policy, rights, and safety gates
- "optimal" claims require optimality proof or bounded optimality gap

### W8501 Product Decision Taxonomy

Unify decision classes across the stack.

Decision classes:

- model route
- local/cloud/human/block selection
- distill vs prompt vs retrieval vs no action
- teacher council allocation
- active learning sample selection
- evaluation fixture selection
- runtime profile selection
- quantization precision plan
- hardware target selection
- batch scheduling
- device rollout cohort
- legal hold/deletion conflict
- incident response action
- marketplace release
- pricing/discount exception
- procurement recommendation
- partner escalation

Each decision class must define:

- allowed actions
- forbidden actions
- required evidence
- metric objective
- failure blast radius
- human approval threshold
- replay fixture type

This prevents every team from inventing its own hidden optimizer.

### W8502 Hard Constraint Compiler

Compile non-negotiable requirements.

Constraint families:

- data rights
- privacy and PHI/PII rules
- data residency
- sovereignty and export control
- device trust
- safety case
- budget ceiling
- latency ceiling
- human approval requirement
- legal hold
- license restrictions
- package/release readiness
- certification claim scope
- hardware capability
- minimum K-Score

Output forms:

- CP-SAT constraints
- MILP constraints
- SMT predicates
- simple deterministic guards
- manual-review obligations

Rule:

- a soft objective can never compensate for a hard constraint failure. Cheaper,
  faster, greener, or higher conversion is irrelevant if the action is illegal,
  unsafe, unsupported, or unproven.

### W8503 Soft Objective And Utility Compiler

Compile objectives after hard constraints pass.

Objectives:

- maximize expected K-Score
- maximize teacher retention
- minimize critical failure risk
- minimize cost per successful task
- minimize p95/p99 latency
- minimize cloud exposure
- minimize human-review load
- maximize learning value
- maximize margin
- minimize carbon/energy
- maximize portability
- maximize audit readiness
- maximize adoption progress

Utility form:

```text
maximize:
  quality_value
  - risk_penalty
  - cost_penalty
  - latency_penalty
  - privacy_penalty
  - support_penalty
  + learning_value
subject to:
  all hard constraints
```

Every term needs units, source evidence, and a claim boundary.

### W8504 Solver Backend Router

Choose the right solver for the problem.

Backends:

- deterministic rule engine for simple gates
- SMT solver for logical satisfiability and unsat cores
- CP-SAT for scheduling, assignment, and integer choices
- MILP/MIP for linear or piecewise-linear portfolio allocation
- min-cost flow for routing and capacity planning
- Bayesian optimization for expensive black-box tuning
- contextual bandit for low-risk online policy tuning
- Monte Carlo tree search for sequential planning
- stochastic programming for demand uncertainty
- quantum-inspired annealing for large rugged binary search spaces
- hardware quantum annealing as research backend only

Router policy:

- simplest sound solver wins
- exact solver beats heuristic when feasible
