  - Compile/distill: training plans use leakage-safe K-Score, label, teacher,
    failure, and data-right signals.
  - Enterprise-to-device: rollout decisions use historical fleet, battery,
    memory, runtime, revocation, and support signals.

### W8800 SignalStoreIR Schema

Define a canonical IR for AI product signals.

Core objects:

- `SignalEntity`
- `SignalDefinition`
- `SignalValue`
- `SignalView`
- `OnlineSignalStore`
- `OfflineSignalStore`
- `PointInTimeJoinPlan`
- `MaterializationJob`
- `WatermarkState`
- `LateEventPolicy`
- `SignalQualityReport`
- `SignalSkewReport`
- `SignalConsistencyReceipt`

Required fields:

- `signal_id`
- `signal_name`
- `entity_key`
- `entity_type`
- `event_time`
- `processing_time`
- `ingestion_time`
- `source_system`
- `source_event_id`
- `feature_version`
- `transformation_hash`
- `value_type`
- `value`
- `null_semantics`
- `ttl`
- `lookback_window`
- `rights_scope`
- `privacy_scope`
- `materialization_state`
- `online_value_hash`
- `offline_value_hash`
- `watermark`
- `late_event_policy`
- `claim_scope`

Acceptance:

- every product decision with learned or computed signals records the signal
  set and point-in-time timestamp
- no route, distill, score, deploy, price, or authority decision may silently
  use today's state for a historical event
- every signal has null semantics, freshness, and rights scope
- signal definitions are versioned and rollbackable
- online and offline values can be compared for skew

### W8801 Event-Time Spine And Watermark Policy

Unify event time across AI traffic, training, eval, and device telemetry.

Time fields:

- event time: when the customer or runtime action happened
- processing time: when Kolm processed it
- ingestion time: when Kolm stored it
- availability time: when it became usable for a decision
- label time: when a quality or human label became available
- correction time: when a late correction arrived

Watermark policy:

- route decisions use latest signal values not later than decision time
- training datasets use label event time with lookback windows
- delayed labels update future training, not past decisions
- late device telemetry can correct dashboards but cannot retroactively justify
  a shipped claim without a revised receipt

Failure mode:

- a model appears accurate because it trained on labels or incident knowledge
  that arrived after the decision being simulated

### W8802 Online/Offline Signal Consistency

Guarantee that online serving and offline replay see consistent features.

Consistency targets:

- same transformation code or declared equivalent
- same entity keys
- same null semantics
- same TTL rules
- same late-event policy
- same privacy filters
- same feature version
- same source rights

Online path:

- low latency
- latest materialized value
- TTL enforcement
- per-tenant isolation
- fallback to conservative default when stale

Offline path:

- historical completeness
- point-in-time joins
- leakage prevention
- reproducible replays
- batch validation

Kolm-specific rule:

- a feature can be "dashboard-grade" without being "decision-grade"
- decision-grade signals must pass online/offline consistency checks

### W8803 Point-In-Time Join For Route, Distill, And K-Score

Apply feature-store point-in-time discipline to AI product decisions.

Decision classes:

- route acceptance
- teacher fallback
- human review
- distillation candidate selection
- architecture search
- quantization profile
- K-Score calibration
- prompt compression
- provider migration
- ROI estimate
- device rollout
- artifact publish
- authority grant

Join rule:

- join each decision row to the latest signal value available before the
  decision's event time, within an explicit lookback window

Signals:

- provider latency
- provider cost
- provider error rate
- teacher reliability
- task K-Score history
- tenant policy
- data rights
- device health
- route confidence
- calibration version
- incident state
- pricing plan

Acceptance:

- every offline simulation can state exactly which signals were known at the
  time
- future labels and policy changes cannot leak into historical training
- replay output changes only when the signal version or join policy changes

### W8804 Capture Signal Registry

Turn captured model traffic into reusable, governed signals.

Signal types:

- task class
- route outcome
- model provider
- token count
- latency
- cost
- confidence
- refusal
- schema validity
- human review outcome
- user correction
- tool call
- data class
- rights state
- prompt compression opportunity
- tokenizer profile

Registry fields:

- source event
- signal owner
- allowed uses
- privacy class
- rights class
- retention
- aggregation policy
- default TTL
- downstream consumers

Product value:

- capture becomes the raw material for routing, distillation, ROI, K-Score, and
  product readiness without becoming an ungoverned data lake

### W8805 Confidence, Quality, And Failure Signal Store

Make quality signals reusable across planners.

Signals:

- K-Score axis
- critical failure class
- teacher disagreement
- verifier disagreement
- human label
- correction label
- abstention outcome
- schema failure
- citation failure
- protected lexeme failure
- redaction failure
- incident link

Consumers:

- route optimizer
- active learning
- distillation planner
- student architecture search
- quantizer
- prompt refactorer
- product graph readiness
- account dashboard

Failure mode:

- distillation planner uses a pass/fail score but ignores critical failure
  class and repeats the same safety regression

### W8806 Cost, Latency, And Provider Signal Store

Make commercial and runtime signals point-in-time correct.

Signals:

- provider price version
- input token cost
- output token cost
- cached token cost
- observed latency p50/p95/p99
- error rate
- throttling rate
- context limit
- model deprecation state
- route policy
- committed-use discount
- marketplace pass-through fee

Use cases:

- historical ROI calculation
- savings-based pricing
- route simulation
- provider migration
- cost anomaly detection
- enterprise invoice reconciliation

Acceptance:

- a historical savings claim uses the price sheet and route state from that
  period or explicitly declares a normalized methodology

### W8807 Authority, Privacy, And Rights Signal Store

Bind decision signals to policy state.

Signals:

- user permission
- group membership
- tenant policy
- data rights
- consent
- deletion state
- legal hold
- data residency
- privacy budget
- authority lease
- revocation epoch
- partner delegation

Rules:

- no signal can be reused for training, aggregate learning, or public benchmark
  unless rights scope allows it
- historical replay must use historical permissions when simulating what the
  system should have done, but current deletion and legal-hold rules govern
  what can be exported now

Product value:

- privacy, authority, and rights stop being post-hoc filters and become
  first-class signal dimensions

### W8808 Device, Runtime, And Fleet Signal Store

Device deployment needs online and historical signals.

Signals:

- device type
- OS version
- accelerator availability
- memory
- battery
- thermal state
- runtime version
- artifact version
- rollback state
- offline duration
- sync age
- inference latency
- local failure class
- update channel

Use cases:

- any-device compile
- fleet rollout
- local K-Score drift
- offline readiness
- device-specific quantization
- support triage
- rollback planning

Failure mode:

- compiler recommends a local artifact using today's device memory profile,
  but the field fleet had less memory at rollout time

### W8809 Feature Freshness, TTL, And Null Semantics

Every signal needs freshness semantics.

Freshness classes:

- real-time
- near-real-time
- hourly
- daily
- batch
- manual
- static until versioned
- unknown

Null semantics:

- unknown
- not applicable
- absent
- denied
- expired
- not yet observed
- redacted
- unavailable due to outage

Product rule:

- "null" is never enough for a product decision
- stale signals must either block, warn, degrade, or route to review

### W8810 Training-Serving Skew Detector

Detect skew between offline simulations and online behavior.

Skew classes:

- transformation skew
- timestamp skew
- null-handling skew
- TTL skew
- rights-filter skew
- provider-price skew
- feature-version skew
- materialization lag
- schema drift
- late-event drift

Detection:

- replay online decisions offline using recorded signals
- compare online and offline feature values
- compare decision outputs
- explain drift by signal, version, and timestamp

Acceptance:

- no route or distill policy promotion if skew exceeds budget
- skew report links to affected product claims

### W8811 Real-Time Signal Materialization

Some signals must be live enough for routing and authority decisions.

Materialization classes:

- route hot path
- authority hot path
- device hot path
- dashboard warm path
- training cold path
- finance close path

Hot path constraints:

- bounded latency
- bounded staleness
- conservative fallback
- per-tenant isolation
- no raw secret or private prompt value

Examples:

- provider p95 latency
- current throttle state
- authority revocation epoch
- tenant spend cap
- device rollout hold
- active incident state

### W8812 Late Event Reconciliation

Late events must correct learning without rewriting history dishonestly.

Late event types:

- delayed human label
- billing correction
- provider outage report
- device sync
- access revocation
- deletion request
- incident classification
- customer correction

Reconciliation rules:

- update future training datasets
- emit revised dashboards
- produce correction receipts
- mark old claims superseded when needed
- never pretend the system knew the late event earlier

Failure mode:

- post-incident label is backfilled into a route simulation and makes the old
  policy look safer than it was

### W8813 Signal Rights And Privacy Compiler

Feature stores often optimize availability; Kolm must optimize permitted use.

Allowed uses:

- online decision
- offline replay
- customer dashboard
- tenant-local training
- cross-tenant aggregate
- public benchmark
- research paper
- marketplace ranking
- support incident

For each signal:

- allowed use
- forbidden use
- retention
- deletion propagation
- aggregation threshold
- DP budget
- clean-room eligibility
- export class

Product rule:

- a signal can be visible to the customer dashboard but forbidden for Kolm's
  global learning loop

### W8814 Signal Versioning, Rollback, And Compatibility

Signal definitions are product contracts.

Versioned objects:

- feature definition
- transformation code
- source schema
- entity key
- null semantics
- TTL
- lookback window
- materialization schedule
- privacy policy
- consumer contract

Rollback:

- online store supports current and previous version during migration
- offline replay declares feature version
- product claims declare signal version
- old decisions remain replayable

Failure mode:

- route confidence feature changes scale from 0-1 to 0-100 and downstream
  policy silently accepts unsafe local routes

### W8815 Signal Graph For Product Decisions

Represent which signals influence which decisions.

Graph nodes:

- signal
- transformation
- source system
- product decision
- route policy
- distillation plan
- K-Score report
- ROI claim
- account readiness state
- incident
- artifact version

Edges:

- derived_from
- consumed_by
- materialized_to
- trained_on
- scored_by
- blocks
- supersedes
- corrected_by

Value:

- when a signal is wrong, Kolm can find every affected route, artifact, claim,
  dashboard, training run, and sales proof packet

### W8816 Signal Quality K-Score Axis

Add signal quality to K-Score.

Submetrics:

- point-in-time correctness
- online/offline consistency
- freshness
- null clarity
- rights correctness
- materialization latency
- skew rate
- late-event reconciliation
- lineage completeness
- replayability

Weighting:

- 18 percent point-in-time correctness
- 14 percent online/offline consistency
- 12 percent rights correctness
- 10 percent freshness
- 10 percent skew rate
- 8 percent materialization latency
- 8 percent null clarity
- 8 percent late-event reconciliation
- 6 percent lineage completeness
- 6 percent replayability

Claim boundary:

- do not claim "signal-ready" unless all decision-grade signal consumers pass
  this axis for the named product surface

### W8817 Account Signal Observability Surface

Post-auth account should expose signal health.

Views:

- signal catalog
- decision-grade vs dashboard-grade signals
- stale signal warnings
- materialization lag
- skew reports
- point-in-time replay status
- rights restrictions
- late event corrections
- feature version changes
- affected decisions
- recommended repairs

UX principle:

- users should not need to understand feature stores to know why a route,
  artifact, savings estimate, or readiness claim is blocked

### W8818 API, CLI, And TUI Research Contract

Research targets:

API:

- `GET /v1/signals/catalog`
- `GET /v1/signals/:id`
- `POST /v1/signals/point-in-time-join`
- `POST /v1/signals/replay-decision`
- `GET /v1/signals/skew`
- `GET /v1/signals/materialization`
- `POST /v1/signals/backfill`
- `GET /v1/signals/graph`

CLI:

- `kolm signals catalog --json`
- `kolm signals replay --decision <id> --json`
- `kolm signals skew --surface routing --json`
- `kolm signals materialization --json`
- `kolm signals graph --artifact <id> --json`

TUI:

- signal catalog
- skew inspector
- replay viewer
- materialization monitor
- rights/use matrix

Important:

- these remain research targets until implementation adds product graph
  ownership, route docs, tests, and claim-scope gates

### W8819 Backfill, Replay, And Historical Rebuilds

Backfills are dangerous unless scoped.

Backfill types:

- source correction
- late label import
- provider billing correction
- signal transformation bug
- privacy reclassification
- feature version migration
- incident annotation

Rules:

- every backfill has a reason, scope, and affected consumers
- old decisions are not overwritten without superseded receipts
- claim-impact report is emitted before public metric changes
- privacy/deletion constraints are enforced during rebuild

Acceptance:

- a backfill can improve future training without rewriting public historical
  evidence dishonestly

### W8820 Experiment And Feature-Flag Signal Coupling

Product experiments need consistent treatment state.

Signals:

- experiment id
- variant
- exposure time
- eligibility
- feature flag value
- override
- rollout cohort
- guardrail state
- rollback state

Rules:

- exposure is event-time stamped
- offline analysis uses exposure state known at decision time
- feature flags cannot silently change treatment definition
- AI safety guardrails are analyzed with the same exposure state

Value:

- experiments, rollout, routing, and K-Score calibration stop disagreeing about
  who saw what behavior

### W8821 Cross-Tenant Aggregate Signal Plane

Kolm's moat needs aggregate learning without raw cross-tenant leakage.

Aggregate signals:

- failure cluster frequency
- teacher reliability by task
- tokenizer cost inflation by language
- device runtime conformance
- redaction class errors
- protected lexeme failures
- route fallback triggers
- incident near-miss classes

Controls:

- rights allow aggregate use
- cohort threshold
- DP or noise policy where needed
- clean-room eligibility
- no tenant-identifying rare signal
- deletion propagation

Product value:

- Kolm improves defaults from broad usage while honoring tenant boundaries

### W8822 Shadow, Canary, And Live Signal Consistency

Promotion gates need the same signals across stages.

Stages:

- offline replay
- shadow
- canary
- beta
- general availability

Consistency checks:

- same signal definitions
- same route policy inputs
- same K-Score axes
- same feature flag exposure
- same rights filters
- same device target classes

Failure mode:

- shadow run passes because it has richer offline features than live hot path

### W8823 Signal Incident Forensics

When AI behavior fails, inspect the signal plane.

Incident questions:

- which signals were used?
- which signal was stale?
- which transformation version ran?
- was there training-serving skew?
- did a late event change the interpretation?
- did rights or permissions change?
- did a feature flag alter behavior?
- which artifacts and claims consumed the signal?

Forensic packet:

- decision replay
- signal values
- signal definitions
- materialization state
- watermarks
- late events
- skew report
- affected consumers
- correction plan

### W8824 FinOps And Revenue Signal Plane

Commercial surfaces need decision-grade signals too.

Signals:

- plan
- entitlement
- discount
- committed spend
- provider invoice
- pass-through cost
- gross margin
- support cost
- savings estimate
- credit balance
- sales stage
- renewal risk

Rules:

- ROI and pricing claims use point-in-time commercial signals
- invoice reconciliation distinguishes estimated and final costs
- sales dashboards cannot use product telemetry forbidden for commercial use

Value:

- sales, billing, and product analytics agree about the same customer reality

### W8825 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Fixture:

- 6 tenants
- 10 signal definitions
- 4 entity types
- 3 online stores
- 2 offline stores
- late labels
- provider price changes
- permission revocations
- device sync delays
- feature version migration
- canary exposure

Tests:

- point-in-time join blocks future leakage
- online/offline skew is detected
- stale signals degrade decisions
- late labels update future training only
- rights-restricted signal is not used for aggregate learning
- pricing replay uses historical price version
- device rollout replay uses historical fleet state
- feature flag exposure is event-time correct

Pass bar:

- no future leakage
- no hidden skew
- no rights violation
- no stale signal accepted for decision-grade use
- affected decisions are discoverable through signal graph

### W8826 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. SignalStoreIR schema.
2. Signal catalog and stable signal IDs.
3. Event-time, processing-time, ingestion-time fields.
4. Point-in-time join simulator.
5. Online/offline signal comparison.
6. Signal rights/use matrix.
7. Skew detector.
8. Materialization freshness monitor.
9. Signal graph.
10. Decision replay receipt.
11. Account signal health surface.
12. CLI/API/TUI parity.
13. Backfill and correction receipts.
14. Cross-tenant aggregate signal controls.

Do not build first:

- a generic data lake
- a dashboard of raw telemetry
- a feature store that ignores rights
- a route optimizer that trains on future labels
- a savings report that uses today's prices for old traffic

Fast MVP:

- catalog route, K-Score, cost, provider, rights, and device signals
- replay historical route decisions with point-in-time joins
- detect online/offline skew for route policy
- show signal health in account

### W8827 Signal Store Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

```text
input:
  decision
  signal definitions
  entity keys
  event time
  online values
  offline values
  feature versions
  watermarks
  rights scopes
checks:
  point-in-time join used
  no future label leakage
  online/offline values consistent
  signal version explicit
  TTL and null semantics explicit
  materialization freshness within budget
  rights allow the requested use
  late events reconciled
  skew report attached
  replay fixture present
fail:
  historical ROI uses current price sheet
  route replay uses post-incident label
  distill planner uses signal without rights scope
  online value differs from offline value without explanation
  stale authority signal allows tool use
pass:
  signed SignalConsistencyReceipt
```

### W8828 Ultimate Signal Compiler Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should become the platform where every AI product decision is made from a
known, versioned, rights-aware, point-in-time signal set. Route choices,
distillation plans, K-Score reports, artifact promotions, ROI claims, device
rollouts, and authority grants should all be replayable from the signals that
were actually available at the time.

Strategic position:
Most AI companies will drown in telemetry and call it learning. Kolm should
turn telemetry into decision-grade signals. The company that owns the signal
plane owns the compounding loop: what happened, what was known, what changed,
what improved, what regressed, and which decision should happen next. That is
how the product becomes better every day without corrupting its own evidence.

## Evidence Lakehouse, Receipt Warehouse, And Queryable Audit Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

- Kolm's strongest product idea is receipts: artifact receipts, K-Score
  reports, route decisions, tokenization receipts, authority receipts, signal
  consistency receipts, device trust receipts, clean-room receipts, compliance
  packets, decision receipts, incident receipts, and ROI receipts. But receipts
  only become a billion-dollar control plane if they are queryable at scale.
- A JSON receipt attached to one artifact is useful for proof. A lakehouse of
  receipts is useful for product management, audits, customer success, sales
  engineering, research, billing, compliance, incident response, and network
  effects. The missing layer is a typed evidence warehouse that turns every
  proof packet into columnar, time-travelable, schema-evolvable, low-cost,
  tenant-scoped analytic data.
- Existing layers define what should be proven. This layer defines how proof is
  stored, queried, exported, compacted, versioned, redacted, joined, and
  retained without breaking the integrity of the original signed receipt.
- The breakthrough is not "put receipts in S3." The breakthrough is a dual
  representation: immutable signed receipt objects for legal/audit proof plus
  typed analytic projections in open table formats for fast product queries,
  enterprise exports, compliance review, and research feedback loops.

Kolm invention: `EvidenceLakehouseReceipt`.

This receipt proves that proof data was stored and projected correctly:

- source receipt id
- receipt type
- receipt schema version
- tenant id
- artifact id
- product surface
- event time
- ingestion time
- storage object hash
- table snapshot id
- projection schema id
- column lineage
- redaction policy
- rights scope
- retention class
- deletion class
- compaction state
- queryability class
- export class
- time-travel pointer
- verification hash
- claim scope

Product goal:
Kolm should answer enterprise questions across all proof objects:

- Which artifacts had K-Score regressions after a provider change?
- Which savings claims used stale token prices?
- Which devices ran an artifact after revocation?
- Which enterprise account is blocked by missing package/certification proof?
- Which customer workflows justify distillation investment?
- Which tool scopes were denied by delegated authority?
- Which vertical has the strongest evidence for a case study?

State-of-the-art anchors:

- Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, and Apache Hudi show how object storage can become
  transactional analytic tables with snapshots, schema evolution, time travel,
  and table metadata.
- Parquet and Arrow show the disk and memory formats needed for cheap,
  vectorized analytic scans and portable data interchange.
- DuckDB and Apache DataFusion show that local, embedded, and server-side
  analytics can query open columnar data without forcing every customer into a
  warehouse vendor.
- Substrait-style query plans point toward portable analytic semantics across
  engines, useful for reproducible audit queries.
- Columnar format research highlights that ML and random-access workloads have
  different access patterns than ordinary BI scans, so Kolm must tune receipt
  projections for both aggregate reporting and targeted incident replay.

Strategic product position:

- AI platforms will all claim auditability. Kolm can make auditability a data
  product: signed objects for proof, columnar tables for analysis, time travel
  for replay, and export packages for enterprise buyers.
- This is a major sales-cycle weapon. Instead of saying "trust our dashboard,"
  Kolm can hand a security team a reproducible evidence query over scoped
  tables and signed source receipts.
- This also increases product velocity. Every new wave emits receipts into a
  common evidence lakehouse, so product strategy can query actual proof
  coverage, not rely on screenshots, anecdotes, or scattered logs.

### W8900 EvidenceLakehouseIR Schema

Define the canonical storage and projection model for Kolm evidence.

Core objects:

- `EvidenceObject`
- `ReceiptEnvelope`
- `ReceiptProjection`
- `EvidenceTable`
- `EvidenceSnapshot`
- `EvidenceManifest`
- `ColumnLineage`
- `RetentionPolicy`
- `RedactionProjection`
- `AuditQuery`
- `ExportPackage`
- `EvidenceLakehouseReceipt`

Required fields:

- `evidence_id`
- `receipt_id`
- `receipt_type`
- `receipt_schema`
- `tenant_id`
- `namespace_id`
- `artifact_id`
- `surface_id`
- `event_time`
- `ingestion_time`
- `source_object_uri`
- `source_object_hash`
- `projection_table`
- `projection_schema_id`
- `snapshot_id`
- `partition_key`
- `sort_key`
- `column_lineage_hash`
- `redaction_policy`
- `rights_scope`
- `retention_class`
- `deletion_class`
- `export_class`
- `verification_hash`
- `claim_scope`

Acceptance:

- every signed receipt has an immutable object representation
- every decision-grade receipt has at least one typed analytic projection
- every projection can trace each column back to source receipt fields
- every export declares whether it is raw, redacted, aggregate, or synthetic
- every table supports schema version tracking and snapshot identification

### W8901 Receipt Object Store And Immutable Proof Layer

Keep the source of truth immutable.

Object layout:

- tenant prefix
- receipt type
- event date
- receipt id
- content hash
- signature
- detached metadata
- optional encrypted payload

Rules:

- never rewrite signed receipt payloads
- never use a projection table as the legal source of truth
- every projection row links to source object hash
- every deleted/redacted export preserves deletion proof without exposing
  forbidden content

Product value:

- legal, compliance, and customer audit teams can verify the original object
  even if analytic projections evolve

### W8902 Columnar Projection Compiler

Compile nested receipts into queryable columns.

Projection classes:

- wide summary table
- normalized entity tables
- event fact table
- metric fact table
- artifact dimension table
- tenant dimension table
- source lineage table
- denial and failure table
- cost and ROI table
- compliance evidence table

Compilation rules:

- keep high-cardinality text out of hot aggregate tables unless needed
- separate private payloads from aggregate-safe columns
- dictionary-encode status and enum fields
- store hashes for sensitive identifiers
- preserve event and ingestion time
- include schema version and claim scope in every row

Failure mode:

- dashboard query reads a projection that dropped the claim-scope field and
  accidentally treats partial proof as shipped proof

### W8903 Open Table Format Strategy

Choose storage formats by workload instead of vendor fashion.

Candidates:

- Iceberg for open table metadata, snapshots, schema evolution, partition
  evolution, and cross-engine analytics
- Delta Lake where customer Databricks ecosystems require it
- Hudi where upsert-heavy streaming/timeline semantics are useful
- Parquet as the common columnar file base
- Arrow for in-memory interchange and zero-copy local analytics
- DuckDB or DataFusion for embedded audit queries and local proof packages

Kolm rule:

- the canonical evidence contract is engine-neutral
- each table-format projection must state supported features and missing
  semantics
- customer export can target their preferred lakehouse without changing source
  receipt truth

### W8904 Snapshot, Time Travel, And Claim Replay

Evidence questions often require "what did we know then?"

Snapshot uses:

- replay product graph at launch date
- reconstruct account readiness at procurement review
- verify K-Score calibration used for a public claim
- compare before/after provider migration
- reproduce billing and savings claims
- investigate incident blast radius
- prove deletion propagation over time

Rules:

- every claim references table snapshot ids or source receipt hashes
- every dashboard can declare "current" or "as of snapshot"
- every export package includes snapshot metadata
- expired snapshots require explicit retention reason when tied to legal hold

Failure mode:

- sales deck cites today's improved readiness for a customer proof packet that
  was generated before the missing evidence existed

### W8905 Schema Evolution And Backward Compatibility

Receipt schemas will evolve constantly.

Compatibility modes:

- additive nullable column
- additive required with default
- enum extension
- field rename with alias
- field deprecation
- nested struct evolution
- breaking version

Rules:

- no breaking schema change without migration projection
- old receipts remain queryable
- query definitions declare schema range
- generated docs list schema versions
- backfills produce correction receipts

Acceptance:

- a five-month-old enterprise audit query still runs after new receipt fields
  are added
- missing old fields render as explicit unavailable status, not silent null

### W8906 Evidence Partitioning And Cost Model

Partition and sort evidence by product usage.

Partition candidates:

- tenant
- receipt type
- event date
- product surface
- artifact id
- region
- claim scope
- retention class

Sort candidates:

- event time
- artifact id
- decision id
- incident id
- account id
- route id

Cost objective:

- minimize scans for account readiness
- minimize scans for incident replay
- minimize scans for audit export
- keep write amplification bounded
- avoid tiny-file explosions
- preserve deletion and legal hold semantics

Metric:

- cost per audit query
- p95 account evidence load time
- p95 incident query time
- storage overhead per receipt
- compaction debt

### W8907 Evidence Query Language

Define query patterns that product and auditors can understand.

Query classes:

- artifact evidence query
- route decision query
- K-Score claim query
- billing and ROI query
- readiness closeout query
- device rollout query
- delegated authority query
- signal consistency query
- incident blast-radius query
- deletion propagation query

Design:

- SQL-compatible where possible
- stable named views for common audits
- query templates for account UI
- exportable query plan
- result hash
- source receipt links

Product value:

- enterprise buyers can rerun a scoped query instead of trusting a static PDF

### W8908 Audit Query Reproducibility

Every serious audit query should be replayable.

Audit query packet:

- query id
- query text or logical plan
- table snapshot ids
- source receipt hash set
- engine name and version
- parameter values
- result hash
- redaction policy
- requester
- purpose
- generated time

Rules:

- query packet is signed
- result rows link to source receipt hashes
- redacted exports preserve row count and reason
- unsupported engine features fail closed

Failure mode:

- auditor cannot reproduce a compliance number because the dashboard used a
  mutable "latest" table view with no snapshot id

### W8909 Evidence Redaction And Export Projections

Export needs are different from internal analysis.

Export projection types:

- raw tenant export
- redacted customer audit export
- aggregate benchmark export
- public case-study export
- regulator packet
- support incident packet
- sales engineering packet
- clean-room packet

Redaction controls:

- remove secrets
- hash identifiers
- bucket rare categories
- suppress small cohorts
- strip private prompts
- preserve metric denominators
- include redaction manifest

Rule:

- no export is just a database dump
- every export is a compiled projection with purpose, rights, and proof scope

### W8910 Evidence Lakehouse For Product Graph And Readiness

Product readiness should be queryable, not hand-curated.

Tables:

- product surface table
- route ownership table
- readiness requirement table
- closeout gate table
- smoke probe table
- test result table
- screenshot audit table
- claim-scope table
- source evidence table

Queries:

- which surface has stale evidence?
- which route group lacks owner?
- which claim lacks source?
- which account page hides a readiness blocker?
- which product surface has no production smoke?

Product value:

- "100 percent done" becomes an evidence query with gaps, not a feeling

### W8911 Evidence Lakehouse For Distillation Science

The research lab needs analytic proof over experiments.

Tables:

- experiment plan
- teacher run
- student candidate
- architecture genome
- curriculum
- K-Score result
- failure cluster
- label batch
- quantization profile
- runtime profile
- paper claim

Queries:

- which architecture family improves healthcare extraction under memory cap?
- which teacher is most reliable by task family?
- which quantization profile fails protected lexemes?
- which failed experiment should not be repeated?

Moat:

- Kolm's private advantage becomes a structured scientific memory, not notes in
  scattered docs

### W8912 Evidence Lakehouse For Sales And Procurement

Sales teams need trustworthy proof fast.

Tables:

- buyer requirement
- evidence asset
- security control
- compliance packet
- case study metric
- ROI result
- procurement objection
- partner certification
- closeout status

Queries:

- show healthcare HIPAA/BAA evidence with date and scope
- show Enterprise custom pricing proof and sales-required path
- show how similar accounts reduced cost without overclaiming
- show missing certification gates honestly

Product rule:

- sales packets must be generated from evidence lakehouse snapshots, not copied
  from stale docs

### W8913 Evidence Lakehouse For Incident Response

Incident teams need fast joins across receipts.

Incident query needs:

- affected artifacts
- affected tenants
- affected devices
- route decisions
- provider calls
- tool calls
- authority leases
- signal values
- K-Score regressions
- deployment cohorts
- support interactions
- revocation status

Design:

- incident id becomes a first-class join key
- every incident packet records table snapshots
- near-miss records and actual incidents share core schema
- customer-visible export is redacted but complete enough for trust

Metric:

- time to first blast-radius estimate
- time to customer evidence packet
- unknown receipt percentage

### W8914 Evidence Lakehouse For Billing And Finance

Finance needs auditable evidence for revenue and cost.

Tables:

- API usage
- provider invoice
- storage usage
- compute usage
- pass-through cost
- plan entitlement
- savings estimate
- committed spend
- credit balance
- refund/adjustment
- gross margin

Rules:

- estimated cost and final invoice are separate fields
- historical price sheets are versioned
- savings claims reference source usage and methodology
- margin dashboards never mix tenant-private unsupported data

Outcome:

- billing disputes become evidence queries
- savings-based pricing can be audited
- CFO controls align with product proof

### W8915 Evidence Lakehouse For Model And Artifact Lineage

Artifact lineage should be analytically queryable.

Tables:

- artifact
- parent artifact
- source dataset
- model weights
- adapter
- tokenizer
- runtime profile
- compile target
- signature
- registry version
- deployment
- revocation

Queries:

- which deployed artifacts descend from a revoked source?
- which artifacts use a vulnerable runtime?
- which marketplace listings lack benchmark proof?
- which students inherit a tokenizer drift risk?

Rule:

- lineage graph remains the source model; lakehouse projection makes it
  queryable at scale

### W8916 Local Evidence Package

Some customers need evidence without granting live warehouse access.

Package modes:

- DuckDB file with Parquet extracts
- Arrow IPC bundle
- signed JSONL plus manifest
- customer lakehouse export
- air-gapped zip
- regulator packet

Package contents:

- query templates
- table snapshots
- source receipt hashes
- redaction manifest
- README
- verification command
- expiration and retention scope

Acceptance:

- customer can verify the package offline
- package does not contain forbidden raw prompts or secrets
- package result hashes match Kolm-generated packet

### W8917 Evidence API And Account Surface

Post-auth UX should expose evidence without making users write SQL.

Account views:

- evidence search
- artifact proof drawer
- readiness evidence
- audit exports
- incident packet builder
- sales/procurement packet builder
- billing proof
- query history
- redaction manifest

API targets:

- `GET /v1/evidence/receipts`
- `GET /v1/evidence/tables`
- `POST /v1/evidence/query`
- `POST /v1/evidence/export`
- `GET /v1/evidence/snapshots`
- `GET /v1/evidence/lineage/:id`

Rules:

- UI defaults to safe scoped queries
- raw SQL is admin/enterprise-only
- every export shows source receipt count and redaction class

### W8918 CLI And TUI Research Contract

Research targets:

CLI:

- `kolm evidence receipts --type kscore --json`
- `kolm evidence query readiness --surface public-docs-sdk --json`
- `kolm evidence export audit --tenant <id> --redacted`
- `kolm evidence snapshot --as-of <time> --json`
- `kolm evidence verify package.zip`
- `kolm evidence lineage --artifact <id> --json`

TUI:

- evidence table browser
- receipt search
- audit query runner
- export builder
- incident blast-radius view
- schema evolution view

Important:

- these are research targets until implementation adds tests, product graph
  ownership, route docs, permissions, and claim gates

### W8919 Query Performance And Index Strategy

Evidence queries must be fast enough for product UX.

Acceleration methods:

- partition pruning
- manifest pruning
- column projection
- predicate pushdown
- materialized views
- small-file compaction
- hot account summaries
- incident join indexes
- artifact lineage indexes
- local DuckDB cache for packages

Metric:

- p95 account evidence drawer load
- p95 export generation time
- p95 incident query time
- bytes scanned per query
- stale materialized view rate

Rule:

- performance optimization cannot drop claim scope, rights, or source hash

### W8920 Evidence Quality K-Score Axis

Add evidence warehouse quality to K-Score.

Submetrics:

- source receipt coverage
- projection completeness
- column lineage completeness
- snapshot reproducibility
- query reproducibility
- redaction correctness
- schema compatibility
- export verifiability
- query performance
- retention/deletion correctness

Weighting:

- 16 percent source receipt coverage
- 14 percent snapshot reproducibility
- 12 percent projection completeness
- 10 percent query reproducibility
- 10 percent redaction correctness
- 10 percent column lineage
- 8 percent schema compatibility
- 8 percent export verifiability
- 6 percent query performance
- 6 percent retention/deletion correctness

Claim boundary:

- "audit-ready" requires this axis by product surface and tenant scope

### W8921 Retention, Legal Hold, And Deletion Semantics

Evidence storage must honor retention without destroying auditability.

Classes:

- short-lived operational evidence
- customer audit evidence
- legal hold
- regulator packet
- public benchmark evidence
- billing evidence
- incident evidence
- deleted-source tombstone

Rules:

- delete forbidden payloads when required
- preserve tombstone and deletion proof
- freeze legal-hold receipts
- expire snapshots only after retention policy allows
- reproject aggregate tables after deletion when necessary

Failure mode:

- deletion request removes source object but aggregate table still exposes a
  rare identifying value

### W8922 Evidence Lakehouse Security Model

The evidence warehouse is high value.

Controls:

- tenant isolation
- row-level policy
- column-level policy
- purpose-based access
- just-in-time audit access
- export approval
- immutable source objects
- signed query packets
- access logs
- break-glass path

Threats:

- insider queries private prompts
- sales export leaks unsupported claim
- support packet exposes another tenant
- aggregate query reveals rare customer event
- warehouse admin bypasses receipt integrity

Rule:

- evidence access itself emits a DelegatedAuthorityReceipt and audit query
  packet

### W8923 Data Quality And Projection Drift Monitor

Projections can go wrong.

Drift checks:

- source receipt count vs projection row count
- null rate changes
- enum drift
- schema mismatch
- partition lag
- compaction errors
- duplicate receipt rows
- missing source hash
- redaction ratio anomaly
- query result delta after migration

Response:

- quarantine affected projection
- fall back to source receipt scan
- emit correction receipt
- mark dashboard and exports as degraded

Acceptance:

- projection corruption cannot silently make account readiness green

### W8924 Evidence Lakehouse For Public Benchmarks

Public benchmark claims need reproducible data packages.

Benchmark tables:

- task
- input
- expected output
- model/artifact
- output
- scorer
- K-Score
- judge result
- deterministic check
- cost
- latency
- environment
- claim scope

Release modes:

- raw public fixture
- redacted public fixture
- aggregate public report
- private customer fixture
- clean-room fixture

Rule:

- benchmark page links raw report, snapshot, scorer version, and unsupported
  claims

### W8925 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Fixture:

- 5 tenants
- 12 receipt types
- 4 schema versions
- 3 table formats
- 2 query engines
- redacted export
- legal hold
- deletion request
- backfilled K-Score
- billing correction
- incident packet

Tests:

- every projection row links to source receipt hash
- old audit query runs after schema evolution
- snapshot query reproduces historical product graph
- redacted export preserves counts without secrets
- deletion request removes private payload but keeps tombstone
- incident packet joins across route, authority, signal, and artifact receipts
- local evidence package verifies offline
- projection drift quarantines dashboard

Pass bar:

- no orphan projection rows
- no unsupported claim from missing scope
- no secret in export
- no deleted payload in aggregate
- no audit query without snapshot id

### W8926 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. EvidenceLakehouseIR schema.
2. Immutable receipt object layout.
3. Source receipt hash and signature index.
4. First columnar projection for product readiness receipts.
5. Snapshot id and table metadata tracking.
6. Query packet format.
7. Redacted export compiler.
8. Schema evolution compatibility gate.
9. Account evidence drawer.
10. CLI evidence query prototype.
11. Incident packet query.
12. Billing/ROI projection.
13. Local evidence package.
14. Evidence quality K-Score axis.

Do not build first:

- a generic analytics dashboard
- a mutable receipt table without source objects
- a warehouse export with raw private prompts
- a public benchmark table without snapshot and scorer version
- a claim dashboard that lacks source receipt hashes

Fast MVP:

- store source receipts immutably
- project readiness/K-Score/ROI summaries to Parquet-compatible tables
- run a reproducible readiness query by snapshot
- export a redacted audit packet with source receipt hashes

### W8927 Evidence Lakehouse Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

```text
input:
  source receipts
  projection schema
  table snapshot
  query packet
  export policy
checks:
  source objects immutable
  source hashes present
  projection rows linked
  schema version explicit
  snapshot id explicit
  claim scope retained
  rights scope retained
  redaction policy applied
  query result hash present
  export package verifiable
  retention/deletion status checked
fail:
  projection row has no source hash
  audit query uses mutable latest view
  export includes raw secret or private prompt
  schema change drops claim scope
  deleted source remains in public aggregate
pass:
  signed EvidenceLakehouseReceipt
```

### W8928 Ultimate Evidence Lakehouse Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should make proof queryable. Every artifact, route, K-Score report,
authority lease, signal decision, token optimization, device rollout, incident,
billing claim, and compliance packet should exist as both an immutable signed
receipt and a typed analytic row that can be queried, time-traveled, exported,
redacted, and verified.

Strategic position:
The winning AI infrastructure company will not merely generate evidence. It
will operationalize evidence. If Kolm owns the evidence lakehouse, it owns the
control room for the whole product: what is shipped, what is proven, what is
blocked, what is stale, what is risky, what can be sold, what can be audited,
and what should be built next. That makes receipts not just a compliance
feature, but the operating data layer for owned AI.

## Proof-Carrying AI, Assurance Cases, And Formal Claim Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

Most AI platforms treat trust as a web page, a model card, or a compliance
questionnaire. That is too weak for production AI. A buyer does not only need
to know that an output was signed, routed, redacted, or evaluated. The buyer
needs to know which claim was made, which assumptions were required, which
evidence supports it, which counterexamples would break it, and which checker
can independently validate the claim after the fact.

The existing Kolm direction already has receipts, K-Score, artifact signatures,
readiness gates, and an evidence lakehouse. The next jump is to compile those
pieces into proof-carrying AI artifacts. A .kolm artifact should not only say
"this was produced by Kolm." It should carry a typed claim graph that says:

- what safety, quality, cost, privacy, provenance, and runtime claims are made;
- what mathematical, empirical, policy, and operational assumptions they depend
  on;
- what evidence rows, benchmark packets, source spans, contracts, evals, and
  signatures support each claim;
- what is intentionally out of scope;
- what machine checker can replay the claim;
- what counterexample class invalidates the claim;
- what product UI must show when a claim is partial, stale, or blocked.

The strategic shift:

Do not compete only on "we route cheaper" or "we distill better." Compete on:
Kolm is the system that turns AI behavior into owned, portable, inspectable,
and machine-checkable claims. That makes Kolm the compiler, evaluator,
evidence warehouse, assurance-case builder, and procurement proof surface for
AI companies.

Research takeaways:

- W3C PROV formalizes provenance as entities, activities, and agents. Kolm
  should map every capture, compile, distill, eval, route, redaction, and export
  into a provenance bundle that can be independently consumed.
- OMG SACM and GSN show how assurance cases can be structured as claims,
  arguments, evidence, contexts, assumptions, and goals. Kolm should compile
  assurance cases automatically instead of asking customers to write them.
- TLA+, Alloy, Dafny, Lean, Rocq, Z3, and SMT-LIB show that different assurance
  levels need different verification engines. Kolm should not force all claims
  through one formal method. It should route claims to the cheapest sufficient
  checker.
- NIST AI RMF shows that governance needs risk framing, measuring, managing,
  and monitoring. Kolm should make those verbs executable through receipts and
  readiness gates.

New primitive:

`ProofCarryingArtifactReceipt`

```
receipt_type: proof_carrying_artifact
artifact_hash: sha256
artifact_version: semver
claim_graph_hash: sha256
assurance_case_hash: sha256
provenance_bundle_hash: sha256
checker_manifest_hash: sha256
counterexample_set_hash: sha256
evidence_snapshot_id: string
claim_scope:
  quality
  safety
  privacy
  cost
  latency
  reproducibility
  portability
  compliance
  runtime
claim_status:
  proven
  empirically_supported
  bounded
  partial
  blocked_external
  expired
checker_results:
  replay_passed: boolean
  formal_passed: boolean
  empirical_passed: boolean
  policy_passed: boolean
  counterexample_budget_remaining: integer
signatures:
  builder
  evaluator
  policy
  tenant
redaction:
  public_view_hash
  private_view_hash
```

Why it is 100x:

- Sales: enterprise buyers get an assurance packet generated from product use,
  not a static spreadsheet.
- Product: account pages can show exact claims, proof status, and blockers.
- Backend: every product route can return claim envelopes with proof links.
- Benchmarks: K-Score becomes not just a number, but a claim with calibration,
  counterexamples, and confidence.
- Distillation: every student model ships with a proof of what it preserves,
  what it loses, and where fallback routing must remain active.
- Runtime: edge deployments get local proof bundles that work without calling
  the cloud.
- Legal: marketing claims become auditable artifacts, reducing accidental
  overclaiming.
- Moat: the proprietary asset becomes the claim graph and counterexample
  library accumulated across thousands of workloads.

Failure mode to avoid:

Proof-carrying AI must not mean pretending statistical properties are formal
theorems. Kolm should distinguish:

- deductive claims: can be proven with type theory, SMT, model checking, or
  static analysis;
- empirical claims: supported by evals, confidence intervals, calibration data,
  and benchmark packets;
- policy claims: supported by policy engines, access logs, and attestation;
- operational claims: supported by live telemetry, SLO windows, and incidents;
- external claims: blocked until certification, partner adoption, or package
  release evidence exists.

If these are mixed, trust collapses. If they are separated and compiled into
one claim graph, Kolm becomes the most honest and most valuable AI
infrastructure layer.

### W9000 ProofClaimIR Schema

Define the typed claim language.

Objects:

- claim id;
- claim type;
- subject;
- scope;
- evidence requirements;
- verifier;
- assumptions;
- known exclusions;
- expiration;
- display contract;
- downstream dependencies.

Claim types:

- quality retention claim;
- hallucination reduction claim;
- redaction coverage claim;
- artifact reproducibility claim;
- cost saving claim;
- latency bound claim;
- device compatibility claim;
- policy compliance claim;
- model provenance claim;
- capture rights claim;
- rollback safety claim;
- calibration validity claim.

A claim must be invalid if it lacks:

- subject hash;
- evidence pointer;
- verifier type;
- scope;
- status;
- expiry;
- UI wording.

Implementation handoff:

- Add `ProofClaimIR` as a JSON schema, not free text.
- Compile all marketing, account, API, and CLI claims from this schema.
- Make stale claims fail the public claim-scope audit.

### W9001 Claim Graph Compiler

Compile individual claims into a dependency graph.

Graph nodes:

- artifact;
- dataset;
- capture namespace;
- teacher model;
- student model;
- eval report;
- redaction policy;
- route policy;
- device target;
- price table;
- legal basis;
- external proof;
- readiness gate.

Graph edges:

- derived_from;
- evaluated_by;
- bounded_by;
- requires;
- excludes;
- supersedes;
- contradicted_by;
- expires_with;
- visible_in;
- blocks_release.

Why this matters:

A website CTA, a pricing calculator, a benchmark page, a CLI command, and an
enterprise security answer can all depend on the same claim. Without a graph,
the site drifts. With a graph, the whole product says one thing.

Simulated example:

```
claim: student retains 97 percent task quality
requires:
  eval report hash
  teacher model version
  student artifact hash
  calibration dataset version
  confidence interval
blocks:
  homepage proof badge if confidence below threshold
  enterprise export if eval expired
```

### W9002 Assurance Case Generator

Generate a human-readable and machine-readable assurance case for every
enterprise artifact.

Generated case sections:

- top-level claim;
- context;
- assumptions;
- argument strategy;
- evidence list;
- unsupported subclaims;
- residual risk;
- reviewer checklist;
- machine replay command.

GSN-inspired structure:

```
goal: Artifact is suitable for regulated production deployment in namespace N
strategy: Decompose by quality, privacy, provenance, runtime, and operations
context: tenant policy, model family, device class, region, data scope
solution: K-Score report, redaction report, route trace, signature bundle
assumption: benchmark set covers task-critical cases
justification: confidence interval and human review threshold
```

Implementation handoff:

- Produce `assurance_case.json`.
- Produce `assurance_case.md`.
- Produce `assurance_case.public.json` with redacted evidence.
- Link every section to evidence lakehouse rows.

### W9003 Provenance Bundle Compiler

Map Kolm receipts into W3C PROV-compatible bundles.

Kolm mapping:

- entity: artifact, model, dataset, eval report, policy, route config;
- activity: capture, compile, distill, quantize, evaluate, sign, deploy;
- agent: tenant, user, service identity, provider, reviewer, automation;
- derivation: source artifact to compiled artifact;
- attribution: artifact to tenant and builder;
- association: activity to agent;
- bundle: versioned proof packet.

The PROV bundle must support:

- public projection;
- private projection;
- regulatory projection;
- legal hold projection;
- offline export.

Success condition:

An auditor should be able to start with a .kolm artifact hash and traverse the
whole chain of custody without logging into Kolm.

### W9004 Formal Checker Router

Route each claim to the cheapest sufficient checker.

Checker classes:

- schema checker;
- static policy checker;
- SMT checker;
- model checker;
- theorem prover;
- statistical verifier;
- replay verifier;
- human-review verifier;
- external-certification verifier.

Routing rules:

- Use schema checks for field shape and envelope invariants.
- Use SMT for bounded arithmetic, policy satisfiability, region constraints,
  quota limits, and route-policy contradictions.
- Use TLA+ or Alloy for concurrent deployment, rollback, approval, cache, and
  lease protocols.
- Use Dafny-style contracts for small deterministic transformations.
- Use Lean or Rocq for highest-assurance core definitions where the cost is
  justified.
- Use empirical verification for model quality and preference claims.

Product effect:

Kolm can show "machine checked" without implying every claim is formally
proven. The badge should say which checker passed.

### W9005 Counterexample Library

Make every claim adversarial by default.

Counterexample types:

- prompt that bypasses redaction;
- input that changes output schema;
- capture row that violates rights scope;
- route trace that exceeds latency bound;
- student answer that fails a critical class;
- provider change that alters semantics;
- device memory profile that breaks deployment;
- pricing table state that invalidates ROI;
- region/policy conflict that blocks export;
- benchmark fixture that exposes regression.

Every proof-carrying artifact should include:

- counterexample classes tested;
- counterexamples not yet tested;
- open counterexample budget;
- last adversarial search date;
- severity by class.

Moat:

Counterexample libraries become proprietary data. Competitors may copy the
compiler shape, but they will not have years of adversarial cases from real
tenant workloads.

### W9006 Empirical Claim Semantics

Define honest semantics for empirical AI claims.

Required fields:

- dataset id;
- sample size;
- task taxonomy;
- label source;
- evaluator version;
- teacher version;
- student version;
- confidence interval;
- metric formula;
- threshold;
- observed failures;
- date;
- freshness window.

Forbidden without evidence:

- "best";
- "guaranteed quality";
- "fully safe";
- "certified";
- "state of the art";
- "human level";
- "production ready";

Allowed when scoped:

- "passed the named benchmark under these conditions";
- "retained X percent K-Score on this task suite";
- "reduced cost by X percent in this measured namespace";
- "bounded by this fallback policy";
- "not certified; controls implemented."

This should feed website copy, docs, account UI, CLI, API docs, and sales
exports.

### W9007 K-Score Proof Envelope

Turn K-Score into an evidence-backed claim, not a raw score.

Envelope:

```
k_score:
  value: number
  axis_scores: object
  calibration_version: string
  confidence_interval: [low, high]
  human_preference_correlation: optional
  dataset_hash: sha256
  judge_hash: sha256
  counterexamples: array
  known_blind_spots: array
  scope_text: string
```

New axes:

- proof completeness;
- counterexample resistance;
- evidence replayability;
- calibration freshness;
- claim honesty.

Why:

If K-Score becomes the market metric, it cannot be just "Kolm says so." It
must be replayable, falsifiable, and separable by axis.

### W9008 Distillation Preservation Proof

Distillation must ship with preservation claims.

Preservation classes:

- intent preservation;
- schema preservation;
- tool-call preservation;
- reasoning-step preservation;
- citation preservation;
- privacy preservation;
- safety refusal preservation;
- latency/cost preservation;
- domain-critical edge-case preservation.

Output:

- preserved;
- degraded but routed;
- degraded and blocked;
- unknown;
- not in scope.

Implementation handoff:

- For every distill job, produce `preservation_matrix.json`.
- Attach the matrix to the artifact.
- Use it in runtime routing: if a claim is degraded on a class, route to cloud
  teacher or ask for review.

### W9009 Quantization Safety Proof

Quantization should not be only a compression step. It should be a claim
transform.

Each quantization job must answer:

- which activations shifted;
- which tokens/classes suffered;
- which layers are sensitive;
- which device targets are safe;
- which calibration corpus was used;
- which rollback threshold is active;
- which fallback runtime is required.

Proof output:

```
quantization_claim:
  compression_ratio
  latency_delta
  memory_delta
  quality_delta_by_class
  unsafe_classes
  fallback_policy
  calibration_hash
  device_targets
```

Impact:

This turns DAQ into an enterprise-safe optimization instead of a risky
black-box size reduction.

### W9010 Runtime Policy Proof

Route policies, fallback policies, approval policies, and device policies must
be checkable.

Properties to prove or test:

- no route leaks forbidden data to a forbidden provider;
- no fallback bypasses policy;
- emergency degradation preserves audit logging;
- tenant quota cannot be exceeded silently;
- child namespace cannot escalate to parent authority;
- edge runtime cannot claim cloud-only capability;
- stale calibration cannot unlock public claim badges.

Formal methods fit:

- SMT for policy satisfiability;
- TLA+ for concurrent rollout and rollback;
- Alloy for relationship and authority modeling;
- replay tests for actual route traces.

### W9011 Product Copy Compiler

Compile copy from claims.

Instead of hand-written product promises, every public sentence that makes a
product claim should have:

- source claim id;
- scope;
- evidence status;
- expiry;
- fallback wording;
- disallowed stronger wording.

Example:

```
claim: cost_savings_namespace_n
status: empirically_supported
copy:
  allowed: "Cut measured API spend on this workload by 42 percent."
  blocked: "Cut all AI costs by 42 percent."
  fallback: "Estimate savings from routing and distillation."
```

This is how Kolm prevents the exact failure mode of a beautiful site with
unclear, overbroad, or mismatched copy.

### W9012 Procurement Proof Packet

Enterprise buyers need a proof packet that answers security, legal, finance,
technical, and executive questions from the same source.

Packet contents:

- one-page executive assurance summary;
- machine-readable claim graph;
- public evidence projection;
- private evidence pointer list;
- trust and compliance status;
- data-flow map;
- provider and region map;
- cost model;
- deployment model;
- incident and rollback model;
- unsupported claims;
- next proof steps.

Sales metric impact:

- shorter security review;
- fewer custom questionnaires;
- better expansion path;
- lower buyer risk;
- higher enterprise close rate.

### W9013 Claim Expiration And Revalidation

Every claim must expire.

Expiry drivers:

- model version changes;
- teacher provider deprecation;
- calibration dataset age;
- policy update;
- region law update;
- device runtime update;
- benchmark methodology update;
- incident;
- drift alert;
- new counterexample.

Revalidation modes:

- automatic replay;
- partial replay;
- human review;
- external certification update;
- block until proof.

UI requirement:

Do not hide expired claims. Account, CLI, docs, and sales exports should show
expired claims as expired, with a revalidation command or owner.

### W9014 Proof-Aware API Envelope

Every backend route that returns product state should be able to return a proof
envelope.

Fields:

- data;
- claim_ids;
- proof_status;
- evidence_snapshot;
- generated_at;
- expires_at;
- verifier;
- open_blockers;
- next_actions.

Example:

```
GET /v1/product/graph?include_proof=1
GET /v1/billing/tiers?include_claims=1
GET /v1/storage/object-readiness?include_proof=1
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/assurance-case
```

Do not implement this blindly in every route first. Start with product graph,
readiness, artifacts, storage, billing, K-Score, distillation, and deployment.

### W9015 Proof-Aware Account UX Contract

The account UI should not show vague readiness.

Post-auth pages should show:

- claim summary;
- proof status;
- expired proof count;
- blocked external proof count;
- public/private evidence split;
- next commands;
- owner;
- last replay time.

Best UI pattern:

- dense operational table;
- small proof tokens;
- no marketing paragraphs;
- filters by product surface;
- deep links to evidence;
- export packet button;
- replay button.

This ties backend truth to UX polish without the backend bot editing the site.

### W9016 Proof-Aware CLI And TUI

CLI/TUI should expose proof status without requiring the web app.

Commands:

```
kolm claims list --artifact <id> --json
kolm claims replay --artifact <id> --json
kolm claims export --artifact <id> --public
kolm claims explain --claim <id>
kolm claims counterexamples --artifact <id>
kolm surfaces --claims --json
```

TUI views:

- claim graph;
- proof replay;
- expired claims;
- counterexamples;
- assurance case;
- export preview.

Operator outcome:

A customer can prove an artifact in an air-gapped or local environment without
waiting on the hosted account UI.

### W9017 Proof-Aware SDK Contract

SDKs should preserve claim metadata through app integration.

SDK objects:

- `KolmClaim`;
- `KolmProofStatus`;
- `KolmEvidencePointer`;
- `KolmAssuranceCase`;
- `KolmCounterexample`;
- `KolmClaimReplayResult`.

Developer ergonomics:

```
const result = await kolm.run(input, { includeClaims: true })
if (result.claims.hasExpired("schema_preservation")) {
  routeToTeacher()
}
```

This makes proof useful in application code, not only in audits.

### W9018 Claim Diff And Regression

Every artifact version should be diffable by claims.

Diff classes:

- claim added;
- claim removed;
- scope narrowed;
- scope widened;
- evidence changed;
- verifier changed;
- confidence changed;
- counterexample added;
- expiry changed;
- external blocker cleared.

Release gates:

- fail if a critical claim is removed without approval;
- fail if a public copy claim widens beyond evidence;
- fail if evidence changed without replay;
- fail if counterexamples increased beyond budget.

This is stronger than a model diff because it diffuses product truth across
model, policy, runtime, evidence, and copy.

### W9019 Proof-Aware Incident Response

Incidents should attach to claims.

Incident examples:

- redaction bypass;
- unsafe route;
- benchmark regression;
- device crash;
- provider output drift;
- cost anomaly;
- auth escalation;
- stale certification.

Incident outputs:

- impacted claims;
- impacted artifacts;
- impacted customers;
- public/private notice text;
- required replays;
- claim status transition;
- remediation evidence.

Metric impact:

- faster incident triage;
- honest customer communication;
- reduced recurrence;
- cleaner audits.

### W9020 Proof-Carrying Marketplace

Artifact marketplace listings should be ranked and filtered by proof, not only
popularity.

Marketplace fields:

- proven claims;
- empirical claims;
- open blockers;
- supported runtimes;
- calibration age;
- counterexample score;
- assurance packet availability;
- public/private evidence split;
- enterprise readiness.

Buyer outcome:

Instead of "download this model," buyers can ask "show me artifacts with
schema-preservation proof, PHI redaction benchmarks, local-device runtime
proof, and SOC 2 scoped controls."

### W9021 Proof-Aware Pricing And ROI Claims

ROI calculators are especially risky. They must be claim-backed.

Required proof fields:

- baseline spend source;
- workload class;
- provider price snapshot;
- route policy;
- distillation policy;
- tokenization assumptions;
- hardware assumptions;
- confidence interval;
- excluded costs.

Allowed output:

- projected savings range;
- measured savings where actual data exists;
- assumptions list;
- proof packet link.

Blocked output:

- universal percentage savings;
- unsupported payback claims;
- hidden pass-through fees.

### W9022 Proof-Aware Legal And Compliance Boundary

Legal claims need a different status taxonomy.

Statuses:

- control implemented;
- contractual template available;
- auditor evidence collected;
- certification awarded;
- external partner pending;
- not applicable;
- blocked.

Do not mix:

- HIPAA-ready controls with signed BAA;
- SOC 2 readiness with SOC 2 Type II;
- EU AI Act tooling with legal compliance outcome;
- SBOM generation with full supply-chain certification.

This should be enforced by claim-scope gates and visible in public trust pages.

### W9023 Proof Compiler For Agentic Workflows

Agentic workflows create multi-step risk.

Proof graph additions:

- tool call;
- tool authority;
- memory read/write;
- human approval;
- external API call;
- delegated credential;
- model decision;
- rollback hook;
- side effect.

Claims:

- tool authority bounded;
- human approval required for high-risk action;
- memory use within tenant policy;
- side effects logged;
- output schema validated;
- replay possible from trace.

This makes Kolm relevant to agent platforms, not only model APIs.

### W9024 Proof-Carrying Edge Deployment

Edge and device deployments need local proof.

Local bundle:

- artifact;
- proof manifest;
- device capability claim;
- quantization proof;
- fallback policy;
- offline verifier;
- redacted evidence;
- expiry clock;
- update policy.

Acceptance:

- device can verify artifact hash;
- device can verify policy hash;
- device can reject expired proof;
- device can export local proof log;
- cloud can reconcile logs later.

This turns "run anywhere" into "prove what ran anywhere."

### W9025 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Simulate a proof-carrying artifact before implementation.

Harness input:

```
artifact_hash: A
claims:
  - schema preservation
  - PHI redaction coverage
  - 40 percent measured savings
  - local iPhone runtime support
evidence:
  eval_report_hash: E1
  redaction_report_hash: E2
  price_snapshot_hash: E3
  device_smoke_hash: E4
counterexamples:
  schema_edge_case: pass
  MRN_context_case: pass
  long_context_cost_case: fail
```

Expected output:

```
schema preservation: empirically_supported
PHI redaction coverage: empirically_supported
40 percent measured savings: partial
local iPhone runtime support: bounded
public homepage claim: blocked
account claim: visible with caveat
sales packet: allowed with excluded-cost note
```

Pass condition:

- no unscoped public claim;
- failed counterexample downgrades the dependent claim;
- assurance case updates automatically;
- claim diff shows the downgrade.

### W9026 Implementation Handoff

Implementation agent build order:

1. Add `ProofClaimIR` schema and validator.
2. Add claim graph compiler over existing product graph, readiness ledger,
   K-Score reports, artifact metadata, and evidence lakehouse rows.
3. Add proof status taxonomy and expiry logic.
4. Add assurance-case generator.
5. Add proof-aware API envelope on one or two route families first.
6. Add CLI export and replay commands.
7. Add account UX contract for proof status.
8. Add product-copy claim compiler.
9. Add counterexample library.
10. Add release gate that fails on widened public claims without evidence.

Non-goals for first implementation:

- do not prove all ML behavior formally;
- do not require Lean or Rocq for ordinary empirical claims;
- do not block local development on external certifications;
- do not expose private evidence in public packets.

### W9027 Proof-Carrying AI Readiness Gate

Readiness checks:

- every public claim has claim id;
- every claim has status;
- every claim has evidence or blocker;
- every claim has expiry;
- every empirical claim has dataset and method;
- every formal claim has checker manifest;
- every policy claim has policy hash;
- every product copy string maps to claim id;
- every blocked external claim is visible as blocked;
- assurance case exports contain no secrets;
- public projection is redacted;
- private projection is complete;
- replay command exists.

Failure examples:

- homepage says "guaranteed" without formal proof;
- account says "certified" with only controls implemented;
- K-Score omits calibration version;
- ROI calculator lacks price snapshot;
- artifact claim expires but remains green;
- proof packet includes private prompt text.

### W9028 Ultimate Proof-Carrying AI Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should make AI claims executable. A customer should be able to ask "can I
trust this artifact for this use case, on this device, under this policy, with
this cost goal?" and receive a portable answer backed by evidence, assumptions,
counterexamples, and replayable verification.

Strategic position:
The AI infrastructure market will fill with routers, eval tools, model
gateways, fine-tuning dashboards, and agent frameworks. The harder category is
claim ownership: who can prove what an AI system is, what it did, why it is
allowed, what it costs, where it can run, and when those claims expire. If Kolm
owns proof-carrying artifacts, it owns the highest-trust layer above models,
clouds, devices, and frameworks.

## Autotuned Kernel Forge, Runtime Profile, And Device-Specific Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

Kolm should not stop at compiling AI behavior into portable artifacts. The
largest durable cost, latency, margin, and device-coverage gains come from
turning every artifact into a measured execution plan for its actual runtime
targets. Distillation, quantization, sparse attention, tiered KV cache, and
edge deployment all become weaker if they end at an abstract graph. The winning
system needs to forge kernels, memory plans, scheduling policies, and fallback
profiles per artifact, per task distribution, per device class, and per
customer SLO.

Existing compiler stacks already prove pieces of this:

- Triton shows that Python-level custom DNN kernels can target modern GPUs at
  high throughput.
- MLIR shows that reusable compiler infrastructure can lower from high-level
  graph and tensor representations into hardware-specific operations.
- TVM shows that tensor programs can be scheduled, optimized, and deployed
  across hardware targets.
- IREE shows that ML programs can be compiled into portable runtime packages
  for heterogeneous deployment.
- OpenXLA shows how ML models can be optimized for high-performance execution
  across backends and hardware platforms.
- TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, SGLang, and ONNX Runtime GenAI show the importance of
  serving-specific runtime features such as KV cache management, speculative
  decoding, guided decoding, batching, streaming, and hardware recipes.
- ExecuTorch and MLC LLM show that edge deployment requires export, lowering,
  backend selection, profiling, memory planning, and device-specific runtime
  integration.

Kolm's unique opening:

Other systems optimize generic models. Kolm can optimize owned artifacts with
their capture distribution, proof claims, K-Score axes, redaction policy,
fallback policy, target devices, and business SLOs. That means the compiler can
specialize on evidence unavailable to a generic compiler:

- task token distribution;
- expected output schema;
- common tool-call shape;
- forbidden output classes;
- teacher fallback threshold;
- K-Score failure taxonomy;
- quantization sensitivity;
- sparse attention pattern;
- KV reuse profile;
- device fleet histogram;
- tenant cost ceiling;
- privacy and residency route rules.

The invention:

Build a Kernel Forge that compiles each .kolm artifact into a portfolio of
runtime profiles. Each profile carries measured kernels, memory plans, batch
rules, cache policies, fallback constraints, and proof receipts. Runtime
selection becomes a verified decision: use this kernel on this device for this
task class because it is measured, bounded, compatible, and claim-safe.

New primitive:

`KernelForgeReceipt`

```
receipt_type: kernel_forge
artifact_hash: sha256
profile_id: string
runtime_target:
  cpu
  cuda
  rocm
  metal
  vulkan
  webgpu
  npu
  dsp
  wasm
device_class: string
task_distribution_hash: sha256
compile_ir_hash: sha256
kernel_manifest_hash: sha256
memory_plan_hash: sha256
autotune_trace_hash: sha256
benchmark_report_hash: sha256
quality_guard_hash: sha256
fallback_policy_hash: sha256
claims:
  max_latency_ms
  p95_latency_ms
  peak_memory_bytes
  tokens_per_second
  energy_estimate
  quality_delta_bound
  supported_context_window
  supported_schema_classes
  unsupported_classes
signatures:
  compiler
  benchmark_runner
  policy
```

Why it is 100x:

- Cost: measured kernels lower per-token cost and improve gross margin.
- Latency: runtime profiles choose the fastest safe execution path per request.
- Device reach: artifacts become deployable across GPU, CPU, phone, browser,
  embedded, and specialized accelerators.
- Quality: quantization and kernel choices are blocked when K-Score or
  preservation claims regress.
- Reliability: fallback policies are tied to measured runtime failure modes.
- Sales: enterprise buyers get a real "runs on your fleet" proof, not a vague
  portability promise.
- Moat: Kolm accumulates a private corpus of artifact shapes, runtime traces,
  kernel choices, device histograms, and failure cases.

### W9100 KernelForgeIR Schema

Define the intermediate representation that bridges .kolm artifact semantics
and backend compiler targets.

Required fields:

- artifact graph;
- operation classes;
- shape constraints;
- token distribution;
- output schema constraints;
- context-window modes;
- attention pattern hints;
- KV cache access profile;
- quantization plan;
- sparse plan;
- fallback plan;
- target devices;
- claim guardrails;
- privacy constraints.

Why:

Generic compiler IRs do not know which task failures are fatal, which token
patterns dominate the workload, or which provider fallback is allowed. The
KernelForgeIR should preserve that product context while lowering into MLIR,
Triton, TVM, IREE, XLA, ONNX Runtime, ExecuTorch, MLC, or other backend paths.

Acceptance:

- IR can represent dense, quantized, sparse, speculative, and KV-heavy runtime
  profiles.
- IR stores claim guards so optimization cannot silently break product truth.
- IR can emit a public profile summary and a private tuning trace.

### W9101 Artifact Shape Profiler

Before compiling kernels, profile the artifact's real workload shape.

Profile dimensions:

- prompt length distribution;
- output length distribution;
- schema depth;
- tool-call rate;
- refusal rate;
- redaction rate;
- attention locality;
- repeated prefix rate;
- KV reuse rate;
- batchability;
- memory pressure;
- fallback frequency.

Data sources:

- capture traces;
- synthetic fixtures;
- redacted customer workload summaries;
- benchmark suites;
- teacher/student comparison outputs;
- device smoke tests.

Output:

```
artifact_shape_profile:
  dominant_prompt_bins
  dominant_output_bins
  schema_classes
  attention_locality_score
  kv_reuse_score
  batchability_score
  kernel_specialization_candidates
  unsafe_specializations
```

### W9102 Runtime Target Matrix

Create a target matrix that makes runtime support explicit.

Targets:

- CUDA GPU;
- ROCm GPU;
- Apple Metal;
- Vulkan;
- WebGPU;
- CPU vector backends;
- WASM;
- iOS;
- Android;
- browser;
- embedded Linux;
- NPU/DSP delegate;
- serverless CPU;
- edge gateway.

For each target:

- supported operations;
- precision support;
- quantization support;
- max memory;
- max context;
- kernel backend;
- package format;
- smoke command;
- known blockers;
- measured profile.

This matrix is the backend counterpart to the website's "run anywhere" claim.
Without it, "run anywhere" is copy. With it, "run anywhere" becomes a measured
contract.

### W9103 Compiler Backend Router

Route the artifact to the correct compiler path.

Routing examples:

- Triton for custom GPU attention, matmul, quantization, and fused decode
  kernels.
- MLIR for dialect-level transformations, lowering, bufferization, vector
  layout, and target-specific operations.
- TVM for tensor program schedules and cross-target compilation.
- IREE for deployable runtime modules and portable packages.
- OpenXLA for XLA-compatible model graphs and persisted autotune profiles.
- TensorRT-LLM for NVIDIA server inference profiles.
- ExecuTorch for mobile and embedded PyTorch-origin deployments.
- ONNX Runtime GenAI for broad runtime distribution and Windows/mobile/server
  integration.
- MLC LLM for universal deployment paths.
- vLLM/SGLang-compatible profiles for serving-time scheduling, cache, and
  structured generation patterns.

Rule:

No backend is the product. The product is the decision system that chooses,
measures, verifies, and exports the right backend profile for the artifact.

### W9104 Autotune Trace Store

Persist tuning attempts as reusable research data.

Trace fields:

- artifact hash;
- target device;
- backend;
- kernel candidate;
- schedule parameters;
- shape bucket;
- precision mode;
- memory plan;
- result metrics;
- compile time;
- failure reason;
- quality guard outcome.

Reuse:

- warm-start similar artifacts;
- avoid failed schedules;
- build device-specific priors;
- detect regressions after compiler upgrades;
- train a meta-autotuner.

Moat:

The autotune trace store becomes proprietary infrastructure data. Competitors
can run compilers; they will not have Kolm's artifact-conditioned schedule
priors.

### W9105 Distillation-Aware Kernel Search

Distilled models are not just smaller generic models. They have task-shaped
activation, token, and attention distributions.

Search inputs:

- teacher/student delta;
- class-level K-Score deltas;
- activation outliers;
- attention locality;
- output schema;
- refusal classes;
- rare-case prompts;
- quantization sensitivity.

Kernel choices:

- fused matmul/dequant;
- activation-aware tiling;
- schema-class decode specialization;
- sparse attention kernel;
- prefix-cache path;
- short-output fast path;
- refusal-path fast path;
- fallback branch.

Acceptance:
