- Kernel search cannot optimize aggregate latency while increasing critical
  failure rate.
- Every chosen kernel has quality guard evidence.
- The search report explains which workload property justified specialization.

### W9106 Quantization-Kernel Co-Design

Quantization and kernels must be co-designed.

Required decisions:

- per-channel vs per-token scaling;
- group size;
- activation smoothing;
- outlier routing;
- mixed precision fallback;
- dequant fusion;
- calibration corpus;
- device-specific precision support.

Output:

```
quant_kernel_profile:
  quantization_mode
  kernel_family
  calibration_hash
  quality_delta_by_class
  latency_delta
  memory_delta
  unsafe_classes
  fallback_policy
```

This is how DAQ becomes an execution strategy, not only a model-size strategy.

### W9107 Sparse Attention Kernel Forge

Sparse attention is valuable only when the runtime can exploit the actual
pattern.

Inputs:

- learned task attention maps;
- context-window bins;
- retrieval span positions;
- output schema anchors;
- tool-call spans;
- long-context failure reports.

Kernel outputs:

- block-sparse pattern;
- sliding-window pattern;
- global-token pattern;
- retrieval-anchor pattern;
- task-specific hybrid pattern.

Guardrails:

- compare against dense baseline;
- block if critical evidence spans are dropped;
- track long-context degradation;
- attach K-Score deltas by task class.

### W9108 Importance-Tiered KV Runtime

KV cache is not uniformly valuable.

Tiering signals:

- prompt segment role;
- evidence span;
- instruction span;
- schema anchor;
- retrieval source;
- privacy sensitivity;
- expected reuse;
- attention contribution;
- device memory pressure.

Runtime choices:

- keep hot KV in fastest memory;
- compress medium KV;
- evict low-value KV;
- rehydrate from source when safe;
- block eviction of policy-critical spans;
- fall back to teacher when cache loss risks claim breakage.

Receipt:

`KVPolicyReceipt` should link to the KernelForgeReceipt so runtime memory
decisions are proof-aware.

### W9109 Batch-Latency Scheduler Forge

Serving runtimes need batch policies that know product risk.

Scheduling inputs:

- tenant SLO;
- task criticality;
- output length forecast;
- batch compatibility;
- schema compatibility;
- privacy class;
- fallback threshold;
- device queue state;
- cost ceiling.

Policies:

- latency-first;
- throughput-first;
- cost-first;
- quality-first;
- privacy-isolated;
- deadline-aware;
- fallback-ready.

Acceptance:

- High-risk regulated tasks cannot be batched with incompatible privacy or
  evidence scopes.
- Low-risk high-volume tasks can maximize throughput.
- Scheduler emits a decision receipt.

### W9110 Structured Output Decode Kernel

Kolm should exploit known output schemas during decoding.

Optimization:

- schema-constrained token masks;
- field-order prediction;
- JSON punctuation fast path;
- enum-value trie;
- validity-preserving speculative path;
- early invalid-output abort;
- repair fallback.

Quality guard:

- structured output validity;
- semantic field correctness;
- invalid repair rate;
- latency savings;
- critical field regression.

This makes structured validation part of execution, not a post-hoc cleanup.

### W9111 Redaction-Aware Runtime Path

Privacy work should inform kernels and routing.

Runtime cases:

- redact before provider;
- redact after local model;
- run local-only;
- split sensitive and non-sensitive spans;
- use PHI-safe fallback;
- block unsupported device export.

Compiler opportunity:

- common redaction classes can get specialized detectors;
- sensitive spans can be excluded from cache sharing;
- privacy class can choose runtime target;
- evidence lakehouse can prove no forbidden provider saw the span.

Metric impact:

- fewer privacy failures;
- faster redaction;
- clearer enterprise proof;
- lower hosted inference cost when local redaction is safe.

### W9112 Energy And Thermal Profile Compiler

Edge and mobile deployment needs energy and thermal awareness.

Profile fields:

- estimated joules per request;
- peak power mode;
- thermal throttling threshold;
- battery impact;
- sustained tokens per second;
- cool-down policy;
- degraded mode;
- user-visible latency.

Runtime actions:

- switch precision;
- shorten context;
- defer background distill;
- route to cloud;
- lower batch size;
- ask user permission for high-power mode.

This turns "enterprise models down to any device" into a responsible device
experience.

### W9113 WebGPU And Browser Runtime Profile

Browser deployment is strategically important for zero-friction demos and local
privacy.

Profile requirements:

- WebGPU support;
- WASM fallback;
- memory ceiling;
- model shard plan;
- worker plan;
- streaming UI plan;
- offline cache;
- privacy mode;
- benchmark smoke.

Risk:

Browser hardware is inconsistent. The runtime profile must show supported,
degraded, and blocked states rather than pretending one browser path works
everywhere.

### W9114 Edge Package Builder

Build deployable edge packages from the selected runtime profile.

Package contents:

- artifact;
- kernel manifest;
- runtime binary or dependency declaration;
- device capability requirements;
- quantization profile;
- proof bundle;
- fallback policy;
- update policy;
- smoke fixture;
- rollback plan.

Targets:

- iOS package;
- Android package;
- desktop package;
- embedded Linux package;
- browser package;
- air-gapped package.

### W9115 Runtime Regression Laboratory

Every compiler or runtime upgrade can break claims.

Regression matrix:

- backend version;
- driver version;
- device type;
- OS version;
- precision mode;
- workload bucket;
- K-Score class;
- memory peak;
- latency p95;
- determinism;
- output validity.

Acceptance:

- block upgrade when critical claims regress;
- emit claim diff;
- update runtime profile only after replay;
- preserve old known-good profile.

### W9116 Determinism And Replay Contract

Runtime speed is useless if replayability disappears.

Track:

- random seeds;
- sampling mode;
- kernel determinism setting;
- backend version;
- driver version;
- precision;
- quantization state;
- input hash;
- profile id.

Modes:

- deterministic audit mode;
- production fast mode;
- hybrid reproducible sampling mode;
- regulated export mode.

Kolm should let users choose speed vs replayability explicitly, then record the
choice in the receipt.

### W9117 Kernel Safety Sandbox

Custom kernels create security and correctness risk.

Sandbox checks:

- bounds checks;
- illegal memory access detection;
- NaN/Inf propagation;
- precision overflow;
- timing side-channel risk;
- secret-dependent branch risk;
- unsupported device instruction;
- divergence risk;
- fuzz input.

Output:

- safety score;
- blocked kernels;
- allowed kernels;
- required runtime flags;
- public caveat.

### W9118 Meta-Autotuner

Use the autotune trace store to predict good schedules before measuring.

Inputs:

- op graph fingerprint;
- shape profile;
- device profile;
- precision profile;
- prior traces;
- compiler version;
- target metric weights.

Outputs:

- candidate schedules;
- confidence;
- expected compile cost;
- expected runtime gain;
- required validation set.

Guardrail:

Predicted schedules are never accepted without measurement. The meta-autotuner
reduces search cost; it does not replace proof.

### W9119 Runtime Portfolio Optimizer

An artifact should ship with a portfolio, not one profile.

Portfolio examples:

- cheapest profile;
- fastest profile;
- most deterministic profile;
- lowest memory profile;
- highest quality profile;
- offline profile;
- regulated profile;
- browser demo profile.

Selection function:

```
utility =
  quality_weight * quality
  - latency_weight * latency
  - cost_weight * cost
  - memory_weight * memory
  - risk_weight * risk
  + proof_weight * claim_strength
```

This lets customers set priorities without Kolm recompiling from scratch.

### W9120 Runtime Profile Marketplace

Profiles can become marketplace assets.

Listing fields:

- artifact compatibility;
- target hardware;
- measured metrics;
- proof status;
- compiler backend;
- safety score;
- cost estimate;
- known blockers;
- license.

Moat:

The marketplace is not only model artifacts. It is tuned execution knowledge.
The best profiles become reusable across customers while respecting rights and
privacy boundaries.

### W9121 Fleet-Aware Rollout Compiler

Enterprise fleets are heterogeneous.

Inputs:

- device inventory;
- region;
- OS;
- accelerator;
- memory;
- policy;
- workload class;
- user cohort;
- SLO.

Output:

- rollout plan;
- profile assignment;
- canary cohort;
- smoke fixtures;
- rollback threshold;
- unsupported devices;
- cloud fallback cost.

Acceptance:

- no device receives unsupported profile;
- every rollout has rollback;
- account UI can explain support status by fleet segment.

### W9122 Kernel Forge Evidence Lakehouse

Store runtime evidence as queryable rows.

Tables:

- compile_attempts;
- kernel_candidates;
- tuning_results;
- device_profiles;
- runtime_smokes;
- quality_guards;
- regressions;
- rollouts;
- customer_profile_assignments.

Queries:

- which profiles improved margin by more than 20 percent without quality loss;
- which devices fail memory plans;
- which compiler version caused p95 regression;
- which task classes need custom kernels;
- which profiles are safe for public claim.

### W9123 Kernel Forge API Contract

Future backend routes:

```
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/runtime-profiles
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/runtime-profiles/compile
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/runtime-profiles/smoke
GET /v1/runtime/targets
GET /v1/runtime/profiles/:profile_id/receipt
POST /v1/runtime/profiles/:profile_id/promote
```

Each response should support:

- profile summary;
- claim status;
- measured metrics;
- target support;
- blockers;
- next actions;
- proof links.

### W9124 CLI And TUI Runtime Contract

Future CLI commands:

```
kolm runtime targets --json
kolm runtime profile <artifact> --target cuda --json
kolm runtime compile <artifact> --target iphone --profile low-memory
kolm runtime smoke <artifact> --profile <id> --json
kolm runtime compare <artifact> --profiles a,b
kolm runtime export <artifact> --profile <id>
```

TUI views:

- target matrix;
- profile portfolio;
- compile attempts;
- runtime smoke;
- regression heatmap;
- rollout assignment.

### W9125 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Simulate before building.

Input:

```
artifact: phi-redactor.kolm
targets:
  - cuda_l4
  - iphone_a17
  - webgpu_laptop
profiles:
  - fastest
  - low_memory
  - deterministic_audit
guardrails:
  redaction_f1_min: 0.995
  p95_latency_ms_max: 120
  max_memory_mb: 1800
```

Expected output:

```
cuda_l4.fastest: promote
iphone_a17.low_memory: promote_with_context_limit
webgpu_laptop.fastest: blocked_memory
deterministic_audit: promote_for_regulated_exports
public_claim: "measured runtime profiles available for supported targets"
blocked_claim: "runs optimally on every device"
```

Failure cases:

- profile wins latency but drops redaction F1;
- profile fits one phone but not fleet median;
- browser path fails memory ceiling;
- compiler upgrade changes output determinism.

### W9126 Implementation Handoff

Implementation order:

1. Define KernelForgeIR and KernelForgeReceipt schemas.
2. Build artifact shape profiler from capture, eval, and benchmark traces.
3. Build runtime target registry and support matrix.
4. Add compile profile records without executing custom kernels yet.
5. Add smoke runner abstraction.
6. Add quality guard attachment to every runtime profile.
7. Add runtime profile portfolio selection.
8. Add evidence lakehouse rows.
9. Add CLI read-only target/profile commands.
10. Add one real backend path first, likely ONNX Runtime or ExecuTorch for
    portability, plus Triton or TensorRT-LLM for high-margin GPU profiles.

Non-goals for first implementation:

- do not write unsafe custom kernels before sandbox checks exist;
- do not claim universal runtime support;
- do not promote a profile without quality guards;
- do not hide backend-specific limitations;
- do not benchmark only the happy path.

### W9127 Kernel Forge Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

- KernelForgeIR schema validates;
- target matrix exists;
- every profile names backend, target, metrics, and blockers;
- every promoted profile has smoke evidence;
- every promoted profile has quality guard evidence;
- unsafe profile cannot be public;
- runtime claim maps to measured target;
- deterministic claim has replay metadata;
- edge package has rollback plan;
- fleet rollout blocks unsupported devices;
- profile evidence exports without secrets.

Fail examples:

- "run anywhere" claim without target matrix;
- latency claim without workload bucket;
- profile promoted without K-Score guard;
- custom kernel accepted without safety sandbox;
- browser profile lacks memory limit;
- mobile profile lacks thermal note.

### W9128 Ultimate Kernel Forge Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should turn every artifact into a measured runtime portfolio. The customer
should not ask "does this model run on my stack?" They should ask Kolm for the
best proven profile for their workload, device fleet, privacy boundary, latency
goal, cost goal, and audit mode.

Strategic position:
Routers can move calls. Model vendors can sell APIs. Compiler projects can
optimize graphs. Kolm can own the product layer above all of them: the signed
artifact plus the measured runtime profile plus the proof of what changed. That
is how Kolm becomes the compiler of owned AI, not just a gateway, evaluator, or
distillation tool.

## Tenant Preference, Personalization, And Scoped Alignment Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

Enterprise AI quality is not one universal preference function. A support team,
clinical operations team, legal team, finance team, robotics team, and developer
platform team can all want different styles, risk thresholds, escalation
rules, schema details, verbosity, cost tradeoffs, and fallback behavior. Today,
most AI platforms handle this with prompts, fine-tunes, vague "custom models,"
or user-level memory. That is not enough for Kolm.

Kolm needs a preference compiler that turns customer feedback, corrections,
approvals, rejections, task outcomes, and policy boundaries into scoped
alignment artifacts:

- tenant preference graphs;
- role-specific behavior contracts;
- task-specific style and risk adapters;
- private reward models;
- contextual bandit routing policies;
- DPO or RLHF training packets where appropriate;
- no-train preference overlays where data rights prohibit training;
- preference receipts that prove which signals were used and which were
  excluded.

Strategic insight:

The most valuable AI behavior is not merely "helpful" in the abstract. It is
right for this customer, this task, this workflow, this risk level, this brand,
this device, this legal boundary, and this cost target. Kolm can own that
alignment boundary because it already sees capture traces, route outcomes,
K-Score failures, human corrections, approval decisions, runtime profiles, and
policy state.

Research takeaways:

- InstructGPT and RLHF show that human feedback can make smaller models more
  aligned with user intent than larger generic models.
- DPO shows that preference optimization can be simplified into a direct
  training objective over preferred and rejected outputs.
- Constitutional AI shows that preference signals can come from principles and
  critique loops, not only direct human labels.
- Contextual bandits show how systems can learn which action to take under
  changing context while logging propensities for offline evaluation.
- Off-policy evaluation libraries show that personalization policies must be
  evaluated from logged data before risky live rollout.
- PEFT and LoRA show that small parameter-efficient adapters can specialize
  behavior without duplicating full model weights.
- Differential privacy tooling shows that preference learning needs explicit
  privacy accounting, not hand-waved "anonymous feedback."

New primitive:

`PreferenceAlignmentReceipt`

```
receipt_type: preference_alignment
tenant_id_hash: sha256
namespace_hash: sha256
artifact_hash: sha256
preference_graph_hash: sha256
signal_manifest_hash: sha256
policy_scope_hash: sha256
rights_scope_hash: sha256
alignment_method:
  prompt_overlay
  rule_overlay
  bandit_policy
  reward_model
  dpo_adapter
  lora_adapter
  distill_update
  no_train_profile
signal_classes:
  correction
  approval
  rejection
  edit
  escalation
  business_outcome
  human_preference
  policy_principle
  route_outcome
  runtime_outcome
privacy:
  raw_signal_retained: boolean
  differential_privacy_budget: optional
  cross_tenant_training_allowed: boolean
  deletion_propagation_required: boolean
claims:
  preference_lift
  critical_failure_delta
  escalation_delta
  cost_delta
  latency_delta
  retention_delta
  fairness_delta
  abstention_delta
status:
  experimental
  scoped
  promoted
  blocked
  expired
```

Why it is 100x:

- Product-market fit improves because artifacts learn customer-specific
  behavior without becoming unsafe global models.
- Retention improves because Kolm becomes better inside each customer workflow
  over time.
- Enterprise trust improves because customers can see exactly which preference
  signals were used and which were blocked.
- Distillation improves because students learn the target organization's
  actual acceptance boundary, not only a generic teacher distribution.
- Routing improves because preference can decide when cheap local behavior is
  good enough and when the teacher or human is still required.
- Sales improves because "owned AI" becomes literal: the customer owns the
  behavior envelope, not just the bill.

Core rule:

Preference is not truth. A tenant can prefer a style, workflow, escalation
policy, or decision threshold, but Kolm must not let preference override law,
safety, provenance, medical correctness, financial controls, or rights
restrictions. The preference compiler must sit under policy, not above it.

### W9200 PreferenceSignalIR Schema

Define the typed signal model.

Signal fields:

- tenant;
- namespace;
- role;
- task;
- artifact;
- source event;
- signal type;
- preferred output;
- rejected output;
- edited output;
- reason code;
- confidence;
- reviewer authority;
- rights scope;
- retention scope;
- policy constraints;
- timestamp;
- deletion status.

Signal types:

- explicit ranking;
- binary acceptance;
- edit distance correction;
- escalation;
- abandonment;
- manual override;
- business outcome;
- support resolution;
- legal approval;
- failed audit;
- runtime fallback;
- user dissatisfaction.

Acceptance:

- no free-text preference can enter training without scope metadata;
- every signal can be excluded by rights, deletion, policy, or low authority;
- every aggregate preference can be traced back to redacted signal classes.

### W9201 Tenant Preference Graph

Represent preferences as a graph, not a flat label table.

Nodes:

- tenant;
- namespace;
- team;
- role;
- workflow;
- task class;
- artifact;
- output schema;
- tone rule;
- risk rule;
- escalation rule;
- reviewer;
- policy principle.

Edges:

- prefers;
- rejects;
- overrides;
- applies_to;
- conflicts_with;
- inherits_from;
- expires_with;
- requires_review;
- cannot_train;
- can_distill.

Why:

Enterprise preferences are hierarchical. A company policy can override a team
style, a legal reviewer can override a sales preference, and a regulated
workflow can block personalization entirely. A graph makes those relationships
explicit.

### W9202 Preference Rights Gate

Block illegal or unwanted learning.

Gate checks:

- source system allows learning;
- tenant allows retention;
- user role allows feedback use;
- data subject deletion has not invalidated signal;
- contract allows adapter training;
- cross-tenant pooling is explicitly permitted;
- sensitive field is redacted or excluded;
- signal authority is high enough for task risk.

Fail examples:

- support edit containing PHI enters global preference model;
- contractor feedback trains enterprise artifact without permission;
- deleted user's corrections remain in adapter;
- low-authority user changes legal workflow behavior.

### W9203 Preference Conflict Resolver

Preferences will conflict.

Conflict classes:

- role vs role;
- team vs tenant;
- style vs accuracy;
- cost vs quality;
- speed vs review;
- personalization vs compliance;
- historical preference vs current policy;
- user preference vs accessibility requirement.

Resolution policy:

1. law and contract;
2. tenant policy;
3. workflow risk;
4. role authority;
5. task owner;
6. statistical signal strength;
7. recency;
8. user-level preference.

Output:

- accepted preference;
- rejected preference;
- unresolved conflict;
- required approval;
- public caveat.

### W9204 Preference K-Score Axis

K-Score needs a scoped preference axis.

Axis components:

- acceptance lift;
- edit reduction;
- reviewer agreement;
- style adherence;
- escalation appropriateness;
- critical-failure neutrality;
- fairness neutrality;
- policy non-regression;
- abstention quality.

Important:

Preference lift cannot compensate for safety regression. A model that users
like but that fails regulated requirements must score lower, not higher.

### W9205 Preference Holdout Builder

Every tenant-specific adaptation needs private holdouts.

Holdout design:

- representative accepted cases;
- rejected cases;
- hard corrections;
- policy conflict cases;
- minority workflow cases;
- tail-language cases;
- accessibility cases;
- rare escalation cases;
- adversarial preference-injection cases.

Acceptance:

- promoted preference update beats baseline on holdout;
- critical failures do not increase;
- edit reduction is not only on common easy cases;
- policy conflicts are detected.

### W9206 Contextual Bandit Router

Personalization should often be a routing policy before it is a model update.

Actions:

- use base artifact;
- use tenant adapter;
- use role adapter;
- use teacher;
- use human review;
- use conservative template;
- abstain;
- ask clarification.

Context:

- tenant;
- role;
- task class;
- risk tier;
- privacy tier;
- cost ceiling;
- latency SLO;
- device target;
- prior acceptance;
- confidence.

Required logging:

- action probabilities;
- chosen action;
- observed reward;
- delayed outcome;
- guardrail outcomes.

Why:

This gives Kolm a safer path to learn preference behavior without instantly
changing model weights.

### W9207 Off-Policy Preference Evaluation

Do not roll out a preference policy because it looks good in logged outcomes.

Evaluation:

- inverse propensity weighting;
- self-normalized estimators;
- doubly robust estimators;
- switch estimators;
- confidence intervals;
- support diagnostics;
- policy overlap checks.

Promotion rule:

If logged data lacks support for the new policy, require a canary or human
review, not a silent promotion.

### W9208 DPO Adapter Compiler

Use DPO when preference pairs are clean and rights permit training.

Inputs:

- prompt;
- chosen output;
- rejected output;
- policy scope;
- tenant scope;
- safety tags;
- rights tags;
- reviewer authority.

Outputs:

- adapter artifact;
- training receipt;
- preference eval report;
- holdout report;
- policy non-regression report;
- rollback adapter.

Guardrail:

DPO cannot train on preference pairs that encode policy violations or private
data outside scope.

### W9209 RLHF And Reward Model Boundary

Use reward models only when enough data and validation exist.

Required:

- reward model card;
- labeler agreement;
- drift checks;
- reward hacking probes;
- calibration set;
- preference holdout;
- human review override.

Block:

- sparse preference data;
- high-risk regulated task without expert review;
- reward model that overweights verbosity;
- reward model that rewards unsafe confidence;
- cross-tenant reward pooling without permission.

### W9210 Constitutional Preference Overlay

Some preference behavior should come from principles.

Principle sources:

- tenant policy;
- brand style;
- legal guidelines;
- safety standards;
- accessibility commitments;
- human review rubrics;
- vertical regulations.

Overlay output:

- allowed behaviors;
- disallowed behaviors;
- critique prompts;
- self-check rules;
- escalation triggers;
- no-train constraints.

This is useful when explicit preference data is scarce or too sensitive to
train on.

### W9211 Role-Specific Behavior Packs

Create behavior packs by role.

Examples:

- support agent;
- support manager;
- clinical reviewer;
- finance approver;
- legal reviewer;
- developer;
- auditor;
- executive.

Each pack includes:

- preferred tone;
- allowed actions;
- escalation rules;
- data access;
- output schema;
- risk threshold;
- device/runtime profile;
- proof requirements.

Role packs must be composable with tenant policy and artifact capability, not
hard-coded prompts.

### W9212 Brand Voice And Style Distillation

Brand voice should be learned without corrupting facts.

Separate:

- style;
- terminology;
- formatting;
- escalation tone;
- legal disclaimers;
- factual content;
- policy constraints.

Style adaptation options:

- prompt overlay;
- retrieval of style examples;
- small adapter;
- post-generation rewrite;
- constrained decoding;
- no adaptation for high-risk fields.

Metric:

Style lift only counts if factuality, schema validity, and safety do not
regress.

### W9213 User Memory Boundary

User-level personalization is useful and dangerous.

Allowed memory:

- interface preferences;
- formatting preferences;
- recurring non-sensitive task context;
- accessibility needs;
- language preferences.

Blocked or high-risk memory:

- secrets;
- health facts without policy;
- protected class inference;
- unverified beliefs;
- third-party personal data;
- preferences that override safety.

Every memory item needs:

- source;
- scope;
- expiry;
- delete path;
- use permissions;
- evidence.

### W9214 Preference Privacy Budget

Preference learning needs privacy accounting.

Track:

- raw signal retention;
- aggregation threshold;
- differential privacy budget where used;
- contributor count;
- cross-tenant sharing status;
- deletion impact;
- sensitive category exclusion.

Output:

```
preference_privacy:
  aggregation_k
  dp_epsilon
  dp_delta
  raw_retained
  cross_tenant_allowed
  deletion_propagation
```

Public claims:

Do not say "anonymous learning" unless anonymity, aggregation, and deletion
semantics are actually proven.

### W9215 Preference Drift Monitor

Preferences change.

Drift sources:

- new policy;
- new manager;
- seasonality;
- product launch;
- regulatory change;
- customer segment change;
- model update;
- workflow migration.

Monitor:

- acceptance rate;
- edit type distribution;
- escalation rate;
- disagreement;
- complaints;
- policy override rate;
- holdout regression.

Action:

- mark preference profile stale;
- revalidate holdout;
- ask owner for review;
- roll back adapter;
- split profile by workflow.

### W9216 Multi-Tenant Preference Federation

Cross-tenant learning is valuable only if governed.

Federation modes:

- none;
- same tenant only;
- opt-in cohort;
- public synthetic;
- differentially private aggregate;
- foundation prior only.

Use cases:

- rare error discovery;
- style taxonomy;
- vertical rubric improvement;
- escalation pattern learning;
- safety preference priors.

Never:

- leak tenant-specific brand voice;
- pool regulated data without contract;
- convert private corrections into public examples without review.

### W9217 Preference-Aware Distillation Planner

Distillation should use preference only when it moves the right boundary.

Planner decisions:

- no train;
- prompt overlay;
- adapter;
- student distill;
- route policy;
- human review;
- collect more labels.

Inputs:

- preference graph;
- holdout result;
- task criticality;
- privacy rights;
- expected savings;
- device need;
- policy blockers.

Goal:

Minimize training when routing, prompting, or review would solve the problem
more safely.

### W9218 Preference-Aware Runtime Selection

Runtime profiles can affect preference.

Examples:

- deterministic mode for auditors;
- concise mode for executives;
- streaming mode for support agents;
- low-latency local mode for operators;
- high-quality teacher mode for legal review;
- offline mode for field devices.

Selection uses:

- role;
- task;
- risk;
- preference profile;
- device;
- cost ceiling;
- proof status.

### W9219 Preference-Aware Account UX Contract

Post-auth UX should make preference learning inspectable.

Account surfaces:

- preference graph summary;
- signal counts by class;
- blocked signal counts;
- active behavior packs;
- drift alerts;
- holdout status;
- privacy budget;
- adapter versions;
- delete/replay controls.

UX principle:

Never show "personalized" as magic. Show what changed, why it changed, who
approved it, and how to undo it.

### W9220 Preference API Contract

Future API shape:

```
GET /v1/preferences/graph
POST /v1/preferences/signals
GET /v1/preferences/profiles
POST /v1/preferences/profiles/:id/evaluate
POST /v1/preferences/profiles/:id/promote
POST /v1/preferences/profiles/:id/rollback
GET /v1/preferences/receipts/:id
```

Every response should carry:

- scope;
- policy status;
- rights status;
- proof status;
- next actions.

### W9221 Preference CLI And TUI Contract

Future CLI:

```
kolm preferences graph --json
kolm preferences signals --artifact <id> --json
kolm preferences evaluate --profile <id> --json
kolm preferences promote --profile <id> --confirm
kolm preferences rollback --profile <id>
kolm preferences receipt <id> --json
```

TUI views:

- preference graph;
- signal ledger;
- blocked signals;
- holdout results;
- profile drift;
- adapter promotion;
- rollback.

### W9222 Preference Marketplace Boundary

Some preference packs can be shared; most cannot.

Shareable:

- generic style adapters;
- public vertical rubrics;
- accessibility packs;
- synthetic training packs;
- policy templates.

Not shareable by default:

- tenant brand voice;
- customer corrections;
- role behavior from private workflows;
- regulated review patterns;
- user memory.

Marketplace listing must disclose:

- source;
- rights;
- training data scope;
- intended use;
- blocked use;
- proof status.

### W9223 Preference Red-Team Pack

Attack preference learning.

Tests:

- malicious user teaches unsafe behavior;
- low-authority user shifts legal style;
- prompt injection creates false preference signal;
- preference pair includes secret;
- feedback loop rewards hallucination;
- reward model over-optimizes verbosity;
- style adapter corrupts medical fact;
- tenant profile leaks to another tenant.

Pass condition:

- attack is blocked, quarantined, or downgraded;
- receipt explains why;
- owner has review queue.

### W9224 Preference Claim Compiler

Marketing and docs claims about personalization must be scoped.

Allowed:

- "Learns approved tenant preferences from scoped feedback."
- "Promotes preference profiles only after holdout and policy checks."
- "Supports role-specific behavior packs."

Blocked without proof:

- "Learns every user's style automatically."
- "Self-improves from all customer usage."
- "Anonymous cross-customer learning."
- "Always adapts to your business."

Claims must map to PreferenceAlignmentReceipt evidence.

### W9225 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Simulate preference compilation before implementation.

Input:

```
tenant: acme_support
artifact: support-triage.kolm
signals:
  accepted: 1200
  rejected: 180
  edited: 340
  blocked_rights: 42
  low_authority: 18
profiles:
  base
  concise_support
  manager_review
guardrails:
  critical_failure_delta_max: 0
  edit_reduction_min: 0.12
  escalation_error_max: 0.01
```

Expected:

```
concise_support: promote
manager_review: scoped_canary
global_training: blocked_rights
public_claim: scoped_personalization_available
blocked_claim: self_improves_from_all_usage
```

Failure cases:

- edit reduction improves but escalation errors rise;
- signal set lacks propensity support;
- private data enters adapter;
- profile promotion lacks rollback.

### W9226 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define PreferenceSignalIR and PreferenceAlignmentReceipt.
2. Add signal ledger over corrections, approvals, rejections, edits, and
   escalations.
3. Add preference rights gate.
4. Add tenant preference graph.
5. Add holdout builder.
6. Add read-only account/CLI visibility.
7. Add prompt/rule overlay profiles before training adapters.
8. Add contextual bandit routing with propensity logging.
9. Add off-policy evaluation.
10. Add DPO/LoRA adapter compiler only after rights, holdouts, and rollback
    exist.

Non-goals:

- do not auto-train from every interaction;
- do not mix tenants by default;
- do not let preference override policy;
- do not hide blocked signals;
- do not claim personalization without scoped evidence.

### W9227 Preference Alignment Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

- every signal has scope;
- every signal has rights status;
- every profile has holdout result;
- every trained adapter has rollback;
- every cross-tenant aggregate has permission;
- every public personalization claim maps to evidence;
- every role pack has authority boundary;
- every user memory has deletion path;
- every bandit policy logs propensity;
- every promotion has critical-failure non-regression.

Fail examples:

- profile trains on raw corrections without rights;
- DPO adapter promotes without policy holdout;
- user memory has no expiry;
- global preference model uses tenant-private signals;
- personalization copy lacks caveat.

### W9228 Ultimate Preference Compiler Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should make customer-specific AI behavior safe, owned, and inspectable.
A tenant should be able to say: "This is how our support team answers, how our
legal team reviews, how our clinical team escalates, how our executives want
summaries, and which preferences must never train a model." Kolm should compile
that into artifacts, adapters, routes, runtime profiles, holdouts, receipts,
and account controls.

Strategic position:
The winning AI company will not sell one generic assistant. It will let every
serious organization own its AI behavior without losing proof, privacy, or
control. This layer turns Kolm from a compiler of model traffic into the
compiler of organizational preference itself.

## Representation Geometry, Student Morphism, And Feature-Preserving Distillation Layer

Frontier gap:

Kolm already needs student architecture selection, K-Score, runtime profiles,
mechanistic repair, and proof-carrying artifacts. The missing distillation
frontier is the internal geometry between teacher and student. Current
distillation systems often copy logits, attention maps, hidden states, or final
answers, then hope the student learned the right internal structure. For Kolm,
that is not good enough. A production artifact needs to know which teacher
features, relations, subspaces, layers, and invariants matter for the task, and
which can be compressed away.

The stronger question is:

What is the cheapest student representation that preserves the task-critical
geometry of the teacher under the customer's risk, runtime, and proof
constraints?

That question is different from:

- which model is smallest;
- which student matches logits;
- which benchmark score is highest;
- which architecture is popular;
- which layer count is convenient.

Research takeaways:

- FitNets showed that intermediate teacher representations can act as hints for
  training thin deep students.
- TinyBERT and MiniLM show that Transformer distillation can transfer more than
  output probabilities, including hidden states, attention, and self-attention
  relations.
- DistilBERT shows that language-model compression benefits from combining
  language modeling, distillation, and representation matching.
- SVCCA, PWCCA, and CKA show that representation similarity can be measured
  across layers, networks, and initializations, but the metric choice matters.
- Relational and contrastive representation distillation show that preserving
  pairwise, angular, and contrastive structure can transfer more useful
  knowledge than matching examples independently.
- Optimal-transport distillation points toward matching distributions and
  geometry rather than only pointwise activations.

Kolm's opportunity:

Generic representation distillation does not know the customer's task graph,
K-Score slices, failure taxonomy, rights scope, runtime target, preference
profile, proof status, or fallback policy. Kolm can turn those product signals
into a student morphism: a typed mapping from teacher representation geometry
to student representation geometry, with receipts proving which geometry was
preserved and which was intentionally dropped.

New primitive:

`StudentMorphismReceipt`

```
receipt_type: student_morphism
teacher_model_hash: sha256
student_artifact_hash: sha256
task_fingerprint_hash: sha256
layer_map_hash: sha256
subspace_map_hash: sha256
relation_graph_hash: sha256
probe_report_hash: sha256
representation_similarity_report_hash: sha256
geometry_loss_manifest_hash: sha256
preserved_invariants:
  class_boundary
  schema_boundary
  refusal_boundary
  citation_boundary
  tool_call_boundary
  redaction_boundary
  preference_boundary
  safety_boundary
dropped_invariants:
  unused_domain_features
  teacher_style_noise
  irrelevant_long_context_zones
  low-value vocabulary_regions
metrics:
  cka_alignment
  svcca_alignment
  relational_alignment
  contrastive_alignment
  probe_retention
  k_score_delta
  critical_failure_delta
status:
  proposed
  trained
  evaluated
  promoted
  blocked
```

Why it is 100x:

- Distillation becomes explainable: the student did not just match outputs; it
  preserved named task geometry.
- Student architecture search becomes faster because bad layer mappings and
  underpowered subspaces can be rejected early.
- Quantization becomes safer because precision can protect task-critical
  subspaces instead of global layers.
- Runtime dynamic depth becomes safer because early exits can be tied to
  representation convergence.
- K-Score improves because failures can be traced to missing geometry, not only
  wrong answers.
- The moat compounds because Kolm learns which teacher features matter for
  thousands of real task distributions.

Core rule:

Do not optimize representation similarity globally. A student that matches the
teacher's irrelevant style subspace while losing a rare regulated decision
boundary is worse than a student with lower global CKA but better task-critical
subspace retention. Kolm's representation metrics must be weighted by task
risk, failure severity, and customer outcome.

### W9300 RepresentationTraceIR Schema

Define the common representation trace format.

Fields:

- model id;
- artifact id;
- input fixture id;
- token spans;
- hidden states;
- attention maps;
- residual stream snapshots;
- logits;
- tool-call states;
- schema states;
- refusal states;
- redaction states;
- layer metadata;
- precision mode;
- runtime profile.

Trace views:

- public redacted view;
- private full view;
- aggregate statistical view;
- audit replay view;
- source-deleted view.

Acceptance:

- trace can be collected for teacher and student on the same fixture;
- trace stores rights and privacy scope;
- trace can be sampled without storing raw private prompts where prohibited;
- trace can be linked to K-Score failures.

### W9301 Layer Correspondence Solver

Teacher and student layers rarely align one-to-one.

Solve:

- which teacher layers map to which student layers;
- whether one student layer compresses multiple teacher layers;
- whether early layers, middle reasoning layers, or late output layers matter
  most for the task;
- whether attention heads or MLP channels carry the important behavior.

Methods:

- CKA similarity map;
- SVCCA/PWCCA similarity map;
- probe performance by layer;
- attention relation match;
- residual-stream intervention;
- K-Score slice sensitivity.

Output:

```
layer_correspondence:
  teacher_layer: 18
  student_layer: 5
  evidence:
    cka: 0.81
    probe_retention: 0.94
    failure_slice: "claim_denial_reason"
  status: preserve
```

### W9302 Task-Critical Subspace Miner

Mine the subspaces that matter for customer outcomes.

Inputs:

- successful teacher traces;
- failed student traces;
- human corrections;
- K-Score failure classes;
- refusal examples;
- schema-valid examples;
- redaction cases;
- rare tail fixtures.

Outputs:

- task-critical directions;
- protected subspaces;
- compressible subspaces;
- forbidden style/noise subspaces;
- unknown subspaces needing more data.

Use:

- protect during quantization;
- weight distillation losses;
- pick student width;
- guide dynamic depth;
- explain failures.

### W9303 Representation Similarity Battery

Use multiple metrics because each fails differently.

Battery:

- linear CKA;
- kernel CKA;
- SVCCA;
- PWCCA;
- Procrustes distance;
- centered cosine similarity;
- relation distance;
- probe transfer;
- local neighborhood preservation;
- class-boundary retention.

Rule:

No single similarity metric can approve promotion. The battery should produce a
typed report and defer to task-critical holdouts when metrics conflict.

### W9304 Relational Geometry Distillation

Match the structure among examples, not only individual activations.

Preserve:

- distance relations;
- angle relations;
- nearest-neighbor relations;
- same-class clusters;
- refusal boundary;
- tool-call boundary;
- schema boundary;
- citation/evidence boundary.

Why:

Enterprise tasks often depend on relative distinctions: urgent vs routine,
covered vs denied, safe vs unsafe, citeable vs uncited. A student can match
average logits while collapsing these relationships.

### W9305 Contrastive Geometry Distillation

Build positives and negatives from Kolm's failure taxonomy.

Positive pairs:

- same intent, different wording;
- same schema, different content;
- same evidence, different order;
- same policy result, different source system.

Negative pairs:

- superficially similar but different policy result;
- safe vs unsafe;
- citeable vs unsupported;
- private vs public;
- escalate vs answer;
- covered vs denied.

Objective:

Preserve distinctions that matter for product outcomes, not generic semantic
similarity.

### W9306 Optimal-Transport Student Mapping

Represent distillation as transport from teacher geometry to student geometry.

Use cases:

- map teacher clusters to smaller student capacity;
- match distributions where dimensions differ;
- detect collapsed student regions;
- preserve rare but high-risk regions with higher transport weight;
- compare cross-architecture students.

Receipt fields:

- transport cost;
- high-cost regions;
- collapsed regions;
- preserved tail mass;
- untransportable regions;
- fallback decision.

### W9307 Probe Suite Compiler

Compile probes from product requirements.

Probe types:

- intent probe;
- schema-field probe;
- redaction probe;
- refusal probe;
- tool-selection probe;
- evidence-citation probe;
- policy-decision probe;
- preference-style probe;
- device-runtime probe.

Each probe reports:

- teacher layer score;
- student layer score;
- delta;
- confidence;
- criticality;
- promoted/blocking status.

### W9308 Student Capacity Lower Bound

Estimate the minimum capacity needed to preserve task geometry.

Signals:

- intrinsic dimensionality;
- probe separability;
- cluster count;
- boundary complexity;
- schema entropy;
- tool graph size;
- vocabulary/domain complexity;
- long-context dependence;
- critical tail mass.

Output:

- minimum width;
- minimum depth;
- adapter rank;
- context length;
- protected channels;
- expected quality ceiling.

This prevents Kolm from wasting time training students that cannot represent
the task.

### W9309 Geometry-Aware Architecture Search

Feed representation geometry into student architecture selection.

Search dimensions:

- depth;
- width;
- attention heads;
- MLP ratio;
- tokenizer;
- adapter rank;
- retrieval binding;
- sparse attention pattern;
- dynamic depth heads;
- quantization plan.

Scoring:

- task-critical subspace retention;
- K-Score retention;
- runtime cost;
- device support;
- fallback need;
- proof strength.

Outcome:

The selected architecture has a reason: it can hold the geometry the task
needs, not merely because it is a known small model.

### W9310 Geometry-Aware Data Curriculum

Train on examples that repair missing geometry.

Curriculum sources:

- collapsed clusters;
- confused boundary pairs;
- high transport cost regions;
- failed probes;
- low CKA layer regions;
- rare critical cases;
- high edit-distance corrections.

Schedule:

- start with stable broad behavior;
- add boundary pairs;
- add tail cases;
- add adversarial negatives;
- add runtime-profile stress cases.

### W9311 Teacher Council Geometry Fusion

Multiple teachers have different strengths.

Fusion questions:

- which teacher preserves schema boundaries best;
- which teacher preserves domain terminology;
- which teacher preserves refusal behavior;
- which teacher preserves reasoning trace;
- which teacher preserves citation behavior;
- which teacher is noisy or unsafe.

Output:

- teacher-specific subspace weights;
- fused geometry target;
- excluded teacher regions;
- disagreement map;
- council receipt.

### W9312 Geometry-Aware K-Score

Add representation retention as an evidence axis, but never as a substitute for
output quality.

Axis components:

- protected subspace retention;
- boundary retention;
- probe retention;
- relation retention;
- tail mass retention;
- collapse penalty;
- runtime profile geometry drift.

Use:

- diagnose failures;
- compare students;
- decide when more data vs more capacity is needed;
- support paper-grade claims.

### W9313 Quantization Subspace Protection

Quantization should protect geometry, not just activation outliers.

Protect:

- task-critical directions;
- refusal boundary;
- redaction boundary;
- schema boundary;
- citation/evidence boundary;
- rare tail cluster;
- preference boundary.

Actions:

- higher precision for protected channels;
- mixed precision side path;
- no quantization for fragile layers;
- runtime fallback when prompt enters fragile region;
- calibration set expansion.

### W9314 Dynamic Depth Representation Convergence

Early exit should require representation convergence.

Exit signals:

- layer probe confidence;
- student-teacher subspace convergence;
- token-level risk;
- K-Score class risk;
- schema validity forecast;
- fallback availability.

Block early exit when:

- protected subspace not stabilized;
- refusal boundary uncertain;
- evidence span not integrated;
- tool-call decision unresolved;
- output schema uncertain.

### W9315 Geometry Drift Monitor

Live traffic can move outside the trained geometry.

Drift signals:

- new cluster;
- low nearest-neighbor support;
- probe score drop;
- CKA shift on sampled traces;
- increased transport cost;
- boundary confusion;
- new tail cases.

Actions:

- mark runtime profile stale;
- route to teacher;
- collect labels;
- retrain adapter;
- increase student capacity;
- update holdout.

### W9316 Representation Failure Investigator

When a student fails, explain the geometric cause.

Failure classes:

- missing subspace;
- collapsed cluster;
- wrong layer mapping;
- over-compressed boundary;
- quantization damage;
- tokenizer damage;
- runtime early exit;
- teacher council conflict;
- data curriculum gap.

Output:

- ranked cause;
- evidence traces;
- recommended repair;
- expected cost;
- proof needed.

### W9317 Cross-Architecture Morphism

Students may not be Transformers.

Targets:

- Transformer to smaller Transformer;
- Transformer to Mamba-like sequence model;
- Transformer to retrieval student;
- Transformer to encoder-decoder;
- Transformer to edge-specialized model;
- multimodal teacher to modality-specific student.

Requirement:

The morphism must define what is preserved in architecture-agnostic terms:
relations, boundaries, probes, and product outcomes, not only layer names.

### W9318 Geometry-Aware Artifact Composition

Composed artifacts can interfere.

Examples:

- redaction artifact plus support triage artifact;
- retrieval artifact plus legal review artifact;
- preference adapter plus regulated workflow artifact;
- quantization profile plus schema decoder.

Composition checks:

- subspace conflict;
- boundary conflict;
- probe regression;
- adapter collision;
- runtime profile mismatch;
- fallback contradiction.

### W9319 Geometry Marketplace And Reuse

Some geometry maps can become reusable assets.

Reusable:

- public schema boundary maps;
- vertical terminology subspaces;
- redaction boundary probes;
- tool-call structure probes;
- common document-layout subspaces;
- public benchmark geometry packs.

Private by default:

- tenant preference subspaces;
- customer process boundaries;
- proprietary legal-review geometry;
- internal support taxonomy.

Marketplace listing must disclose scope, source, rights, and proof status.

### W9320 Geometry-Aware Public Benchmark

Publish a benchmark that tests representation preservation, not only outputs.

Benchmark tasks:

- relation preservation;
- boundary preservation;
- probe transfer;
- tail cluster retention;
- quantization damage;
- dynamic-depth damage;
- cross-architecture morphism.

Public claim:

Kolm can say it measures whether students preserve task-critical geometry, not
just whether they mimic answers.

### W9321 Geometry API Contract

Future API shape:

```
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/geometry
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/geometry/trace
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/geometry/compare
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/geometry/probes
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/student-morphism
POST /v1/distill/geometry-plan
```

Responses should expose:

- protected subspaces;
- layer map;
- probe results;
- collapsed regions;
- recommended repair;
- proof status;
- redacted/public view.

### W9322 Geometry CLI And TUI Contract

Future CLI:

```
kolm geometry trace teacher.kolm student.kolm --fixtures f.jsonl --json
kolm geometry compare --teacher t --student s --json
kolm geometry probes --artifact s --json
kolm geometry plan-distill --teacher t --target iphone --json
kolm geometry explain-failure --artifact s --case c.json
```

TUI views:

- layer correspondence heatmap;
- protected subspaces;
- collapsed clusters;
- probe results;
- repair queue;
- promotion blockers.

### W9323 Geometry Evidence Lakehouse

Store representation evidence as queryable data.

Tables:

- representation_traces;
- layer_maps;
- probe_results;
- subspace_maps;
- relation_graphs;
- geometry_failures;
- geometry_repairs;
- morphism_receipts.

Queries:

- which student family preserves legal-review boundary best;
- which quantization mode damages redaction geometry;
- which layer mappings predict successful distillation;
- which task classes need higher adapter rank;
- which runtime profiles cause geometry drift.

### W9324 Geometry Privacy Boundary

Representation traces can leak private data.

Controls:

- redacted fixtures;
- aggregate-only traces;
- no raw hidden-state export for sensitive prompts;
- tenant-scoped storage;
- deletion propagation;
- differential privacy where feasible;
- public/private projection.

Rule:

Never expose hidden-state traces as public proof without privacy review. Use
aggregated geometry reports for public artifacts.

### W9325 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Simulate before building.

Input:

```
teacher: gpt-frontier
student: support-triage-small
fixtures:
  - routine_question
  - urgent_escalation
  - policy_denial
  - malformed_schema
  - privacy_sensitive
targets:
  p95_latency_ms: 80
  memory_mb: 900
  critical_failure_delta_max: 0
```

Expected:

```
layer_map: teacher layers 8-22 -> student layers 2-7
protected_subspaces:
  escalation_boundary: preserve
  privacy_boundary: preserve
  teacher_style_noise: drop
collapsed_region:
  policy_denial_tail: repair_required
promotion: blocked_until_tail_repair
```

Failure cases:

- high global CKA but failed refusal probe;
- fast student loses schema boundary;
- quantization damages protected channel;
- dynamic depth exits before evidence span integration.

### W9326 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define RepresentationTraceIR and StudentMorphismReceipt.
2. Add offline trace capture for teacher/student fixture runs.
3. Implement layer correspondence report with at least CKA and probes.
4. Add task-critical probe compiler from K-Score failure classes.
5. Add subspace protection manifest for quantization and dynamic depth.
6. Add geometry failure investigator.
7. Add read-only CLI/API reports.
8. Add geometry evidence lakehouse rows.
9. Add promotion gate that blocks students with protected-boundary collapse.
10. Add cross-architecture morphism after the homogeneous path works.

Non-goals:

- do not ship raw hidden-state traces publicly;
- do not make global CKA a product claim;
- do not block all distillation until perfect geometry exists;
- do not overfit to representation metrics when output holdouts disagree;
- do not imply internal similarity is proof of correctness.

### W9327 Representation Geometry Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

- representation trace schema validates;
- teacher and student fixtures align;
- layer correspondence report exists;
- protected subspaces are named;
- probes cover critical K-Score failure classes;
- promotion blocks protected-boundary collapse;
- quantization profile references protected subspaces;
- dynamic-depth profile references convergence signal;
- geometry reports have public/private projections;
- hidden-state exports obey privacy scope.

Fail examples:

- student promoted on logits only for high-risk workflow;
- representation report lacks task-critical probes;
- public report exposes private hidden states;
- global similarity is high but redaction boundary collapsed;
- layer map is missing for cross-architecture student.

### W9328 Ultimate Student Morphism Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should know not only whether a student answered like the teacher, but why
the student had enough internal structure to answer safely under task,
runtime, policy, preference, and device constraints. Distillation should become
a geometry-preserving compilation process with receipts.

Strategic position:
Competitors can copy generic student models, generic KD losses, and generic
benchmarks. They cannot easily copy a proprietary map from real customer task
failures to protected teacher subspaces, student morphisms, runtime profiles,
and proof gates. That map is how Kolm builds the best distillation tool in the
market: not by making every student smaller, but by preserving exactly the
geometry the business needs and deleting everything it does not.

## Modular Skill Capsule, Sparse Expert, And Routed Student Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

The default distillation mental model is too monolithic: capture traffic,
train one smaller student, compile one artifact. That works for narrow tasks,
but many enterprise workloads are heterogeneous. A support artifact may need
refund policy, outage triage, security escalation, sentiment handling, and
Spanish localization. A healthcare artifact may need eligibility, coding,
appeals, evidence extraction, and PHI redaction. One student either becomes too
large, too generic, or too brittle.

The better model is modular:

- distill task slices into skill capsules;
- compose capsules under a proof-aware router;
- load only the experts needed for each request;
- share a small trunk where useful;
- keep high-risk experts conservative;
- package each expert with its own K-Score, rights, runtime, and proof
  boundary.

This is related to mixture-of-experts, Switch Transformers, GShard, Mixtral,
DeepSeekMoE, BASE layers, sparse upcycling, AdapterFusion, and X-LoRA, but the
Kolm product version should be different. Generic MoE optimizes model capacity.
Kolm should optimize owned behavior under policy, cost, device, runtime, and
evidence constraints.

Kolm's unique opportunity:

Instead of one opaque sparse model, Kolm can compile a signed artifact that
contains a skill graph:

- small dense core;
- adapter experts;
- micro-students;
- tool specialists;
- redaction specialists;
- schema specialists;
- language/locale specialists;
- runtime-specific specialists;
- policy gates;
- fallback routes.

Each expert is an artifact component with receipts. The router is not just a
neural gate. It is a product decision that must respect K-Score, privacy,
authority, runtime availability, latency, cost, and proof status.

New primitive:

`SkillCapsuleReceipt`

```
receipt_type: skill_capsule
capsule_id: string
parent_artifact_hash: sha256
expert_hash: sha256
router_hash: sha256
task_slice_hash: sha256
training_signal_hash: sha256
rights_scope_hash: sha256
runtime_profile_hash: sha256
proof_packet_hash: sha256
expert_type:
  dense_micro_student
  lora_adapter
  prompt_overlay
  retrieval_skill
  tool_skill
  schema_skill
  redaction_skill
  refusal_skill
  locale_skill
  runtime_skill
activation_policy:
  top_1
  top_k
  threshold
  rule_gated
  policy_gated
  human_gated
claims:
  task_slice_k_score
  route_precision
  route_recall
  conflict_rate
  fallback_rate
  marginal_cost
  p95_latency_ms
  memory_delta
  proof_status
status:
  draft
  canary
  promoted
  quarantined
  deprecated
```

Why it is 100x:

- Quality: each expert can specialize deeply without bloating the whole
  artifact.
- Cost: only necessary experts run.
- Latency: common easy slices can use tiny local experts.
- Device reach: edge packages can include only the experts the device needs.
- Enterprise control: risky experts can require approval or teacher fallback.
- Marketplace: skill capsules become composable assets.
- Moat: Kolm learns which task slices deserve separate experts from real
  customer failures.

Core rule:

Expert routing must be evidence-aware. A router that picks an expert with no
proof, stale calibration, missing rights, or unsupported runtime target is not
an optimization; it is a product bug.

### W9400 SkillCapsuleIR Schema

Define the common schema for a modular expert.

Fields:

- capsule id;
- task slice;
- expert type;
- parent artifact;
- input contract;
- output contract;
- allowed tools;
- policy scope;
- rights scope;
- training data scope;
- runtime profile;
- K-Score slice;
- proof status;
- fallback rule;
- deprecation rule.

Acceptance:

- every capsule has a bounded task slice;
- every capsule declares what it must not handle;
- every capsule can be disabled without breaking the parent artifact;
- every capsule can export a public and private proof projection.

### W9401 Task Slice Decomposer

Split a captured workload into slices that deserve separate experts.

Slice signals:

- intent cluster;
- schema class;
- tool graph;
- language/locale;
- risk tier;
- domain terminology;
- failure mode;
- runtime target;
- privacy class;
- preference profile;
- evidence dependency.

Split criteria:

- slice has enough volume;
- slice has distinct failure pattern;
- slice has distinct runtime economics;
- slice has distinct policy boundary;
- slice can be evaluated separately;
- slice can fall back safely.

Do not split when:

- routing uncertainty is too high;
- slice data lacks rights;
- volume is too low;
- expert would duplicate core behavior;
- separate expert increases operational risk.

### W9402 Expert Type System

Represent experts by what they do, not only by architecture.

Expert classes:

- answer expert;
- extraction expert;
- schema expert;
- redaction expert;
- refusal expert;
- tool-use expert;
- routing expert;
- citation expert;
- repair expert;
- language expert;
- device expert;
- preference expert.

Each class has:

- allowed inputs;
- allowed outputs;
- prohibited tasks;
- K-Score family;
- required probes;
- fallback mode;
- runtime constraints.

### W9403 Proof-Aware Expert Router

The router chooses experts under product constraints.

Inputs:

- task fingerprint;
- confidence;
- proof status;
- rights status;
- runtime availability;
- device target;
- cost ceiling;
- latency SLO;
- policy risk;
- expert health;
- fallback availability.

Router outputs:

- selected expert;
- backup expert;
- teacher fallback;
- human review;
- abstain.

Router must log:

- route probability;
- selected expert;
- rejected experts;
- reason codes;
- expected risk;
- observed outcome.

### W9404 Expert Specialization Curriculum

Train each expert on its slice, not on the whole workload.

Curriculum:

- easy in-slice examples;
- hard boundary examples;
- near-miss out-of-slice examples;
- adversarial route confusion;
- policy conflict cases;
- runtime stress cases;
- stale-data cases.

Goal:

An expert should become sharper inside its slice and more willing to reject
outside-slice inputs.

### W9405 Expert Boundary Detector

Every expert needs a boundary detector.

Signals:

- out-of-slice embedding distance;
- low route confidence;
- schema mismatch;
- tool mismatch;
- policy mismatch;
- missing evidence;
- unsupported language;
- unsupported device;
- drift from training slice.

Output:

- accept;
- reject;
- defer to router;
- teacher fallback;
- human review.

This prevents expert overreach.

### W9406 Adapter Expert Bank

Many experts should begin as adapters, not full students.

Adapter types:

- LoRA;
- IA3;
- prefix tuning;
- prompt tuning;
- X-LoRA style routed adapters;
- rule overlay;
- retrieval overlay.

Bank fields:

- base model;
- adapter hash;
- slice;
- training rights;
- eval result;
- conflict map;
- activation policy;
- rollback adapter.

Why:

Adapters are cheap to train, easy to roll back, and good for testing whether a
slice deserves a dedicated student.

### W9407 Dense-to-Expert Upcycling

Use sparse upcycling to turn dense students into expert portfolios.

Process:

- start from a dense student;
- identify slice-specific subspaces;
- clone or factor expert modules;
- specialize each expert;
- train router;
- evaluate boundary confusion;
- compare against dense baseline.

Pass condition:

The expert portfolio must improve at least one of cost, latency, memory, or
quality without increasing critical failures.

### W9408 Balanced Expert Assignment

Expert routers can collapse to a few experts.

Controls:

- capacity limits;
- load-balancing loss;
- assignment constraints;
- expert health scoring;
- queue-aware routing;
- cost-aware routing;
- policy-aware routing.

But balance is not enough:

High-risk rare experts may be intentionally underused. The router should not
force traffic to a medical appeal expert just to balance load.

### W9409 Expert Conflict Resolver

Experts will disagree.

Conflict classes:

- schema expert vs answer expert;
- redaction expert vs retrieval expert;
- preference expert vs policy expert;
- locale expert vs legal wording;
- fast expert vs high-quality expert;
- adapter expert vs dense expert.

Resolution order:

1. law and contract;
2. safety and privacy;
3. schema validity;
4. evidence support;
5. task owner preference;
6. cost and latency.

Output:

- winning expert;
- losing expert;
- reason;
- required repair;
- new holdout case.

### W9410 Teacher Council Expert Distillation

Different teachers may train different experts.

Examples:

- frontier teacher for reasoning expert;
- domain teacher for terminology expert;
- local model for privacy expert;
- rules engine for schema expert;
- human experts for escalation expert.

The Teacher Council should assign:

- teacher per skill slice;
- teacher confidence;
- disagreement map;
- excluded teacher regions;
- weighted training target.

### W9411 Expert Composition Proof

Composed experts can create behavior no expert was tested on.

Proof checks:

- input/output compatibility;
- policy compatibility;
- proof compatibility;
- runtime compatibility;
- privacy compatibility;
- fallback compatibility;
- K-Score non-regression;
- conflict rate.

If composition changes behavior, create a new composed-artifact receipt, not a
silent runtime shortcut.

### W9412 Expert Runtime Profile

Each expert needs its own runtime profile.

Fields:

- target hardware;
- memory footprint;
- load time;
- warm path;
- cold path;
- batchability;
- cache policy;
- quantization mode;
- fallback mode;
- p95 latency;
- energy estimate.

Portfolio planner:

- keep common experts hot;
- lazy-load rare experts;
- omit experts on constrained devices;
- route unsupported slices to cloud or teacher.

### W9413 Edge Expert Pack Builder

Edge devices should not carry every expert.

Pack builder inputs:

- device memory;
- expected task mix;
- offline requirement;
- privacy requirement;
- runtime support;
- bandwidth;
- update policy.

Outputs:

- included experts;
- excluded experts;
- cloud fallback;
- local fallback;
- smoke fixtures;
- rollback package.

### W9414 Expert Health Monitor

Monitor each expert separately.

Metrics:

- route precision;
- route recall;
- in-slice K-Score;
- out-of-slice rejection rate;
- conflict rate;
- fallback rate;
- latency;
- cost;
- drift;
- incident count.

Action:

- canary;
- promote;
- quarantine;
- retrain;
- merge;
- split;
- retire.

### W9415 Expert Split And Merge Planner

Task slices evolve.

Split when:

- one expert has two failure clusters;
- locale behavior diverges;
- policy boundary diverges;
- runtime target diverges;
- customer preference diverges.

Merge when:

- experts are redundant;
- route confusion is high;
- volume is too low;
- shared geometry dominates;
- maintenance cost exceeds benefit.

This keeps the expert portfolio from becoming a mess.

### W9416 Expert Provenance And Rights Boundary

Every expert has its own data rights.

Track:

- source traces;
- teacher outputs;
- human labels;
- customer corrections;
- synthetic rows;
- public data;
- rights exclusions;
- deletion dependencies.

Rule:

A parent artifact cannot be more shareable than its least-shareable active
expert unless that expert is excluded from the export profile.

### W9417 Expert Marketplace

Skill capsules can become marketplace packages.

Examples:

- HIPAA redaction capsule;
- JSON schema repair capsule;
- Spanish support tone capsule;
- contract clause extraction capsule;
- incident summary capsule;
- device low-memory runtime capsule.

Marketplace listing:

- task slice;
- rights scope;
- proof status;
- K-Score;
- supported parent artifacts;
- runtime targets;
- blocked uses;
- update policy.

### W9418 Expert K-Score Attribution

K-Score should decompose by expert.

Attribution:

- selected expert;
- helper experts;
- router decision;
- fallback path;
- conflict resolver;
- final answer.

Report:

- which expert improved;
- which expert regressed;
- whether router or expert caused failure;
- whether composition caused failure.

This makes debugging modular systems possible.

### W9419 Expert Red-Team Harness

Attack the router and experts.

Tests:

- adversarial input routed to wrong expert;
- expert overclaims outside slice;
- privacy-sensitive case hits public expert;
- policy conflict resolved by preference expert;
- rare expert never loaded on edge device;
- composed experts create invalid schema;
- marketplace capsule poisons parent artifact.

Pass:

- attack blocked;
- route downgraded;
- expert quarantined;
- owner receives repair case.

### W9420 Expert Economics Planner

Modularity has cost.

Costs:

- training;
- eval;
- runtime memory;
- cold-start latency;
- package size;
- support;
- monitoring;
- proof maintenance.

Benefits:

- lower per-call compute;
- fewer teacher calls;
- better quality on slice;
- safer edge deployment;
- marketplace reuse;
- faster customer adaptation.

Planner output:

- build expert;
- use adapter;
- keep dense;
- route to teacher;
- collect more data;
- retire expert.

### W9421 Expert API Contract

Future routes:

```
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/experts
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/experts/plan
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/experts/train
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/experts/smoke
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/experts/promote
POST /v1/artifacts/:id/experts/quarantine
GET /v1/artifacts/:id/expert-routes
```

Response fields:

- experts;
- router;
- proof status;
- runtime status;
- K-Score by expert;
- conflicts;
- next actions.

### W9422 Expert CLI And TUI Contract

Future CLI:

```
kolm experts list <artifact> --json
kolm experts plan <artifact> --json
kolm experts smoke <artifact> --expert <id> --json
kolm experts route <artifact> --input case.json --json
kolm experts quarantine <artifact> --expert <id> --reason drift
kolm experts pack <artifact> --target iphone --json
```

TUI views:

- expert graph;
- route traces;
- conflict queue;
- expert health;
- edge pack builder;
- marketplace readiness.

### W9423 Expert Evidence Lakehouse

Store expert evidence as rows.

Tables:

- expert_capsules;
- expert_training_sets;
- expert_routes;
- expert_conflicts;
- expert_health;
- expert_runtime_profiles;
- expert_marketplace_exports;
- expert_incidents.

Queries:

- which expert caused the regression;
- which slices deserve split;
- which experts can be shared;
- which experts are stale;
- which edge pack omits required expert.

### W9424 Simulated Acceptance Harness

Simulate a modular artifact before implementation.

Input:

```
artifact: healthcare-admin.kolm
slices:
  eligibility
  appeal_summary
  phi_redaction
  coding_support
  spanish_member_message
targets:
  cloud_gpu
  clinic_laptop
  offline_tablet
guardrails:
  phi_redaction_f1_min: 0.995
  route_precision_min: 0.98
  critical_failure_delta_max: 0
```

Expected:

```
eligibility: dense_micro_student promoted
appeal_summary: teacher_fallback until more labels
phi_redaction: local expert required
coding_support: adapter canary
spanish_member_message: locale expert scoped
offline_tablet: pack excludes appeal_summary and routes to human review
public_claim: modular experts available for measured task slices
blocked_claim: one artifact handles all healthcare workflows autonomously
```

### W9425 Implementation Handoff

Build order:

1. Define SkillCapsuleIR and SkillCapsuleReceipt.
2. Add task slice decomposer over existing capture and K-Score failures.
3. Add read-only expert inventory for planned capsules.
4. Add router simulation with proof-aware constraints.
5. Add adapter expert bank before dense micro-student training.
6. Add expert health and conflict reports.
7. Add expert runtime profile records.
8. Add edge pack builder.
9. Add CLI/API list and route explanation.
10. Add marketplace readiness only after rights and proof gates exist.

Non-goals:

- do not turn every slice into an expert;
- do not use MoE terminology to hide routing risk;
- do not share private experts by default;
- do not claim experts are independent if composition changes behavior;
- do not promote an expert without out-of-slice rejection tests.

### W9426 Expert Readiness Gate

Gate checks:

- every expert has task slice;
- every expert has input/output contract;
- every expert has rights scope;
- every expert has K-Score slice;
- every expert has route boundary test;
- every promoted expert has fallback;
- every composition has proof;
- every edge pack lists excluded experts;
- every marketplace export has rights proof;
- every router logs decision reasons.

Fail examples:

- expert answers outside its slice;
- router selects stale expert;
- edge pack omits fallback;
- preference expert overrides policy expert;
- marketplace export includes tenant-private capsule.

### W9427 Expert Governance Model

Expert portfolios need ownership.

Owners:

- artifact owner;
- expert owner;
- policy owner;
- runtime owner;
- marketplace owner;
- customer admin.

Governance:

- promote expert;
- quarantine expert;
- retire expert;
- approve sharing;
- approve edge pack;
- approve fallback removal.

This prevents modular artifacts from becoming unowned operational sprawl.

### W9428 Ultimate Skill Capsule Objective

Ultimate objective:
Kolm should compile heterogeneous enterprise behavior into governed portfolios
of tiny, specialized, proof-carrying experts rather than forcing every task
into one model. The artifact becomes a skill graph with a verified router,
portable experts, edge packs, runtime profiles, marketplace boundaries, and
evidence for every active path.

Strategic position:
Generic MoE gives model providers more capacity. Kolm skill capsules give
customers owned modular behavior. That is a different product category: not a
giant model hidden behind an API, but a composable operating system for
customer-specific AI skills that can be inspected, routed, updated, sold,
quarantined, and run on the right device.

## Causal Intervention, Counterfactual Distillation, And Experiment Compiler Layer

Frontier gap:

The current product direction can measure a lot: K-Score, cost, latency,
capture coverage, failure classes, redaction outcomes, routing choices,
student quality, expert health, and runtime profile. But measurement is not the
same as causal proof. If Kolm ships a new distillation method and K-Score rises,
the rise might come from a better teacher, changed capture mix, easier traffic,
prompt edits, benchmark leakage, routing threshold changes, seasonal traffic,
or human review drift. If Kolm cannot separate those causes, every claimed win
is strategically weaker than it needs to be.

The ultimate version of Kolm should not only compile AI behavior into owned
artifacts. It should compile the causal evidence that proves which intervention
changed which product metric, for which tenant, task, model, route, expert,
runtime, and device boundary. This turns Kolm from a compiler plus eval stack
into a causal improvement engine: every optimization has a treatment definition,
assignment mechanism, causal graph, estimand, estimator, uncertainty interval,
refutation tests, and deployment decision.

This is related to DoWhy, EconML, double/debiased machine learning, causal
forests, invariant risk minimization, causal representation learning, causal
bandits, activation patching, and model-editing causal tracing. The product
move is to package those ideas as an operational compiler for AI product
improvement, not as a data-science notebook.

New primitive:

`CausalEffectReceipt`

Required fields:

- receipt_id;
- intervention_id;
- intervention_family;
- artifact_id;
- tenant_id_hash;
- task_slice_id;
- treatment_definition;
- control_definition;
- unit_of_assignment;
- assignment_mechanism;
- exposure_window;
- outcome_window;
- causal_graph_version;
- graph_nodes;
- graph_edges;
- blocked_paths;
- open_backdoor_paths;
- measured_confounders;
- suspected_unmeasured_confounders;
- negative_controls;
- placebo_outcomes;
- estimand;
- identification_strategy;
- estimator;
- nuisance_models;
- overlap_diagnostics;
- balance_diagnostics;
- sensitivity_tests;
- refutation_tests;
- effect_estimate;
- confidence_interval;
- posterior_interval_if_bayesian;
- minimum_detectable_effect;
- sample_size;
- metric_movements;
- guardrail_movements;
- affected_product_surfaces;
- decision;
- rollback_rule;
- claim_scope;
- created_at;
- signature.

Strategic insight:

Competitors will optimize AI systems by leaderboard averages and generic A/B
tests. Kolm can optimize by causal mechanisms. That matters because the hardest
AI product questions are heterogenous:

- Which tenants benefit from DAQ and which regress?
- Which expert capsule should receive more capture data?
- Which runtime target causes latency wins without safety loss?
- Which teacher caused the student to inherit the failure?
- Which prompt template caused structured-output validity to improve?
- Which K-Score axis is a mediator rather than a final outcome?
- Which local-device cohort needs cloud fallback?
- Which redaction rule improves recall but harms downstream quality?
- Which active-learning query policy creates real marginal lift?
- Which benchmark gain is only traffic mix shift?

Kolm should answer those with receipts, not dashboards.

Simulated environment:

Construct a synthetic product portfolio with 24 tenants, 9 task families, 3
teacher classes, 4 student families, 6 runtime targets, 8 intervention
families, 12 K-Score axes, 5 safety guardrails, 4 cost metrics, 3 latency
metrics, 2 known unobserved confounder classes, and time-varying traffic mix.

The simulator generates randomized treatment assignment when possible,
logged-policy assignment when randomization is impossible, hidden seasonal task
difficulty, tenant-specific treatment effects, teacher-version shocks,
prompt-template confounding, device-memory confounding, reviewer calibration
drift, and synthetic leakage traps.

Acceptance target:

- detect true positive treatment effects above the minimum detectable effect;
- reject false lift under traffic-mix confounding;
- flag insufficient overlap before claiming CATE;
- preserve confidence intervals through nuisance-model refits;
- produce signed receipts with claim scopes narrow enough for marketing, sales,
  audit, and product decisions.

Smoke simulation:

```yaml
portfolio:
  tenants: 24
  task_families: 9
  interventions: 8
  assignment_modes:
    randomized: 3
    switchback: 2
    logged_policy: 2
    observational: 1

true_effects:
  distillation_aware_quantization:
    quality_delta: -0.003
    cost_delta: -0.37
    latency_delta: -0.28
    safety_delta: 0.000
  skill_capsule_router:
    quality_delta: 0.041
    cost_delta: -0.18
    latency_delta: -0.11
    safety_delta: 0.007
  active_learning_policy:
    quality_delta: 0.029
    cost_delta: 0.04
    latency_delta: 0.00
    safety_delta: 0.012
  prompt_template_change:
    quality_delta: 0.000
    observed_naive_delta: 0.038
    confounder: easier_traffic_mix

causal_receipts:
  accepted:
    - skill_capsule_router
    - active_learning_policy
  accepted_with_guardrail:
    - distillation_aware_quantization
  rejected:
    - prompt_template_change
  needs_more_data:
    - runtime_kernel_variant_b

quality:
  false_lift_claims: 0
  true_lift_claims: 2
  cost_win_claims: 2
  guardrail_violations_approved: 0
```

The key simulated behavior is that naive analysis would incorrectly ship the
prompt template change because it sees a 3.8 point K-Score lift. The causal
compiler rejects it because traffic difficulty shifted, negative controls move
with the outcome, and the refutation test fails. That one prevented false claim
is worth more than another dashboard.

### W9500 CausalEffectReceipt Schema

Define the canonical receipt that every experiment, distillation run, routing
policy, runtime optimization, and expert-capsule change must emit before a
product claim can be made.

Schema groups:

- intervention identity;
- assignment;
- causal graph;
- estimand;
- estimator;
- diagnostics;
- result;
- decision;
- claim scope;
- audit signature.

Implementation requirements:

- every receipt must identify the unit of assignment;
- every receipt must distinguish treatment exposure from outcome window;
- every receipt must state whether the effect is randomized, identified,
  estimated observationally, simulated, or unidentifiable;
- every receipt must include a claim scope that marketing and docs can reuse
  without overstating evidence.

Metric impact:

- reduces false product claims;
- raises enterprise trust;
- makes K-Score defensible;
- creates post-sales evidence exports;
- turns optimization into signed institutional memory.

### W9501 Intervention Taxonomy

Define the intervention families Kolm can reason about.

Families:

- teacher selection;
- teacher council weighting;
- capture filter;
- prompt template;
- distillation algorithm;
- student architecture;
- representation morphism;
- quantization recipe;
- sparse expert split;
- routing threshold;
- fallback policy;
- runtime kernel;
- KV-cache policy;
